g: The catalogue root is a navigation container for the field journal rather than a biological ancestor or taxological rank.
tax: Domain-ranked origin systems and editorial guides begin beneath this root. Reference and Catalogue tags are editorial statuses, not biological ranks.
ap: The root has no organismal body. Its display value is the interface structure: index tree, rank badges, tabs, filters, warning marks, and entry panels.

c.g: This guide explains how to read the catalogue without mistaking guide pages, transformation pathways, inferred stems, and ordinary taxa for the same kind of entry.
c.tax: Use taxological ranks only for taxological entries: Domain, Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, and Species. Reference pages carry the Reference tag.
c.ap: Start with the rank badge and path breadcrumb. Inferred entries are visually softened; fossil entries receive a dagger; hierarchy warnings receive a red exclamation mark and underline.
c.eco: Open the branch you need from the index, then read the entry in context. The tag or rank line is the first authority for how the page should be interpreted.
c.beh: Do not convert jobs, guilds, castes, combat roles, regional offices, or cultural titles into biological species. Put culture in behavior or background under the biological entry.
c.traitsText: A complete taxon should answer what it is, where it belongs, how it is recognized, how it persists, how it behaves, what stable traits it carries, what it can do, and why it matters.
c.abilities: When editing, correct the data entry itself: rank, parentage, duplicate wrappers, and role-splits should be fixed in the taxonomy rather than hidden with prose.
c.bg: This page is a navigation and editorial guide, not a creature entry.
c.note: Guide entry: displayed headings are guide-specific rather than taxon-specific.

c.g: This guide defines rank placement discipline: an entry’s rank must describe what the entry is, not merely where it appears in the indentation of the tree.
c.tax: The expected ladder is Domain > Kingdom > Phylum > Class > Order > Family > Genus > Species. Accepted inferred orphan species may skip intervening ranks when they are reconstructed stems without ordinary subordinate hierarchy.
c.ap: Rank mismatch warnings appear as a red bold exclamation mark and red underline on the entry name. These marks are editorial flags, not creature traits.
c.eco: Use this guide when moving branches, promoting or demoting containers, or deciding whether an entry is a true taxon, transformation pathway, divine status, or reference page.
c.beh: Convergent cave shapes, occupations, spell traditions, guild roles, and regional variants should not create ranks by themselves.
c.traitsText: A well-placed entry has a parent appropriate to its rank, siblings at comparable biological scope, and descendants that step downward through the ladder unless intentionally accepted as inferred orphan species.
c.abilities: Correct structural errors before prose polishing. Duplicate wrappers, one-child filler ranks, and role-based pseudo-species should be merged, moved, or converted into background details.
c.bg: This rule prevents ecological resemblance from being mistaken for descent in a catalogue full of cave convergence.

c.g: This guide defines what a useful finished entry should contain and how to distinguish an informative field note from a stub.
c.tax: The entry should be complete across eight reader-facing functions: summary, placement, appearance, ecology or persistence, behavior, traits, abilities, and background relevance.
c.ap: Write for identification in darkness and from fragments: silhouette, limb count, posture, skull, mouthparts, integument, eyeshine, scent, gait, spoor, scale, and fossilizable structures.
c.eco: Every non-guide entry should explain persistence: reproduction, spores, fruiting bodies, corpse animation, curse transmission, planar leakage, relic anchoring, social reproduction, or an equivalent mechanism.
c.beh: Separate nature from training. A shaman, warrior, guild clerk, scout, or cultist is not a species unless the body, inheritance, or metaphysical condition is distinct.
c.traitsText: Stable traits include sensory range, size, speed, intelligence, resistances, vulnerabilities, environmental tolerances, language capacity, tool aptitude, diet constraints, and reliable field signs.
c.abilities: Abilities should be concrete and bounded. Avoid generic claims unless the power is named, observable, and limited to the correct entry.
c.bg: Strong background explains why a traveller, naturalist, Herald, route-maker, Pall-Broker, Beast-Bonded, or local community needs the information.

c.g: This guide defines a 100-point scoring system for entry quality. Score only the written entry and its immediate structural placement, not creature importance, danger, rarity, or preference. Begin every entry at 0, add points for measurable evidence, subtract penalties, then convert the final score to a letter grade.
c.tax: Base score: Summary 10; placement 12; appearance 14; ecology or persistence 14; behavior 10; traits 12; abilities 10; background relevance 8; structural hygiene 10. Award points only for entry-specific claims. A missing section earns 0 for that section. A sentence that could be pasted unchanged onto five unrelated entries earns no detail credit.
c.ap: Quantitative thresholds: a full section normally has at least 45 meaningful words and at least three concrete, entry-specific details. Appearance needs at least three observable markers, such as silhouette, limb count, posture, skull, mouthparts, integument, eyeshine, scent, gait, spoor, size, or fossilizable structures. Ecology needs at least three persistence facts, such as diet, habitat, reproduction, spores, lifecycle, formation, transmission, prey, predator, symbiont, zone, or lair dependence.
c.beh: Penalty rules: subtract 25 for a duplicate sibling or duplicate ID; 20 for a role, caste, profession, or culture treated as a biological species; 15 for rank or parentage mismatch; 12 for a placeholder wrapper with one child and no independent definition; 10 for two or more sections made of boilerplate; 8 for missing persistence; 6 for no concrete identification markers; 5 for contradiction with a parent or sibling; 3 for unclear common/scientific naming.
c.traitsText: Stub detection is automatic when any two are true: total prose under 180 meaningful words; four or more scored sections below 20 percent of available points; fewer than eight concrete details across the whole entry; no ecology or persistence mechanism; no observable physical marker; or the entry mainly restates its name and parent. Automatic stubs cannot score above D+ until expanded.
c.abilities: High-quality detection requires all of the following before assigning A- or higher: at least 420 meaningful words; all eight content functions present; at least 24 concrete details; at least one contrast with a neighboring branch; at least one field-identification marker; a defined persistence mechanism; bounded abilities rather than vague powers; and no duplicate, role-split, or unresolved hierarchy penalty. S-range entries should exceed 650 meaningful words and 40 concrete details.
c.bg: Review workflow: score the base 100 points, apply penalties, cap automatic stubs at D+, cap entries with unresolved duplicate or role-split at C-, cap entries with rank mismatch at B- unless the mismatch is an accepted inferred orphan species, then assign the grade from the cutoff table. Record the weakest three categories first so revision work is measurable.
c.note: Quantitative rubric only; internal scale calibration is not written into the catalogue.

c.g: This guide explains branches that alter organisms without being ordinary organisms themselves: curses, infections, planar pressures, divine scars, and other host-rewriting pathways.
c.tax: A pathway can be taxological in the extended catalogue sense even when it has no independent body. Rank and place it by what it reliably changes, transmits, or produces.
c.ap: The pathway’s field signs are usually effects on the host: altered teeth, shadows, scars, eyeshine, hybrid bones, moon-cycle symptoms, glamour marks, planar seams, or negative-energy residue.
c.eco: Describe transmission ecology rather than diet: bites, burial failures, oaths, relic exposure, planar breaches, inherited curses, bargains, corpse conditions, seasonal triggers, or Nadir pressure.
c.beh: Behavioral effects should be written as pressures on the host: hunger, territorial compulsion, grief loops, pack instinct, glamour etiquette, contract reflexes, shadow avoidance, or memory distortion.
c.traitsText: Separate original species traits from pathway traits so transformed dwarves, grey-folk, troglodytes, beasts, and fey-touched bodies do not collapse into one generic category.
c.abilities: Only stable pathway powers belong here: hybridization, reanimation, possession, planar resistance, glamour, regeneration, curse spread, or other repeated effects.
c.bg: This guide prevents transformed beings from being filed as ordinary descendants of the host or the thing they resemble.

c.g: This guide records current broad corrections so future passes do not reintroduce older rank and branch errors.
c.tax: Use it as an editorial checklist for domain/kingdom/phylum placement, accepted inferred orphan species, Primal naming, deific expansion, and branch-specific cleanup.
c.ap: Body-plan names must not erase anatomy: true dragons, wyrms, draconids, drakes, kobolds, wyverns, and lung dragons are not interchangeable merely because they are draconic.
c.eco: Ecological similarity should be checked against descent and transformation rules before moving an entry. Cave convergence is common in the Throat.
c.beh: Role names remain behavior or background unless they imply a distinct body, inheritance, or metaphysical condition.
c.traitsText: Branch traits must remain local to the entry. Do not copy breath weapons, psionics, regeneration, planar travel, or social traits across cousins without evidence.
c.abilities: Use corrections to fix structure first, then expand prose. A well-written paragraph cannot compensate for the wrong parent or rank.
c.bg: This page is an editorial memory aid for the revision history of the catalogue.

c.g: Inner Primordial Life is the domain of elemental, fey, unlife, and material-plane derivation: the world-facing inheritance of the Inner Primordials. It includes ordinary material organisms through Proto-Material, but also the elemental and animating substrates that predate flesh and can produce non-biological life forms.
c.tax: Domain-rank branch for life and life-like systems derived from Inner-Primordial dissolution. Elementals, Unlife, Fey, and Proto-Material are subordinate here because their origins lie in elemental matter, animating force, dreamlike living nature, or material biological descent rather than the outer planes or Far Realm.
c.ap: The domain has no common silhouette. Elementals may be bodies of fire, stone, air, or water; unlife may be animated negative persistence; fey express glamour-bound living nature; material organisms retain ordinary tissues, bones, spores, cells, or shells. Anatomy must be read at kingdom or lower rank.

c.g: Entities composed of elemental matter given animating force — not creatures made of stone or fire, but stone and fire that have become aware. The Throat holds predominantly earth and fire elementals.
c.tax: Physical substance identical to element of origin; no biological substrate whatsoever; immune to all biological conditions; typically immune to non-elemental attack. Distinguished from Beasts or Aberrations by the complete absence of biological tissue.
c.ap: Earth: roughly humanoid shapes of fractured rock 2–6 m tall with glowing mineral seams. Magma: slightly-brighter currents within the Nadir's general luminosity. Vapor: columns of superheated steam that move with directional purpose.

c.g: True Elementals is the phylum-rank grouping of organisms built wholly from one of the four primary elements within the True Elementals. Its members share a body that is pure earth, fire, water, or air — no flesh, organs, or animal ancestry — formed from concentrated elemental pressure rather than by breeding, and bound to the medium that sustains it, and divide below into class groups by the primary element of the body: earth, fire, water, or air. They explain why parts of the Throat behave like a plane intruding into a cavern: a walking bench, a flame that hunts, a sump that rears, a shaft that throws.
c.tax: True Elementals is the phylum of pure single-element life beneath the Elementals kingdom. Membership requires a body composed wholly of one primary element and a stable identity within one of the four classes — Earth, Fire, Water, or Air. Composition and formation mechanics fix the placement, never colour, size, or danger. A new class would require a primary element not already represented; the four observed classes sit directly beneath this phylum.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a mass of one element — stone, flame, water, ice, or air — behaving against the ordinary logic of its medium. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a medium-specific sign left where the body stood: tremor or shed mineral, scorch or ash, a high-water mark or breath-frost, spiralling dust or a guttering flame — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: True Elementals occupies regions of concentrated elemental pressure throughout the Throat: faulted stone, vents, sumps and falls, cold seeps, and draught and storm shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where concentrated earth, fire, water, or air pressure animates its medium into a coherent body and recur with the same pressure renews the body from the same medium after it disperses, sustaining themselves on tectonic, combustive, hydraulic, or atmospheric pressure depending on class.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members are territorial expressions of their medium, lying inert until disturbed and answering as landslide, wildfire, flood, or storm; their decisions are pressure thresholds, not plans. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a pure-element body, immunity or resistance to the mundane hazards of its own element, and a defining weakness to disruption by an opposed force. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each class and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: elemental motion, engulfing, burning, drowning, freezing, buffeting, shocking, or stone-shaping, each bounded to the body's own element. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, True Elementals matters because they account for the gorge's most dangerous 'natural' events — rockfalls, fires, floods, and storms that move with intent. It lets survey reports sort elemental hazards by element, so a crew knows whether the threat is stone, fire, water, or air before it is even seen without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Proto-Elementals (Primordia elementalis) is the theorised undifferentiated ancestor-state from which the four elemental classes are inferred to diverge. Documented from no living specimen; inferred from shared elemental properties, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — an undifferentiated elemental matrix not yet committed to earth, fire, water, or air — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is retained as an inferred orphan, not a collected body: a reconstruction from what all true elementals share. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves none observed; the form is theorised, not recorded and no carcass.
c.tax: Proto-Elementals is placed at species rank beneath (inferred stem) ((stem) order, True Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on the shared elemental properties — single-element commitment, pressure-formation, medium-failure — projected back to an uncommitted root. It is separated from its sibling the four committed classes by its uncommitted matrix versus the fixed single-element body of every observed elemental; the two share the genus body-plan but never the committed single element. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: an inferred uncommitted elemental matrix.
c.ap: Proto-Elementals stands unknown; inferred, never measured. Its silhouette is reconstructed as an unstable matrix prior to elemental commitment. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: no field marker; the entry is a reconstruction; inferred instability, resolving toward one element under local pressure; and shared traits projected from the four classes rather than observed. It moves with not observed, and shows not observed. Even after dispersal it can be identified from none; the taxon is theorised from shared properties, not from remains, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Proto-Elementals persists in no habitat; the form is inferred as the common state prior to the earth/fire/water/air divergence. It does not breed; it forms where theorised to arise where raw elemental pressure has not yet committed to one element, and recurs whenever not observed; inferred to resolve immediately into a committed class. It sustains its mass on raw uncommitted elemental pressure, drawing in inferred to draw on whatever element it resolves toward. Range is bounded by not applicable: outside that band the body theorised to collapse into a committed element rather than persist. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as not observed, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Proto-Elementals is roused by local elemental pressure, inferred to force commitment and otherwise lies not observed. It claims not applicable and answers intrusion with inferred to resolve toward the locally dominant element. Toward grey-folk it is never encountered; a reconstruction only; survey crews report that Stonesong Heralds treat it as a teaching figure, not a field hazard. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Proto-Elementals: a body of an inferred uncommitted elemental matrix; inferred resistance to disruption only until it commits; and a defining weakness — inferred instability forcing rapid commitment to one element. Its senses run through the medium itself: not observed. It tolerates not applicable but fails at commitment to a single element. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the uncommitted matrix posited as the shared root, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Proto-Elementals are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: inferred capacity to resolve toward any one element; no observed bounded abilities; inferred to express the resolving element's powers once committed; and none recorded. Each is a direct expression of raw uncommitted elemental pressure, so it cannot, for instance, be collected, measured, or confirmed as a living body. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Proto-Elementals matters because it organises the four classes under one inferred root without claiming a specimen exists. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an inferred orphan stem retained for comparison, not a field taxon, and its presence reshapes routes through no routes; it is a reconstruction in the index. It is marked theorised — no specimen — and kept as an accepted inferred orphan rather than a collected elemental.

c.g: Earth Elementals is the class-rank grouping of true elementals composed of the earth element in all its states within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of solid matter — rock, crystal, or metal — that depends on contact with the ground and forms from earth-pressure or earth-charge rather than breeding, and divide below into order groups by the state and ordering of that matter: bulk rock (Lithic), ordered crystal (Crystalline), or native metal (Metalline). Earth is the most populous elemental class in the Throat because the gorge is, above all, stone.
c.tax: Placed at class rank, Earth Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a pure earth-element body of stone, crystal, or metal, animated by tectonic pressure, seep chemistry, or earth-charge. A new order belongs here only if it shows a distinct earth-state body-plan with its own formation mechanism and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a mineral mass — boulder, crystal cluster, or metal coil — that moves against the logic of settled terrain. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — ground tremor, shed crystal, verdigris, or rust left where the body stood or passed — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Earth Elementals occupies the load-bearing stone of the gorge: benches, chutes, clay and dust galleries, crystal seams, copper galleries, and iron bogs. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fault flexes, a mineral seep saturates, or an ore-seam takes a charge and recur with the same tremor, seep, or charge renews the body from the same substrate, sustaining themselves on tectonic pressure, gravity, mineral supersaturation, or earth-charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members lie inert as terrain and rouse only to disturbance of their own substrate, answering as rockfall, shard-growth, or current. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a contact-bound earth body, immunity to the hazards of its own state, and a specific medium-failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each order and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: reshaping and absorbing ground, mass or momentum or current attacks, and concealment as terrain. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Earth Elementals matters because most fatal 'natural' stone, crystal, and ore hazards on the worked tiers are roused Earth elementals. It lets survey reports sort earth hazards by state, so a crew knows whether to fear mass, shard, smother, abrasion, or current without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Lithic Elementals is the order-rank grouping of elementals whose bodies are bulk rock, rubble, clay, silt, or shaped-stone humanoid form within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of undifferentiated stone-stuff held by earth-pressure and a complete dependence on contact with the ground, and divide below into family groups by the state of the rock — solid boulder, loose rubble, wet clay, dry dust, or shaped bipedal form (Terrae-Kin). It is the most familiar earth order to Throat crews, because most 'rockfalls', 'mud-slumps', and 'stones that walk like people' fall here.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Lithic Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: bulk-mineral composition with no crystal lattice or native-metal seam, animated by fault or seep pressure. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct rock-state body-plan with its own formation trigger and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a mass that matches its substrate — boulder, scree-sphere, mud-mound, or dust-column — and moves against the slope's logic. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — ground tremor or a wrong-way drag, swept groove, drying pan, or shed film left on the floor — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Lithic Elementals occupies the load-bearing terrain itself: benches, chutes, clay galleries, and dust shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fault flexes, a seep saturates, or a dry bed takes a static charge and recur with the next tremor, refill, or recharge of the same substrate, sustaining themselves on tectonic pressure, gravity, osmotic swelling, or static earth-charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members lie inert as terrain and rouse only to disturbance of their own substrate, answering as rockfall, mudslide, or duststorm. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a contact-bound stone body, immunity to crushing, and a specific medium-failure (immersion, drought, draught, or flat ground). These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: reshaping and absorbing surrounding ground, mass or momentum attacks, and concealment as terrain. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Lithic Elementals matters because most fatal 'natural' rockfalls, mudslides, and dust events on the worked tiers are in fact roused Lithic elementals. It lets survey reports sort terrain hazards by rock-state, so a crew knows whether to fear mass, momentum, smother, or abrasion without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Boulder-Kin is the family-rank grouping of solid-rock lithic elementals that keep a single coherent stone body within the True Elementals. Its members share a welded mass of solid rock that moves as one piece, and divide below into genus groups by whether that mass walks upright or rolls. The Boulder-Kin are the heaviest true elementals a Tier III crew is likely to wake.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Boulder-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a single welded stone body, not a bound aggregate of loose rubble. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a coherent solid-rock body with its own locomotion.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a boulder that has a low centre of mass and rights itself. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — paired pressure prints or a swept rolling groove — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Boulder-Kin occupies granite benches and steep stone chutes. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where fault pressure welds a settled stone mass and recur with renewed fault flex, sustaining themselves on tectonic pressure and gravity.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: they pose as boulders and rouse to footfall or quarrying. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: immense mass, crushing immunity, and blindness away from contact. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: stone-fusing repair and mass or momentum attacks. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Boulder-Kin matters because they are the deadliest misidentified boulders on the worked benches. It lets survey reports sort solid-rock hazards from loose-rubble ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Galeb Dur is the genus-rank grouping of walking solid-boulder elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share an upright two-legged granite body that fuses stone to heal, and divide below into species groups by size and the fault system that wakes them. The classic 'walking boulder' of the lower gorge.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Galeb Dur is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an upright, self-righting granite body with fault-seam features. A new species belongs here only if it shows a walking solid-rock body of the granite type.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a hunched boulder on two stub-legs. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — paired flat pressure prints a metre apart — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Galeb Dur occupies granite scree-benches. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where fault pressure saturates a boulder-pile and recur with tremor re-wakes the body, sustaining themselves on tectonic pressure.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as terrain, rises to block and crush. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: two-tonne granite mass and tremor-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: stone-fusing and boulder-rolling. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Galeb Dur matters because the commonest fatal 'rockfall' that walked. It lets survey reports sort walking boulders from rolling ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Greater Galeb Duhr (Saxum voluble) is a boulder-bodied stone elemental that wakes from a settled scree-pile and walks on stub-limbs of fused rock. Documented from the broken-stone benches of lower-gorge Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — grey granite mass, fault-line joints, and a gravel hide — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. At rest it is indistinguishable from an ordinary 2 m boulder, which is the source of most fatal misidentifications. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a ring of fresh-fractured granite and a shallow depression where its mass stood and no carcass.
c.tax: Greater Galeb Duhr is placed at species rank beneath Galeb Dur (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on fault-jointed granite body, retractable stub-limbs, and a low centre of mass that lets it right itself after a fall. It is separated from its sibling the Scree Tumblestone by its deliberate two-legged walk versus the Tumblestone's downhill roll; the two share the genus body-plan but never the rolling locomotion. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: coarse granite and pegmatite welded along its own old fracture planes.
c.ap: Greater Galeb Duhr stands 1.8–2.4 m and roughly two tonnes. Its silhouette is a hunched ovoid boulder with two short legs and two grasping fore-stubs. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a horizontal fault-seam that opens into a mouth-like grin when it speaks in grinding tones; quartz nodules set where eyes would be, catching lamplight as twin dull glints; and lichen and old cave-dust still clinging to the upper hide, betraying long stillness. It moves with a slow, ground-shaking two-step that registers on a Wall-Warden's tremor-line at thirty paces, and shows no light of its own, only the cold mineral glint of its quartz eye-nodules. Even after dispersal it can be identified from paired flat-bottomed pressure prints a metre apart and a trail of shed gravel, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Greater Galeb Duhr persists in the granite scree-benches and old rockfall fans of Tier III, where settled boulder-fields give it both camouflage and raw mass. It does not breed; it forms where a long-still boulder-pile is saturated by earth-pressure welling up from a fault, and recurs whenever that fault flexes again after a tremor, re-waking the same body or assembling a fresh one from the same scree. It sustains its mass on the slow grind of tectonic pressure, drawing in loose stone, which it fuses to its mass to heal and grow. Range is bounded by the reach of the active fault: outside that band the body loses cohesion and slumps back into an ordinary boulder. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a boulder that has rolled uphill against the slope's logic, leaving a wrong-way drag-scar, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Greater Galeb Duhr is roused by footfalls, hammering, or any quarrying within its bench and otherwise lies utterly inert, posing as terrain. It claims a single scree-bench and the path that crosses it and answers intrusion with rising to block the path and rolling stone down on intruders. Toward grey-folk it is indifferent until quarried; survey crews report that Forgemasters who chip its bench wake it and rarely finish the survey. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Greater Galeb Duhr: a body of coarse granite and pegmatite welded along its own old fracture planes; immune to crushing, falling stone, and ordinary fire; and a defining weakness — splitting wedges driven into its fault-seam, which can cleave it. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor-reading through the rock it touches; it is effectively blind and deaf to anything not in contact with stone. It tolerates heat, dust, and total darkness but fails at prolonged immersion, which floods its joints and locks its limbs. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the low ovoid build and the fault-seam mouth, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Greater Galeb Duhr are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it shapes and fuses surrounding stone to itself, healing damage; it rolls boulders down a slope as ranged attack; it senses approach through ground tremor at thirty paces; and it can sink into loose scree and vanish from sight. Each is a direct expression of tectonic pressure moving through a granite body, so it cannot, for instance, produce flame, fly, or cross deep water. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Greater Galeb Duhr matters because it is the commonest fatal 'rockfall' on Tier III that was in fact a defended elemental, and quarry crews need to read its wrong-way drag-scars. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a terrain hazard to be sounded with a tremor-line before any bench is worked, and its presence reshapes routes through the granite benches, which crews now cross only after sounding for a walking boulder. Its waking is taken by the Stonesong Heralds as an omen that a sleeping fault has stirred.

c.g: Tumblestone is the genus-rank grouping of rolling rubble-sphere elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a spherical body of bound talus that hunts by downhill momentum, and divide below into species groups by chute steepness and rubble grade. Fast, downhill-only, and helpless on the flat.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Tumblestone is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a self-binding rubble sphere, not a welded solid mass. A new species belongs here only if it shows a rolling rubble body with a downhill attack run.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a rough sphere that flattens at rest. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a swept central groove and a rubble fan — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Tumblestone occupies steep talus chutes. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where pressure binds loaded talus mid-slip and recur with a fresh rockfall reloads the chute, sustaining themselves on gravity.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: settles as a gravel mound, releases downhill. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: momentum mass and vibration-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: acceleration and dust-plume. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Tumblestone matters because turns every steep chute into a one-way trap. It lets survey reports sort rolling hazards from walking ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Scree Tumblestone (Saxum rotundans) is a fast lithic elemental that bundles loose talus into a self-rolling sphere and runs downhill. Documented from the steep talus chutes between Tier II and Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a packed sphere of fist-sized rubble bound by earth-pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the only lithic elemental that attacks by momentum rather than mass, reaching a sprinter's pace down a chute. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden fan of loose rubble strewn across the chute mouth and no carcass.
c.tax: Scree Tumblestone is placed at species rank beneath Tumblestone (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a spherical rubble body, a downhill-only attack run, and an inability to climb. It is separated from its sibling the Greater Galeb Duhr by its rolling sphere and downhill rush versus the Galeb Duhr's deliberate two-legged walk; the two share the genus body-plan but never the upright walking. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: loose talus, gravel, and chute-rubble compacted into a rolling ball.
c.ap: Scree Tumblestone stands 1–1.5 m across and a few hundred kilograms. Its silhouette is a rough sphere of packed rubble that flattens slightly at rest. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a banded surface where finer gravel has migrated outward under spin; a hollow grinding roar audible before it is seen, rising in pitch as it accelerates; and no fixed face, but a brief dark gap that opens uphill to draw in fresh rubble. It moves with a downhill roll that builds to running pace, helpless on the flat and unable to climb, and shows none; it is recognised by sound and the dust-plume it throws up. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a swept, gravel-scoured groove down the centre of a chute and a rubble fan at the bottom, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Scree Tumblestone persists in steep talus chutes and rockfall gullies on the Tier II–III margin, where gravity does its hunting for it. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure pulses through a loaded talus chute and binds the loose stone mid-slip, and recurs whenever a fresh rockfall reloads the chute and the next pressure-pulse rebuilds it. It sustains its mass on gravitational potential of loaded slopes, drawing in loose rubble swept up as it rolls, replacing what it sheds. Range is bounded by the bottom of its chute: outside that band the body disintegrates into an ordinary rubble fan on reaching the flat. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a chute that rattles and self-sorts its gravel without any creature visible, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Scree Tumblestone is roused by vibration upslope of it — a footstep, a dropped pack, a tremor and otherwise lies settled as an innocuous gravel mound at a chute head. It claims the length of a single chute and answers intrusion with releasing downhill to flatten whatever is below. Toward grey-folk it is purely reactive; survey crews report that Lanternkin learn never to rest at the foot of a sorted-gravel chute. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Scree Tumblestone: a body of loose talus, gravel, and chute-rubble compacted into a rolling ball; immune to bludgeoning and breaks apart only to reform; and a defining weakness — any barrier that stops its roll, after which it collapses inert. Its senses run through the medium itself: vibration only, read uphill through the loaded slope. It tolerates dust, heat, and darkness but fails at flat ground and standing water, both of which arrest it. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the spherical body and downhill-only run, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Scree Tumblestone are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it accelerates to running pace down any loaded chute; it absorbs loose rubble to repair mid-roll; it throws a blinding dust-plume ahead of itself; and it can pause as a gravel mound to ambush. Each is a direct expression of gravity acting on a self-binding rubble sphere, so it cannot, for instance, climb, cross level ground, or strike anything above it. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Scree Tumblestone matters because it turns every steep chute into a one-way trap, and crews must cross talus margins above the load line, never below. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a slope hazard logged with the chute, not the open passage, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier II–III talus chutes, now crossed high and never rested beneath. Wall-Wardens map active chutes by the swept central groove a Tumblestone leaves.

c.g: Pelitic Earthkin is the family-rank grouping of fine-sediment lithic elementals of clay and silt within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of fine sediment — wet clay or dry dust — rather than coarse rock, and divide below into genus groups by whether the sediment is wet and swelling or dry and abrasive. The Pelitic kin hunt by smother or abrasion, never by mass.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Pelitic Earthkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a fine-grained sediment body, clay or silt, not coarse stone. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a fine-sediment body with its own wet/dry formation mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a slumping mud-mound or an upright dust-column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a drying clay pan or a shed grey film — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Pelitic Earthkin occupies wet clay galleries and dry dust shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a seep wets a clay bed or a static charge raises a dust bed and recur with seep refill or dust recharge, sustaining themselves on osmotic swelling or static earth-charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: they ambush from damp or drift through still air. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: fine-sediment body, immunity to piercing, and medium-failure on drying or wetting. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing smother or abrasive choking. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Pelitic Earthkin matters because they make the clay galleries and dust shafts lethal in opposite ways. It lets survey reports sort smother hazards from abrasion hazards without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Claymaw is the genus-rank grouping of wet swelling-clay elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a bentonite body that swells on wetting to engulf, and divide below into species groups by clay chemistry and seep strength. It doubles its size on contact with water.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Claymaw is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a water-swelling clay body that smothers. A new species belongs here only if it shows a swelling-clay body fed by a permanent seep.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a low slumping mound with a folding lip. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a radial-cracked drying pan — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Claymaw occupies wet clay galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a seep keeps a bentonite bed animated and recur with the seep refills after drought, sustaining themselves on osmotic swelling pressure.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: rears a fold and collapses it over prey. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: swelling mass and moisture-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing and hard-setting. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Claymaw matters because makes wet galleries a smothering trap. It lets survey reports sort wet smother from dry abrasion without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Bentonite Claymaw (Argilla vorax) is a swelling clay elemental that engulfs prey in expanding wet bentonite and suffocates it in mud. Documented from the wet clay galleries below the Still-water seeps, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a slumping mound of grey swelling clay laced with grit — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. On contact with water it can double its volume in moments, which is both its attack and its undoing. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a drying, cracked clay pan where the body collapsed and lost its water and no carcass.
c.tax: Bentonite Claymaw is placed at species rank beneath Claymaw (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a water-swelling bentonite body, an engulfing fold rather than a bite, and rapid drying-failure. It is separated from its sibling the Loess Siltwalker by its wet, swelling, engulfing mass versus the Siltwalker's dry, drifting, abrasive column; the two share the genus body-plan but never the dry locomotion. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: sodium-rich bentonite clay that swells on wetting and sets when dry.
c.ap: Bentonite Claymaw stands 1.2–2 m mound, variable as it swells. Its silhouette is a low slumping mound that rears into a folding lip to engulf. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a glossy wet sheen that dulls to cracked grey as it dries; a slow internal churn, like a mudpot, visible at the surface; and embedded grit and shell-trace it has swallowed, studding the fold like teeth. It moves with a creeping mud-flow toward any water source, never fast, never far from damp, and shows none; faintly reflective when wet. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a polished clay smear and, later, a cracked drying pan ringed with swallowed grit, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Bentonite Claymaw persists in wet clay galleries, seep-fed mud benches, and the drip-lines under Still-water cisterns, where standing damp keeps it swollen. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure animates a bentonite bed kept permanently wet by a seep, and recurs whenever the seep refills the bed after a drought breaks the body. It sustains its mass on osmotic swelling pressure, drawing in grit, bone, and organic debris folded in and slowly dissolved. Range is bounded by the wetted margin of its seep: outside that band the body dries, cracks, and sets into a hard inert clay pan. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a clay bench that swells and slumps without rain, leaving fresh fold-marks, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Bentonite Claymaw is roused by anything that breaks its surface — a footstep into the mud, a dropped torch and otherwise lies a still glossy mound at a seep. It claims a single wet gallery and answers intrusion with rearing a fold and collapsing it over the intruder. Toward grey-folk it is ambush-passive; survey crews report that Pall-Brokers prize the cracked pans it leaves, which preserve swallowed bone. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Bentonite Claymaw: a body of sodium-rich bentonite clay that swells on wetting and sets when dry; unharmed by piercing and crushing, which it simply folds around; and a defining weakness — fire and drought, which dry and shatter it. Its senses run through the medium itself: moisture-gradient and surface-pressure sensing; blind otherwise. It tolerates acid seeps and total dark but fails at dry heat, which sets it solid. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the slumping mound and engulfing fold, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Bentonite Claymaw are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it swells rapidly on wetting to engulf and smother; it sets hard when threatened by fire, becoming a barrier; it dissolves swallowed organic matter over hours; and it seeps through cracks as soft mud and reforms. Each is a direct expression of the osmotic swelling of wet clay, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, cross dry ground, or strike at range. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Bentonite Claymaw matters because it makes the wet clay galleries a smothering trap, and crews now probe glossy mud with a pole before stepping. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a damp-gallery hazard mapped to seep lines, and its presence reshapes routes through the seep-fed clay benches, now bridged with planks rather than crossed on foot. A drying clay pan that cracks in radial fans marks where a Claymaw died and is a reliable seep indicator.

c.g: Siltwalker is the genus-rank grouping of dry abrasive-silt elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share an upright dust column held by static charge that abrades and chokes, and divide below into species groups by sediment fineness and shaft airflow. The dry-country sibling of the Claymaw.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Siltwalker is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a dry static-bound silt column. A new species belongs here only if it shows a dry upright silt body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a leaning spindle of silt. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a self-swept floor and grey film — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Siltwalker occupies dry dust shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a dry bed takes a static charge and recur with the bed recharges as dust resettles, sustaining themselves on static earth-charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: drifts into breathing-space to abrade and choke. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: abrasive dust body and air-pressure sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: abrasion and air-clouding. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Siltwalker matters because makes still dust shafts a suffocation hazard. It lets survey reports sort abrasion hazards from smother ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Loess Siltwalker (Pulvis ambulans) is a dry silt elemental that walks as a slow upright column of bound dust and scours skin and lungs. Documented from the dry dust shafts and wind-cut loess shelves of the upper tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a head-high column of fine bound silt held upright by static earth-charge — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the dry-country sibling of the Claymaw, hunting by abrasion and inhalation rather than engulfment. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden settling of fine silt that coats every surface in a fingerprint-grey film and no carcass.
c.tax: Loess Siltwalker is placed at species rank beneath Siltwalker (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a dry upright silt column, a static-bound surface, and abrasion as its primary harm. It is separated from its sibling the Bentonite Claymaw by its dry drifting column and abrasive attack versus the Claymaw's wet swelling engulfment; the two share the genus body-plan but never the wet engulfment. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: wind-sorted loess and rock-flour held by static earth-charge.
c.ap: Loess Siltwalker stands 1.6–2 m tall, a slender shifting column. Its silhouette is an upright spindle of silt, broad at the base, that leans as it walks. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a faint crackle and the smell of dry stone before contact; a surface that ripples in slow standing waves like a sand-clock mid-pour; and a grey film it sheds onto everything within a pace, the clearest field sign. It moves with a gliding lean-and-recover drift across flat dust, unable to hold form in a strong cross-draught, and shows none; it dulls lamplight by clouding the air around it. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a clean-swept floor flanked by silt drifts and a grey film on nearby surfaces, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Loess Siltwalker persists in dry dust shafts, wind-cut loess shelves, and abandoned upper galleries where fine sediment lies undisturbed. It does not breed; it forms where a deep dry loess bed takes a static earth-charge and stands up, and recurs whenever the bed re-charges after the dust resettles, raising the column again. It sustains its mass on static earth-charge in dry sediment, drawing in loose dust drawn up to replace what it sheds. Range is bounded by the still-air pocket it stands in: outside that band the body a strong draught disperses it into a settling cloud. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a dust shaft where the floor self-sweeps and a film appears on the walls, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Loess Siltwalker is roused by movement that stirs its dust or a light that warms the air and otherwise lies a tall still drift indistinguishable from a dune. It claims a single still-air dust shaft and answers intrusion with advancing into the intruder's breathing-space to abrade and choke. Toward grey-folk it is slow and avoidable if seen; survey crews report that Mist-Touched scouts wear sealed masks in any shaft showing a self-swept floor. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Loess Siltwalker: a body of wind-sorted loess and rock-flour held by static earth-charge; immune to piercing and slashing, which pass through it; and a defining weakness — water and strong draught, which knock it down at once. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-pressure and warmth sensing through its dust; blind to still cold objects. It tolerates heat and darkness but fails at any wind over a walking pace, and any wetting. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the upright spindle and shed film, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Loess Siltwalker are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it abrades exposed skin and fouls lungs at close range; it clouds the air to dim lights and blind pursuers; it draws up loose dust to repair and grow; and it can collapse and re-rise a short distance to dodge. Each is a direct expression of static-charged dry sediment standing in still air, so it cannot, for instance, cross water, hold form in wind, or strike with mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Loess Siltwalker matters because it makes still dust shafts a slow suffocation hazard, and the self-swept floor it leaves is now a standard mask-up signal. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an air-quality hazard logged with dry-shaft routes, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper dust shafts, now ventilated or masked before entry. Its grey film, dated by depth, lets surveyors tell how long a shaft has been still.

c.g: Terrae-Kin is the family-rank grouping of roughly humanoid fault-pressure stone elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a bipedal body of stone shaped by fault pressure into a recognisably humanoid form with orbital glows, and divide below into genus groups by fault depth and stone type, which determine the four body-plans of the Terrae genus. The Terrae-Kin family formalises the classic 'walking stone person' of grey-folk tradition as a distinct family within Lithic, separated from the Boulder-Kin by persistent humanoid form.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Terrae-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a bipedal fault-pressure stone body with persistent humanoid proportions and orbital glows, not a walker or roller. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a bipedal stone body with orbital glows and plinth-foot impressions.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a stone humanoid ranging 1.2–6 m with amber orbital hollows. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — orbital amber glow and plinth-impressions rather than round boulder-prints — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Terrae-Kin occupies fault zones, shallow seams, and convergent deep faults throughout the tiers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where fault pressure shapes stone into a bipedal form and recur with the fault re-pressurises and the bipedal form re-assembles, sustaining themselves on fault pressure at depth.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: all Terrae-Kin are territorial, set rigid when resting, and sense approach through the floor; the nobilis form is additionally reported to signal intent. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: bipedal stone body, amber orbital glow, plinth-foot field sign, fault dependence, and vibration failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: mass strikes, stone incorporation, floor-sensing, and rigid disguise. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae-Kin matters because accounts for most formally recorded Throat fatalities in fault zones. It lets survey reports sort humanoid stone hazards by size and stone type without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Terrae is the genus-rank grouping of roughly humanoid stone-body elementals of the fault-pressure kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a bipedal stone body held by fault pressure, with a featureless or faceted head-mass and orbital glows distinguishing them from boulders and columns, and divide below into species groups by the depth and type of fault pressure — shallow/compact (minor), standard (single fault), massive/dark (major), or metamorphic/deep-fold (nobilis). The Terrae genus is the journal's formal name for what grey-folk call 'the stone that walks like a person' — the classic bipedal earth elemental recognised across all four known forms.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Terrae is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a bipedal fault-pressure stone body distinguishable from the Boulder-Kin by its persistent humanoid form and orbital glow. A new species belongs here only if it shows a bipedal stone body with its own fault-pressure formation mechanism, stone-type composition, and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a roughly humanoid stone mass with orbital glows, ranging 1.2 m (minor) to 6 m (major). A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — orbital amber glow, plinth-impressions rather than round prints, and a double-beat floor-shake different from a Galeb Dur's single roll — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Terrae occupies fault zones and fault convergences throughout the Throat's lower tiers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where fault pressure — shallow, deep, or convergent — animates a stone mass into a bipedal form and recur with the fault re-pressurises and the same stone mass re-forms, sustaining themselves on fault pressure at various depths and types.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: all Terrae forms are territorial, set themselves rigid when at rest, and sense approach through the floor; they differ in size, speed, stone type, and the complexity of their response. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a bipedal stone body, amber orbital glow, plinth-foot impressions, fault pressure dependence, and a vibration-failure mode specific to each form. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: mass strikes, stone incorporation, floor-tremor sensing, and rigid-stone disguise; the nobilis form adds digit precision, vibration redirection, and fold-communication. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae matters because the Terrae genus accounts for most grey-folk fatalities attributed to 'the rock coming alive' and is the primary subject of Stonesong Herald classification work. It lets survey reports sort humanoid stone hazards by size and stone type, enabling a crew to identify which form is active and plan accordingly without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Terrae Elementalis (Terrae elementalis) is the standard earth elemental: a roughly humanoid walking mass of living stone 2–3 m tall, the most commonly encountered Terrae form in the gorge. Documented from the fault-veined stone passages and quarry faces of the mid-lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a humanoid body of mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz — held in rough bipedal form by tectonic pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Galeb Dur is a boulder that walks, the Terrae is stone shaped roughly into a person: a mass that has found a convenient bipedal form for crossing passages. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scatter of mixed stone rubble in a rough humanoid outline, slowly sinking into the floor and no carcass.
c.tax: Terrae Elementalis is placed at species rank beneath Terrae (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a roughly humanoid stone body 2–3 m tall with a featureless head-mass, two arm-masses, and a broad stone trunk. It is separated from its sibling Terrae elementalis minor by its mid-sized 2–3 m standard body versus the minor form's compact sub-2 m mass; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the compact sub-2 m form. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz veins — shaped by tectonic pressure into a bipedal form.
c.ap: Terrae Elementalis stands 2–3 m and 3–6 tonnes. Its silhouette is a roughly humanoid mass with a rounded head, two distinct arm-masses, and legs that merge below the knee into a broad stone plinth. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a featureless smooth head-mass with two deep orbital hollows that glow faint amber in active bodies; arm-masses ending in blunt stone mitten-hands, leaving flat-edged impact marks rather than claw strikes; and a gait that shakes the floor measurably at 10 m, distinguishable from a Galeb Dur's two-step by the double-beat of leg-plinth and body-mass. It moves with a slow powerful stride on a broad base, shaking the floor at 10 m, leaving two plinth-impressions and a body-draught, and shows faint amber glow in the two orbital hollows, visible up to 30 m in dark passages. Even after dispersal it can be identified from two rounded plinth-impressions flanking a central drag-groove from the body-mass, and scored stone where its fists struck, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Terrae Elementalis persists in fault-veined passages, worked quarry faces, and stone-pressure zones of the mid-lower tiers where tectonic pressure can sustain a large mass. It does not breed; it forms where deep tectonic pressure along a major fault line animates mixed stone into a bipedal form, and recurs whenever the fault re-pressurises and the same volume of stone re-forms at the same site. It sustains its mass on deep tectonic pressure, drawing in loose stone and fault-debris incorporated into the mass. Range is bounded by the fault pressure zone: outside that band the body slows, loses bipedal coherence, and slumps into rubble. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a fault exposure where stone fragments have been moving upward rather than falling, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Terrae Elementalis is roused by sustained vibration (drilling, blasting) or a crew crossing its fault zone and otherwise lies an irregular standing stone mass indistinguishable from a pressure-split column. It claims a single fault zone or quarry face and answers intrusion with striking with flat-mitted fists and attempting to push prey into walls or corners. Toward grey-folk it is territorial but predictable; survey crews report that Stonesong Heralds mark fault zones with a three-notch chisel-sign meaning 'standing stone that walks'. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Terrae Elementalis: a body of mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz veins — shaped by tectonic pressure into a bipedal form; immune to crushing and fire; and a defining weakness — sustained vibration at its natural resonance, which disaggregates the mixed-stone body. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor sensing through its plinth-base at 50 m, and pressure sensing through the fault it stands on. It tolerates heat, dust, and darkness but fails at sustained resonance and loss of fault pressure. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the 2–3 m bipedal form and amber orbital glow, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Terrae Elementalis are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it strikes with flat-mitted fists, leaving flat-edged bruises rather than cuts; it incorporates loose stone to repair damage on the move; it senses approach through its plinth-base at 50 m before the crew can see it; and it can set itself rigid as natural stone, requiring resonance testing to identify. Each is a direct expression of deep tectonic pressure shaping a mixed-stone bipedal form, so it cannot, for instance, conduct current, fly, cross deep water, or produce flame. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae Elementalis matters because it is the most frequently encountered large elemental in the worked fault zones, and the three-notch chisel-sign now marks every quarry wall it has been observed on. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a fault-zone territorial hazard, logged by the Stonesong Heralds before any quarry lease is signed, and its presence reshapes routes through the mid-lower fault passages, now surveyed by tremor-rod before deep drilling begins. The Stonesong Heralds have a classification tradition that the standard Terrae is the 'primary voice' of the fault — the most common body the stone takes when it speaks.

c.g: Terrae Elementalis Minor (Terrae elementalis minor) is the small-form earth elemental: a compact, quick Terrae body under 1.8 m, formed by shallow fault pressure and dispersed more easily than its standard kin. Documented from the narrow side-passages, collapse galleries, and shallow fault seams of the upper-mid tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a compact humanoid body of lighter stone — sandstone, schist, and thin quartz — held by shallow fault pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Minor is not a juvenile of the standard form — it is a separate heritable body-plan formed at shallower pressure, with its own anatomy and field signs. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scatter of smaller, lighter stone fragments — sandstone and schist — across the floor and no carcass.
c.tax: Terrae Elementalis Minor is placed at species rank beneath Terrae (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a compact sub-1.8 m stone humanoid, lighter stone types, and faster movement than the standard form. It is separated from its sibling Terrae elementalis by its compact sub-1.8 m body and lighter stone versus the standard form's 2–3 m mixed-stone mass; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the larger mixed-stone standard mass. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: lighter stone types — sandstone, schist, and thin quartz — shaped by shallow fault pressure into a compact bipedal form.
c.ap: Terrae Elementalis Minor stands 1.2–1.8 m and 0.5–1.5 tonnes. Its silhouette is a compact stone humanoid with finer proportions, narrower plinth-feet, and a head-mass that shows some faceting. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: lighter stone types (sandstone, schist) visible in the body surface, contrasting with the darker mixed-stone standard form; faster, lighter movement — under half the floor-shake of the standard form at 10 m; and head-mass that shows slight faceting around the orbitals, giving it a more defined face than the standard's smooth oval. It moves with a quick, light stride that registers at 4–5 m on a tremor-rod rather than the standard's 10 m, and shows faint amber orbitals, dimmer than the standard form. Even after dispersal it can be identified from narrow plinth-impressions (20 cm wide vs 40 cm for standard) and lighter, shallower drag-groove, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Terrae Elementalis Minor persists in narrow side-passages, collapse galleries, and shallow fault seams of the upper-mid tiers where shallow pressure can hold a smaller mass. It does not breed; it forms where shallow fault pressure animates lighter stone into a compact humanoid body, and recurs whenever the shallow fault re-pressurises and the compact body re-forms from the same lighter stone. It sustains its mass on shallow fault pressure, drawing in lighter stone debris and schist flakes incorporated into the compact mass. Range is bounded by the shallow fault seam zone: outside that band the body loses coherence faster than the standard form; compact bodies break apart at greater distance from pressure. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a narrow side-passage where schist and sandstone fragments have moved upward in a concentrated line, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Terrae Elementalis Minor is roused by approach to within 5 m of its seam or the sound of tools in adjacent passages and otherwise lies a compact stone pile in a narrow passage, easily mistaken for a collapse. It claims a single narrow passage or side-gallery and answers intrusion with quick targeted blows and rapid retreat into the narrow passage where its smaller size is an advantage. Toward grey-folk it is more reactive and faster than the standard form; survey crews report that Crews in narrow side-passages now carry a short tremor-rod, since the minor form's 4–5 m detection range gives little warning. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Terrae Elementalis Minor: a body of lighter stone types — sandstone, schist, and thin quartz — shaped by shallow fault pressure into a compact bipedal form; immune to crushing but disperses faster under vibration than the standard form due to lighter stone; and a defining weakness — vibration and loss of shallow pressure, both of which disperse it rapidly. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor sensing through plinth-base at 20 m, and shallow pressure sensing in its seam. It tolerates confined spaces, cold, and darkness but fails at vibration and loss of shallow fault pressure. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the compact 1.2–1.8 m body, lighter stone types, and narrow plinth-impressions, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Terrae Elementalis Minor are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it delivers quick targeted blows and retreats into the narrow passage; it uses the passage geometry to avoid retaliation from larger targets; it repairs from lighter stone debris faster than the standard form (smaller mass to fill); and it can squeeze through a 0.6 m gap, which the standard form cannot. Each is a direct expression of shallow fault pressure shaping a compact lighter-stone bipedal form, so it cannot, for instance, match the mass force of the standard or major form, or sustain itself far from its shallow seam. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae Elementalis Minor matters because it patrols the narrow side-passages crews use as access routes and is faster and harder to detect than the standard form at short range. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a narrow-passage territorial hazard, requiring a short tremor-rod for detection, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper-mid side-passages, now tremor-tested before use as access routes. The Stonesong Heralds classify the minor form as the 'secondary voice' — the stone speaking in tight places where only a smaller body fits.

c.g: Terrae Elementalis Major (Terrae elementalis major) is the great-form earth elemental: a 4–6 m massive Terrae body born of deep fault convergences, capable of bringing down a passage ceiling with a single strike. Documented from the major fault convergences and deep pressure zones of the lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a massive humanoid body of coarse granite, gabbro, and iron-oxide inclusions, held by deep convergent fault pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. The major form is not a grown-up standard — it is a distinct body-plan that emerges only from convergent faults, where multiple pressure lines meet. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a collapse of coarse granite and gabbro blocks in a rough giant-humanoid outline and no carcass.
c.tax: Terrae Elementalis Major is placed at species rank beneath Terrae (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a 4–6 m stone humanoid of coarse dark rock, convergent-fault formation, and ceiling-shaking stride. It is separated from its sibling Terrae elementalis by its 4–6 m coarse dark body from a convergent fault versus the standard form's 2–3 m mixed stone from a single fault; the two share the genus body-plan but never the single-fault formation. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: coarse granite, gabbro, and iron-oxide inclusions drawn from a convergent fault where multiple pressure lines meet.
c.ap: Terrae Elementalis Major stands 4–6 m and 20–50 tonnes. Its silhouette is a massive humanoid with a broad flat head, arm-columns wider than a grey-folk torso, and leg-columns that leave half-metre imprints. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: coarse dark gabbro and iron-oxide inclusions visible in the surface — dark grey-black with rust-red veins — unlike the pale mixed standard form; a stride that registers on a tremor-rod at 100 m, and causes loose ceiling stone to fall at 20 m; and arm-columns that score parallel grooves in soft passage walls as it passes, each groove 30–40 cm wide. It moves with a deep slow stride that vibrates the floor at 100 m and causes ceiling-stone to fall at 20 m, and shows deep amber orbitals, the brightest of the three standard Terrae forms, visible at 80 m. Even after dispersal it can be identified from half-metre plinth-impressions pressed 10 cm into soft stone, parallel wall-grooves from passing arm-columns, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Terrae Elementalis Major persists in major fault convergences and deep pressure zones of the lower tiers where multiple fault lines supply enough pressure to sustain a 20–50 tonne mass.
