It does not breed; it forms where convergent deep fault pressure along two or more intersecting faults animates coarse rock into a massive humanoid body, and recurs whenever the convergent fault re-charges and the massive body re-forms from the same coarse rock in the same zone. It sustains its mass on convergent deep fault pressure, drawing in coarse rock and fault-breccia incorporated into the massive form. Range is bounded by the convergent fault pressure zone: outside that band the body loses cohesion at the junction edges; the major form fragments into boulder-scale debris rather than rubble. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a passage where two fault lines cross and the junction has begun to produce upward-moving coarse fragments, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Terrae Elementalis Major is roused by any quarrying, blasting, or heavy machinery in its fault convergence zone and otherwise lies a standing coarse-stone mass the height of a loaded cart, easily mistaken for a natural block. It claims a single fault convergence zone and its approach passages and answers intrusion with ceiling strikes first to collapse the passage, cutting off retreat, then advancing on the trapped crew. Toward grey-folk it is extremely hazardous and unpredictable; survey crews report that The Forgemasters consult the Stonesong Heralds before any work near a fault convergence, since a woken major form cannot be outrun. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Terrae Elementalis Major: a body of coarse granite, gabbro, and iron-oxide inclusions drawn from a convergent fault where multiple pressure lines meet; immune to crushing, fire, and ordinary tools; the 20–50 tonne mass makes physical displacement impossible for unaided crews; and a defining weakness — the same convergent resonance that formed it — a matched vibration from two directions simultaneously. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor sensing at 200 m through the convergent fault; it often knows a crew is coming before the crew has found the passage. It tolerates extreme heat, dust, and darkness but fails at matched convergent vibration disrupting the dual-fault pressure. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the 4–6 m dark coarse body, deep amber orbitals, and ceiling-shake stride, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Terrae Elementalis Major are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it strikes ceilings to collapse passages and trap prey; it engulfs smaller prey in the grip of its arm-columns; it incorporates large boulders to repair damage rapidly; and it can set itself as a solid block across a passage mouth, sealing a crew inside. Each is a direct expression of convergent deep fault pressure sustaining a 20–50 tonne mixed-dark-rock humanoid form, so it cannot, for instance, fit into passages under 3 m wide, move quietly, or persist away from its convergent fault. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae Elementalis Major matters because it is the most dangerous single elemental the Throat's working crews are likely to encounter, and its 100 m tremor detection range makes blasting near convergent faults effectively suicidal without prior Stonesong Herald survey. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an extreme territorial hazard requiring Herald consultation and dual-vibration dispersal equipment before any work near a convergent fault, and its presence reshapes routes through the lower fault-convergence zones, now strictly Herald-surveyed before any lease. The Stonesong Heralds classify the major form as the 'third voice' — the stone at its most authoritative, speaking from where the faults agree.

c.g: Terrae Elementalis Nobilis (Terrae elementalis nobilis) is the high-form earth elemental: a rare Terrae body-plan distinguished from the standard, minor, and major forms by a larger and more complex cranial mass, articulated stone digits rather than mitten-hands, and formation only at exceptional deep-fold pressure — a heritable anatomy, not a rank. Documented from the deepest tectonic fold-pressure zones; not confirmed in the active Gorge, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a precisely articulated stone humanoid of layered gneiss, marble, and primary feldspar, held by deep fold-pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. The nobilis form has been treated as a 'prince' class in many cultural traditions, but this journal classifies it by anatomy: it is a distinct body-plan with a larger cranial mass, articulated stone digits, and layered metamorphic stone that no other Terrae form shares. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a carefully stratified collapse of layered gneiss and marble that preserves rough limb outlines longer than other forms and no carcass.
c.tax: Terrae Elementalis Nobilis is placed at species rank beneath Terrae (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a precisely articulated layered-metamorphic body with a large-domed cranial mass, true stone fingers rather than mitten-hands, and a formation requirement of deep fold-pressure. It is separated from its sibling Terrae elementalis major by its precisely articulated layered-metamorphic anatomy with stone fingers and a large domed cranium versus the major form's coarse undifferentiated dark mass and mitten-hands; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the coarse undifferentiated major mass. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: layered gneiss, marble, and primary feldspar arranged in a differentiated humanoid body by deep fold-pressure; the stone types are qualitatively different from all other Terrae, not merely larger.
c.ap: Terrae Elementalis Nobilis stands 3–4 m and 10–20 tonnes — smaller than the major form but far more precisely structured. Its silhouette is a humanoid body with distinct layered banding across its surface, a clearly domed cranial mass larger relative to body than any other Terrae form, and individual stone digits on each hand rather than a mitten. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: distinct layered banding of gneiss and marble across the body surface, unlike the undifferentiated mixed stone of standard and major forms; individual articulated stone digits — five per hand — the only Terrae form with this trait; these leave five-point pressure marks rather than flat-edged impact marks; and a cranial mass that is one-quarter body width at its widest, visibly larger relative to body than in the standard or major form, and that carries a bright amber orbital glow visible at 150 m. It moves with a precisely balanced stride at 30 m tremor detection range — less than the major form's 100 m, because the layered body is better jointed and transfers less energy through the floor, and shows the brightest orbital glow of all Terrae forms, visible at 150 m in total darkness. Even after dispersal it can be identified from five-point hand-impressions 40 cm across where it has steadied itself, and layered gneiss-flakes shed along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Terrae Elementalis Nobilis persists in deep fold-pressure zones where metamorphic rock has been repeatedly recrystallised — gneiss terrains, marble formations, and feldspar-rich deep intrusions; not confirmed in the active Gorge but inferred from the layered stone it sheds. It does not breed; it forms where deep fold-pressure acting on already-metamorphic rock recrystallises it into a precisely layered body, a process requiring more sustained and deeper pressure than the standard or major form, and recurs whenever the fold-pressure re-charges and the layered body re-forms, though the process takes far longer than standard fault-pressure reformation. It sustains its mass on deep fold-pressure acting on metamorphic stone, drawing in metamorphic rock incorporated at the fold — specifically the layered types that match its body-plan chemistry. Range is bounded by the deep metamorphic fold-pressure zone: outside that band the body sheds layered flakes progressively and loses digit articulation before the body itself fails, unlike the rubble-collapse of other forms. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a metamorphic fold-exposure where gneiss layers have begun moving in parallel rather than slipping against each other, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Terrae Elementalis Nobilis is roused by disturbance of the deep fold it stands in; seismic events rather than drilling, given the depth and otherwise lies a stratified standing mass that geologists initially classify as a metamorphic column. It claims a deep fold zone and the approach tunnels cut toward it and answers intrusion with communication attempts before force — the nobilis form is the only Terrae consistently reported to acknowledge grey-folk as distinct from random vibration, gesturing them away before striking. Toward grey-folk it is context-dependent; reported to gesture away non-threatening crews rather than strike immediately, though this is not universal and should not be relied upon; survey crews report that the Stonesong Heralds hold a formal protocol for approaching a nobilis site — three slow raps of a stone rod on the floor, spaced five seconds apart — which is understood to mean 'we are not drilling'. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Terrae Elementalis Nobilis: a body of layered gneiss, marble, and primary feldspar arranged in a differentiated humanoid body by deep fold-pressure; the stone types are qualitatively different from all other Terrae, not merely larger; immune to crushing, fire, and vibration more effectively than other Terrae forms; the layered gneiss body absorbs and redirects vibration rather than resonating with it; and a defining weakness — targeted acid on the marble layers, which dissolves the binding chemistry; this fails the body more rapidly than any vibration approach. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor sensing at 300 m through fold-pressure; pressure sensing that extends to adjacent fault systems not directly connected; the large cranial mass is associated with an expanded sensing range in all Stonesong Herald records. It tolerates extreme heat, deep pressure, and total darkness but fails at targeted acid on the marble binding layers. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the layered banding, domed cranial mass, individual stone digits, and 150 m orbital glow, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Terrae Elementalis Nobilis are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it can redirect vibration through its layered body rather than resonating with it, making standard Terrae dispersal approaches ineffective; it shapes stone with its articulated digits rather than mass-striking — it can notch, groove, and seal passages with precision; it can communicate intent through the floor to other Terrae in its fold zone, alerting standard and major forms to a threat; and it repairs from its specific metamorphic stone types selectively — it will not incorporate basalt or sandstone, unlike other Terrae. Each is a direct expression of deep fold-pressure acting on a precisely layered metamorphic body with an enlarged cranial sensing mass, so it cannot, for instance, be found in shallow fault zones, move quickly, or form in the active Gorge at recorded pressure depths. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Terrae Elementalis Nobilis matters because its five-point hand-impressions and gneiss-flake trail, if found in the Gorge's deeper surveys, would indicate a fold-pressure zone too deep and metamorphic for the current working tiers — a significant finding for Forgemaster route-planning. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as not an active field hazard in the Gorge; retained as a context entry and a comparison form for Terrae anatomy classification, and its presence reshapes routes through no active route impact; five-point hand-impressions in a deep survey passage would close the lease pending Herald assessment. Stonesong Herald oral records refer to the nobilis form as the 'deep counsel' — not a ruler, but a body that the stone makes when it has the time and pressure to be precise. The three-rap protocol is old enough that no Herald alive knows who taught it.

c.g: Crystalline Elementals is the order-rank grouping of elementals whose bodies are ordered crystal lattices grown from mineral-rich seep within the True Elementals. Its members share a self-templating crystal body that grows, sheds, and regrows faster than bulk rock can be fused, and divide below into family groups by the mineral and its habit — hard ringing quartz or soft cleaving gypsum. The Crystalline order is what the Forgemasters most want to harvest and most often die harvesting.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Crystalline Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an ordered crystal-lattice body fed by mineral supersaturation, not a bound rubble or sediment mass. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct crystal habit with its own seep chemistry and regrowth mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a faceted cluster or blade-sheaf that walks on grown crystal and regrows what it sheds. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a litter of shed crystal or fibre and glassy scratch-grooves on the floor — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Crystalline Elementals occupies vugs, crystal seams, and gypsum-curtain chambers fed by mineral seep. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a supersaturated seep nucleates a self-templating lattice around a pressure node and recur with the seep re-saturates and the lattice re-nucleates, sustaining themselves on silica or sulphate supersaturation in seep water.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members pose as natural crystal and rouse to the stimulus their lattice resonates to — sound or touch. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: an ordered crystal body, slashing and piercing immunity, rapid regrowth, and a specific resonance or dryness failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: directed crystal growth, light or sound effects, and self-sharpening blades. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Crystalline Elementals matters because its galleries are the richest crystal sources in the Throat and the most lethal to work. It lets survey reports sort crystal hazards by habit, so a crew knows whether silence or distance keeps them alive without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Quartz-Kin is the family-rank grouping of hard silicate crystalline elementals built on quartz lattices within the True Elementals. Its members share a hard glassy quartz body that rings and refracts, and divide below into genus groups by solid shard-growth versus hollow resonant geode form. The hardest-bodied of the crystalline elementals.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Quartz-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a hard quartz-lattice body fed by silica seep. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a hard silicate body with its own growth habit.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a faceted glassy cluster throwing scattered glints. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — shed perfect crystals and glassy grooves — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Quartz-Kin occupies silica-rich vugs and quartz seams. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where silica seep nucleates a quartz lattice and recur with the seep re-saturates the vug, sustaining themselves on silica supersaturation.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as a crystal cluster, grows blades to a stimulus. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: hard glassy body and acoustic resonance. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: shard-growth and light-refraction. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Quartz-Kin matters because the richest and most dangerous crystal to harvest. It lets survey reports sort hard quartz hazards from soft gypsum ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Latticeling is the genus-rank grouping of hollow resonant quartz-shell elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a hollow faceted shell with a ringing core and outward shard-growth, and divide below into species groups by crystal colour and core volume. Recognised by its bell-like ring when struck.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Latticeling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a hollow self-templating quartz shell. A new species belongs here only if it shows a hollow ringing quartz body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a clustered geode on crystal stilts. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a bell ring and scattered glints — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Latticeling occupies vug-lined crystal galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where silica seep crystallises a hollow shell and recur with the vug re-saturates, sustaining themselves on silica supersaturation.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: rings and grows blades to noise. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: hollow ringing shell and refraction. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: shard-growth and disorienting tone. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Latticeling matters because kills careless crystal crews. It lets survey reports sort hollow ringing forms from solid ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Geode Latticeling (Crystallus geodicus) is a crystalline elemental that grows as a hollow geode-shell of interlocking quartz and strikes with shard-growth. Documented from the vug-pocketed crystal galleries of Tier II, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a faceted shell of clear and amethyst quartz around a hollow resonant core — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It builds and sheds blade-sharp crystal faster than any lithic kin can fuse stone. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scatter of perfect loose crystals and a cracked hollow shell and no carcass.
c.tax: Geode Latticeling is placed at species rank beneath Latticeling (Crystalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a hollow faceted quartz shell, directed crystal growth as attack, and a resonant ringing core. It is separated from its sibling the Selenite Bladeling by its hard ringing quartz shell versus the Bladeling's soft cleaving gypsum blades; the two share the genus body-plan but never the cleavage softness. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: interlocking quartz and amethyst grown on a self-templating lattice.
c.ap: Geode Latticeling stands 0.8–1.4 m, a clustered geode that walks on crystal stilts. Its silhouette is a faceted hollow ball bristling with point-terminated crystals. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a clear bell-like ring when struck, from its hollow core; internal light refraction that throws lamp-glints in scattered points; and fresh blade-bright crystal at any recent break, regrown within minutes. It moves with a tinkling stilt-walk on grown crystal legs that snap and regrow as it moves, and shows no native light but intense refraction that splinters any lamp into a constellation. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a litter of shed perfect crystals and scratched glassy grooves on the floor, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Geode Latticeling persists in vug-lined crystal galleries and quartz seams of Tier II, where silica-rich seep keeps its lattice fed. It does not breed; it forms where silica-saturated seep crystallises around a pressure node into a self-templating shell, and recurs whenever the seep re-saturates the vug and the lattice re-nucleates. It sustains its mass on silica supersaturation in seep water, drawing in dissolved silica drawn from seep, regrown as shell and blades. Range is bounded by the silica-rich seep zone: outside that band the body stops regrowing and is slowly worn to loose crystal. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a vug whose crystals point the wrong way, against the usual inward growth, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Geode Latticeling is roused by a sharp sound or strike, which its core resonates to and otherwise lies indistinguishable from a natural crystal cluster. It claims a single crystal gallery and answers intrusion with growing blades outward to impale and ringing to disorient. Toward grey-folk it is reactive to noise; survey crews report that Forgemasters crave its shed crystals but learn to work the gallery in silence. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Geode Latticeling: a body of interlocking quartz and amethyst grown on a self-templating lattice; immune to slashing and piercing, which only chip regrowing facets; and a defining weakness — a sustained resonant tone that shatters its hollow shell. Its senses run through the medium itself: acoustic resonance through its core; effectively deaf to anything that does not vibrate it. It tolerates heat and acid seep but fails at a matched resonant frequency, which fractures it. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the hollow ringing shell and point-terminated crystals, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Geode Latticeling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it grows blade-sharp crystal outward to impale at arm's reach; it rings a disorienting tone from its hollow core; it regrows broken facets within minutes from seep silica; and it refracts light to dazzle and confuse aim. Each is a direct expression of self-templating silica crystallisation around a resonant void, so it cannot, for instance, produce flame, cross dry ground, or strike beyond blade reach. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Geode Latticeling matters because its galleries are prized for crystal yet kill the careless, and crews now work them in silence to avoid resonating the core. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a noise-triggered hazard logged with crystal seams, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier II crystal galleries, now worked quietly and shored against shard-growth. A vug of wrong-pointing crystals is the Forgemasters' warning that a Latticeling is nucleating.

c.g: Evaporite Crystalkin is the family-rank grouping of soft sulphate and salt crystalline elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a soft, often fibrous evaporite body that cleaves rather than shatters, and divide below into genus groups by the evaporite mineral — fibrous gypsum among them. Soft, silent, and self-sharpening, the opposite of the Quartz-Kin.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Evaporite Crystalkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a soft evaporite-lattice body fed by sulphate or salt seep. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a soft evaporite body with its own cleavage habit.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a satin blade-sheaf or salt cluster with a soft sheen. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — silky fibre-heaps and parallel scratch-fans — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Evaporite Crystalkin occupies gypsum curtains and salt-evaporite seeps. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a sulphate seep grows a fibrous mass and recur with the evaporite seep refeeds it, sustaining themselves on sulphate or salt supersaturation.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as a curtain, sweeps cleaving blades to touch. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: soft cleaving body and crushing immunity. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: self-sharpening blades. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Evaporite Crystalkin matters because mistaken for harmless curtain selenite. It lets survey reports sort soft cleaving hazards from hard ringing ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Bladeling is the genus-rank grouping of fibrous-gypsum crystalline elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a fibrous selenite body whose blades cleave and re-sharpen, and divide below into species groups by fibre length and seep sulphate strength. Silent and endlessly self-sharpening.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Bladeling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a fibrous gypsum body that cleaves lengthwise. A new species belongs here only if it shows a cleaving gypsum body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an upright fanning blade-sheaf. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — silky fibre-heaps — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Bladeling occupies gypsum-curtain chambers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a sulphate seep grows fibrous selenite and recur with the evaporite seep refeeds the fibres, sustaining themselves on sulphate supersaturation.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: sweeps self-cleaving blades to touch. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: fibrous body and satin sheen. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: self-sharpening cleaving blades. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Bladeling matters because its soft sheen hides a live edge. It lets survey reports sort soft cleaving forms from hard ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Selenite Bladeling (Crystallus gypseus) is a crystalline elemental of soft fibrous gypsum that fights with cleaving, regrowing satin-spar blades. Documented from the gypsum-curtain chambers near evaporite seeps, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — bundled fibres of translucent selenite that cleave into fresh edges — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Latticeling rings and refracts, the Bladeling is silent, soft, and endlessly self-sharpening. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a heap of silky white fibres that crumble to powder underfoot and no carcass.
c.tax: Selenite Bladeling is placed at species rank beneath Bladeling (Crystalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a fibrous gypsum body, self-cleaving regrowing blades, and a silent satin sheen. It is separated from its sibling the Geode Latticeling by its soft silent cleaving gypsum versus the Latticeling's hard ringing quartz; the two share the genus body-plan but never the resonant ringing. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: fibrous selenite gypsum that splits along its length into satin blades.
c.ap: Selenite Bladeling stands 1–1.6 m, a sheaf of upright crystal blades. Its silhouette is an upright bundle of long fibrous blades fanning from a base node. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a satin, silky lustre rather than the glassy glint of quartz kin; blades that split lengthwise into ever-finer edges instead of snapping; and a faint sulphur-evaporite smell from its seep origin. It moves with a gliding shuffle on a blade-base, slow but tireless, and shows a soft diffuse sheen, no sharp glints. Even after dispersal it can be identified from silky white fibre-heaps and fine parallel scratch-fans on the floor, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Selenite Bladeling persists in gypsum-curtain chambers and evaporite seeps where sulphate-rich water feeds soft crystal growth. It does not breed; it forms where a sulphate seep grows a fibrous selenite mass around a pressure node, and recurs whenever the evaporite seep refeeds the fibres after they crumble. It sustains its mass on sulphate supersaturation in evaporite seep, drawing in dissolved gypsum drawn from seep, regrown as blades. Range is bounded by the sulphate seep zone: outside that band the body dehydrates to crumbling powder. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a gypsum curtain that sprouts fresh upright blades against the drip-grain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Selenite Bladeling is roused by touch or close movement, not sound and otherwise lies a still curtain of crystal indistinguishable from natural selenite. It claims a single gypsum chamber and answers intrusion with sweeping cleaving blades that re-sharpen with every cut. Toward grey-folk it is slow and avoidable; survey crews report that Pall-Brokers harvest its fibre for binding but learn the soft sheen means a live edge. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Selenite Bladeling: a body of fibrous selenite gypsum that splits along its length into satin blades; immune to crushing, which only compacts and re-splits its fibres; and a defining weakness — dry heat, which dehydrates and crumbles it. Its senses run through the medium itself: touch and vibration through its blade-base. It tolerates acid and darkness but fails at dry heat and prolonged dryness. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the fibrous blade-sheaf and satin lustre, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Selenite Bladeling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it sweeps self-cleaving blades that stay sharp through any cut; it regrows severed blades within minutes from seep; it splits a struck blade into several finer edges; and it raises a blade-thicket to wall off a passage. Each is a direct expression of the lengthwise cleavage of fibrous gypsum fed by seep, so it cannot, for instance, ring, refract, produce heat, or cross dry ground. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Selenite Bladeling matters because its soft sheen is mistaken for harmless curtain selenite, and crews now treat any satin-lustre fan as a live edge. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a touch-triggered hazard logged with gypsum seeps, and its presence reshapes routes through the evaporite chambers, now skirted rather than brushed past. Its crumbled fibre-heaps, unlike quartz litter, powder underfoot and mark a dead Bladeling.

c.g: Metalline Elementals is the order-rank grouping of elementals whose bodies are native or precipitated metal drawn from ore within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of coherent metal — ductile copper or porous iron — animated from an ore-seam or iron bog, and divide below into family groups by the metal and its mode of harm — conduction or magnetic mass. The Metalline order is the Forgemasters' obsession, since its bodies are walking ore.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Metalline Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a native or precipitated metal body, not a stone, crystal, or sediment one. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct metal with its own ore source and attack mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a metallic coil or a rust-red magnetic mass moving against the ore-grain. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a verdigris smear and arc-scorch, or a rust-bleed and clinging iron filings — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Metalline Elementals occupies copper-seamed galleries and iron-stained bogs. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-charge or earth-pressure animates an ore-seam or iron bloom and recur with the seam re-grows or the bog re-precipitates metal, sustaining themselves on earth-charge through conductive ore, or earth-pressure in iron mud.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members pose as exposed ore and rouse to metal tools or wet footfall, answering with current or magnetic mass. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a coherent metal body, immunity to its own mode (electricity or magnetism), and a chemical or thermal failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: electrical discharge and constriction, or magnetic mass-attack and tool-stripping. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Metalline Elementals matters because its bodies are literally ore, drawing Forgemaster crews into galleries and bogs that kill them. It lets survey reports sort metal hazards by mode, so a crew knows whether to go dry-shod or strip their iron without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Cuprous Metalkin is the family-rank grouping of conductive native-metal elementals of the copper kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a ductile conductive metal body that discharges earth-charge, and divide below into genus groups by metal purity and coil form. The only earth elementals that hunt with current.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Cuprous Metalkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a conductive native-metal body fed by ore-seam. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a conductive metal body with its own discharge mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a bright metallic ribbon or coil. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a verdigris smear and arc-scorch on damp rock — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Cuprous Metalkin occupies copper-seamed wet galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-charge animates an ore-seam and recur with the seam re-grows the ribbon, sustaining themselves on earth-charge through conductive ore.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as exposed ore, discharges through wet stone. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: ductile conductive body and field-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: discharge and constriction. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Cuprous Metalkin matters because kills wet-booted crews on the gallery floor. It lets survey reports sort conductive hazards from magnetic ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Metalcoil is the genus-rank grouping of serpentine conductive-copper elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a coiling native-copper ribbon that discharges and constricts, and divide below into species groups by ribbon length and seam charge. A copper serpent that strikes with current.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Metalcoil is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a serpentine native-copper body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a coiling conductive copper body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a coiling metallic serpent. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — verdigris smear and arc-marks — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Metalcoil occupies copper-seamed wet galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-charge animates a copper seam and recur with the seam rebuilds the ribbon, sustaining themselves on earth-charge through copper.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: rears and discharges through the wet floor. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: ductile copper body and field-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: electrical discharge and copper constriction. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Metalcoil matters because lethal to wet-shod copper crews. It lets survey reports sort coiling conductive forms from massive ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Native Copper Coil (Metallum cupreum) is a metalline elemental of native copper that coils as a serpentine ribbon and discharges through wet stone. Documented from the copper-seamed wet galleries of Tier II, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a sinuous ribbon of bright and verdigris-green native copper — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the only earth elemental that hunts with current rather than mass, conducting earth-charge into anything it touches. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a tangle of cooling copper wire and a green verdigris stain on the stone and no carcass.
c.tax: Native Copper Coil is placed at species rank beneath Metalcoil (Metalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a native-copper ribbon body, electrical discharge through wet rock, and a verdigris trail. It is separated from its sibling the Bog-Iron Bloomling by its conductive coiling copper versus the Bloomling's inert porous iron mass; the two share the genus body-plan but never the electrical discharge. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: native copper drawn from ore-seam, kept ductile by earth-charge.
c.ap: Native Copper Coil stands 2–3 m unwound, a finger-thick ribbon. Its silhouette is a coiling metallic serpent that rears and lashes. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a bright metallic gleam fading to green verdigris along older coils; a faint ozone smell and hair-raising static before it strikes; and a low hum, rising before discharge, audible in a quiet gallery. It moves with a fluid serpentine slither, fastest over wet conductive stone, and shows a faint blue arc-glint at the coil tips before discharge. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a green verdigris smear and scorched arc-marks on damp rock, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Native Copper Coil persists in copper-seamed wet galleries of Tier II, where ore and standing damp let it both feed and conduct. It does not breed; it forms where earth-charge animates a native-copper seam into a coherent ribbon, and recurs whenever the charge rebuilds the ribbon from the same seam after it cools and breaks. It sustains its mass on earth-charge conducted through ore and wet stone, drawing in native copper drawn from the seam to repair and grow. Range is bounded by the conductive damp zone of its seam: outside that band the body loses charge on dry ground and falls slack. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a copper seam that grows bright tendrils against the grain of the ore, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Native Copper Coil is roused by footfall on its wet floor or contact with metal tools and otherwise lies a coil of bright wire posing as exposed ore. It claims a single copper gallery and answers intrusion with rearing and discharging through the wet floor at anything grounded. Toward grey-folk it is reactive to metal and damp; survey crews report that Forgemasters cut its galleries with wooden tools and dry boots after losing crews to the floor-charge. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Native Copper Coil: a body of native copper drawn from ore-seam, kept ductile by earth-charge; immune to electricity and to slashing, which it simply re-coils; and a defining weakness — dry insulation and heat, which cut its charge. Its senses run through the medium itself: electrical-field sensing through the wet stone; blind to dry, ungrounded objects. It tolerates acid seep and darkness but fails at dry ground and strong heat, both of which break its conduction. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the ribbon body and coil-and-rear strike, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Native Copper Coil are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it discharges earth-charge through wet stone to stun grounded prey; it constricts in copper coils to crush and hold; it draws copper from the seam to repair and lengthen; and it senses footfalls as field-changes through the wet floor. Each is a direct expression of earth-charge moving through a conductive copper body, so it cannot, for instance, cross dry ground, fly, or strike an insulated target. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Native Copper Coil matters because it makes copper galleries lethal to wet-booted crews, and Forgemasters now work them dry and wooden-tooled. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an electrical hazard logged with copper seams and damp floors, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier II copper galleries, now entered dry-shod with insulated tools. A copper seam growing bright against the ore-grain is the Forgemasters' sign a Coil is forming.

c.g: Ferrous Metalkin is the family-rank grouping of precipitated-iron elementals of the bog and ore-mass kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a heavy magnetic ferrous body that attacks by mass, not current, and divide below into genus groups by iron form — porous bog-bloom among them. Inert, magnetic, and crushingly heavy.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Ferrous Metalkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a precipitated or massive iron body fed by iron bog or ore. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a ferrous body with its own magnetic mass-mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a rust-red magnetic mass dragging iron toward it. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a rust-bleed trail and clinging iron filings — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Ferrous Metalkin occupies iron-stained bogs and ore masses. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-pressure cements an iron bloom and recur with the bog re-precipitates iron, sustaining themselves on earth-pressure in iron mud.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as a bog-iron bank, rams with magnetic mass. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: heavy magnetic body and pressure-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: mass-ram and tool-stripping. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ferrous Metalkin matters because disarms crews by dragging off their iron. It lets survey reports sort magnetic mass hazards from conductive ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Bloomling is the genus-rank grouping of porous bog-iron elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a porous magnetic bog-iron body that rams and strips tools, and divide below into species groups by iron porosity and bog strength. A rust-bleeding mass that drags iron to itself.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Bloomling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a porous bog-iron body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a porous ferrous bog body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a hunched rust-red spongy mound. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — an orange rust-bleed and iron filings — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Bloomling occupies iron-stained Still-water bogs. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-pressure cements a bog-iron bloom and recur with the bog re-precipitates the bloom, sustaining themselves on earth-pressure in iron mud.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: lurches to ram and magnetically pin. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: porous magnetic body and pressure-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: mass-ram and magnetic tool-drag. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Bloomling matters because turns iron bogs into disarming traps. It lets survey reports sort porous bog-iron forms from dense ore ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Bog-Iron Bloomling (Metallum ferrugineum) is a metalline elemental of porous bog-iron that lumbers as a rust-bleeding mass and rams with sheer ferrous weight. Documented from the iron-stained Still-water bogs of Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a spongy, rust-red mass of porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Copper Coil conducts and lashes, the Bloomling is inert, magnetic, and crushingly heavy. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a slumped pile of rust-red iron sponge bleeding orange into the water and no carcass.
c.tax: Bog-Iron Bloomling is placed at species rank beneath Bloomling (Metalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a porous bog-iron body, magnetic mass-attack, and a bleeding rust trail. It is separated from its sibling the Native Copper Coil by its inert magnetic ferrous mass versus the Coil's conductive lashing ribbon; the two share the genus body-plan but never the electrical conduction. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud cemented by earth-pressure.
c.ap: Bog-Iron Bloomling stands 1.5–2.2 m, a heavy lumpen mass. Its silhouette is a hunched spongy mound that hauls itself forward on iron pseudopods. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a rust-red, pitted sponge surface that bleeds orange in water; a strong magnetism that drags iron tools and fittings toward it; and a metallic, blood-like tang of iron on the air around it. It moves with a slow, heaving lurch, slowest of all the metalline kin, and shows none; recognised by rust-colour and magnetic pull. Even after dispersal it can be identified from an orange rust-bleed trail and iron filings clinging to nearby fittings, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Bog-Iron Bloomling persists in iron-stained Still-water bogs of Tier III, where ferruginous mud and standing water cement its porous body. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure cements a bog-iron bloom out of ferruginous mud, and recurs whenever the bog re-precipitates iron and the bloom re-cements. It sustains its mass on earth-pressure in iron-rich mud, drawing in bog-iron and ferruginous mud drawn in to heal and grow. Range is bounded by the iron-rich bog margin: outside that band the body dries, oxidises, and crumbles to rust away from the bog. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a bog patch where iron tools drift and the water runs orange without rain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Bog-Iron Bloomling is roused by metal carried into its bog or heavy footfall and otherwise lies a rust-coloured mound posing as a bog-iron bank. It claims a single iron bog and answers intrusion with lurching to ram and pin with magnetic mass. Toward grey-folk it is slow but inexorable; survey crews report that Forgemasters strip iron fittings before crossing an orange-bleeding bog. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Bog-Iron Bloomling: a body of porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud cemented by earth-pressure; immune to piercing and to magnetism-based effects; and a defining weakness — acid, which dissolves its porous iron, and fire-drying. Its senses run through the medium itself: magnetic and pressure sensing; blind to non-ferrous, non-moving objects. It tolerates cold, damp, and darkness but fails at acid seep and dry heat. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the porous mound and magnetic mass, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Bog-Iron Bloomling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it rams with crushing ferrous mass to pin prey; it drags iron tools and armour to itself magnetically; it draws bog-iron in to seal damage; and it sinks into the bog to ambush and resurface. Each is a direct expression of earth-pressure in a heavy magnetic iron body, so it cannot, for instance, conduct charge, move fast, or cross dry ground. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Bog-Iron Bloomling matters because it makes iron bogs a magnetic trap that disarms crews, who now strip ferrous gear before crossing. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a magnetic mass hazard logged with iron bogs, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier III iron bogs, now crossed stripped of ferrous fittings. An orange bleed with no rain, and tools that drift bog-ward, mark a forming Bloomling.

c.g: Argillic Elementals is the order-rank grouping of elementals whose bodies are chemically transformed or biogenically influenced earth — worked, mixed, or precipitated within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of earth that has passed through chemical or organic transformation: lime-precipitated, mixed clay-limestone, or organics-laden loam, and divide below into family groups by whether the chemistry is calcareous (chalk/tufa) or mixed organic-mineral (marl/loam). Argillic elementals are the earth class that most surprises Throat crews, because their bodies resemble soil and flowstone, not bare rock.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Argillic Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a chemically or organically transformed earth body, not simple bulk rock or crystal. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct transformed-earth body-plan with its own chemistry and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a shimmering lime sheet, a hollow tufa tower, a pale crumbling figure, or a dark slumping mound. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a white lime film or clicking advance, or a gritty pale crumble versus a dark humic smear — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Argillic Elementals occupies lime-seep terraces, drip caverns, clay-limestone contacts, and organic-rich root channels. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where lime supersaturation deposits a calcite body, or earth-pressure animates a mixed-chemistry seam and recur with the seep, drip, or mixed seam replenishes the same nucleus, sustaining themselves on lime supersaturation in seep or drip, or earth-pressure in mixed chemistry.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members pose as natural flowstone, soil, or marl and rouse to chemical triggers — seep pressure, warmth, organic gradient — answering by jetting, spreading, entombing, or adhering. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a transformed-earth body, immunity to dilute acid (calcareous) or to piercing (organic), and a specific chemistry-failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: jetting, crust-spreading, entombing, and adhering. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Argillic Elementals matters because they make cavern floors, lime terraces, and root channels deceptively safe until trodden or tipped. It lets survey reports sort transformed-earth hazards by chemistry, so a crew knows whether to fear caustic jet, sealing crust, crumbling mass, or adhesion without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Chalk-Kin Family is the family-rank grouping of calcareous precipitate elementals of lime-seep and drip origin within the True Elementals. Its members share a calcium-carbonate body grown from supersaturated seep or drip, and divide below into genus groups by hollow tube growth versus flat sheet growth. The chalk family makes active lime-water features into hazards.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Chalk-Kin Family is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a calcite body fed by lime-supersaturated water. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a calcite body with its own seep or drip source.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a hollow tufa tower or a flat shimmering crust. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a white lime film on surrounding stone — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Chalk-Kin Family occupies active tufa terraces and lime-drip caverns. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a calcium-supersaturated seep or drip deposits a calcite nucleus and recur with the seep or drip refills, sustaining themselves on lime supersaturation.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: jets or spreads on approach. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: calcite body and hydraulic sensing. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: lime-water jetting and crust-sealing. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Chalk-Kin Family matters because makes lime-seep terraces and drip caverns caustic hazards. It lets survey reports sort calcareous hazards by habit — jet versus spread without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Chalk-Kin is the genus-rank grouping of calcareous precipitate elementals of the calcite and tufa kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a calcium-carbonate body fed by lime-supersaturated seep or drip, and divide below into species groups by whether the calcite grows hollow and vertical or flat and spreading. The chalk kin bridge the mineral and the aqueous: their bodies are stone deposited by water.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Chalk-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a calcium-carbonate body maintained by seep chemistry, not bulk earth-pressure alone. A new species belongs here only if it shows a calcite body with its own seep source and growth habit.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a hollow tufa tower or a shimmering flat crust on damp stone. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a white lime film and either a hollow ring when struck or a clicking advance — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Chalk-Kin occupies tufa terraces and lime-drip caverns. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a calcium-supersaturated seep deposits a calcite nucleus and recur with the seep or drip continues feeding the nucleus, sustaining themselves on lime supersaturation in seep or drip.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as natural travertine or flowstone, jets or spreads on approach. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a calcium-carbonate body, acid-immunity in dilute doses, and strong-acid failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: jetting lime-water or spreading sealing crust. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Chalk-Kin matters because makes lime-seep terraces and drip caverns caustic or sealing hazards. It lets survey reports sort calcareous hazards by habit, so a crew knows whether to wear a visor or test the floor without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Calcite Crust Elemental (Calcarius stratus) is a flat-spreading calcareous elemental that creeps as a thin calcite sheet and seals exits, lungs, and gear under lime crust. Documented from the still, damp cavern floors and wall-faces fed by lime-heavy drip, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a spreading sheet of thin calcite crust just thick enough to move and grasp — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Tufa Column jets vertically, the Calcite Crust spreads invisibly — the flattest and least-noticed of the chalk kin. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a thin white calcite film hardening over every surface, gradually losing coherence and no carcass.
c.tax: Calcite Crust Elemental is placed at species rank beneath Chalk-Kin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a flat spreading calcite sheet, crust-sealing attack, and a film that hardens over surfaces it has left. It is separated from its sibling the Tufa Column by its flat spreading sheet and sealing crust versus the Column's vertical hollow jet; the two share the genus body-plan but never the vertical jetting. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: thin precipitated calcite kept flexible by a film of supersaturated lime water.
c.ap: Calcite Crust Elemental stands indefinite creeping sheet, typically 0.3–0.5 cm thick. Its silhouette is a flat shimmering crust spreading across floor and wall. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a shimmering wet calcite film on surfaces that dries to a soft-clicking crust; a faint lime smell and a slight tackiness underfoot before the crust is visible; and a clicking sound as it advances, caused by micro-fracture and re-deposit. It moves with a creeping radial spread from a drip-point, up to 2 m per hour on wet stone, and shows a faint sheen on wet sections. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a white calcite film that hardens room-temperature over 6–8 hours, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Calcite Crust Elemental persists in still damp cavern floors and wall-faces fed by a persistent lime-heavy drip, where a flat body has no draught to disperse it. It does not breed; it forms where a lime-heavy drip animates a calcite nucleus into a spreading sheet, and recurs whenever the drip continues and the sheet regrows from the same nucleus. It sustains its mass on lime supersaturation in drip water, drawing in dissolved calcium from drip absorbed into the advancing edge. Range is bounded by the drip-fed damp zone: outside that band the body dries to hard inert crust off the wet stone. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a drip-point where calcite deposits faster than the drip alone could explain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Calcite Crust Elemental is roused by warmth, breath, or moisture near its edge and otherwise lies a still crust coating the cavern floor, indistinguishable from natural flowstone. It claims a single drip-fed cavern floor and answers intrusion with spreading over gear, openings, and breathing faces to seal and suffocate. Toward grey-folk it is passive and slow, the danger is in not noticing it; survey crews report that Lanternkin test cavern floors with a blade-tip before resting; a tacky pulling sensation means live crust. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Calcite Crust Elemental: a body of thin precipitated calcite kept flexible by a film of supersaturated lime water; immune to piercing, which it re-covers, and to acid in dilute quantities; and a defining weakness — strong concentrated acid, which dissolves it faster than it repairs, and sudden heat. Its senses run through the medium itself: moisture and warmth sensing through its spreading edge. It tolerates complete darkness and low air but fails at strong acid and sudden heat. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the flat sheet form and clicking advance, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Calcite Crust Elemental are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it spreads over openings and gear, sealing and immobilising; it coats breathing surfaces to suffocate prey; it regrows from a drip-fed nucleus after scraping; and it thickens over time, armour-plating surfaces with hard crust. Each is a direct expression of lime supersaturation in a flat spreading calcite sheet, so it cannot, for instance, jet, stand upright, move fast, or cross dry stone. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Calcite Crust Elemental matters because it is the most commonly overlooked cavern elemental, and a tacky floor in a drip-cave is now the Lanternkin's first hazard check. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a sealing suffocation hazard logged with lime-drip cavern floors, and its presence reshapes routes through the still drip-caverns, now floor-tested and never rested on without scraping. A drip-point where calcite deposits faster than the water flow alone could explain marks a Calcite Crust nucleating.

c.g: Tufa Column (Calcarius tubulans) is a chalky calcareous elemental that grows as a hollow tufa column and jets pressurised lime-water through pores. Documented from the active tufa terraces and lime-seep cascades of the mid tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a hollow calcite column riddled with seep-channels and lime-encrusted pores — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the hollow-bodied chalk kin: where the Calcite Crust spreads flat, the Tufa Column grows vertical and jets. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a fractured hollow column and a spreading white lime film on the floor and no carcass.
c.tax: Tufa Column is placed at species rank beneath Tufakin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a hollow vertical calcite body, pressurised lime-water jets, and a coral-like pore surface. It is separated from its sibling the Calcite Crust Elemental by its vertical hollow-column form and jetting jets versus the Crust's flat spreading sheet; the two share the genus body-plan but never the flat spreading. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: precipitated calcite tufa grown around a pressurised seep channel.
c.ap: Tufa Column stands 0.8–1.6 m tall hollow column. Its silhouette is an upright branching tufa tower with pore-covered skin. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a hollow interior that rings dully when struck, unlike solid limestone; thin jets of lime-water sprayed from pores on approach, 1–2 m range; and a white lime encrustation on the floor and walls for two paces around it. It moves with a slow rocking advance on a spreading calcite base, and shows none; milk-white body. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a lime-crust trail and hollow fractured tufa shards where it walked, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Tufa Column persists in active tufa terraces, lime-seep cascades, and hot-spring margins of the mid tiers where calcium-rich water jets and deposits. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure drives a calcium-supersaturated seep through a calcite nucleus, growing it hollow and mobile, and recurs whenever the seep re-pressurises and the fractured column regrows from the same nucleus. It sustains its mass on hydraulic pressure in calcium-supersaturated seep water, drawing in dissolved calcium drawn from seep to grow and repair. Range is bounded by the pressurised seep zone: outside that band the body dries, stops jetting, and sets inert. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a tufa terrace that grows against the seep-flow, adding height rather than breadth, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Tufa Column is roused by approach within 3 m or any impact on its column and otherwise lies a still tufa formation indistinguishable from natural travertine. It claims a single tufa terrace and answers intrusion with jetting lime-water into eyes and gear, then advancing to force the intruder off the terrace. Toward grey-folk it is slow but caustic; survey crews report that Wall-Wardens wear visors on any terrace growing against the seep-flow. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Tufa Column: a body of precipitated calcite tufa grown around a pressurised seep channel; immune to acid (the lime neutralises it) and to crushing, which it regrows; and a defining weakness — strong acid in quantity, which dissolves calcite faster than it can deposit. Its senses run through the medium itself: hydraulic-pressure sensing through its seep channel; blind to still dry objects. It tolerates heat, mineral seep, and darkness but fails at loss of seep pressure and prolonged dryness. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the hollow column form and white lime encrustation, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Tufa Column are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it jets pressurised lime-water to blind and burn; it advances its base to push prey from its terrace; it regrows fractured sections from the same seep nucleus; and it seals cracks by depositing calcite, slowly armour-plating itself. Each is a direct expression of hydraulic pressure driving calcium supersaturation through a hollow calcite body, so it cannot, for instance, move fast, cross dry ground, or survive strong acid. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Tufa Column matters because it makes active tufa terraces a blinding-jet hazard, and Wall-Wardens now visor-up whenever a terrace grows against its own flow. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a caustic-jet hazard logged with active tufa terraces, and its presence reshapes routes through the lime-seep terraces, now approached with visors and always crossed above the active deposition line. A tufa formation growing against its seep-flow, adding height rather than breadth, marks an animating column.

c.g: Worked-Earth Family is the family-rank grouping of mixed organic-mineral earth elementals of marl and loam within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of earth containing both mineral and organic chemistry, and divide below into genus groups by lime-dominant pale marl versus organics-dominant dark loam. The worked-earth family haunts the softer soils of the upper tiers.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Worked-Earth Family is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a mixed-chemistry earth body from a contact seam or organic channel. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a mixed-earth body with both mineral and organic components.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a pale crumbling figure or a dark slumping mound. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — gritty pale crumbles or a dark humic smear — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Worked-Earth Family occupies marl flats, clay-limestone contacts, and root channels. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-pressure animates a mixed-chemistry accumulation and recur with the contact or channel replenishes, sustaining themselves on earth-pressure in mixed chemistry.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: entombs or adheres with its bulk. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: mixed-earth body and seam-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: entombing and adhesion. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Worked-Earth Family matters because makes marl flats and organic channels entombment traps. It lets survey reports sort mixed-earth hazards by chemistry — crumbling pale mass versus dark adhesion without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Worked-Earthkin is the genus-rank grouping of organics-bearing and mixed-chemistry loam and marl elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of worked or mixed earth — marl or loam — containing both mineral and organic chemistry, and divide below into species groups by whether the earth is lime-dominated and pale-stiff (marl) or organics-dominated and dark-soft (loam). These are the elementals of soils and contact zones, the bridge between pure mineral and biological-matrix earth.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Worked-Earthkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a mixed-chemistry earth body drawing from a clay-limestone contact or organic-rich channel. A new species belongs here only if it shows a mixed-earth body with both mineral and organic components.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a pale crumbling upright figure or a dark slumping mound. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — pale gritty crumbles with a chalk smell, or a dark smear with a humic smell — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Worked-Earthkin occupies marl flats, clay-limestone contacts, and organic-rich root channels. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-pressure animates a mixed-chemistry seam or accumulation and recur with the contact or channel replenishes from both source layers, sustaining themselves on earth-pressure in a mixed-chemistry seam.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as a natural mound or accumulation, advances to entomb or adhere. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a mixed-earth body, crushing immunity, and a seam-chemical failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: entombing, adhering, and seam-draw repair. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Worked-Earthkin matters because makes marl flats and organic channels slow-entombment and adhesion traps. It lets survey reports sort mixed-earth hazards by chemistry, so a crew knows whether to expect crumbling mass or sticky slump without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Marl Walker (Argilla calcarea) is a worked-earth elemental of lime-rich marl that forms at the interface of clay and limestone, walking as a pale, crumbling figure. Documented from the marl flats and clay-limestone contact zones of the mid tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a pale, gritty marl body that crumbles at the edges and regrows from the contact layer — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Marl is the mixed substance between clay and chalk; the Walker is likewise between the Pelitic clay kin and the chalk kin, sharing neither's extremes. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a slow collapse into pale gritty rubble that the seep can reconsolidate and no carcass.
c.tax: Marl Walker is placed at species rank beneath Worked-Earthkin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a pale mixed clay-limestone body, crumbling edges that regrow, and a gritty texture unlike either pure clay or pure chalk. It is separated from its sibling the Loam Creeper by its pale stiff marl body and upright gait versus the Creeper's dark organics-rich slumping form; the two share the genus body-plan but never the organic-rich slump. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: calcium-rich clay marl at a clay-limestone contact seam, animated by earth-pressure.
c.ap: Marl Walker stands 1.4–1.8 m upright figure. Its silhouette is a rough humanoid outline of pale crumbling marl with an irregular surface. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a pale buff-grey colour with gritty white specks, unlike the dark grey of Pelitic clay kin; slow crumbling at limb-edges that re-consolidates within minutes from the contact layer; and a faint chalk-and-clay smell, half mineral, half earthy, unlike either pure type. It moves with a slow stiff walk on a broad base, leaving rounded imprints in soft floor, and shows none; pale buff-grey. Even after dispersal it can be identified from rounded foot-impressions in soft ground and a litter of pale gritty crumbles along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Marl Walker persists in marl flats and clay-limestone contact zones where a mixed seam provides the worked-earth body its dual chemistry. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure at a clay-limestone contact seam animates the marl layer into a coherent walking body, and recurs whenever the contact seam replenishes from both layers after dispersal. It sustains its mass on earth-pressure at a dual-chemistry contact seam, drawing in marl drawn from the contact layer to repair and grow. Range is bounded by the clay-limestone contact zone: outside that band the body crumbles to loose grit away from its seam. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a marl flat where the contact layer has developed a rounded imprint-pattern too regular for water, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Marl Walker is roused by quarrying or heavy footfall near its marl flat and otherwise lies a pale crumbling mound indistinguishable from a marl hummock.
