It claims a single marl flat or contact-seam exposure and answers intrusion with advancing to push and bury, using its crumbling mass to obstruct and entomb. Toward grey-folk it is slow but obstructive; survey crews report that Forgemasters who chip marl exposures for flux find the hummocks that push back. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Marl Walker: a body of calcium-rich clay marl at a clay-limestone contact seam, animated by earth-pressure; immune to crushing, which only speeds the regrowth cycle; and a defining weakness — water saturation, which makes it too fluid to hold form, and sustained acid. Its senses run through the medium itself: earth-pressure sensing through the contact seam beneath it. It tolerates cold, damp, and darkness but fails at water saturation and acid dissolution. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the pale buff-grey body and crumbling-regrowth cycle, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Marl Walker are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it advances to entomb and obstruct with its bulk; it crumbles and reconsolidates to flow around barriers; it draws marl from the contact layer to repair any damage; and it can flatten to a low mound, posing as a natural feature. Each is a direct expression of earth-pressure in a mixed clay-limestone marl body, so it cannot, for instance, jet, conduct current, or cross water. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Marl Walker matters because it turns marl flats into slow entombing hazards, and Forgemasters now mark contact-seam hummocks before sampling. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an entombment hazard logged with clay-limestone contact zones, and its presence reshapes routes through the marl flats, now avoided as bedding and marked with contact-seam flags. A marl flat with regular rounded imprints that pre-date any crew passage marks a Walker's territory.

c.g: Loam Creeper (Argilla humosa) is a worked-earth elemental of dark organic loam that slumps and spreads along decomposing organic channels in the gorge. Documented from the decaying root-mat and organic-rich sediment channels of the upper tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a dark, organics-rich loam body held coherent by earth-pressure and humic chemistry — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. The Loam Creeper is the dark kin of the Marl Walker: where the Walker is pale and stiff from lime, the Creeper is dark and soft from organic content. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a spreading dark wet smear and a smell of decayed matter where the body collapsed and no carcass.
c.tax: Loam Creeper is placed at species rank beneath Worked-Earthkin (Argillic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a dark organic-rich loam body, slumping spread along root channels, and a humic smell. It is separated from its sibling the Marl Walker by its dark organic-rich slumping form versus the Walker's pale stiff upright gait; the two share the genus body-plan but never the pale stiff upright gait. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: dark humic loam rich in decayed organic matter, animated by earth-pressure in organic-rich channels.
c.ap: Loam Creeper stands 1–1.6 m, a low slumping figure. Its silhouette is a rounded low shape of dark moist loam that moves as a slow slide. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a dark grey-brown, almost black surface that smears rather than crumbles; a strong humic smell, like old fungal mat pressed under stone; and a trail of dark smear marks on paler stone where it has passed. It moves with a slow forward slump-and-pull, following root-channel gradients, and shows none; very dark. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a dark smear trail and the smell of old organic matter along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Loam Creeper persists in decaying root-mat channels, organic-rich sediment fills, and peat-like accumulations of the upper tiers where organic chemistry mixes with mineral pressure. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure animates an organics-saturated loam accumulation at a root channel, and recurs whenever the root channel re-accumulates organic matter and the loam re-animates. It sustains its mass on earth-pressure in organics-rich loam, drawing in decayed organic matter and clay drawn from the channel to repair. Range is bounded by the organic-rich channel: outside that band the body dries to an inert dark crust away from organic input. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a root channel whose loam accumulation smears against the water-flow rather than with it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Loam Creeper is roused by disturbance of its root channel or warmth that speeds its organic chemistry and otherwise lies an undisturbed dark loam accumulation. It claims a single root channel or organic accumulation and answers intrusion with slumping forward to engulf and adhere to prey with sticky organic loam. Toward grey-folk it is passive, hard to see in dark passages; survey crews report that Beast-Bonded scouts note when their bond animals refuse to walk a dark accumulation floor. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Loam Creeper: a body of dark humic loam rich in decayed organic matter, animated by earth-pressure in organic-rich channels; immune to piercing and to physical separation, which it just rejoins; and a defining weakness — fire and lime, which destroy the organic chemistry holding it together. Its senses run through the medium itself: organic-gradient and earth-pressure sensing through its root channel. It tolerates darkness, cold, and acid seep but fails at fire and lime contact. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the dark slumping form and humic smell, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Loam Creeper are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it slumps to engulf and adhere with sticky organic loam; it flows along root channels to reach prey in tight gaps; it draws organic matter from the channel to rebuild after damage; and it can adhere to boots and gear to slow and exhaust. Each is a direct expression of earth-pressure in an organics-rich loam body, so it cannot, for instance, jet, conduct, stand upright, or survive fire. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Loam Creeper matters because it makes dark organic-channel floors an adhesion hazard, and bond-animals that refuse a floor are now the accepted field warning. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an adhesion hazard logged with organic-rich sediment channels, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper-tier root channels, now checked by bond-animal reaction before a crew crosses. A root channel whose loam smears against the water-flow marks a Loam Creeper territory.

c.g: Fire Elementals is the class-rank grouping of true elementals composed of the fire element, from open flame to molten and cooled rock within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of heat — combustion or fire-heated rock — that consumes fuel or holds vent-heat rather than breeding, and divide below into order groups by whether the heat is open combustion (Pyric) or heated rock (Magmatic). Fire is the most actively spreading elemental class, and the one the Lanternkin and Forgemasters fear at lamp and forge.
c.tax: Placed at class rank, Fire Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a pure fire-element body of combustion or heated rock, animated by fire-pressure, fuel, or vent heat. A new order belongs here only if it shows a distinct fire-state body-plan with its own fuel or heat source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a flame, a creeping glow, a lava lobe, or a glass figure giving off heat. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — scorch and soot, an ash-creep, a cooled basalt lobe, or a glass-flake litter — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Fire Elementals occupies gas-seeps, oil stores, fungal mat, lava benches, thermal vents, and obsidian margins. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fire-pressure spark ignites fuel, or vent heat melts or quenches rock and recur with the vent refills, the mat regrows, or the rock reheats and re-forms, sustaining themselves on combustion of fuel or thermal-vent heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members chase fuel and heat, spreading or flowing, and either die without fuel or fall dormant as cooled rock. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a heat body, immunity to fire, and a smothering, flooding, or cooling failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each order and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition and spread, or engulfing heat and cutting glass. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Fire Elementals matters because they turn fuel pockets and warm benches into spreading-fire, radiant-heat, and shrapnel hazards. It lets survey reports sort fire hazards by state, so a crew knows whether to shutter lamps, rod-test mat, read glowing cracks, or avoid striking glass without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Pyric Elementals is the order-rank grouping of fire elementals whose bodies are open combustion — leaping flame, creeping smoulder, or sustained bipedal flame-form (Ignis-Kin) — within the True Elementals. Its members share a body that is the combustion itself, wholly dependent on fuel and air, and divide below into family groups by the mode of burning: open leaping flame (Flamelings), hidden creeping smoulder (Cinderlings), and vent-sustained humanoid form (Ignis-Kin). Pyric elementals are the fastest-spreading hazard in the worked tiers because their body is the fire.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Pyric Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a fuel-and-air-dependent combustion body, not a molten-rock one. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct combustion mode with its own fuel and ignition trigger.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a flame-ball or a creeping glow-front moving along fuel. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — scorch-rings and soot, or a creeping ash-band through mat — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Pyric Elementals occupies gas-seeps, oil stores, and combustible mat. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fire-pressure spark ignites a fuel pocket or detritus bed and recur with the vent refills or the mat regrows and re-ignites, sustaining themselves on combustion of ambient fuel at high or low oxygen.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members chase fuel and air, spreading as flame or smoulder, dying the moment fuel or air fails. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a combustion body, immunity to fire, and instant failure on smothering, flooding, or fuel-loss. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition, spread, flare, and dazzle. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Pyric Elementals matters because they turn fuel pockets and mat into spreading-fire traps that kill whole crews. It lets survey reports sort fire hazards by mode, so a crew knows whether to shutter lamps or rod-test the floor without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Open-Flame Pyrics is the family-rank grouping of bright open-combustion fire elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a leaping visible flame body that ignites on contact, and divide below into genus groups by flame size and fuel type. The brightest and fastest-spreading of the fire kin.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Open-Flame Pyrics is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an open visible-flame body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows an open-flame body with its own fuel mode.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a leaping flame-ball with heat-shimmer. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — scorch-rings and soot — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Open-Flame Pyrics occupies gas-seeps and oil stores. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a spark ignites a fuel pocket and recur with the vent refills, sustaining themselves on open combustion of fuel.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: darts along fuel, igniting what it touches. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: open-flame body and self-light. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition and flare. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Open-Flame Pyrics matters because spreads fire through fuel stores. It lets survey reports sort open-flame hazards from smouldering ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Flameling is the genus-rank grouping of leaping flame-ball elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a darting open-flame body that ignites and spreads, and divide below into species groups by fuel colour and flame size. A self-feeding ball of fire.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Flameling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a leaping open-flame body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a flame-ball body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a bobbing flame-ball. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — scorch-rings and soot films — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Flameling occupies gas-seep vents and oil stores. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a spark ignites a fuel pocket and recur with the vent refills with fuel, sustaining themselves on open combustion.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: flits along fuel and ignites contact. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: open-flame body and bright self-light. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition, leaping spread, and dazzle. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Flameling matters because spreads fire through gas and oil. It lets survey reports sort leaping flame forms from creeping ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Ember Flameling (Ignis vagus) is a small open-flame elemental that flits as a self-feeding ball of fire, igniting whatever it touches. Documented from the gas-seep vents and lamp-oil stores of the worked tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a flickering core of orange flame wrapped in heat-shimmer — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It has no solid part at all; its 'body' is the combustion itself, sustained as long as it finds fuel. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scorch-ring and a puff of soot where the flame guttered out and no carcass.
c.tax: Ember Flameling is placed at species rank beneath Flameling (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a fuel-dependent open-flame body, ignition on contact, and instant death without fuel or air. It is separated from its sibling the Smoulder Cinderling by its bright leaping open flame versus the Cinderling's creeping smokeless smoulder; the two share the genus body-plan but never the visible flame. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: open combustion sustained by ambient fuel gas and oxygen.
c.ap: Ember Flameling stands 0.4–0.8 m, a leaping flame-ball. Its silhouette is a roughly spherical flame with trailing licks and a brighter core. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a colour that shifts with its fuel — orange on oil, blue on gas-seep; heat-shimmer that distorts the passage behind it; and a sharp crackle and the smell of whatever it is burning. It moves with a darting, bobbing flit along fuel gradients, drawn to any new fuel source, and shows bright self-light, the strongest of any elemental. Even after dispersal it can be identified from scorch-rings, soot films, and charred patches along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Ember Flameling persists in gas-seep vents, lamp-oil stores, fungal methane pockets, and anywhere combustible vapour collects on the worked tiers. It does not breed; it forms where a fire-pressure spark ignites a fuel-rich pocket into a self-sustaining flame-body, and recurs whenever the same vent refills with fuel and the next spark re-ignites it. It sustains its mass on combustion of ambient fuel, drawing in any flammable gas, oil, or fibre it can reach. Range is bounded by the fuelled, oxygenated zone: outside that band the body starves and goes out the instant fuel or air fails. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a vent-flame that wanders off its source against the draught, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Ember Flameling is roused by fresh fuel, a draught, or a torch brought near and otherwise lies it cannot rest; without fuel it simply dies. It claims the fuelled pocket it feeds in and answers intrusion with leaping to ignite clothing, oil, and fungal mat. Toward grey-folk it is drawn to lamps and oil; survey crews report that Lanternkin shutter their flames in any gallery that smells of gas-seep. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Ember Flameling: a body of open combustion sustained by ambient fuel gas and oxygen; immune to fire and heat, and unharmed by piercing or cutting; and a defining weakness — smothering, water, and fuel-starvation, any of which kill it at once. Its senses run through the medium itself: fuel-gradient and heat sensing through the air. It tolerates darkness only in the sense that it makes its own light but fails at loss of fuel, air, or a dousing. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the spherical flame and fuel-colour shift, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Ember Flameling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it ignites flammable material on contact; it leaps along fuel gradients to spread; it flares brighter and hotter when fed fresh fuel; and it sheds blinding light to dazzle. Each is a direct expression of self-sustaining combustion of ambient fuel, so it cannot, for instance, persist without fuel or air, cross water, or carry mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ember Flameling matters because it turns every gas-seep and oil store into a spreading-fire hazard, and crews now shutter lamps near fuel-smell. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a fire hazard logged with fuel pockets and oil stores, and its presence reshapes routes through the gas-seep galleries, now ventilated and lamp-shuttered before entry. A vent-flame that wanders upwind off its source is the Lanternkin's signal to douse all open light.

c.g: Smouldering Pyrics is the family-rank grouping of low-oxygen creeping-combustion fire elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a hidden smoulder body that creeps through detritus and flares on air, and divide below into genus groups by fuel bed and oxygen level. Patient and hidden, the opposite of the open-flame kin.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Smouldering Pyrics is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a low-oxygen smouldering body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a smouldering body with its own detritus fuel.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a creeping glow-front in mat. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a creeping ash-band — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Smouldering Pyrics occupies fungal mats and dust-floors. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a spark smoulders in low-oxygen detritus and recur with mat regrows and re-seeds the smoulder, sustaining themselves on slow low-oxygen combustion.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: creeps unseen, flares to flame on a draught. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: smouldering body and near-invisibility. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: hidden spread and air-triggered flare. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Smouldering Pyrics matters because burns crews from below before being seen. It lets survey reports sort smouldering hazards from open-flame ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Cinderling is the genus-rank grouping of creeping smoulder elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a smokeless smoulder body that hides in mat and flares on air, and divide below into species groups by mat type and oxygen level. A hidden creeping fire that wakes to a draught.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Cinderling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a creeping low-oxygen smoulder body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a smouldering mat-body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dull-red glow-front in mat. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a grey ash-band through mat — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Cinderling occupies deep fungal mats. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a spark smoulders in low-oxygen mat and recur with fresh mat re-seeds the smoulder, sustaining themselves on slow combustion of mat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: creeps unseen and flares on air. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: smouldering body and ash-creep. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: hidden spread and re-ignition. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Cinderling matters because burns boots before it is seen. It lets survey reports sort creeping smoulder forms from leaping ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Smoulder Cinderling (Ignis latens) is a creeping fire elemental of smokeless smoulder that hides in fungal mat and dust and flares only when fed air. Documented from the deep fungal mats and dust-floors of low-oxygen galleries, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a dull red glow buried in slow-burning detritus, almost smokeless — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the patient, hidden sibling of the Flameling, spreading unseen through mat until a draught wakes it to open flame. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a creeping band of grey ash through fungal mat with no open flame seen and no carcass.
c.tax: Smoulder Cinderling is placed at species rank beneath Cinderling (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a low-oxygen smouldering body, near-invisibility until fed air, and an ash-creep trail. It is separated from its sibling the Ember Flameling by its hidden smokeless smoulder versus the Flameling's bright leaping flame; the two share the genus body-plan but never the open visible flame. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: slow smokeless combustion creeping through detritus at low oxygen.
c.ap: Smoulder Cinderling stands indefinite, a creeping front up to several metres wide. Its silhouette is a glowing edge advancing through mat, with no fixed shape. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a dull red line of heat with little or no flame; a creeping band of grey ash left behind the glowing front; and a faint warmth and acrid smell in still air before any glow is seen. It moves with a slow creep along combustible mat and dust, quickening into open flame if a draught reaches it, and shows a faint dull-red line, easily missed. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a band of grey ash through fungal mat and a slow-spreading warm zone, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Smoulder Cinderling persists in deep fungal mats, dust-floors, and peat-like detritus in low-oxygen galleries where slow combustion can hide. It does not breed; it forms where fire-pressure ignites a slow smoulder in low-oxygen detritus, and recurs whenever fresh mat regrows over the burn and the smoulder re-seeds. It sustains its mass on slow combustion of detritus at low oxygen, drawing in fungal mat, dust, and peat-like debris. Range is bounded by the extent of combustible mat: outside that band the body dies on bare stone or in standing water. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a patch of mat that warms and greys without visible flame, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Smoulder Cinderling is roused by a draught of fresh air, which flares it to open flame and otherwise lies a near-invisible warm glow in the mat. It claims a single mat-floored gallery and answers intrusion with flaring into open flame when air reaches it, igniting all around. Toward grey-folk it is a hidden trap underfoot; survey crews report that Beast-Bonded test mat with a damp rod, since a Cinderling burns boots before it is seen. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Smoulder Cinderling: a body of slow smokeless combustion creeping through detritus at low oxygen; immune to fire and smothering at depth, where little oxygen reaches it; and a defining weakness — flooding and the removal of its mat fuel. Its senses run through the medium itself: heat and air-gradient sensing through the mat. It tolerates darkness and low oxygen, which it prefers but fails at flooding and fuel-removal. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the creeping glow-front and ash-band, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Smoulder Cinderling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it spreads unseen through mat and dust; it flares to open flame when fed a draught; it reignites from buried embers after seeming dead; and it heats the floor enough to burn through boots. Each is a direct expression of slow low-oxygen combustion of detritus, so it cannot, for instance, leap, light a passage, cross bare stone, or survive flooding. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Smoulder Cinderling matters because it burns crews from below before it is seen, and mat is now tested with a damp rod before camp is made. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a hidden underfoot fire hazard logged with mat galleries, and its presence reshapes routes through the fungal-mat galleries, now rod-tested and never camped on warm mat. A grey ash-band creeping through living mat marks an active Cinderling front.

c.g: Ignis-Kin is the family-rank grouping of roughly humanoid vent-pressure flame elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a bipedal body of combustion held by vent-fault pressure into a recognisably humanoid form with a bright torso-core and char-ring footprint, and divide below into genus groups by vent pressure type and resulting combustion colour — narrow (blue-white minor), single (orange-yellow standard), convergent (deep red-orange major), deep primary (three-layer nobilis). The Ignis-Kin family formalises the classic 'walking fire person' of grey-folk tradition within the Pyric order, separated from the Flameling and Cinderling by persistent humanoid form and the char-ring field sign.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Ignis-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a bipedal vent-pressure flame body with a persistent humanoid form and char-ring footprint, not a fuel-ball or a creeping smoulder. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a bipedal flame body with char-ring footprint and a combustion-colour field sign.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a flame humanoid ranging 1.2–6 m with a bright torso-core. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — combustion colour (the field sign key), char-ring size and depth at each footstep, and ceiling-blackening span for the major form — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Ignis-Kin occupies vent passages, vent jets, convergences, and primary vent zones throughout the lower tiers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where vent-fault pressure ignites a humanoid combustion form and recur with the vent re-pressurises and the bipedal form re-ignites, sustaining themselves on vent-fault fuel.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: all Ignis-Kin are territorial, hold a vent zone, and track approach by radiant heat; they differ in size, colour, speed, and threat display. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: bipedal flame body, combustion-colour and char-ring field sign, vent dependence, and a form-specific suppression failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition, passage-filling, fuel-draw repair, and low-flame disguise. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis-Kin matters because accounts for most formally recorded Throat fatalities in vent passages. It lets survey reports sort humanoid flame hazards by combustion colour and char-ring pattern without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Ignis is the genus-rank grouping of roughly humanoid flame-body elementals of the vent-pressure kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a bipedal combustion body held by vent-fault pressure, with a bright torso-core and char-ring footprint distinguishing them from ordinary gas-flames, and divide below into species groups by the vent pressure type — narrow high-pressure (minor), standard vent (standard), convergent multi-vent (major), or deep primary differentiated (nobilis). The Ignis genus is the journal's formal name for what grey-folk call 'the fire that walks like a person' — the classic bipedal fire elemental, recognised across all four known forms by char-ring pattern and combustion colour.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Ignis is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a bipedal vent-pressure combustion body distinguishable from the Flameling and Cinderling by persistent humanoid form and char-ring footprint. A new species belongs here only if it shows a bipedal combustion body with its own vent-pressure formation mechanism, combustion colour, and char-ring field sign.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a flame humanoid ranging 1.2 m (minor, blue-white) to 6 m (major, deep red-orange). A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — combustion colour (blue-white for minor, orange-yellow standard, deep red-orange major, three-layer for nobilis), char-ring size and depth, and ceiling-blackening pattern — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Ignis occupies vent passages, vent jets, vent convergences, and primary vent zones throughout the lower tiers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where vent-fault pressure — narrow, single, convergent, or primary — ignites a combustion form into a bipedal shape and recur with the vent re-pressurises and the combustion form re-ignites, sustaining themselves on vent-fault fuel at various pressures.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: all Ignis forms are territorial, hold a passage or vent zone, and sense approach by radiant heat; they differ in combustion colour, char-ring pattern, movement speed, and threat response (advance vs dart vs fill-passage vs inside-out display). Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a bipedal flame body, combustion-colour field sign, char-ring footprint, vent dependence, and a smothering/flooding/fuel-starvation failure mode specific to each form. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: ignition, passage-filling, fuel-draw repair, and low-flame disguise; the nobilis form adds fuel-independence, inside-out threat display, and Ignis-detection sensing. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis matters because the Ignis genus accounts for most fatal vent-passage fires in the lower gorge and is the primary subject of Forgemaster vent-clearance protocols. It lets survey reports sort humanoid flame hazards by combustion colour and char-ring pattern, enabling a crew to identify which form is active and plan the correct suppression approach without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Ignis Elementalis (Ignis elementalis) is the standard fire elemental: a roughly humanoid body of living flame 2–3 m tall, sustained by ambient fuel gas and a self-maintaining combustion core. Documented from the gas-vent passages and warm fuel-rich galleries of the mid-lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a roughly humanoid body of orange-yellow flame with a denser white-hot core where a torso-mass would be — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Flameling is a wandering fuel-dependent ball, the Ignis is a humanoid that holds its form and moves with intent even in lower-fuel conditions. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a char-ring on the floor in a vague humanoid outline, with soot on the walls from the arms' reach and no carcass.
c.tax: Ignis Elementalis is placed at species rank beneath Ignis (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a roughly humanoid flame 2–3 m tall with a white-hot torso-core, distinct arm-flame extensions, and a char-ring footprint. It is separated from its sibling Ignis elementalis minor by its 2–3 m standard height and orange-yellow body versus the minor form's sub-2 m hotter-burning blue-white body; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the blue-white hotter minor form. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: sustained combustion maintained in a humanoid shape by fire-pressure along a vent-fault.
c.ap: Ignis Elementalis stands 2–3 m and roughly the thermal output of a forge fire. Its silhouette is a roughly humanoid column of orange-yellow flame with arm-extensions and a brighter white-hot torso-core. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a white-hot torso-core brighter than the limb-flames, visible through the body at 50 m; a char-ring footprint 50–60 cm across where it stands, charring floor stone to 2 cm depth; and arm-extensions that trail behind when it turns, leaving soot arcs on the passage walls. It moves with a floating stride that produces a char-ring at each step and scorches the air 2 m ahead, and shows orange-yellow with a white torso-core, 50 m ambient light radius in darkness. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a trail of char-rings spaced 60–70 cm apart and soot arcs on the walls from its arm-extensions, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Ignis Elementalis persists in gas-vent passages, warm fuel-rich galleries, and combustion-pressure zones of the mid-lower tiers where vent gas sustains its core. It does not breed; it forms where fire-pressure along a vent-fault ignites a combustion form into a self-sustaining humanoid shape, and recurs whenever the vent-fault refuels and the same combustion form re-ignites. It sustains its mass on vent-fault combustion pressure, drawing in ambient fuel gas drawn from the vent to sustain its core. Range is bounded by the vent-fuelled zone: outside that band the body loses the white-hot torso-core, becomes an ordinary Flameling-scale flame, and disperses. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a vent passage where gas-flames have begun taking a consistent bilateral shape against the draught, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Ignis Elementalis is roused by movement through its passage or any light source that defines the space around it and otherwise lies a still column of low flame barely distinguishable from a vent gas-flame. It claims a single vent passage or warm gallery and answers intrusion with advancing with arms extended to ignite everything within reach and corraling prey against walls. Toward grey-folk it is reactive to movement and light; survey crews report that Forgemasters approaching a vent passage in low light carry a shielded cold-lamp; an unshielded warm lamp may provoke a resting Ignis. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Ignis Elementalis: a body of sustained combustion maintained in a humanoid shape by fire-pressure along a vent-fault; immune to fire and heat; and to any physical impact, which passes through the flame body; and a defining weakness — smothering, water, and fuel-starvation, any of which collapse the torso-core within seconds. Its senses run through the medium itself: radiant-heat and fuel-gradient sensing through the vent; it tracks warm bodies by their heat signature rather than sound or sight. It tolerates complete darkness (it makes its own light), heat, and fuel-rich air but fails at smothering, flooding, and fuel-starvation. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the humanoid form, white-hot torso-core, and char-ring footprint, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Ignis Elementalis are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it advances with arms extended, igniting everything it contacts; it corrals prey against walls by filling the passage width with arm-extensions; it draws vent fuel to sustain the white-hot core under suppression attempts; and it can briefly collapse to a low flame to avoid detection, then re-expand. Each is a direct expression of fire-pressure in a vent-fault sustaining a humanoid combustion shape, so it cannot, for instance, survive smothering, flooding, or fuel-starvation; pass through stone; or strike with mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis Elementalis matters because it is the most recognisable fire elemental in the gorge and the primary subject of Forgemaster vent-clearance protocols — its char-ring trail marks every passage where it has walked. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a vent-passage territorial hazard, logged by char-ring survey before any vent drilling begins, and its presence reshapes routes through the warm fuel-rich passages, now char-ring surveyed before drill deployment. Forgemaster tradition holds that a vent with consistent bilateral gas-flames is a forming Ignis — a signal to close the lease until the Herald has assessed the pressure behind it.

c.g: Ignis Elementalis Minor (Ignis elementalis minor) is the small-form fire elemental: a compact, hotter-burning Ignis under 1.8 m with a blue-white combustion colour, formed from a narrow high-pressure vent jet rather than a broad fuel gallery. Documented from the narrow high-pressure vent jets and crack-fuelled galleries of the upper-mid tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a compact humanoid body of blue-white flame with a dense brilliant core, hotter than the standard but smaller — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Ignis minor burns hotter than the standard form because its narrow high-pressure vent concentrates fuel without the volume to sustain a larger body — it is a different formation condition, not a younger version. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a very small char-ring under 35 cm across and blue-white ash marks where the arms contacted the walls and no carcass.
c.tax: Ignis Elementalis Minor is placed at species rank beneath Ignis (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a compact sub-1.8 m blue-white flame humanoid with a char-ring under 35 cm and hotter contact burns than the standard orange form. It is separated from its sibling Ignis elementalis by its compact sub-1.8 m blue-white body from a narrow high-pressure vent versus the standard form's 2–3 m orange-yellow body from a broad fuel gallery; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the broad-gallery orange standard form. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: hotter blue-white combustion in a compact humanoid shape sustained by a narrow high-pressure vent jet.
c.ap: Ignis Elementalis Minor stands 1.2–1.8 m, hotter but smaller than standard. Its silhouette is a compact blue-white flame humanoid with a brilliant near-ultraviolet core. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: blue-white combustion colour — the hottest visible fire colour — versus the orange-yellow of the standard form; char-rings under 35 cm (versus 50–60 cm standard), but charred to 5 cm depth (deeper than standard's 2 cm) due to higher temperature; and near-ultraviolet flickers at the core edge that may cause temporary afterimage effects in crews observing it directly. It moves with faster movement than the standard form, darting between vent-jets rather than striding, and shows blue-white, intensely bright, causing temporary afterimage at under 20 m in darkness. Even after dispersal it can be identified from small, deep char-rings and blue-white ash wall-marks with an afterimage-bright soot, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Ignis Elementalis Minor persists in narrow high-pressure vent jets, crack-fuelled galleries, and tight combustion passages of the upper-mid tiers where pressure is concentrated into a narrow stream. It does not breed; it forms where a narrow high-pressure vent jet ignites a concentrated combustion form into a compact humanoid shape, and recurs whenever the jet re-pressurises and the compact blue-white form re-ignites from the same vent crack. It sustains its mass on narrow high-pressure vent jet fuel, drawing in concentrated fuel from the jet to sustain the hotter core. Range is bounded by the pressurised vent jet zone: outside that band the body loses the concentrated fuel and fails faster than the standard form, because it has no broad gallery fuel to fall back on. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a vent crack where the gas-flame has a blue-white colour and is stationary against a significant draught, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Ignis Elementalis Minor is roused by approach to within 3 m of its vent crack or a change in the draught that affects its fuel stream and otherwise lies a still blue-white jet-flame at a crack, visually similar to a high-pressure gas leak. It claims a single vent jet and the passage immediately around it and answers intrusion with rapid darting ignition passes rather than the standard form's sustained advance — it hits and retreats to its vent rather than corraling. Toward grey-folk it is a hit-and-retreat hazard rather than a sustained one; survey crews report that Lanternkin are instructed not to wave a blue-white jet-flame out — a flame that hot won't be extinguished by air movement and the attempt provokes a dart-attack. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Ignis Elementalis Minor: a body of hotter blue-white combustion in a compact humanoid shape sustained by a narrow high-pressure vent jet; immune to fire and heat; also harder to smother than standard because the narrow vent keeps repressurising through partial smothering; and a defining weakness — total fuel cut-off and water flooding, which the standard form also fails to. Its senses run through the medium itself: fuel-pressure and radiant-heat sensing through the vent crack; it tracks heat signatures entering its pressure zone. It tolerates darkness, high pressure, and narrow confinement, which it actually prefers but fails at total fuel cut-off and water flooding. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the blue-white colour, sub-35 cm char-ring depth of 5 cm, and dart-attack pattern, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Ignis Elementalis Minor are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it makes rapid darting ignition passes and returns to its vent; it ignites at higher temperature — contact burns are deeper than standard form; it feeds from a pressurised vent that partially resists smothering; and it can contract to a near-invisible blue spark at the vent crack, then re-expand. Each is a direct expression of narrow high-pressure vent fuel in a compact blue-white humanoid combustion form, so it cannot, for instance, sustain itself away from its vent jet, match the mass or sustained-advance of the standard form, or corral prey in a wide passage. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis Elementalis Minor matters because its similarity to a high-pressure gas leak means crews have tried to extinguish it as a vent hazard and received a dart-attack; the blue colour is now a formal 'do not ventilate' signal in Lanternkin protocols. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a narrow high-pressure vent hazard requiring fuel cut-off rather than ventilation, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper-mid vent-crack passages, now blue-colour-checked before ventilation decisions. Lanternkin notation: a blue-white vent flame that does not waver in a 3 m/s draught is a living Ignis minor, not a gas leak.

c.g: Ignis Elementalis Major (Ignis elementalis major) is the great-form fire elemental: a 4–6 m towering Ignis sustained by a major vent convergence, capable of filling a passage with flame and sustaining it for hours. Documented from the major vent convergences and fuel-rich lava-floor galleries of the lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a massive humanoid pillar of deep red-orange flame with a near-white core that radiates killing heat at 10 m — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. The major form is not a sustained standard form — it forms only where two or more vent-faults converge, and its deeper red-orange colour reflects a richer but slower burn than standard. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a wide char-circle 1.5 m across and blackened walls up to the ceiling along its entire passage length and no carcass.
c.tax: Ignis Elementalis Major is placed at species rank beneath Ignis (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a 4–6 m deep red-orange flame humanoid with a 1.5 m char-circle footprint, formed at vent convergences. It is separated from its sibling Ignis elementalis by its 4–6 m deep red-orange body from a vent convergence versus the standard form's 2–3 m orange-yellow body from a single vent; the two share the genus body-plan but never the single-vent formation. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: deep red-orange combustion in a massive humanoid shape sustained by convergent multi-vent fuel.
c.ap: Ignis Elementalis Major stands 4–6 m and the thermal output of multiple forges. Its silhouette is a massive humanoid pillar of deep red-orange flame with a near-white inner core. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: deep red-orange colour — lower temperature than the minor but far greater volume — producing a 10 m radiant-heat zone that kills exposed skin in under a minute; a char-circle 1.5 m across at each footstep position, blackened 8 cm deep; and ceiling blackening visible 20 m along its passage even after it has moved on, indicating it has held the space for extended periods. It moves with a slow powerful float that chars the ceiling as well as the floor, leaving a 20 m black passage where it has walked, and shows deep red-orange with near-white core, visible ambient glow at 200 m; radiant heat felt at 20 m. Even after dispersal it can be identified from 1.5 m char-circles, ceiling blackening to 20 m, and a near-total absence of flammable material along its passage, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Ignis Elementalis Major persists in major vent convergences and lava-floor galleries of the lower tiers where multiple fuel streams sustain a massive combustion mass. It does not breed; it forms where convergent multi-vent fuel pressure ignites a massive combustion form into a sustained humanoid shape, and recurs whenever the vent convergence refuels and the massive form re-ignites from the same convergence zone. It sustains its mass on convergent multi-vent fuel from two or more vent-faults, drawing in multi-stream vent fuel drawn from the convergence to sustain the massive volume. Range is bounded by the multi-vent convergence zone: outside that band the body reduces to standard size where the secondary vents fail, then disperses. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a passage where two vent-fuelled zones have begun producing a shared bilateral gas-flame at their junction, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Ignis Elementalis Major is roused by any crew entering its passage or any reduction in its fuel supply (which makes it range-extend to find more) and otherwise lies a pillar of slow deep fire filling a passage, potentially mistaken for a vent field or lava glow. It claims a single vent convergence and the passages that feed it and answers intrusion with filling the passage with its body width to block retreat, then advancing; it does not dart or corral — it simply fills the space. Toward grey-folk it is an extreme continuous-radiant-heat hazard; survey crews report that Forgemasters do not approach a passage with ceiling-blackening over 15 m of continuous span; that span indicates the major form has held it. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Ignis Elementalis Major: a body of deep red-orange combustion in a massive humanoid shape sustained by convergent multi-vent fuel; immune to fire, heat, and physical impact; the vent convergence resupplies it through partial smothering faster than the standard form; and a defining weakness — total convergent fuel cut-off requiring all supply vents to be sealed simultaneously — sealing one vent only redirects and enrages it. Its senses run through the medium itself: radiant-heat sensing at 300 m through the vent convergence; it detects warm bodies at the edge of its passage before they see the red glow. It tolerates extreme heat, fuel-rich air, and enclosed passages but fails at simultaneous sealing of all supply vents. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the 4–6 m deep red-orange body, 1.5 m char-circle, and ceiling-blackened passage, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Ignis Elementalis Major are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it fills the passage width with its body, blocking all retreat routes; it advances to maintain contact with its convergence fuel supply, stretching up to 20 m from the junction while sustaining full form; it re-fuels through partial smothering if even one supply vent remains open; and it radiates killing heat at 10 m without direct contact — a crew can be fatally burned without ever touching the body. Each is a direct expression of convergent multi-vent fuel in a massive deep-red humanoid combustion form, so it cannot, for instance, fit into passages under 2 m wide, move quickly, or sustain itself when all supply vents are sealed. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis Elementalis Major matters because ceiling-blackening over 15 m of continuous passage span is the definitive field sign of a major form territory, and Forgemasters now refuse all drilling leases within 200 m of a 15 m blackened span. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an extreme vent-convergence hazard requiring simultaneous multi-vent sealing and mandatory black-span survey before any lease, and its presence reshapes routes through the lower vent-convergence passages, now hard-banned within 200 m of a continuous 15 m ceiling-blackened span. The Forgemasters' saying 'the ceiling tells you where it lives' references the major form's habit of holding a passage for days, blackening the stone above, before ranging out.

c.g: Ignis Elementalis Nobilis (Ignis elementalis nobilis) is the high-form fire elemental: a rare Ignis body-plan of unusual structural persistence, distinguished from the standard, minor, and major forms by a differentiated internal flame-architecture — a cool blue outer layer, a white-hot inner shell, and a violet-ultraviolet core — a heritable body-plan, not a rank. Documented from deep primary vent-pressure zones of metamorphic terrain; not confirmed in the active Gorge, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a precisely layered flame humanoid with three distinct combustion zones: cool outer, white-hot middle, and violet-ultraviolet inner core — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Cultural traditions across the underdark describe the nobilis form as a 'prince' or 'lord' of fire, but this journal classifies it anatomically: its three-layer combustion structure and its ability to maintain form without consuming fuel are what separate it from the other three Ignis forms. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a slow extinction leaving three concentric char-rings — cool outer (1 m), white-hot middle (50 cm), and a violet-soot central mark (15 cm) and no carcass.
c.tax: Ignis Elementalis Nobilis is placed at species rank beneath Ignis (Pyric order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a three-layer combustion architecture (cool outer, white-hot middle, violet-ultraviolet core), ability to sustain form without consuming fuel, and three-ring dispersal sign. It is separated from its sibling Ignis elementalis major by its precise three-layer differentiated combustion architecture and fuel-independent persistence versus the major form's undifferentiated massive single-layer deep-red combustion; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the undifferentiated single-layer major body. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: a precisely differentiated flame humanoid with a cool blue outer layer, white-hot middle shell, and violet-ultraviolet inner core sustained by deep primary vent-pressure.
c.ap: Ignis Elementalis Nobilis stands 3–4 m — comparable to the nobilis Terrae form, smaller than the Ignis major. Its silhouette is a humanoid of visibly differentiated flame layers: a cool blue outer body that does not ignite contact, a white-hot middle shell visible as a bright ring through the outer layer, and a brilliant violet-ultraviolet core at the centre of the torso-mass. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a cool outer flame layer that does not scorch on touch — the only Ignis form that can be approached to 0.5 m without contact burns, because the outer layer is below ignition temperature; a violet-ultraviolet inner core visible as a brilliant spot through two outer layers, distinguishable from the minor's blue-white by the concentric layering around it; and three concentric char-rings on dispersal: an outer cool-soot ring at 1 m, a white-hot char ring at 50 cm (8 cm deep), and a violet-soot central mark of 15 cm. It moves with a slow, precise stride producing the three-ring char pattern — the cool outer layer means only the footstep centre chars, not the full foot-width, and shows a complex glow: cool blue ambient outer light at 100 m, white-hot ring visible through it at 60 m, and violet-ultraviolet core flicker visible at 200 m in total darkness. Even after dispersal it can be identified from the three-ring char pattern at each step, and violet-tinted soot on passage walls from the inner core's radiation, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Ignis Elementalis Nobilis persists in deep primary vent-pressure zones of metamorphic terrain where the oldest geological fuel sources provide sustained and differentiated combustion; not confirmed in the active Gorge but inferred from the three-ring dispersal traces found at depth. It does not breed; it forms where deep primary vent-pressure in metamorphic terrain differentiates a combustion form into three distinct combustion zones, a process requiring older and more sustained fuel than the standard or major form, and recurs whenever the primary vent re-pressurises the differentiated form over a much longer interval than standard or major Ignis — weeks to months rather than hours. It sustains its mass on deep primary vent-pressure in metamorphic terrain, drawing in minimal; the nobilis form is unique among Ignis forms in that it does not consume ambient fuel at rest — it sustains through its own internal recombustion cycle, consuming only when it expands its outer layer. Range is bounded by the deep primary vent zone: outside that band the body does not collapse immediately like other Ignis forms — the three-layer architecture allows it to reduce to a cool-outer-only state (cool blue ambient glow, no internal heat perceptible) before failing; it can remain in this reduced state for days. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a vent passage in metamorphic terrain where three distinct flame-colour zones are visible at one vent mouth, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Ignis Elementalis Nobilis is roused by a threat to its vent, a significant seismic event, or another Ignis form encroaching on its primary vent zone and otherwise lies a reduced cool-blue ambient glow, producing no char-ring and no scorching — the only resting Ignis form that does not damage the floor below it. It claims a single primary vent zone and its approach passages and answers intrusion with expanding the white-hot middle layer outward before the cool outer layer — effectively reversing its normal architecture to expose the lethal middle layer to the environment — which it can hold for up to 30 minutes before the structure destabilises. Toward grey-folk it is the only Ignis form with recorded behavioural differences based on crew approach — at rest it neither tracks nor reacts to passing warmth; it responds only to direct vent-threat or another Ignis encroachment; survey crews report that Stonesong Heralds and Forgemaster senior surveyors have a shared protocol: a resting nobilis site is flagged with a violet chalk mark and left undisturbed; the three-ring char pattern in an otherwise undamaged passage is sufficient to close a lease. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Ignis Elementalis Nobilis: a body of a precisely differentiated flame humanoid with a cool blue outer layer, white-hot middle shell, and violet-ultraviolet inner core sustained by deep primary vent-pressure; immune to fire and heat in all three layers; the cool outer layer also provides a buffer against smothering attempts, since the outer layer is not consuming fuel and does not fail under smothering the way other Ignis forms do; and a defining weakness — the three-layer structure fails if the white-hot middle layer is directly targeted — specifically, a continuous water-jet sustained for over 3 minutes aimed at the middle-layer ring, which disrupts the combustion differentiation. Its senses run through the medium itself: a combination of radiant-heat sensing (standard Ignis), fuel-gradient sensing (standard), and a novel pressure-sensitivity through the primary vent that other Ignis forms lack — it detects other Ignis forms at 500 m through fuel-competition sensing. It tolerates extreme heat, darkness, and prolonged reduced-state (cool outer only) but fails at sustained water-jet targeting the white-hot middle layer. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the three-layer combustion architecture (cool blue outer, white-hot middle ring, violet core), resting non-charring, and three-ring dispersal mark, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Ignis Elementalis Nobilis are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it expands the lethal white-hot middle layer outward to expose it — 'inside-out' threat display — for up to 30 minutes; it sustains form without consuming fuel through an internal recombustion cycle, making standard fuel-starvation approaches ineffective; it detects other Ignis forms at 500 m through fuel-competition sensing and alerts them through vent-pressure modulation; and it can reduce to a cool-state (non-charring) and hold that state for days, making it effectively invisible to standard fire-hazard detection. Each is a direct expression of deep primary vent-pressure differentiating a flame body into three distinct combustion zones, so it cannot, for instance, be found in ordinary vent-fault passages, be identified by standard char-ring survey methods, or be safely extinguished by smothering. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ignis Elementalis Nobilis matters because its three-ring dispersal trace, if found at depth in the Gorge, indicates a primary vent with geological age and structural differentiation that would redefine the Forgemasters' understanding of the lower fuel system. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as not an active field hazard in the Gorge; retained as a context entry and a comparison form for Ignis anatomy; a violet chalk close-lease marker is used wherever three-ring char is found, and its presence reshapes routes through no active route impact; a three-ring char trace in a deep survey passage triggers a violet chalk close-lease mark and Herald assessment. The Heralds' notation for a nobilis site is three concentric circles in violet chalk — the same pattern as its dispersal mark. The outer circle is drawn with a dashed line: cool, approach-safe. The middle circle solid: lethal. The inner dot: the core, which remains for weeks.

c.g: Magmatic Elementals is the order-rank grouping of fire elementals whose bodies are molten or cooled volcanic rock within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of fire-heated rock that carries its own heat and can persist by cooling rather than dying outright, and divide below into family groups by whether the rock is molten and flowing or cooled to mobile glass. Magmatic elementals bridge fire and earth, which is why their dormant forms fool crews into walking onto live benches.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Magmatic Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a fire-heated rock body, molten or glassy, not an open-combustion one. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct rock-heat state with its own formation and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a glowing lava lobe or an angular black-glass figure. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a cooled basalt lobe, scorched track, or a litter of glass flakes — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Magmatic Elementals occupies lava benches, thermal vents, and quenched obsidian margins. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where vent heat melts basalt or a fast quench freezes it to glass and recur with the vent reheats or re-quenches the same rock, sustaining themselves on thermal-vent heat and residual rock-heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members flow or stalk while warm and fall dormant as stone or glass when cooled, fooling crews into crossing. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a rock-heat body, fire immunity, and a cooling-failure mode. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing heat or cutting glass, rock-melting repair, and dormancy disguise. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Magmatic Elementals matters because their dormant forms are walking benches and slag-figures that wake under crews' feet. It lets survey reports sort rock-heat hazards by state, so a crew knows whether to fear heat-flow or glass-shrapnel without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Molten Magmatics is the family-rank grouping of liquid-rock fire elementals of the molten kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a flowing molten-rock body that radiates heat and engulfs, and divide below into genus groups by lava chemistry and vent heat. The hottest-bodied of all elementals.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Molten Magmatics is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a molten flowing rock body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a molten body with its own vent source.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a roiling lava lobe with a cracking crust. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a cooled basalt lobe and scorched track — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Molten Magmatics occupies lava benches and thermal vents. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where vent heat melts a basalt mass and recur with the vent reheats the basalt, sustaining themselves on thermal-vent heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: flows to engulf and radiates killing heat. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: molten body and heat-radiance. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing and heat-radiation. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Molten Magmatics matters because kills by radiant heat on the warm benches. It lets survey reports sort molten hazards from cooled-glass ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Roiler is the genus-rank grouping of flowing molten-basalt elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a roiling lava body that engulfs and radiates heat, and divide below into species groups by lava lobe size and vent strength. A self-contained flow of living lava.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Roiler is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a molten-basalt flowing body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a molten lava body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a roiling crust-skinned lava lobe. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a cooled basalt lobe and glazed track — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Roiler occupies lava benches and vents. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where vent heat melts basalt and recur with the vent reheats the cooled lobe, sustaining themselves on thermal-vent heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: flows to engulf and radiates heat. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: molten body and glowing-crack core. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing and heat-radiation. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Roiler matters because lethal by radiant heat alone. It lets survey reports sort flowing molten forms from glassy ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Magma Roiler (Lava fluens) is a molten-rock elemental that flows as a self-contained lava body and sets stone-hard when it cools. Documented from the lava benches and thermal vents of the warm lower gorge, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a glowing mass of molten basalt skinned in dark cooling crust — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Unlike open flame, it carries its own heat as molten rock and survives losing its fuel, merely cooling toward dormancy. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a cooled black basalt lobe frozen in mid-flow across the bench and no carcass.
c.tax: Magma Roiler is placed at species rank beneath Roiler (Magmatic order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a molten-basalt body, a dark cooling crust over a glowing core, and stone-hard dormancy when cooled. It is separated from its sibling the Obsidian Slagling by its hot flowing molten body versus the Slagling's cooled, sharp-edged glass body; the two share the genus body-plan but never the cooled glass form. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: molten basalt held above its melting point by thermal-vent heat.
c.ap: Magma Roiler stands 2–4 m flowing lobe, several tonnes. Its silhouette is a roiling lava lobe with a crusting skin that cracks to show the glow. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a dark crust webbed with glowing orange cracks; radiant heat felt at several paces, scorching the air; and a sulphurous volcanic reek and the hiss of contact with damp. It moves with a slow viscous flow downhill, faster when freshly fed by a vent, and shows a deep orange core-glow through its cracking crust. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a cooled basalt lobe and a scorched, glazed track across the bench, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Magma Roiler persists in lava benches, thermal vents, and basalt flats of the warm lower gorge, where vent heat keeps it molten. It does not breed; it forms where fire-pressure and vent heat melt a basalt mass into a coherent flowing body, and recurs whenever the vent reheats the cooled basalt and it flows again. It sustains its mass on thermal-vent heat, drawing in rock melted into its mass, which it incorporates to grow. Range is bounded by the heated vent zone: outside that band the body cools, crusts over, and sets into dormant basalt. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a basalt bench that glows and flows uphill against the cooling gradient, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Magma Roiler is roused by a fresh pulse of vent heat or an intrusion onto its bench and otherwise lies a cooled basalt lobe that can re-melt without warning. It claims a single lava bench and answers intrusion with flowing to engulf and ignite, and radiating killing heat. Toward grey-folk it is slow but deadly by heat alone; survey crews report that Forgemasters read a bench's glowing cracks to judge whether the basalt is dormant or roused. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Magma Roiler: a body of molten basalt held above its melting point by thermal-vent heat; immune to fire and heat, and unharmed by piercing; and a defining weakness — rapid cooling — flooding or cold blast crusts and immobilises it. Its senses run through the medium itself: heat-gradient sensing; blind to cool, still objects. It tolerates open flame, sulphur, and darkness but fails at rapid cooling and full immersion. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the crusted lobe and glowing-crack core, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Magma Roiler are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it engulfs and incinerates with molten mass; it radiates killing heat at several paces; it melts and incorporates rock to repair and grow; and it crusts hard to shield itself, then re-melts. Each is a direct expression of vent-heated molten basalt, so it cannot, for instance, leap, fly, cross deep water, or persist once fully cooled and immersed. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Magma Roiler matters because it makes the warm lower benches lethal by radiant heat alone, and crews now read glowing cracks before crossing. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a heat-and-engulf hazard logged with lava benches, and its presence reshapes routes through the lower-gorge lava benches, now crossed only when the basalt reads dormant. Basalt that flows uphill against its cooling gradient is the Forgemasters' sign of a roused Roiler.

c.g: Vitric Magmatics is the family-rank grouping of cooled-glass fire elementals of the volcanic-glass kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a brittle volcanic-glass body that cuts and shatters into shrapnel, and divide below into genus groups by glass type and residual heat. What a molten elemental becomes when chilled fast.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Vitric Magmatics is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a cooled volcanic-glass body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a glass body with its own quench origin.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an angular black-glass figure. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a litter of glass flakes and cut-marks — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Vitric Magmatics occupies quenched lava margins and obsidian flows. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fast quench freezes lava to glass and recur with a fresh quench re-forms the glass, sustaining themselves on residual rock-heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: stalks and slashes while warm, shatters under a hard blow. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: glass body and razor edges. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: cutting and shrapnel-burst. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Vitric Magmatics matters because cuts and scatters shrapnel on the obsidian margins. It lets survey reports sort cooled-glass hazards from molten ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.
