c.g: Slagling is the genus-rank grouping of obsidian-glass elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a razor-edged obsidian body that bursts into shrapnel when struck, and divide below into species groups by glass quench and residual heat. A walking blade of volcanic glass.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Slagling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an obsidian-glass body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a brittle glass body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a jagged black-glass figure. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — black glass flakes and cut-marks — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Slagling occupies quenched obsidian margins. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a fast quench freezes lava to glass and recur with a fresh quench re-forms it, sustaining themselves on residual heat.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: grinds forward to slash, bursts when struck. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: glass body and razor edges. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: cutting and shrapnel-burst. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Slagling matters because cuts crews and scatters shrapnel. It lets survey reports sort brittle glass forms from molten ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Obsidian Slagling (Vitrum igneum) is a cooled magmatic elemental of volcanic glass that moves as a brittle, razor-edged body and shatters into shrapnel. Documented from the quenched lava margins and obsidian flows of the lower gorge, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a body of black volcanic glass shot through with dim residual heat — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is what a Roiler becomes when chilled fast and kept just warm enough to move: a walking blade of glass. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a burst of black glass shards scattered across the floor and no carcass.
c.tax: Obsidian Slagling is placed at species rank beneath Slagling (Magmatic order, Fire Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a volcanic-glass body, razor conchoidal edges, and a shrapnel-burst on heavy impact. It is separated from its sibling the Magma Roiler by its cooled brittle glass body and shrapnel versus the Roiler's hot molten flow and radiant heat; the two share the genus body-plan but never the molten flow. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: volcanic glass quenched from basalt, kept barely mobile by residual heat.
c.ap: Obsidian Slagling stands 1.2–1.8 m, an angular glassy figure. Its silhouette is a jagged man-shaped mass of fused black glass shards. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a glossy black conchoidal surface that catches light in curved facets; a dim red residual heat-glint deep in its core, far less than a Roiler's; and razor edges everywhere, so it cuts anything it brushes. It moves with a grinding, brittle stalk, slower and stiffer than the molten Roiler, and shows a faint deep-red core-glint, easily missed in lamplight. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a litter of black glass flakes and fine cut-marks on the floor and walls, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Obsidian Slagling persists in quenched lava margins, obsidian flows, and cooled vent-edges where fast chilling made glass instead of crystal. It does not breed; it forms where a Roiler's flow is quenched fast at a wet margin and frozen as mobile glass, and recurs whenever a fresh quench at the margin re-forms the glass body. It sustains its mass on residual heat in cooled glass, drawing in obsidian flakes drawn in and fused to repair. Range is bounded by the warm quench-margin: outside that band the body cools fully and stops, an inert glass statue. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as an obsidian margin that grows angular limbs against the flow-grain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Obsidian Slagling is roused by contact or a heavy blow and otherwise lies an inert-looking glass figure at a flow margin. It claims a single obsidian margin and answers intrusion with grinding forward to slash, and bursting into shrapnel if struck hard. Toward grey-folk it is passive until brushed; survey crews report that Forgemasters value its glass for blades but learn that striking it scatters razor shrapnel. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Obsidian Slagling: a body of volcanic glass quenched from basalt, kept barely mobile by residual heat; immune to fire and heat, and to cutting, which only chips it; and a defining weakness — heavy bludgeoning, which shatters it into harmless shards, and full cooling. Its senses run through the medium itself: heat-gradient and vibration through its glass; blind otherwise. It tolerates heat, sulphur, and darkness but fails at a crushing blow and full cooling. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the angular glass body and razor edges, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Obsidian Slagling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it slashes with razor conchoidal edges on contact; it bursts into a cone of glass shrapnel when struck hard; it fuses loose obsidian flakes to repair edges; and it stands utterly still, mistaken for cooled slag. Each is a direct expression of quenched mobile volcanic glass, so it cannot, for instance, flow, radiate strong heat, fly, or survive a hard crushing blow. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Obsidian Slagling matters because it makes obsidian margins a cutting-and-shrapnel hazard, and crews now never strike a glass figure they cannot identify. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a blade-and-shrapnel hazard logged with obsidian flows, and its presence reshapes routes through the quenched lava margins, now skirted and never hammered. Angular limbs growing from an obsidian margin against the flow-grain mark a forming Slagling.

c.g: Water Elementals is the class-rank grouping of true elementals composed of the water element, liquid and frozen within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of water — liquid or ice — bound to a water source, drawing mass from it rather than breeding, and divide below into order groups by whether the water is liquid (Fluvial) or frozen (Glacial). Water is the Stillwardens' and Mist-Touched scouts' chief elemental concern, lurking in every sump, fall, seep, and cold shaft.
c.tax: Placed at class rank, Water Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a pure water-element body, liquid or ice, animated by water-pressure and bound to a source. A new order belongs here only if it shows a distinct water-state body-plan with its own source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a standing column, foam-churn, creeping rime, or hail-cloud where water should behave normally. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a windless standing wave, scoured plunge-pool, breath-frost, or melting hail — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Water Elementals occupies sumps, pools, falls, cold seeps, and cold updraft shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure animates still water, moving water, a cold seep, or a chilled updraft and recur with the sump refills, the fall runs, or the cold source re-chills, sustaining themselves on water-pressure, falling-water energy, or cold-seep and updraft chill.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members hide as ordinary water, frost, or mist and rouse to intrusion, drowning, battering, locking, or pelting. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a water or ice body, immunity to cutting and to its own state, and a freezing or warmth failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each order and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: surging, battering, freezing, and hail-shot. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Water Elementals matters because they make every body of water and patch of cold a drowning, battering, or immobilising trap. It lets survey reports sort water hazards by state, so a crew knows whether to sound a pool, rope below a fall, or move fast through cold without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Fluvial Elementals is the order-rank grouping of water elementals whose bodies are liquid water, still or moving within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of coherent liquid water bound to a water source, dependent on it for mass, and divide below into family groups by whether the water is still and deep or aerated and falling. Fluvial elementals are the Stillwardens' chief concern, since they hide in every sump and fall.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Fluvial Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a liquid-water body, not ice or vapour, bound to a water source. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct liquid-water mode with its own source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a standing water column or a churning foam-body where water should spread or fall normally. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a windless standing wave, scoured plunge-pool, or high-water mark — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Fluvial Elementals occupies sumps, pools, cascades, and falls. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure animates still or moving water at a source and recur with the sump refills or the fall keeps running, sustaining themselves on water-pressure or the kinetic energy of falling water.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members hide as ordinary water and rouse to intrusion, drowning by surge or battering by churn. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a coherent liquid body, immunity to cutting, and a freezing or draining failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: surging, drowning, sweeping, and spray. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Fluvial Elementals matters because they make every sump and fall a drowning trap. It lets survey reports sort water hazards by motion, so a crew knows whether to sound a pool or rope below a fall without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Still-Water Fluvials is the family-rank grouping of deep still-water elementals of sumps and pools within the True Elementals. Its members share a dark coherent still-water body that surges from depth, and divide below into genus groups by sump depth and water chemistry. The drowning hazard of every quiet pool.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Still-Water Fluvials is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a still-water body bound to a sump. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a still-water body with its own pool source.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a standing dark water column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a windless standing wave and high-water mark — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Still-Water Fluvials occupies flooded sumps and pools. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure animates a sump and recur with the sump refills, sustaining themselves on water-pressure.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: hides as a pool, surges to drown. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: coherent still-water body and pressure-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: surging and drowning. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Still-Water Fluvials matters because makes quiet pools drowning traps. It lets survey reports sort still-water hazards from white-water ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Surger is the genus-rank grouping of rising still-water column elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a still-water column that rears and breaks to drown, and divide below into species groups by sump depth and surge force. A column of water that should not stand.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Surger is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a rising still-water column body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a still-water column body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an upright breaking water column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a windless standing wave — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Surger occupies flooded sump-chambers. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure raises a sump column and recur with the sump refills, sustaining themselves on water-pressure.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: rears and breaks over intruders. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: coherent column and pressure-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: surging, drowning, and footing-sweep. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Surger matters because drowns crews in unsounded sumps. It lets survey reports sort rising column forms from churning ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Sump Surger (Unda profunda) is a liquid-water elemental that rises as a coherent column from a flooded sump and drowns prey in a directed surge. Documented from the flooded sumps and sump-chambers of the lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a self-cohering column of dark water that holds its shape against gravity — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It carries no solid part; its body is water given will, holding a column where water should spread and fall. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden flat flood across the chamber floor and a draining gurgle and no carcass.
c.tax: Sump Surger is placed at species rank beneath Surger (Fluvial order, Water Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a self-cohering liquid-water column, a directed drowning surge, and collapse to a flat flood when dispersed. It is separated from its sibling the Cataract Spume by its dark still-water surge from depth versus the Spume's aerated white-water churn from falls; the two share the genus body-plan but never the aerated white water. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: still sump-water held coherent by water-pressure animation.
c.ap: Sump Surger stands 2–3 m column, tonnes of water. Its silhouette is an upright water column that rears and curls into a breaking wave. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a column of water standing where water should pool, with a slick coherent skin; silt and debris suspended in a fixed pattern rather than settling; and a low surge-sound and a pressure-swell felt through the floor before it rises. It moves with a gliding surge across wet floor, fastest toward and through standing water, and shows none; dark and reflective. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a high-water mark, scattered silt, and a drained sump where it drew its mass, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Sump Surger persists in flooded sumps, sump-chambers, and standing pools of the lower tiers, where a body of water is always to hand. It does not breed; it forms where water-pressure animates a flooded sump into a coherent column, and recurs whenever the sump refills and the next pressure-pulse raises it again. It sustains its mass on water-pressure in flooded chambers, drawing in water drawn from its sump to replace what it sheds. Range is bounded by the wetted, water-supplied zone: outside that band the body collapses into a flat flood on dry, draining ground. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a sump whose surface stands in a low standing wave without any wind, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Sump Surger is roused by entry into its water or heavy footfall on its wet floor and otherwise lies a still flooded pool indistinguishable from any sump. It claims a single sump-chamber and answers intrusion with rearing and breaking over the intruder to drown and sweep. Toward grey-folk it is reactive to anything entering its water; survey crews report that Stillwardens sound a sump's depth from dry stone, never wade an unsounded pool. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Sump Surger: a body of still sump-water held coherent by water-pressure animation; immune to piercing, slashing, and cutting, which pass through it; and a defining weakness — freezing and absorbent earth, which lock or drain its body. Its senses run through the medium itself: pressure and motion sensing through the water; blind to dry, still objects. It tolerates darkness, depth, and cold short of freezing but fails at freezing and draining onto absorbent ground. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the coherent column and breaking-surge form, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Sump Surger are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it surges to engulf and drown prey; it sweeps footing out from under crews with a directed wave; it draws water from its sump to repair and grow; and it sinks into the pool to ambush and resurge. Each is a direct expression of water-pressure animating still water, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, hold form when frozen, or cross dry absorbent ground. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Sump Surger matters because it makes every unsounded sump a drowning trap, and crews now sound depth from dry stone before wading. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a drowning hazard logged with sumps and standing pools, and its presence reshapes routes through the lower-tier sumps, now sounded from dry stone and bridged rather than waded. A sump surface standing in a windless standing wave is the Stillwardens' sign a Surger has formed.

c.g: White-Water Fluvials is the family-rank grouping of aerated moving-water elementals of falls and cascades within the True Elementals. Its members share a foaming aerated body bound to falling water, and divide below into genus groups by fall height and flow rate. Born of moving water, anchored to its fall.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, White-Water Fluvials is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an aerated white-water body bound to a cascade. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a white-water body with its own fall.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a churning foam-body at a fall. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a scoured plunge-pool and high spray-line — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: White-Water Fluvials occupies cataracts and cascades. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure animates a cascade's churn and recur with the fall keeps running, sustaining themselves on kinetic energy of falling water.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: churns at its fall, drags prey in to batter. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: aerated foam-body and motion-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: battering, drowning, and spray. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, White-Water Fluvials matters because makes fords near falls lethal. It lets survey reports sort white-water hazards from still-water ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Spume is the genus-rank grouping of cascade white-water elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a foaming cascade body that batters and drowns, and divide below into species groups by fall height and aeration. A roaring churn anchored to a fall.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Spume is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an aerated cascade-bound body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a foaming white-water body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a churning foam-vortex at a fall. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a scoured plunge-pool — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Spume occupies cataracts and falls. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure animates the churn and recur with the fall keeps running, sustaining themselves on falling-water energy.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: drags prey into the battering churn. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: foam-body and motion-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: battering, drowning, and driven spray. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Spume matters because kills crews fording near falls. It lets survey reports sort cascade-churn forms from still-column ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Cataract Spume (Unda spumans) is a white-water elemental that churns at waterfalls and cascades, drowning and battering in aerated foam. Documented from the cataracts, cascades, and falls of the running-water passages, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a roaring body of aerated white water and driven spray — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Surger is dark, still, and rising, the Spume is white, falling, and battering, born of moving water. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden clearing of the foam and a return to ordinary cascade flow and no carcass.
c.tax: Cataract Spume is placed at species rank beneath Spume (Fluvial order, Water Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on an aerated white-water body, a battering-and-drowning churn, and collapse to ordinary flow when dispersed. It is separated from its sibling the Sump Surger by its aerated white churn at falls versus the Surger's dark still surge from depth; the two share the genus body-plan but never the still-water surge. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: aerated, fast-moving water held coherent at a cascade.
c.ap: Cataract Spume stands 2–4 m churning mass at a fall. Its silhouette is a roaring vortex of foam clinging to a cascade. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a churning white foam-body brighter than the surrounding flow; a roar pitched differently from the natural fall, rising as it rouses; and driven spray thrown against the current rather than with it. It moves with anchored to its fall, able to surge a short way up or down the cascade but not leave running water, and shows none; pale with foam. Even after dispersal it can be identified from an unusually scoured plunge-pool and spray-wetted stone above the natural splash-line, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Cataract Spume persists in cataracts, cascades, and falls of the running-water passages, where aerated moving water sustains it. It does not breed; it forms where water-pressure animates the aerated churn at a cascade, and recurs whenever the fall keeps running and the churn re-coheres after dispersal. It sustains its mass on kinetic energy of falling water, drawing in the cascade's own flow, drawn into its churn. Range is bounded by the length of its cascade: outside that band the body collapses to ordinary flow away from the fall. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a fall whose foam churns against the current and roars off-pitch, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Cataract Spume is roused by anything entering its plunge-pool or crossing near its fall and otherwise lies it cannot truly rest while the fall runs; it churns on. It claims a single cascade or fall and answers intrusion with dragging prey into the churn to batter and drown. Toward grey-folk it is hazard to anyone fording near a fall; survey crews report that Stillwardens rope crossings well below a roaring fall, never at the plunge-pool. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Cataract Spume: a body of aerated, fast-moving water held coherent at a cascade; immune to cutting and bludgeoning, which pass through foam; and a defining weakness — freezing and diversion of its fall, which starve it. Its senses run through the medium itself: motion and pressure through the churn. It tolerates darkness, cold short of freezing, and depth but fails at freezing and the diversion or drying of its fall. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the foam-body and fall-anchored churn, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Cataract Spume are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it drags prey into a battering, drowning churn; it throws driven spray to blind and unbalance; it scours and undercuts footing at its plunge-pool; and it surges a short way up or down its cascade to reach prey. Each is a direct expression of the kinetic churn of aerated falling water, so it cannot, for instance, leave running water, produce heat, or hold form when frozen. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Cataract Spume matters because it makes fords near falls lethal, and crews now rope crossings well below any roaring plunge-pool. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a drowning-and-battering hazard logged with falls and cascades, and its presence reshapes routes through the cascade fords, now roped low and never crossed at the plunge-pool. A fall whose foam roars off-pitch and throws spray upstream marks a roused Spume.

c.g: Glacial Elementals is the order-rank grouping of water elementals whose bodies are ice and cold, ground-bound or airborne within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of frozen or freezing water that grows by chilling its surroundings and fails in warmth, and divide below into family groups by whether the ice creeps along the ground or forms and flings in the air. Glacial elementals are the cold-country water kin, hazards of the chill high tiers.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Glacial Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: an ice or cold body grown from frozen water, not liquid or vapour. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct ice mode with its own cold source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a creeping rime-front or a hail-forming cloud where cold collects. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — breath-frost, a rime-sheet, or melting hailstones — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Glacial Elementals occupies cold seeps, ice-rimed galleries, and cold updraft shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure freezes a cold seep or chills an updraft and recur with the cold seep refreezes or the updraft re-chills, sustaining themselves on cold-seep water or chilled updraft air.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members pose as frost or mist and rouse to warmth or intrusion, locking prey or flinging ice. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: an ice body, immunity to cold, and a warmth-failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: freezing-advance, immobilising, and hail-shot. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Glacial Elementals matters because they make the chill tiers an immobilising or ranged-ice hazard. It lets survey reports sort cold hazards by mode, so a crew knows whether to fear ground-frost lock or flung hail without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Rime Glacials is the family-rank grouping of creeping ground-ice elementals of the rime kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a creeping rime body that freezes the ground and locks prey, and divide below into genus groups by seep chemistry and creep rate. The immobilising frost of the cold galleries.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Rime Glacials is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a creeping rime-ice body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a creeping ice body with its own cold seep.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an advancing rime-front. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — breath-frost and a rime-sheet — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Rime Glacials occupies cold seeps and ice-rimed galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure freezes a cold seep and recur with the seep refreezes, sustaining themselves on cold-seep water.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: creeps as frost, freezes feet to lock prey. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: rime body and cold-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: freezing-advance and immobilising. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Rime Glacials matters because locks crews in cold galleries. It lets survey reports sort creeping-frost hazards from flung-ice ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Rimeling is the genus-rank grouping of rime-ice creeping elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a needle-ice body that creeps and freezes prey in place, and divide below into species groups by ice habit and creep rate. A freezing crust that grows toward you.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Rimeling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a creeping needle-ice body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a rime-ice body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a feathery rime-front. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — breath-frost and frost-crack rings — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Rimeling occupies cold seeps and ice galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure freezes a cold seep and recur with the cold seep refreezes, sustaining themselves on cold-seep water.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: creeps and locks feet in fresh ice. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: rime body and cold-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: freezing-advance and immobilising. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Rimeling matters because immobilises crews in cold galleries. It lets survey reports sort creeping-rime forms from hail-cloud ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Rime Creeper (Glacies repens) is a glacial elemental of creeping rime-ice that advances as a freezing crust and locks prey in place. Documented from the cold seeps and ice-rimed galleries of the high tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a spreading crust of needle-ice and rime fed by cold seep — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Unlike liquid kin it spreads by freezing the water around it, growing its body from whatever it chills. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a patch of melting rime and a wet stain where the ice gave way and no carcass.
c.tax: Rime Creeper is placed at species rank beneath Rimeling (Glacial order, Water Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a creeping rime-ice body, freezing-advance that locks prey, and melt-failure in warmth. It is separated from its sibling the Hail Sleetling by its slow creeping ground-frost versus the Sleetling's flung ice-shot; the two share the genus body-plan but never the flung projectiles. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: rime and needle-ice grown from frozen seep-water.
c.ap: Rime Creeper stands indefinite creeping front, up to 3 m across. Its silhouette is an advancing crust of feathery rime with a freezing edge. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a leading edge of fresh needle-ice growing visibly outward; a cold that frosts breath and fittings before the ice is touched; and a crackling tick as it freezes new water into its body. It moves with a slow creep across any wet or damp surface, freezing as it goes, halted by warmth, and shows a pale ice-sheen, no native light. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a sheet of rime that later melts to a wet stain ringed with frost-cracks, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Rime Creeper persists in cold seeps, ice-rimed galleries, and chill high-tier passages where freezing water can be grown. It does not breed; it forms where water-pressure animates a cold seep and freezes it into a creeping rime body, and recurs whenever the cold seep refreezes after a thaw and the rime re-advances. It sustains its mass on cold-seep water frozen into the body, drawing in ambient water and damp, frozen and added to its mass. Range is bounded by the cold zone of its seep: outside that band the body melts and fails in any warmth. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a damp patch that grows feathery rime against a warm draught, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Rime Creeper is roused by contact with its freezing edge or warmth that it can chill and otherwise lies a still rime-sheet posing as natural frost. It claims a single cold gallery and answers intrusion with freezing over feet and gear to lock prey in place. Toward grey-folk it is a slow trap that immobilises; survey crews report that Mist-Touched scouts watch for breath-frost in a still gallery as the first sign. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Rime Creeper: a body of rime and needle-ice grown from frozen seep-water; immune to cold and to piercing, which only chips its crust; and a defining weakness — fire and warmth, which melt it at once. Its senses run through the medium itself: cold and moisture-gradient sensing. It tolerates cold, depth, and darkness but fails at warmth and fire. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the creeping rime-front and freezing edge, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Rime Creeper are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it freezes water and damp to grow its body; it locks feet and gear in fresh ice to immobilise; it advances a freezing front across any wet surface; and it refreezes from a buried ice-seed after seeming to melt. Each is a direct expression of the freezing of seep-water into rime, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, fling projectiles, or survive warmth. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Rime Creeper matters because it locks crews in place in cold galleries, and breath-frost in still air is now the first mask-and-move signal. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an immobilising cold hazard logged with cold seeps, and its presence reshapes routes through the high-tier cold galleries, now crossed quickly before rime can lock footing. Feathery rime growing against a warm draught marks an advancing Rime Creeper.

c.g: Sleet Glacials is the family-rank grouping of airborne ice-forming elementals of the hail kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a supercooled cloud body that forms and flings hail, and divide below into genus groups by updraft chill and droplet load. The ranged cold hazard of the high shafts.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Sleet Glacials is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a supercooled cloud body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows an airborne ice body with its own cold updraft.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a swirling hail-forming cloud. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — melting hailstones and ice-pocked stone — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Sleet Glacials occupies cold updraft shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure chills an updraft into a hail cloud and recur with the updraft re-chills, sustaining themselves on chilled updraft air.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: drifts in cold shafts, flings hail at intruders. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: cloud body and air-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: hail-shot and air-chilling. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Sleet Glacials matters because makes cold shafts a ranged ice hazard. It lets survey reports sort flung-ice hazards from creeping-frost ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Sleetling is the genus-rank grouping of hail-flinging cloud elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a supercooled cloud body that flings stinging hail, and divide below into species groups by updraft chill and hail size. A cloud that hurls ice; the only Water genus that attacks at range.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Sleetling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a hail-forming cloud body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a supercooled cloud body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a swirling grey hail-cloud 1.5 to 2.5 m across, denser than ordinary cave mist, that bulges before each volley. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — melting hailstones, ice-pocked stone, and a sudden chill that frosts breath before any cloud is seen — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Sleetling occupies cold updraft shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where water-pressure chills an updraft and recur with the updraft re-chills, sustaining themselves on chilled updraft air.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: flings hail volleys at intruders, chills the air to numb pursuers, and thickens to dim lights. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a supercooled cloud body, air-motion and warmth sensing, cold-immunity, and a defining warmth-failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: hail-shot and air-chilling. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Sleetling matters because ranged ice hazard in cold shafts. It lets survey reports sort hail-cloud forms from creeping-rime ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Hail Sleetling (Glacies iaculans) is a glacial elemental that forms hail in its core and flings stinging ice-shot at intruders. Documented from the cold updraft shafts where chilled damp air rises, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a swirling cloud-body of supercooled droplets and forming hail — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Rime Creeper freezes the ground, the Sleetling freezes the air and hurls the result. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scatter of melting hailstones across the shaft floor and no carcass.
c.tax: Hail Sleetling is placed at species rank beneath Sleetling (Glacial order, Water Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a supercooled cloud-body, flung hail-shot as attack, and melt-failure in warmth. It is separated from its sibling the Rime Creeper by its airborne flung ice-shot versus the Creeper's creeping ground-frost; the two share the genus body-plan but never the creeping ground-frost. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: supercooled droplets and hail held in a swirling cold cloud.
c.ap: Hail Sleetling stands 1.5–2.5 m churning cloud. Its silhouette is a swirling grey cloud-body that bulges and flings ice. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a swirling grey cloud denser than ordinary cave mist; a sudden chill and the rattle of forming hail within it; and bursts of flung ice-shot that clatter off the walls. It moves with a drifting float along cold updrafts, slow and air-bound, and shows none; a dim grey cloud. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a scatter of fast-melting hailstones and ice-pocked stone, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Hail Sleetling persists in cold updraft shafts and chill vents where rising damp air can be supercooled. It does not breed; it forms where water-pressure animates a chilled damp updraft into a hail-forming cloud, and recurs whenever the cold updraft re-chills and the cloud re-forms. It sustains its mass on cold damp updraft air, drawing in supercooled droplets drawn from the rising air. Range is bounded by the cold updraft: outside that band the body warms, melts, and disperses to ordinary mist. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as an updraft mist that rattles with forming ice against the warm draught, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Hail Sleetling is roused by movement into its shaft or a warm light and otherwise lies a still grey cloud posing as cave mist. It claims a single cold updraft shaft and answers intrusion with flinging volleys of stinging hail-shot. Toward grey-folk it is a ranged hazard in cold shafts; survey crews report that Mist-Touched scouts shield their lamps, since light draws its volleys. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Hail Sleetling: a body of supercooled droplets and hail held in a swirling cold cloud; immune to cold and to flung debris; and a defining weakness — fire and warmth, which melt and disperse it. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-motion and warmth sensing. It tolerates cold, darkness, and thin air but fails at warmth and fire. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the swirling cloud and hail-shot bursts, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Hail Sleetling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it flings volleys of stinging hail-shot; it chills the air to numb and slow prey; it draws supercooled droplets to rebuild its cloud; and it thickens to dim lights and obscure aim. Each is a direct expression of supercooled droplets forming and flinging hail, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, freeze the ground, or survive warmth. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Hail Sleetling matters because it makes cold updraft shafts a ranged ice hazard, and scouts now shield lamps that would draw its volleys. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a ranged cold hazard logged with cold updraft shafts, and its presence reshapes routes through the cold shafts, now passed with shielded lamps and quick footing. A cave mist that rattles with forming ice against a warm draught marks a roused Sleetling.

c.g: Air Elementals is the class-rank grouping of true elementals composed of the air element, from gust to storm within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of bounded air — moving, settled, or charged — drawn from draught, seep, or storm rather than bred, and divide below into order groups by whether the air is uncharged (Aeolian) or charged (Fulgurant). Air is the hardest elemental class to see and so the one that kills the unwary scout, betrayed only by dust, dying flame, or crawling arcs.
c.tax: Placed at class rank, Air Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a pure air-element body, moving, settled, or charged, animated by air-pressure. A new order belongs here only if it shows a distinct air-state body-plan with its own source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dust-laden column, an invisible flame-killing pool, an arc-veined cloud, or a clinging glowing mote. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — spiralling dust, a guttering flame, arc-scorch, or a fizzing static residue — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Air Elementals occupies shafts, draught galleries, low dips, charged shafts, and dry static galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure animates a draught, foul-gas pocket, charged pocket, or surface static and recur with the draught resumes or the gas, charge, or static rebuilds, sustaining themselves on air-pressure, channelled draught, foul gas, or electrical charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members hide as ordinary air, mist, or a faint glow and rouse to intrusion, flinging, suffocating, striking, or shocking. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a bounded-air body, immunity to physical attack, and an enclosure, ventilation, grounding, or damp failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each order and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: flinging, suffocation, lightning, and contact-shock. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Air Elementals matters because they make ledges, low dips, and charged galleries lethal in invisible ways. It lets survey reports sort air hazards by state, so a crew knows whether to double-clip, test-flame, stow metal, or dry-wax their lines without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Aeolian Elementals is the order-rank grouping of air elementals whose bodies are moving or settled air without electrical charge within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of bounded air, spinning or pooling, dependent on draught or seep for coherence, and divide below into family groups by whether the air is fast and spinning or slow and settled. Aeolian elementals are the hardest to see, since their bodies are air itself, betrayed only by dust or dying flame.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Aeolian Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a bounded-air body, moving or still, without the electrical charge of storm kin. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct air mode with its own draught or seep source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dust-laden spinning column or an invisible pool that kills flame. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — dust spiralling against the draught, or a flame that gutters in still air — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Aeolian Elementals occupies vertical shafts, draught galleries, and low still dips. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure animates a channelled draught or a pocket of foul gas and recur with the draught resumes or the foul-gas seep refills, sustaining themselves on air-pressure and channelled draught or foul gas.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members hide as ordinary air and rouse to intrusion, flinging crews or suffocating them. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a bounded-air body, immunity to physical attack, and an enclosure or ventilation failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: flinging, lifting, and silent suffocation. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Aeolian Elementals matters because they make exposed ledges and low dips lethal in invisible ways. It lets survey reports sort air hazards by motion, so a crew knows whether to double-clip a ledge or test-flame a dip without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Gust Aeolians is the family-rank grouping of fast spinning-air elementals of the whirlwind kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a spinning air-column body that flings crews and debris, and divide below into genus groups by shaft geometry and spin force. The fall hazard of every shaft.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Gust Aeolians is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a spinning bounded-air body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a spinning air body with its own draught.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dust-laden spinning column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — dust spiralling against the draught — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Gust Aeolians occupies vertical shafts and chimneys. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure spins a shaft draught and recur with the draught resumes, sustaining themselves on channelled draught.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: spins up to fling crews from ledges. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: spinning air body and air-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: flinging and lifting. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Gust Aeolians matters because flings crews from exposed ledges. It lets survey reports sort spinning-air hazards from settled-air ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Whirler is the genus-rank grouping of contained whirlwind elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a spinning shaft-air column that flings crews and debris, and divide below into species groups by shaft height and spin force. An invisible whirlwind bounded to a shaft.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Whirler is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a spinning shaft-air column body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a spinning air-column body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dust-marked spinning column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — upward-spiralling dust — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Whirler occupies vertical shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure spins a draught and recur with the draught resumes, sustaining themselves on channelled draught.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: flings crews from ledges and ropes. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: spinning column and air-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: flinging, lifting, and sudden stilling. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Whirler matters because flings crews from ledges. It lets survey reports sort spinning-column forms from pooling ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Gust Whirler (Ventus vertens) is an air elemental that spins as a contained whirlwind in a shaft and flings loose debris and crews from ledges. Documented from the vertical shafts and draught-swept galleries of the tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a coherent spinning column of air made visible only by the dust it carries — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Its body is bounded air given spin and will, invisible but for the debris it lifts. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden stilling of the air and a rain of dropped debris and no carcass.
c.tax: Gust Whirler is placed at species rank beneath Whirler (Aeolian order, Air Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a contained spinning air-column, debris flung from ledges, and stilling when dispersed. It is separated from its sibling the Pall Choke-Wind by its fast spinning gust versus the Choke-Wind's slow settling foul air; the two share the genus body-plan but never the foul still air. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: bounded shaft-air held in coherent spin by air-pressure.
c.ap: Gust Whirler stands 2–4 m tall spinning column. Its silhouette is a whirling column visible only through caught dust and grit. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: caught dust and grit spiralling in a column where the air should be still; a rising whistle and tug felt before it is seen; and loose debris lifted and circling at its base. It moves with a swift drifting spin along shafts and draught-lines, fastest in vertical shafts, and shows none; invisible but for its debris. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a clean-blown patch ringed by flung debris and a scoured ledge, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Gust Whirler persists in vertical shafts, draught-swept galleries, and chimney passages where moving air collects and channels. It does not breed; it forms where air-pressure animates a shaft draught into a coherent spinning column, and recurs whenever the draught resumes and the spin re-coheres. It sustains its mass on air-pressure and channelled draught, drawing in moving air drawn into its spin. Range is bounded by the draught-fed shaft: outside that band the body stills into ordinary air where the draught fails. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a shaft where dust spirals upward against the draught with no wind to explain it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Gust Whirler is roused by a crew on a ledge or rope, or a change in the draught and otherwise lies a still draught indistinguishable from ordinary shaft air. It claims a single shaft or chimney and answers intrusion with spinning up to fling crews from ledges and ropes. Toward grey-folk it is a fall hazard on exposed ledges; survey crews report that Wall-Wardens clip double on any ledge where dust spirals the wrong way. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Gust Whirler: a body of bounded shaft-air held in coherent spin by air-pressure; immune to piercing and cutting, which pass through it; and a defining weakness — enclosure and dead-air pockets, which collapse its spin. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-pressure and motion sensing. It tolerates darkness, heat, and cold short of freezing but fails at enclosure and loss of draught. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the spinning column and lifted-debris ring, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Gust Whirler are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it flings crews and debris from ledges and ropes; it lifts and hurls loose stone and gear; it draws in draught to rebuild its spin; and it stills suddenly to drop a hauled victim. Each is a direct expression of bounded air held in coherent spin, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, hold form in dead air, or strike with mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Gust Whirler matters because it flings crews from exposed ledges, and Wall-Wardens now double-clip where dust spirals wrongly. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a fall hazard logged with shafts and exposed ledges, and its presence reshapes routes through the vertical shafts, now rigged with double-clip lines where draughts spin. Dust spiralling upward against the draught is the Wall-Wardens' sign a Whirler has formed.

c.g: Choke Aeolians is the family-rank grouping of settled foul-air elementals of the choke-damp kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a heavy foul-air body that pools low and suffocates silently, and divide below into genus groups by gas chemistry and pooling ground. The silent killer of the low galleries.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Choke Aeolians is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a settled foul-air body. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a foul-air body with its own gas seep.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an invisible low pool that kills flame. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a guttering flame in still air — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Choke Aeolians occupies low still galleries and dips. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure animates a foul-gas pocket and recur with the foul-gas seep refills, sustaining themselves on heavy foul gas.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: pools low, follows and suffocates prey. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: foul-air body and motion-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: silent suffocation and downhill following. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Choke Aeolians matters because kills silently in low ground. It lets survey reports sort settled-air hazards from spinning ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Choke-Wind is the genus-rank grouping of foul settled-air elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a heavy foul-air body that suffocates without a sound, and divide below into species groups by gas type and pooling depth. An invisible pool of un-breathable air.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Choke-Wind is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a foul settled-air body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a heavy foul-air body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an invisible flame-killing pool. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a dying flame in still air — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Choke-Wind occupies low still dips. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure animates a foul-gas pocket and recur with the foul-gas seep refills, sustaining themselves on heavy foul gas.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: pools low and silently suffocates. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: foul-air body and motion-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: suffocation and downhill following. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Choke-Wind matters because silent killer of low galleries. It lets survey reports sort pooling foul-air forms from spinning ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Pall Choke-Wind (Aer mortifer) is an air elemental of foul settled gas that pools in low galleries and suffocates without a sound. Documented from the low, still galleries where heavy foul air collects, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a coherent body of heavy, oxygen-poor foul air pooling in low ground — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the still, deadly sibling of the Whirler: not motion but the absence of breathable air, given just enough will to follow prey down. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a guttering of every flame and a slow return of breathable draught and no carcass.
c.tax: Pall Choke-Wind is placed at species rank beneath Choke-Wind (Aeolian order, Air Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a heavy foul-air body, silent suffocation, and flame-guttering as its only visible sign. It is separated from its sibling the Gust Whirler by its slow settling foul still air versus the Whirler's fast spinning gust; the two share the genus body-plan but never the spinning motion. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: heavy oxygen-poor foul gas held coherent in low ground.
c.ap: Pall Choke-Wind stands indefinite, a pooling body filling a low gallery. Its silhouette is an invisible heavy pool, betrayed only by guttering flame. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: lamps and torches that dim, gutter, and die for no draught; a faint foul or sweetish reek in still low ground; and a sense of breathlessness and heavy limbs before the danger is understood. It moves with a slow gravity-fed flow into the lowest ground, following prey downhill, and shows none; invisible, betrayed by dying flame. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a low gallery where flames will not hold and small bones of suffocated vermin collect, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Pall Choke-Wind persists in low still galleries, sumps' air-pockets, and dead-end dips where heavy foul gas can pool and persist. It does not breed; it forms where air-pressure animates a pocket of heavy foul gas seeping from rock or rot, and recurs whenever the seep refills the low ground with foul air and the body re-coheres. It sustains its mass on air-pressure in heavy foul gas, drawing in foul gas drawn from seep and rot to maintain its body. Range is bounded by the low ground it can pool in: outside that band the body disperses and breaks up where draught reaches it. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a low dip where a test-flame dims without any draught to explain it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Pall Choke-Wind is roused by a breathing crew descending into its low ground and otherwise lies a still invisible pool in a quiet dip. It claims a single low gallery or dip and answers intrusion with flowing over a descending crew to suffocate silently. Toward grey-folk it is the deadliest because invisible; survey crews report that Lanternkin carry a low test-flame into every dip, since a dying flame is the only warning. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
c.traitsText: Stable characters of Pall Choke-Wind: a body of heavy oxygen-poor foul gas held coherent in low ground; immune to all physical attack, being only foul air; and a defining weakness — ventilation and strong draught, which scatter it. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-motion sensing; it follows the breath and warmth of prey. It tolerates darkness, heat, and cold but fails at ventilation and strong draught. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the low-pooling body and flame-guttering sign, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
c.abilities: Abilities of Pall Choke-Wind are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it suffocates silently by displacing breathable air; it follows prey downhill into low ground; it draws foul gas from seep and rot to renew itself; and it pools invisibly, betrayed only by guttering flame. Each is a direct expression of a coherent body of heavy oxygen-poor gas, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, spin, strike with force, or persist in moving air. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
c.bg: In the Throat, Pall Choke-Wind matters because it kills silently in low ground, and a low test-flame carried into every dip is now standard practice. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a suffocation hazard logged with low still galleries, and its presence reshapes routes through the low dips and dead-ends, now entered only behind a test-flame and ventilation. A test-flame that dims in a windless dip is the Lanternkin's one warning of a Choke-Wind.

c.g: Fulgurant Elementals is the order-rank grouping of air elementals whose bodies carry electrical charge within the True Elementals. Its members share a charged air body that hunts by discharge and bleeds away when grounded or wetted, and divide below into family groups by whether the charge strikes from the air or crawls along surfaces. Fulgurant elementals are the storm kin of air, hazards wherever charge can build in dry galleries.
c.tax: Placed at order rank, Fulgurant Elementals is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a charged-air body, not a plain moving-air one. A new family belongs here only if it shows a distinct charge mode with its own build-up source and field signs.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an arc-veined cloud or a clinging glowing mote. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — arc-scorch and ozone, or a fizzing static residue — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Fulgurant Elementals occupies charged storm-shafts and dry static galleries. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure animates a charged-air pocket or a surface-static build-up and recur with the shaft or gallery re-charges, sustaining themselves on atmospheric or surface electrical charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: members hide as charged mist or a faint glow and rouse to grounding metal or contact, striking or shocking. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a charged-air body, immunity to electricity and physical attack, and a grounding or damp failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each family and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: lightning strikes, stunning discharge, and contact-shock. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Fulgurant Elementals matters because they make charged galleries a strike-and-shock hazard wherever metal or damp invites the charge. It lets survey reports sort charge hazards by mode, so a crew knows whether to stow metal or dry-wax their lines without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Arc Fulgurants is the family-rank grouping of lightning-striking charged-air elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a charged cloud body that strikes with branching lightning, and divide below into genus groups by charge build and strike range. The lightning hazard of charged shafts.
c.tax: Placed at family rank, Arc Fulgurants is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a charged cloud body that strikes. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a striking charged-air body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by an arc-veined charged cloud. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — arc-scorch and ozone — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Arc Fulgurants occupies charged storm-shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure charges a storm-air pocket and recur with the shaft re-charges, sustaining themselves on atmospheric charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: strikes the best-grounded target. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: charged cloud and field-sense. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: lightning strikes and stunning discharge. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Arc Fulgurants matters because lightning hazard near metal. It lets survey reports sort striking charge hazards from clinging ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Stormling is the genus-rank grouping of lightning-striking storm-cloud elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a charged cloud body that strikes with branching lightning, and divide below into species groups by charge build and strike range. A living storm-cloud that hurls lightning; the only Air genus that strikes at range.
c.tax: Placed at genus rank, Stormling is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a striking charged-cloud body. A new species belongs here only if it shows a lightning-striking charged body.
c.ap: The grouping is recognised by a dark charged cloud 2 to 3 m across, crawling with blue-white arcs and veined with living discharge. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — arc-scorch, fused stone, an ozone tang, and hair-raising charge that builds before each strike — which survives even when the body disperses.
c.eco: Stormling occupies charged storm-shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where air-pressure charges a storm pocket and recur with the shaft re-charges, sustaining themselves on atmospheric charge.
c.beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: strikes grounded metal and wet crews, builds to a stunning discharge, and crawls arcs along metal to reach distant prey. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
c.traitsText: Shared characters: a charged cloud body, electrical-field sensing, electricity-immunity, and a defining grounding-failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
c.abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: branching lightning and stunning discharge. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Stormling matters because lightning hazard near metal. It lets survey reports sort striking-cloud forms from clinging-mote ones without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.

c.g: Arc Stormling (Fulgur vivum) is a storm-air elemental that crackles with living charge and strikes with branching lightning. Documented from the charged storm-shafts and ozone-tinged galleries of the upper tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a churning charged cloud-body veined with crawling lightning — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is air given electrical charge, hunting by the arc rather than the gust. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a fading crackle, a smell of ozone, and scorched arc-marks on the stone and no carcass.
c.tax: Arc Stormling is placed at species rank beneath Stormling (Fulgurant order, Air Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a charged cloud-body, branching lightning strikes, and arc-scorch as its trail. It is separated from its sibling the Static Sparkmote by its branching lightning strikes versus the Sparkmote's clinging crawling static; the two share the genus body-plan but never the crawling surface static. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: charged storm-air veined with living electrical discharge.
c.ap: Arc Stormling stands 2–3 m churning cloud. Its silhouette is a dark charged cloud-body crawling with blue-white arcs. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: crawling blue-white arcs across its surface; a sharp ozone reek and hair-raising charge before it strikes; and a building crackle that peaks just before discharge. It moves with a drifting float along charged air, fastest toward grounded metal, and shows a flickering blue-white arc-light. Even after dispersal it can be identified from scorched arc-marks, fused stone, and an ozone tang along its path, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
c.eco: Arc Stormling persists in charged storm-shafts, ozone-tinged galleries, and metal-veined passages where charge can build and ground. It does not breed; it forms where air-pressure animates a charged storm-air pocket into a coherent body, and recurs whenever the shaft re-charges and the cloud re-coheres. It sustains its mass on atmospheric electrical charge, drawing in charged air and grounding paths drawn into its body. Range is bounded by the charged-air zone: outside that band the body discharges to ground and disperses where charge fails. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a shaft where hair rises and faint arcs crawl with no storm to explain it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
c.beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Arc Stormling is roused by grounded metal, a wet crew, or close approach and otherwise lies a still dark cloud posing as charged mist.
