c.g: Cap-and-gill-bearing fungi — the dominant macroscopic fungal organisms of both surface and subterranean environments worldwide. Their reproductive fruiting bodies are the visible expression of a usually much larger mycelial network; the cap is the tip of the organism, not the whole of it. In underdark environments, Basidiomycota include the critical bioluminescent Glowgrowth species that provide the ambient light of inhabited caves, the Stone Biofilm that coats every passage floor, and the Myconids.
c.tax: Chitin cell walls; heterotrophic metabolism; basidium-bearing spore structures as reproductive organ; mycelial network as primary organism body; includes all cap-and-gill, shelf, and related fungal forms. Distinguished from Ascomycota by basidium (not ascus) spore-bearing structure.
c.ap: Cap-and-gill morphology in macroscopic forms; mycelial network invisible except where surface-expressed; size range from millimetre-scale surface colonizers to organisms whose mycelial network spans kilometers.

c.g: Bioluminescent fungi — the organisms whose steady light fills the upper tiers with the closest thing to a natural sky the Throat has ever had. Without the Glowgrowth, every passage above the Central Glare would be in total darkness save for manufactured light.
c.tax: Bioluminescent Basidiomycota; energy derived from chemical processes that produce light as a primary ecological output; chitin cell walls confirm Fungi classification despite formerly being listed as Plants.
c.ap: Blue-green to amber; ceiling-growing or web-hanging; colonies cover entire surfaces in overlapping caps or draped web-structures.

c.g: The dominant ceiling-growing bioluminescent fungi; the most commonly encountered organisms in the Throat and the source of most ambient blue-green light.
c.tax: Glowgrowth of cap-and-gill morphology; ceiling-growing; blue-green bioluminescent pigment.
c.ap: 8–18 cm cap diameter; overlapping colonies covering ceilings entirely; slightly translucent caps reveal internal bioluminescent tissue.

c.g: The Deep Glowcap. Standard bioluminescent ceiling-fungus of Tiers I and II. Brightens sharply when disturbed by vibration or a large organism passing beneath — which the Lanternkin suppress in the city as it creates irregular light-flaring competing with their lamp-commerce. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Glowcap with vibration-response brightening; primary ambient light source of inhabited passages.
c.ap: 10–14 cm cap; overlapping colony covering full ceiling; steady blue-green; brightens to near-full-lamp intensity when touched; returns to ambient after 20 minutes.
c.eco: Deep Glowcap feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Deep Glowcap behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.
c.abilities: Deep Glowcap abilities may include spores, hallucination, poison, rapid rot, bioluminescence, corpse digestion, symbiotic feeding, or mycelial communication depending on the entry.
c.bg: In the Throat, Deep Glowcap is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Red Glowcap. Shifts bioluminescent pigment to warm amber-red near hydrothermal water. The Beast-Bonded use the transition zone between L. profunda and L. rubra as a reliable hot-spring proximity indicator. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Glowcap with thermally-responsive pigment shift; amber-red rather than blue-green.
c.ap: 12–16 cm cap; warm amber-red; less dense colonies than L. profunda; transition zone between the two species spans 5–10 m of ceiling.
c.eco: Red Glowcap feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Red Glowcap behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.
c.abilities: Red Glowcap abilities may include spores, hallucination, poison, rapid rot, bioluminescence, corpse digestion, symbiotic feeding, or mycelial communication depending on the entry.
c.bg: In the Throat, Red Glowcap is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The East-Growth Glowcap — a regional colour variant of Lucerna profunda, found exclusively in passages east of the Central Glare where mineral runoff from the copper-bearing strata has shifted its bioluminescent pigment toward a warm green rather than blue-green. Functionally identical to Lucerna profunda; distinguished solely by colour zone. The Lanternkin use the colour boundary as a navigation marker.
c.tax: Regional pigment variant of Lucerna profunda; distinguished by warm-green rather than blue-green bioluminescence caused by copper-ion incorporation in the bioluminescent substrate.
c.ap: As Lucerna profunda in all respects except colour: warm green with a slight yellow edge rather than blue-green; the distinction is clear in direct lamplight, ambiguous in low light.
c.eco: East-Growth Glowcap feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: East-Growth Glowcap behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.
c.abilities: East-Growth Glowcap abilities may include spores, hallucination, poison, rapid rot, bioluminescence, corpse digestion, symbiotic feeding, or mycelial communication depending on the entry.
c.bg: In the Throat, East-Growth Glowcap is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Black Glowcap — a Lucerna profunda colony growing over an adamantine ore seam that has incorporated iron into its bioluminescent chemistry, almost entirely suppressing the light output. The cap is present, the colony is viable, but the glow is reduced to a faint pulse visible only in absolute darkness at close range. The Beast-Bonded use its near-absence of light as cover in passages where bioluminescence normally makes concealment impossible.
c.tax: Lucerna profunda of iron-rich substrate; almost-absent bioluminescence due to iron-ion light-suppression in the pigment chemistry; structurally identical to Lucerna profunda.
c.ap: As Lucerna profunda but cap colour is dark grey to near-black rather than translucent; the glow in total darkness is a faint brownish pulse at 2–3 cm range.
c.eco: Black Glowcap feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Black Glowcap behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.

c.g: Bioluminescent web-forming fungi hanging freely from ceilings. Large Web-Glow colonies in the Murk are the only fixed-position natural lights and serve as navigation landmarks for fog-pilots.
c.tax: Glowgrowth of web rather than cap morphology; free-hanging rather than ceiling-pressed; blue-white bioluminescent pigment.
c.ap: Web structures 50 cm to 30 m diameter; hang 20 cm to 2 m below ceiling; steady blue-white glow.

c.g: The Great Web. Largest single Web-Glow organism; mature colonies span up to 30 meters and are the primary navigation landmarks of the Murk. The Beast-Bonded name each large colony and treat damage to one as vandalism worth significant retaliation. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Web-Glow with colonial growth to 30 m span; primary Murk navigation landmark.
c.ap: Web spans up to 30 m; hangs 1–2 m below ceiling; blue-white steady glow; individual strands as thick as a finger at anchor points.
c.eco: Great Web feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Great Web behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.

c.bg: In the Throat, Great Web is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Drifting Web. Small, mobile web-colonies that become airborne in the Murk's stronger air currents and drift, causing inexperienced fog-pilots to mistake moving lights for fixed landmarks with reliably fatal results. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Web-Glow of small size and airborne mobility; drifts in air currents.
c.ap: Web 30–60 cm diameter; luminous enough to see from 10 m in fog; when drifting, trails a few anchor strands behind it like a sailing jellyfish.
c.eco: Drifting Web feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Drifting Web behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.

c.bg: In the Throat, Drifting Web is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Dense Web — a Phantomyces webglow colony of unusually close anchor-spacing, producing a mesh-like rather than open-lattice web. The close spacing makes it far stronger than a standard Phantomyces immanis colony; it has been observed to slow and tangle a Plafondator immanis that fell through it, which the Heralds consider the most useful thing a Plant has ever done.
c.tax: Web-Glow of dense anchor-spacing architecture; mesh rather than open lattice; higher tensile strength than standard Phantomyces; same bioluminescent output.
c.ap: As Phantomyces immanis in scale but visually denser — the interior of the web is partially opaque rather than transparent; in lamplight appears as a solid blue-white sheet from some angles.
c.eco: Dense Web feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: The ubiquitous biofilm coating every passage floor in the inhabited Gorge — not a single organism but a community dominated by this species. It makes the floors slightly slippery, processes organic fallout, and provides a nutritional base for Stone Mites. Without it, the entire detritivore food web collapses. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Basidiomycota biofilm; mycelial network architecture rather than fruiting bodies; passage-floor substrate specialist; primary base of the passage-floor food web.
c.ap: Flat grey-green to grey-brown film 0.5–2 mm thick covering all passage floors in inhabited areas; slightly slippery underfoot; in lamplight shows a faint bioluminescent shimmer at the leading edge of growing fronts.
c.eco: Stone Biofilm feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: Cup fungi and their relatives — the most species-diverse fungal phylum and the dominant Deepfungus lineage in the Throat. The Ascomycota include the metal-chelating Gold-Fungus and Silver-Thread used by the Forgemasters and Heralds, the Stone-Lichen slowly reclaiming the Works, and the Acid-Lichen of the Quick-pool margins. They represent the Throat's primary chemical decomposition infrastructure.
c.tax: Chitin cell walls; heterotrophic metabolism; ascus-bearing spore structures; includes cup fungi, morels, truffles, and numerous cave-adapted forms. Distinguished from Basidiomycota by ascus rather than basidium spore-bearing structure.
c.ap: Variable: cup-forms, crust-forms, thread-forms. Deep-adapted Ascomycota in the Throat range from dense metallic-fruiting masses (Metal-Fungi) to flat lithophagic crusts (Stone-Lichen) to orange-rubber acid-margin growth (Acid-Lichen).

c.g: Decomposing and metal-concentrating cave fungi of the deep — they break down organic matter, recycle metals, and make the ore bodies of the Throat economically accessible.
c.tax: Ascomycota-grade cave fungi; heterotrophic by saprotrophic or chemotrophic metabolism; no bioluminescence; classified by ecological role as decomposer-recycler.
c.ap: Variable: Metal-Fungi as dense waxy irregular masses with metallic fruiting bodies; Stone-Lichen as flat grey crusts on rock surfaces; Acid-Lichen as orange-yellow rubbery growth.

c.g: Deepfungus that selectively chelate precious metals from substrate, concentrating them into fruiting bodies. A rich infestation indicates a rich ore body beneath.
c.tax: Deepfungus with metal-chelating metabolism producing metallic fruiting bodies; ore-prospecting indicator.
c.ap: Dense irregular masses 10–30 cm; waxy texture; fruiting bodies capped with concentrated metal — gold-yellow for Aurichelatia, silver-grey for Mithralophaga.

c.g: The Gold-Fungus. Concentrates ionic gold into metallic fruiting body deposits. The Forgemasters follow its growth patterns as primary prospecting method.
c.tax: Metal-Fungus concentrating gold ions into smelting-grade fruiting bodies.
c.ap: 15–25 cm irregular mass; waxy pale-yellow body; fruiting bodies 2–4 cm diameter, gold-metallic; glints from across a passage when mature.
c.eco: Gold-Fungus feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.
c.beh: Gold-Fungus behaviour is chemical and colonial: it blooms, releases spores, digests, signals through mycelium, or colonizes wounds and walls. Mobile or intelligent examples still retain fungal growth logic.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.
c.abilities: Gold-Fungus abilities may include spores, hallucination, poison, rapid rot, bioluminescence, corpse digestion, symbiotic feeding, or mycelial communication depending on the entry.
c.bg: In the Throat, Gold-Fungus is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Silver-Thread. Concentrates mithril into thread-like fruiting bodies. The Heralds harvest dried Silver-Thread as supplementary relay conductor; it loses conductivity within a week of harvest, which the Heralds consider a theological inconvenience.
c.tax: Metal-Fungus concentrating mithril into thread-form fruiting bodies with temporary conductivity.
c.ap: 15–20 cm silver-grey mass; fruiting bodies thread-like, 5–10 cm, silver-metallic; resonates faintly in Stonesong-active passages.
c.eco: Silver-Thread Fungus feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: The Heavy Gold-Fungus — an Aurichelatia vascula growing in a particularly gold-saturated substrate where the chelation rate has exceeded the organism's ability to redistribute metal through its body. The excess gold has precipitated into the mycelium itself rather than concentrating into fruiting bodies alone, making the entire organism gold-dense. The Forgemasters harvest the whole organism rather than just the fruiting bodies.
c.tax: Aurichelatia vascula of hypersaturated gold substrate; gold precipitated throughout mycelium, not only in fruiting bodies; entire organism harvestable as ore.
c.ap: 15–25 cm; heavier than typical Aurichelatia; the body is dull gold-flecked throughout rather than just at the fruiting body caps; makes a faint metallic sound when tapped.
c.eco: Heavy Gold-Fungus feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: Slow-growing Deepfungus forming flat grey crusts on rock surfaces, slowly digesting the stone — the primary biological force eroding the carved walls of the Works back toward natural stone.
c.tax: Deepfungus with lithophagic (stone-eating) metabolism; classified as a lichen-analog despite absence of a true algal partner; growth rate 2–3 cm per year.
c.ap: Flat grey-to-green crust 1–5 mm thick; irregular patches 5–50 cm diameter; surface finely pitted; extremely difficult to remove from carved stone.

c.g: The Stone-Eater. Primary Stone-Lichen of Tiers II–III; primary biological force in the Living Stone's slow reclamation of worked passages. Heavy infestation signals structural softening beneath the surface. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone-Lichen with active passage-floor structural weakening; rate 2–3 cm per year.
c.ap: Grey-green flat crust; pitted surface; passage-floor patches become trip hazards as stone beneath softens.
c.eco: Stone-Eater Lichen feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include spore print, smell, bruising colour, gill or pore structure, mycelial cords, toxin profile, and reaction to flame or salt. These traits remain useful after the cap is damaged.

c.bg: In the Throat, Stone-Eater Lichen is food, medicine, toxin, light source, disease vector, or ecological recycler, and records should state which role it plays on a route.

c.g: The Wall-Creep. Upper-Works version — smaller, slower, preferring carved stone. Found growing into lamp-standard bases within two shifts of a new installation. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone-Lichen with preference for carved over natural stone; colonizes infrastructure.
c.ap: Grey flat crust; found primarily at wall-floor junctions; detected by softened stone beneath it before the growth is visible.

c.g: Deepfungus colonizing extreme Quick-water pool margins — the only plant-analog in the Throat completely indifferent to acid splash.
c.tax: Deepfungus with acid-stable metabolism; colonizes acid-pool margins inaccessible to other decomposers; processes mineral-encrusted organic matter.
c.ap: Orange-yellow to orange-brown; rubbery and flexible rather than brittle; 5–20 cm patches on Quick-pool margin rock; slightly foamy surface.

c.g: The Orange-Crust. Primary decomposer of acid-zone organic matter. Completely indifferent to acid exposure and to the loss of sections of itself to that exposure. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Acid-Lichen tolerant of near-pool-strength acid exposure; primary acid-zone decomposer.
c.ap: Orange rubbery crust; 10–20 cm patches; wet-looking even in dry conditions; produces immediate burning sensation on contact with bare skin.
c.eco: Orange-Crust feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: A Deepfungus with a defensive spore-ejection mechanism so violent it has been described as an explosion. The spores are mildly toxic and stick to everything; a disturbed cluster contaminates a 5-meter radius for hours. The Forgemasters occasionally use it deliberately as a passage-denial measure.
c.tax: Ascomycota Deepfungus with explosive spore-ejection defense; spore toxicity mild but persistent; passage-floor junction specialist.
c.ap: Brown-grey irregular masses 15–20 cm diameter in clusters; surface appears dusted with dark powder (pre-ejection spore coat); when disturbed, ejects a visible cloud of dark brown spores that hangs in air for hours.
c.eco: Burst-Fungus feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: A parasitic Deepfungus that infects Cave Stalkers and Wall-Horror juveniles, growing from the host's wounds and eventually fruiting from the living body. The infected host shows no apparent distress until the fruiting stage. The Beast-Bonded vaccinate their bonded animals against it.
c.tax: Parasitic Ascomycota; infection through wound entry; internal growth then external fruiting from living host; host-specific to warm-biology cave fauna.
c.ap: Internal phase invisible; fruiting phase produces grey-white mushroom-cap growths 2–5 cm emerging from wound sites or joint margins of the host; the host continues moving normally until fruiting is well advanced.
c.note: The Beast-Bonded maintain a rival-fungal-preparation that prevents Flesh-Knot infection in bonded animals. The preparation is a clan secret.
c.eco: Flesh-Knot feeds through mycelium, rot, mineral chemistry, symbiosis, or parasitism. Fruiting bodies are seasonal or triggered by moisture, corpses, heat, or disturbance; the visible cap may be less important than the hidden mat.

c.g: Clusters of 4–12 in nutrient-dense substrate zones. Enzyme causes rapid tissue necrosis; grey-folk standard protocol is detection-by-pole-prod before entering any passage with accumulated organic matter. Acid-resistant — Quick-water does not deter it.
c.tax: Violet Fungi of confirmed Gorge passage-ecology; cluster-forming; tissue-necrosis enzyme; acid-resistant integument.
c.ap: 40–60 cm cap; deep violet-purple; 6–10 tentacle-fronds of 60 cm; frond movement visible from 5 m as slight agitation when prey enters range.
c.eco: Violet Fungi lives by photosynthesis, chemosynthesis, parasitism, carrion trapping, or mineral uptake according to its described habitat. Reproduction occurs through seed, spore, runner, cutting, or magical propagation, with growth concentrated around water, light, heat, or nutrient seams.
c.beh: Violet Fungi behaviour is tropism and response: it turns, opens, closes, strikes, releases spores, or withholds fruit according to vibration, scent, heat, or light. Intelligence, if present, should be recorded as a separate trait rather than assumed from movement.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include anchorage, sap, tissue toughness, chemical defence, sensory hairs or fronds, and resistance to pain or blood loss. Damage patterns show tearing fibre, cut stems, resin, or pollen rather than animal bleeding.
c.abilities: Violet Fungi abilities may include grasping tendrils, toxins, thorns, camouflage, regeneration from roots, spore clouds, light production, or mineral hardening as appropriate to the described form.
c.bg: In the Throat, Violet Fungi matters by shaping food webs, choke points, medicine, poison, rope-fibre, shelter, or route hazards.

c.g: Natural animals shaped by ordinary evolutionary pressures — the cave-adapted vertebrate-analogs of the Throat with instinct-driven behavior, no language, and no civilization. Beasts are distinguished from Monstrosities by the credibility of their evolutionary history.
c.tax: Beast phylum of natural animal lineages. The former Chordate and Invertebrate order containers have been removed; their subordinate lineages now appear directly as Beast classes. Proto-Beasts remains as an inferred orphan species rather than a parent container. Reptilia is retained, and Sauria is added as a separate Beast class.
c.ap: Cave Swimmers: pale grey fish-forms, no eyes, lateral-line sensing. Cave Gliders: disc-form rays 50 cm–2 m wingspan. Cave Stalkers: 4-legged wall-climbers, stone-grey, whisker-array sensing. Ooze-form invertebrates: translucent to opaque masses by chemistry type. Arthropoda: 1 mm–8 cm, pale, jointed-limb, antennae-primary. Annelida: grey worms 5–60 cm, floor-sediment dwelling.
c.note: Contains direct Beast classes for Arthropoda, Annelida, Pisces, Amphibia, Avians, Reptilia, Sauria, Mammalia, Cave Stalkers, and Behemoths. The old Proto-Invertebrate and Proto-Chordate orders were removed as redundant hierarchy.

c.g: The theorized common ancestor of all Beasts — the first multicellular animal ancestor prior to the vertebrate/invertebrate divergence. Inferred from the shared biological architecture of all animal lineages: motility, heterotrophic feeding, cell-membrane-based rather than cell-wall-based construction, and sexual or asexual reproduction through germ cells. No form recoverable. Its existence is a necessary inference from the existence of its descendants. Behemoths are treated as beast-line mythic megafauna when they reproduce as animals and carry load-bearing skeletons rather than elemental bodies.
c.tax: Species-level inferred stem ancestor of Beast life. It is retained as an orphan reconstruction rather than as a formal intervening order, so the observed beast classes can sit directly beneath the Beast phylum.
c.ap: Unknown. Inferred to be small, aquatic, and soft-bodied — probably resembling a modern marine flatworm or simple annelid in general organization.
c.eco: Proto-Beast occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.
c.beh: Proto-Beast behaviour is animal-specific: feeding, hiding, mating, nesting, territorial display, migration, or pack activity. Intelligence should be inferred from problem solving and social signals, not from combat difficulty.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include size, gait, limb count, dentition, hide, senses, speed, climbing or swimming ability, and environmental tolerance. Bones and tracks should be sufficient to separate it from a humanoid or monster.
c.abilities: Proto-Beast abilities are natural: bite, claws, venom, constriction, flight, tremorsense, echolocation, camouflage, charge, webbing, or pack tactics only where its anatomy supports them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Proto-Beast matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: Jointed-limb exoskeletal animals — formerly classified separately as 'Vermin kingdom'. Phylogenetic analysis confirmed they are a class of Beasts alongside Mammalia, Reptilia, Pisces, and others. The colloquial term 'vermin' persists informally.
c.tax: Exoskeletal chitin-analog body plan; jointed limbs (4–12); compound or multiple simple eyes; antenna-based chemosensory common; body length 1 mm to 15 cm in the Throat. Distinguished from Annelida by exoskeleton; from Chordata by absence of internal skeleton.
c.ap: Pale to grey; jointed limbs; antennae prominent. Individual specimens largely invisible against stone until they move. In aggregation appear as a moving pale tide across stone.
c.note: Formerly classified as the 'Vermin kingdom'. Reclassified as a class under Beasts when phylogenetic analysis showed their internal skeleton-absent, exoskeletal plan derives from the same animal ancestor as fish, mammals, and worms — they are simply a heavily divergent class.

c.g: Tiny ceiling-and-wall-surface grazers processing Lucerna biofilm. The primary consumers of the bioluminescent mat.
c.tax: Arthropoda of 1–3 mm body size; 8-legged; ceiling-surface habitat; fungal-mat grazing ecology.
c.ap: 1–3 mm body; cream-colored; 8 legs; antennae longer than body; in large numbers appear as a slow-moving texture on the ceiling.

c.g: The Common Stone Mite. Most abundant organism in the Throat by individual count. If you stand in any inhabited passage and look at the ceiling for thirty seconds, you will see them moving. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone Mite of standard size; primary Lucerna-mat consumer; most numerous Throat organism.
c.ap: 2 mm body; cream; 8 legs; moves constantly; the ceiling of any inhabited cave is gently animated by their traffic.
c.eco: Common Stone Mite occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.
c.beh: Common Stone Mite behaviour is animal-specific: feeding, hiding, mating, nesting, territorial display, migration, or pack activity. Intelligence should be inferred from problem solving and social signals, not from combat difficulty.

c.bg: In the Throat, Common Stone Mite matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: The Pale Farmer Mite. Slightly larger; observed apparently moving Lucerna fragments to bare ceiling areas. Whether this is intentional cultivation or accidental spore dispersal has been debated for eighty years without resolution. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone Mite with apparent fragment-relocation behavior; cultivation vs. dispersal debate unresolved.
c.ap: 3 mm body; pale almost-white; slightly slower and more deliberate than the Common.
c.eco: Pale Farmer Mite occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.bg: In the Throat, Pale Farmer Mite matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: The Giant Stone Mite — a Stone Mite at the upper limit of its body plan, distinguishable from Microscopus lapidea not by ecology or habit but simply by scale. Its larger size has made it a preferred subject for the Heralds' study of Stone Mite neuroanatomy, which has been ongoing for sixty years without conclusive results.
c.tax: Stone Mite of maximum observed body size for the family; otherwise identical ecology and habit to Microscopus lapidea; distinguished purely by size.
c.ap: 5–6 mm body; otherwise identical to Microscopus lapidea; visible to the eye without concentrating attention, unlike smaller congeners; moves in the same ceiling-traffic pattern.
c.eco: Giant Stone Mite occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include size, gait, limb count, dentition, hide, senses, speed, climbing or swimming ability, and environmental tolerance. Bones and tracks should be sufficient to separate it from a humanoid or monster.
c.abilities: Giant Stone Mite abilities are natural: bite, claws, venom, constriction, flight, tremorsense, echolocation, camouflage, charge, webbing, or pack tactics only where its anatomy supports them.

c.ap: 4–8 cm body; 6 legs; dark grey to black waxy cuticle; move in short rapid bursts across Quick-pool margins.

c.g: Web-spinning Arthropoda of the Throat's passage systems — they construct funnel or sheet webs across low-traffic passages and harvest whatever walks into them.

c.ap: 2–8 cm body; 8 legs; pale grey to cream; silk-spinnerets prominent at abdomen; webs 20–80 cm spanning low passage sections.

c.g: The Sheet-Web Spider. Constructs dense horizontal sheet-webs across the passage floor that Stone Mites walk into rather than flying into. The webs are invisible in low light and are the most common cause of minor injuries in passages not maintained by the lamplighters. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Spider of sheet-web type; floor-level rather than aerial web placement; Stone Mite specialist predator.
c.ap: 4–6 cm body; pale grey; positions below the sheet web; the web itself barely visible even in good light.

c.g: The Hunting Spider. Does not use webs; hunts by pursuit, using vibration-sensing to track prey. The largest Cave Spider in the Works; the Lanternkin pay a bonus bounty for them as they prey on lamp-standard tenders. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.

c.ap: 6–8 cm body; grey-brown; moves in rapid bursts; the vibration-sensing leg-hair visible as a slight fuzz along the forelegs.

c.g: Large many-legged Arthropoda of the deep passage system — significant predators at the Tier II-III transition, preying on other large Arthropoda, Cave Stalkers, and occasionally juvenile Wall-Walkers.
c.tax: Arthropoda of multi-legged elongated body plan; venomous forcipules (modified front legs) as primary weapon; 30–60+ segments.
c.ap: 20–50 cm body; 40–100 legs; dark brown to black; forcipules prominent; moves in a rapid sinusoidal wave.

c.g: The Stone Centipede. Acid-resistant cuticle allows it to hunt Quick-pool margins where most predators cannot follow. Venom causes significant tissue damage and localized numbness but is non-lethal to grey-folk. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Deep Centipede with acid-resistant cuticle; Quick-pool margin habitat specialist.
c.ap: 30–40 cm; dark brown; 60 legs; cuticle has a slight waxy sheen from acid-resistance.
c.eco: Stone Centipede occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: The Great Passage Centipede. Large enough to prey on juvenile Wall-Walkers. Its venom causes paralysis in Cave Stalker-sized targets. The Wall-Wardens consider it a significant infrastructure nuisance. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Deep Centipede of maximum size; paralytic venom; preys on cave vertebrates.
c.ap: 40–50 cm; jet black; 80+ legs; forcipules large enough to be visible without magnification; produces a faint chemical smell when agitated.
c.note: Apex arthropod predator of the Throat's passage ecosystem. Its paralytic venom is of ongoing research interest to the Heralds, who have not yet managed to harvest it without incident.

c.g: Segmented worms — the detritivore backbone of subterranean ecosystems worldwide. Without Annelida processing the organic fallout of the cave floor, the nutrients locked in dead organisms would remain inaccessible and passage-floor ecology would cease to function. They are among the most successful body plans in the biological record: simple, efficient, and found in every environment that has organic matter to process.
c.tax: Worm-grade body plan: bilaterally symmetric, elongated, segmented; no distinct limbs; predominantly chemosensory; detritivore or scavenger feeding strategy by most species; a small number are parasitic or predatory. Distinguished from Arthropoda by absence of exoskeleton and jointed limbs.
c.ap: 2 mm to 60 cm body length; pale grey to stone-grey or red-brown by species; smooth cuticle in most; moves by peristaltic contraction; looks like a crack in the stone floor until it moves.

c.g: The primary detritivores of the cave-system passage floor — worm-grade annelids that process everything the cave ecosystem discards: dead Vermin, shed cuticle, fungal mat, bone fragments, and organic sediment accumulating in passage floor crevices. Without them, the passage floor ecology would collapse into sterile mineral sediment within years. They are found in every underdark cave system with sufficient organic fallout, which is every underdark cave system with any macrolife at all. In the Throat they are ubiquitous.
c.tax: Annelida of passage-floor detritivore ecology; no functional eyes; chemosensory primary; peristaltic locomotion; passive feeding on accumulated organic matter; distinguished from Marrow Worms by ambient-temperature habitat.
c.ap: 15–50 cm elongated body; pale grey; no visible eyes; smooth or slightly roughened cuticle by species; moves by peristaltic contraction; easily mistaken for a groove in the passage floor until the groove moves.

c.g: The Armored Worm. Incorporates rock fragments into its cuticle, appearing identical to the passage floor. The Beast-Bonded use its activity as baseline passage-floor structural assessment. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone Worm with rock-fragment cuticle incorporation; floor camouflage.
c.ap: 30–40 cm; covered in adhered stone fragments 2–5 mm diameter; when still appears as a line of gravel.

c.g: The Undertaker Worm. Primary scavenger of large carcasses; dissolves Monstrosity chitin with a secreted acid nearly as strong as the Quick. The only scavenger capable of processing armored megafauna remains. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone Worm of scavenger ecology; acid-secreting for hard-material processing.
c.ap: 50–60 cm; pale grey-brown; slightly thicker; when feeding on a carcass it creates an audible acid-fizzing.
c.eco: Undertaker Worm occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: The Bone-Worm. Specializes in processing skeletal remains — the only Annelida with hardened mandibles capable of fragmenting bone. A concentration of them on a passage floor indicates something large died there recently. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Stone Worm with bone-processing mandibles; skeletal-remains specialist; indicates recent large-carcass presence.
c.ap: 40–60 cm; pale yellow-white (bone-mineral incorporation in cuticle); slightly thicker than other Stone Worms; when feeding on bone produces a soft grinding sound.
c.eco: Bone-Worm occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: Heat-adapted annelids of the upper Marrow margin and Roil-vent passages — they occupy the thermal gradient zone where most other organisms cannot survive, processing organic material that falls or washes from cooler passages above onto hot rock surfaces. Their heat-stable biochemistry represents one of the most extreme biological adaptations in the Throat, and they share their ecological role in hot environments worldwide with no close underdark relatives.
c.tax: Annelida with heat-stable internal chemistry replacing standard water-based biology with mineral-solution equivalent; Marrow-temperature habitat; distinguishing from Stone Worms by thermal-stability adaptation and hot-rock rather than floor-sediment ecology.
c.ap: 20–35 cm; dark red-brown heat-adapted cuticle; slightly flattened profile to maximize contact with hot stone surfaces; moves in rapid bursts between thermal lulls; leaves a faint mineral precipitate on the hot surfaces it crosses.

c.g: Parasitic Annelida of the Throat's water margins — attach to Cave Swimmers, Aquatic Arthropoda, and occasionally grey-folk who wade in Still-water pools.
c.tax: Parasitic Annelida with sucker-attachment feeding; blood or fluid feeding on host; temporary attachment rather than permanent parasitism.
c.ap: 5–15 cm elongated body; dark grey to black; both ends with sucker-structures; moves by inchworm extension when not attached.

c.g: The Still-Water Leech. Primary parasite of Still-water lake margins; attaches to wading grey-folk and still-water Cave Swimmers. Painless attachment due to an analgesic secretion; discovered by feel rather than sensation. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Leech of still-water habitat; analgesic attachment secretion; grey-folk parasite.
c.ap: 10–15 cm; black; smooth; entirely painless on the skin; attachment ring slightly visible as a raised dark circle after removal.
c.eco: Still-Water Leech occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: The Hot-Spring Leech. Thermally adapted for hot-spring margins; attaches to Thermabates profundus fish. The only Annelida in the Throat thermally stable to 80°C. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Leech with thermal-stability adaptation; hot-spring habitat; primary host is Thermabates profundus.
c.ap: 5–8 cm; dark red-brown from thermal adaptation; heat-stable cuticle slightly more opaque.
c.eco: Hot-Spring Leech occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: Fish-grade chordates — the aquatic vertebrate-analog lineage. In underdark environments they represent the dominant mobile vertebrate in water bodies, filling ecological roles from filter-feeding and grazing through apex aquatic predation. Cave-adapted branches have reduced or eliminated visual organs over generations in perpetual dark, enhancing lateral-line and electrochemical sensing instead.
c.tax: Aquatic vertebrate-analog; internal cartilaginous or bony skeleton; gill-respiration; no limbs in primary form (dorsal and pectoral fins only); cold-blooded; spawn reproduction in most lineages. Cave-adapted branches show eye-reduction and lateral-line enhancement.
c.ap: Enormous variation by family; cave-adapted forms consistently pale (grey to translucent) and eyeless or reduced-eye. Fin arrangements, scale types, and size ranges vary widely.

c.g: The aquatic vertebrate-analogs of cave lake systems worldwide — fish-form organisms adapted to perpetual dark by replacing or suppressing visual organs in favour of pressure-sensing lateral lines and electrochemical detection. They constitute the primary vertebrate prey base in any underground lake system and the first species a new aquatic predator targets upon establishing territory. The Throat's lake systems support a diverse Cave Swimmer community across all three water types.
c.tax: Pisces-grade vertebrate-analog of aquatic cave habitat; no functional eyes in most species; lateral-line pressure sensing; gill-respiration; spawn or live-birth reproduction. Distinguished from surface fish by eye-reduction and lateral-line enhancement.
c.ap: Elongated streamlined forms 15–200 cm; pale grey to translucent; smooth or lightly scaled; no external eye openings in most species, replaced by pressure-sensing pits; fin arrangement varies by species.

c.g: The Blind Pale Fish. Most common aquatic vertebrate in the Throat lake systems; forms the primary prey base for all larger aquatic predators. Shoals of thousands visible as pale swirling clouds when a lamp enters the lake. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Swimmer of shoaling ecology; primary aquatic prey-base organism.
c.ap: 30–40 cm body; translucent-grey; no eye openings; lateral line prominent as a dark line along the flank.
c.eco: Blind Pale Fish occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: The Shocking Fish. Predatory Cave Swimmer with a weak electric organ in its gill-chambers — not strong enough to stun prey reliably, but apparently sufficient to disorient shoaling fish of other species for brief captures.
c.tax: Cave Swimmer with electric-organ supplement to predatory strategy; lone hunter.
c.ap: 50–70 cm; grey; slightly larger head; the electric organ visible as a dark patch behind gill-covers.
c.eco: Shocking Fish occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: The Tile-Scale. Named for distinctive rectangular scales that overlap like roof tiles — a thermal adaptation reducing heat-exchange surface. Feeds on chemosynthetic mats; tolerates water temperatures that would cook other fish in minutes.
c.tax: Hot-spring Cave Swimmer with tile-scale thermal adaptation; chemosynthetic-mat grazer.
c.ap: 50–80 cm; dark brown-grey; scales rectangular and thick, overlapping like tiles; head blunt; moves in slow deliberate sweeps.
c.eco: Tile-Scale occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: A sessile cave fish-analog that anchors itself to the Still-water pool floor with a modified dorsal fin and filter-feeds mineral-rich water through a wide gape. Its mineral-concentrated tissues are harvested by the Forgemasters as an ore-trace supplement.
c.tax: Cave Swimmer adapted to sessile filter-feeding; modified dorsal fin as anchor; gape-based mineral filtration.
c.ap: 30–50 cm body; very wide gape (head 30% of body length); pale grey; dorsal fin elongated into an anchor hook; no active locomotion in adult form.
c.eco: Stone Filter-Fish occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: Derived aerial Beasts — flattened, gliding rather than actively flying, exploiting Roil thermal updrafts in large void chambers. Derived from Pisces-grade ancestors with enlarged pectoral-fin analogs adapted for gliding.
c.tax: Derived Pisces-grade with enlarged pectoral-fin analog for thermal gliding; buoyancy-chamber for altitude maintenance; no powered flight.
c.ap: Disc-to-kite-shaped body; 30 cm to 2 m wingspan; very flat profile; pale grey to translucent; no visible legs when gliding.

c.g: The Small Cave Ray. Most numerous Cave Glider; feeds on aerial plankton carried upward by the Roil. In updrafts appears as a cloud of pale discs slowly ascending toward the ceiling. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Glider of disc form; primary aerial plankton consumer.
c.ap: Small Cave Ray is an animal-form organism; identify it by limb count, skull, dentition, eyes, hide, gait, tracks, scat, and feeding damage rather than by danger level alone.

c.g: The Diving Ray. Slightly larger, predatory; dives onto surface prey by collapsing its glide-profile and dropping fast, spreading back just above target. The Beast-Bonded lose small animals to it constantly. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave Glider with dive-predation strategy; wing-fold for speed-dive.
c.ap: 80 cm to 1.2 m wingspan; slightly thicker profile; dives with a distinctive wing-fold producing an audible hiss before impact.

c.g: Dual-life vertebrate-analogs — aquatic larvae, terrestrial adults. Rare in the Gorge but confirmed in the water-margin zones of the Fogreach; the deep environment has selected for adults that remain semi-aquatic rather than committing to full terrestrial life.
c.tax: Internal-skeleton vertebrate-analog; aquatic larvae, terrestrial adults; permeable skin.
c.ap: Amphibia is an animal-form organism; identify it by limb count, skull, dentition, eyes, hide, gait, tracks, scat, and feeding damage rather than by danger level alone.

c.g: The Pale Cave Salamander. Semi-aquatic of the Fogreach lake margins; adults retain external gill-plumes rather than completing full terrestrial metamorphosis. Feeds on Cave Leech larvae and Ghost Shrimp analogs. The Beast-Bonded keep them as early-warning animals for Still-water contamination — they react visibly to chemical changes grey-folk cannot detect.
c.tax: Amphibia of cave-adaptation; no functional eyes; chemosensory primary; semi-aquatic adult form retaining larval gill-plumes.
c.ap: 15–25 cm body; pale white-translucent; 4 short legs; external gill-plumes retained in adult form, bright red-orange in contrast to pale body.
c.eco: Pale Cave Salamander occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: Avians are the feathered or feather-analog beast class. The Throat preserves few stable avian lineages, but the class is retained for winged, beaked, egg-bearing animal forms when they are found or reconstructed.
c.tax: Class of bird-bodied beasts: beaked jaws, forelimbs modified toward flight or display, egg-bearing reproduction, and feather or feather-analog integument. Added as a formal Beast class so avian material is not folded into Saurians or Mammalia.
c.ap: Expected avian silhouettes include compact bodies, beaks, clawed hind feet, folded forelimb wings or reduced wing remnants, and featherlike insulation often abraded by cave life.
c.eco: Avian ecology in the Throat is usually roosting, scavenging, insect-hunting, or shaft-gliding, though no settled subordinate lineage is yet recorded here.
c.beh: Avian beasts are generally alert, territorial around roosts, and responsive to echoes and air movement.
c.traitsText: Likely traits include light bones, strong balance, sharp hearing, roost fidelity, and egg-laying reproduction.
c.abilities: No class-wide supernatural ability is assigned. Flight, gliding, mimicry, and echolocation belong to subordinate taxa when recorded.
c.bg: This class is included to keep future bird-bodied discoveries from being misfiled under Saurians or cave bats.

c.g: Reptilia are retained as the broad reptile-bodied Beast class, separate from the newly added Sauria class. This preserves the original reptile placement rather than replacing it.
c.tax: Class of reptile-bodied beast lineages in the Throat. Reptilia is retained as its own Beast class; Sauria is added as a separate class rather than as a replacement name.
c.ap: Variable by family. Cave-adapted forms consistently paler than surface relatives; eyes enlarged for low-light conditions; movement typically slow and deliberate in cool zones, more active near Roil-vent heat.

c.g: Four-limbed scaled reptiles of cave passage walls — insectivores and small-arthropod hunters that exploit the warm microhabitats near Roil-vents and around lamp-standards in inhabited passages. They are common in underdark cave systems worldwide wherever arthropod prey is available, which is everywhere.
c.tax: Reptilia of four-limbed wall-climbing body plan; insectivore-to-small-predator ecology; cave-adapted with reduced pigmentation and enlarged eyes. Distinguished from Cave Stalkers by ectothermic metabolism and scaled integument.
c.ap: 10–35 cm body; pale grey to translucent-beige; scaled; four limbs with adhesive-padded feet for wall climbing; eyes proportionally very large; moves in rapid short bursts and then freezes completely.

c.g: The dominant cave lizard of the Works and Fogreach — common throughout underdark cave systems; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional. Its adhesive foot-pads allow it to occupy the same ceiling and wall space as Stone Mites, on which it primarily feeds. The Lanternkin find them useful as informal Mite-management and tolerate them around lamp-standards.
c.tax: Cave Lizard of gecko-form body plan; adhesive toe-pads allowing wall and ceiling locomotion; Stone Mite primary prey; no eyelids — the eye is cleaned by a long translucent tongue.
c.ap: 15–22 cm body; pale translucent-beige skin through which internal structure is faintly visible; large lidless eyes; adhesive toe-pads prominent; moves in rapid bursts between complete stillness; produces a faint clicking sound when active.
c.eco: Cave Gecko occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.
c.beh: Cave Gecko behaviour is animal-specific: feeding, hiding, mating, nesting, territorial display, migration, or pack activity. Intelligence should be inferred from problem solving and social signals, not from combat difficulty.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include size, gait, limb count, dentition, hide, senses, speed, climbing or swimming ability, and environmental tolerance. Bones and tracks should be sufficient to separate it from a humanoid or monster.
c.abilities: Cave Gecko abilities are natural: bite, claws, venom, constriction, flight, tremorsense, echolocation, camouflage, charge, webbing, or pack tactics only where its anatomy supports them.
c.bg: In the Throat, Cave Gecko matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: A heat-adapted skink-form reptile of the Roil-vent passage margins — the only common reptile in the Gorge that actively seeks high-temperature environments rather than tolerating them. Feeds on Thermivermis Heat Worms and other Marrow-margin invertebrates that only a heat-tolerant predator can access. Common wherever geological heat meets accessible passage-floor.
c.tax: Cave Lizard of skink-form body plan with heat-adapted ectothermy; Roil-vent habitat specialist; heat-tolerance allowing active hunting at temperatures prohibitive to most other Beasts.
c.ap: 20–30 cm body; dark red-brown scales (heat-adapted coloration absorbing rather than reflecting); stocky build; short limbs for passage-floor rather than wall locomotion; skin slightly warm to the touch even away from a vent.

c.g: Limbless scaled reptiles of cave passage systems — the apex reptilian predators of the enclosed passage ecosystem, feeding on Cave Lizards, Wall-Creepers, and other small cave Beasts. Cave snakes are ambush predators that use the passage geometry to their advantage: a snake blocking a narrow passage has effectively closed it to anything smaller than itself. Common in underdark systems worldwide.
c.tax: Reptilia of limbless elongated body plan; constriction or venom as primary prey-subduing method; jaw-expansion capacity allowing prey significantly larger than head diameter; heat-sensing pit organs in most species replace or supplement visual sensing.
c.ap: 40 cm to 1.5 m body; pale grey to stone-grey; smooth scaled integument; no limbs; moves by lateral undulation; heat-sensing pits visible as small depressions on either side of the jaw; coils when inactive and resembles a mineral formation.

c.g: The standard cave constrictor — widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional. Feeds primarily on Cave Lizards and Wall-Creepers, occasionally on very large Arthropoda. Sits motionless in passage constrictions for hours between hunts; the grey-folk step over them routinely without incident unless the snake has recently eaten and is moving to a digestion location.
c.tax: Cave Snake of constrictor ecology; ambush strategy in passage constrictions; heat-sensing primary; feeds on lizards and small Beasts; non-venomous; constriction sufficient to subdue Cave Gecko-grade prey.
c.ap: 60–90 cm; pale grey; smooth; coils into a shape easily mistaken for a coiled rope or mineral formation; when active moves in smooth lateral undulation; the heat-sensing pits visible as small notches beside the upper jaw.
c.eco: Common Cave Snake occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: A venomous cave snake of the deeper passage system — larger and more aggressive than the Common Cave Snake, with venom sufficient to subdue prey up to wall-creeper size and to cause significant tissue damage in grey-folk-sized targets. The venom is of ongoing research interest to the Heralds as a potential anti-coagulant treatment; the research programme has experienced several setbacks that the Heralds consider instructive.
c.tax: Cave Snake of venom-predation ecology; haemotoxic venom; larger prey range than standard cave constrictors; heat-sensing pits more developed, effectively functional eyes in total dark.
c.ap: 90–150 cm; darker grey with a faint banded pattern visible under direct lamplight; triangular head (venom-gland enlargement); sits coiled with head elevated when hunting; the banded pattern provides near-perfect camouflage against banded mineral strata.
c.eco: Blind Cave Viper occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: Sauria is a separate Beast class reserved for saurian-bodied animal lineages, added alongside rather than in place of Reptilia.
c.tax: Class of saurian beast lineages. It is deliberately separate from Reptilia in this catalogue so saurian material can be filed without erasing the broader reptile class.
c.ap: Saurian silhouettes are expected to emphasize long-tailed, scale-skinned, clawed, reptilelike bodies, with diagnostic details assigned only to subordinate taxa when recorded.
c.eco: Sauria has no settled subordinate ecology yet in this revision; future entries should record nesting, basking, hunting range, and cave-temperature dependence.
c.beh: Behavior should be described by subordinate taxa rather than inferred broadly for all saurian beasts.
c.traitsText: Likely traits include scaled integument, clawed feet, egg-laying or hard-shelled reproduction, and strong dependence on environmental heat, but these remain subordinate-specific unless stated.
c.abilities: No class-wide supernatural ability is assigned to Sauria.
c.bg: This class was added to correct the previous revision, which renamed Reptilia instead of preserving Reptilia and adding Sauria separately.

c.g: Warm-blooded vertebrate-analogs with live-birth and milk production — the mammalian body plan is less common in underdark environments than surface ecologies but not absent. Cave bats are the most successful mammalian underdark organisms; cave rats and similar small mammals follow any food source that persists, including the grey-folk food supply; and occasional larger mammals descend into the upper tiers seasonally or following prey.
c.tax: Warm-blooded vertebrate-analog; live-birth (almost universally); maternal milk production; hair or fur as primary integument; endothermic thermoregulation. Distinguished from all other Beasts by endothermy and milk production.
c.ap: Variable by family. Cave bats: small, furred, winged, echolocating; cave rats and small mammals: small, furred, whisker-primary sensing; larger mammals rare and typically surface-connected.

c.g: Small mammals of the rodent lineage — where the grey-folk have established food storage, the rats and mice have arrived. They followed the first descent, ate the first provisions, and have been a feature of the Works infrastructure ever since. They are simultaneously a food source for cave snakes and predatory lizards, a reservoir for several minor parasitic infections of concern to the Stillwardens, and an ecological indicator: Rat density tracks food-storage density with perfect reliability.
c.tax: Small Mammalia of rodent body plan (whisker-primary chemosensory, gnawing dentition, high reproductive rate); opportunistic feeding; cave-adapted forms with enlarged eyes and reduced pigmentation; distinguished from surface rats by pale coloration and slightly enlarged eyes.
c.ap: 8–25 cm body; pale grey to white (cave-adapted forms); large eyes; prominent whiskers; distinctive gnawing-incisor teeth; moves in rapid short bursts; colonial; found exclusively in association with grey-folk food storage or fungal-mat accumulations.

c.g: The standard cave rat — present in every inhabited underdark settlement worldwide within two years of establishment, having arrived by what route no one has satisfactorily explained. In the Gorge it is a permanent and unmanaged feature of the Works food-storage infrastructure. The Stillwardens test stored water for rat contamination as standard procedure. The Haulwrights consider it a structural indicator: where rats gnaw, the material is soft enough to be concerned about.
c.tax: Cave rodent of Tier I food-storage habitat; primary food sources are grain stores and fungal mat; secondary prey of cave snakes; parasite reservoir for several Stillwarden-monitored infections; widespread in underdark cave systems globally.
c.ap: 20–25 cm body plus equal-length tail; pale grey to white; large dark eyes; prominent whiskers; moves in rapid runs between cover; colonial groups of 20–200; produces a characteristic squeaking audible through thin walls.
c.eco: Cave Rat occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.bg: In the Throat, Cave Rat matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: A smaller cave rodent that has penetrated further into the Throat than the Cave Rat, feeding on fungal mat and organic debris in the Fogreach. The Beast-Bonded use it as prey-scent bait for certain bond-animal training exercises, which means they maintain a small captive population whose living conditions the Pall-Brokers consider a matter of philosophical interest.
c.tax: Cave rodent of mid-tier passage ecology; fungal mat and organic-debris feeding; smaller than Cave Rat enabling access to smaller passage systems; ecologically distinct from Cave Rat by habitat depth and food source.
c.ap: 8–12 cm body plus tail; very pale grey, almost white; eyes disproportionately large even for a cave rodent; whiskers notably long; moves in slower more cautious patterns than Cave Rat; solitary or pairs rather than colonial.

c.bg: In the Throat, Deep Mouse matters as food, threat, omen, livestock, scavenger, route-blocker, or keystone species according to its ecological niche.

c.g: Echolocating flying mammals — the most successful mammalian family in underdark environments worldwide. Bats colonize any cave system accessible from the surface that provides insect or invertebrate prey, which is all of them. In the Gorge they occupy Tier I and the upper Fogreach in large colonies, feeding on aerial arthropods and Stone Mites, and descending from surface roosts at night through the daylight shaft. Enormously important for nutrient cycling: bat guano is a primary nitrogen input to cave soils.
c.tax: Mammalia of powered-flight body plan; echolocation as primary navigation and prey-detection; small body (3–40 g); colonial roosting; insectivore or frugivore ecology. Distinguished from Cave Gliders by powered (not thermal-riding) flight and warm-blooded endothermy.
c.ap: 3 cm to 20 cm body; furred; leathery wings extending from elongated finger bones; large ears for echolocation; tiny eyes (functional but secondary); upside-down roosting posture; produces constant high-frequency chirps inaudible to most grey-folk.

c.g: The standard insectivorous cave bat — common throughout underdark cave systems globally where surface access allows colony establishment. Thousands roost in the Tier I ceiling vaults, descending from the surface each dusk and returning at dawn through the daylight shaft. The guano accumulations beneath roosting colonies are a significant nutrient input for passage-floor ecosystems. The Lanternkin have a complicated relationship with them involving both the guano-trade and the lamp-contamination problem.
c.tax: Cave Bat of insectivore ecology; Tier I colony roosting; daylight-shaft surface access; echolocation at 40–80 kHz; wingspan 25–35 cm.
c.ap: 8–10 cm body; dark brown fur; 28–32 cm wingspan; large rounded ears; colonial roosts of 500–5000 individuals visible as a rustling brown mass on the ceiling of vault chambers; in flight, produces an audible leathery rustling and high-pitched chirping.
c.eco: Common Cave Bat occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.

c.g: A larger predatory bat that hunts small Cave Lizards and large arthropods in addition to flying insects — the apex mammalian aerial predator of the Gorge's upper tiers. Less colonial than the Common Cave Bat, roosting in groups of 20–50 rather than thousands. The Beast-Bonded have attempted to work with them as passage-clearing tools with mixed success; they are considerably more independent-minded than the birds the Beast-Bonded usually partner.
c.tax: Cave Bat of mixed insectivore-and-small-vertebrate prey ecology; semi-colonial roosting in small groups; larger body plan enabling vertebrate prey; echolocation frequency lower than Common Cave Bat, tuned for larger targets.
c.ap: 18–20 cm body; dark grey fur; 45–55 cm wingspan; elongated lower jaw enabling vertebrate prey handling; solo or small-group flight; produces a notably deeper echolocation chirp audible to grey-folk at close range.
c.eco: Great Cave Bat occupies a prey, predator, scavenger, mount, pest, or megafaunal niche. Diet, denning, breeding season, young, predators, and spoor should be recorded from tracks and remains.
