c.g: Deep gnomes, or Svirfneblin, are the subterranean gnomish subspecies. Scout, lapidary, and ward-keeper are roles within the same people, not separate species.
c.tax: Svirfneblin subspecies defined by deep-environment adaptation: stone-grey skin, acute vibration sense, minor earth-magic, and strong concealment in rock passages.
c.ap: Deep Gnome has small gnomish body with stone-grey skin, large dark-adapted eyes, compact hands, and clothing that breaks the outline against rock. Identification should use body plan, proportions, gait, eyes, and ears rather than clothing or profession.
c.eco: Deep Gnome populations favour deep tunnels, gem seams, fungal margins, and hidden villages where silence and camouflage keep the population alive. Diet is omnivorous and culturally mediated rather than monstrous: grains, roots, fungi, fruit, game, preserved foods, and trade goods depending on settlement.
c.beh: Deep Gnome behaviour is wary, private, dry-humoured, and intensely observant; strangers are watched long before being challenged. These are population tendencies, not class archetypes; scouts, mages, hunters, and artisans remain the same subspecies.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits are small size, darkvision, stone camouflage, gemcraft, careful hearing, and resistance to underground hazards. Skeletally it remains a humanoid: upright pelvis, tool-using hands, shortened muzzle, and social braincase.
c.abilities: Innate abilities include minor illusion, stone hiding, quiet movement, and deep-route memory. Learned spell lists, military drills, guild posts, and professions are recorded as culture, not species.
c.bg: Within the Throat index, Deep Gnome serves as a bridge between surface gnome taxonomy and the Grey-Folk/svirfneblin context of the Throat.
c.note: Svirfneblin scouting role removed; deep gnomes are ranked with surface gnomes.

c.g: Forest gnomes are a small woodland-adapted gnomish subspecies with quiet movement, animal communication traditions, and stronger concealment behaviour than rock gnomes.
c.tax: Surface Gnome subspecies defined by woodland habitat, small-animal rapport, and camouflage-oriented magic.
c.ap: Forest Gnome has small gnomish body with quick hands, bright eyes, expressive face, and clothing textured with bark, moss, seed, and leaf motifs. Identification should use body plan, proportions, gait, eyes, and ears rather than clothing or profession.
c.eco: Forest Gnome populations favour woodland burrows, root cellars, and animal-neighbour settlements where small scale and concealment are advantages. Diet is omnivorous and culturally mediated rather than monstrous: grains, roots, fungi, fruit, game, preserved foods, and trade goods depending on settlement.
c.beh: Forest Gnome behaviour is playful, secretive, talkative with animals, and reluctant to meet large folk openly. These are population tendencies, not class archetypes; scouts, mages, hunters, and artisans remain the same subspecies.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits are small size, nimble fingers, keen hearing, animal empathy, and illusion-friendly temperament. Skeletally it remains a humanoid: upright pelvis, tool-using hands, shortened muzzle, and social braincase.
c.abilities: Innate abilities include minor illusion, small-beast communication, hiding, and quick repair of tiny devices. Learned spell lists, military drills, guild posts, and professions are recorded as culture, not species.
c.bg: Within the Throat index, Forest Gnome serves as a context entry distinguishing surface forest gnomes from deep gnomes and rock gnomes.

c.g: Rock gnomes are the engineering and burrow-settlement surface subspecies, with strong craft curiosity and tool culture.
c.tax: Surface Gnome subspecies defined by mechanical aptitude, mineral-burrow habitat, and tinkering cognition.
c.ap: Rock Gnome has small sturdy gnomish body with clever hands, tool scars, bright eyes behind lenses or goggles, and clothing full of pockets. Identification should use body plan, proportions, gait, eyes, and ears rather than clothing or profession.
c.eco: Rock Gnome populations favour workshops, burrows, and trade towns where metal, glass, clockwork, and alchemy support the household. Diet is omnivorous and culturally mediated rather than monstrous: grains, roots, fungi, fruit, game, preserved foods, and trade goods depending on settlement.
c.beh: Rock Gnome behaviour is inventive, sociable, argumentative, and delighted by mechanisms that are almost too complicated. These are population tendencies, not class archetypes; scouts, mages, hunters, and artisans remain the same subspecies.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits are small size, dexterous hands, tool memory, alchemical caution, and resistance to some magical confusion. Skeletally it remains a humanoid: upright pelvis, tool-using hands, shortened muzzle, and social braincase.
c.abilities: Innate abilities include minor tinkering devices, illusion tricks, quick diagnostics, and persistent craft focus. Learned spell lists, military drills, guild posts, and professions are recorded as culture, not species.
c.bg: Within the Throat index, Rock Gnome serves as a context entry for surface craft-gnomes, useful when separating biological gnomes from Grey-Folk guild roles.

c.g: Surface dwarves of the deep mountains — slightly more distant relatives, but the most geographically proximate to the conditions that produced the Duergar-Kin split.
c.tax: Mountain-dwelling dwarf culture; stone-sense and underground navigation; intermediate between surface and deep adaptations.
c.ap: 1.5–1.7 m; grey-brown skin; dark hair; heavily built; mountain-environment adapted.
c.eco: Mountain Dwarves ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Mountain Dwarves behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.bg: In the Throat index, Mountain Dwarves matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.

c.g: Surface dwarves of the northern traditions — the nearest non-duergar relatives of the Grey-Folk. Not present in the Throat.
c.tax: Shield-bearing martial dwarf culture; standard surface-dwarf physiology; nearest non-deep relative to the Grey-Folk.
c.ap: Shield Dwarves is an upright tool-using humanoid with two arms, two legs, forward-facing eyes, speech anatomy, and hands capable of weapons, craft, or ritual gesture. Field identification should record height, build, ears, dentition, skin, hair, posture, and habitual equipment separately from rank or profession.

c.g: Surface dwarves of the gentler hills and plains — the nearest non-martial surface relatives of the Grey-Folk.
c.tax: Hill-dwelling dwarf culture; standard surface-dwarf physiology; secondary nearest non-deep relative.
c.ap: 1.4–1.5 m; warm brown skin; brown hair; slightly lighter build than Shield Dwarves; surface-adapted.
c.eco: Hill Dwarves ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.

c.g: The surface duergar — the direct cousins of the Grey-Folk, maintaining their ancient duergar culture and the psychic powers the Grey-Folk have largely lost. Not present in the Throat in any confirmed capacity.
c.tax: Duergar-kin with retained psionic capacity (enlarge/invisibility at will); grey skin; distinctive beard and dress culture of the deep dwarven tradition.
c.ap: 1.4–1.6 m; grey skin; white hair; notable psychic aura; not present in the Throat.
c.eco: True Duergar ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: True Duergar behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of True Duergar include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of True Duergar are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, True Duergar matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.
c.note: Duergar-kin container removed; duergar now rank alongside surface dwarves.

c.g: The duergar-kin people of the Throat — not duergar by strict surface classification, but close enough in ancestry that surface dwarves would recognize the kinship. They have been in the dark long enough that duergar feels like a word from someone else's history.
c.tax: Share all Humanoid characteristics; distinguished from surface humanoids by: tapetum lucidum in eyes for low-light vision; slightly broadened rib cage for reduced-oxygen environments; skin melanin reduced to grey-mauve; circadian rhythm entrained to the Roil thermal cycle rather than sunlight.
c.ap: Grey-Folk are deep dwarven humanoids with compact torsos, strong hands, low-light pupils, ash-grey to stone-brown skin, mineral-dusted hair, and dense bones adapted to climbing, hauling, and living under pressure. Guild, clan, and office marks appear as clothing, tattoos, tools, and dialect, not as separate species characters.
c.note: Former child entries (Haulwright, Bellowsmith, Stillwarden, Lanternkin, Forgemaster, Stonesong Herald, Wall-Warden, Beast-Bonded, Pall-Broker, Mist-Touched) are guilds, clans, offices, or professions. They are retained as cultural roles in this note rather than as separate species.
c.beh: Grey-Folk behaviour is cautious, procedural, and memory-heavy because a single bad survey can kill a district. They value maps, oaths, repair work, and controlled access; guild identity shapes behaviour culturally but does not create new taxa.
c.eco: Grey-Folk ecology is settlement ecology: fungus farms, cisterns, preserved meat, trade minerals, managed vents, and defended stair-routes. They reproduce as dwarven humanoids, raise children inside clan households and guild apprenticeships, and survive by turning unsafe stone into maintainable infrastructure.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include dwarven build, darkvision, stone-sense, strong grip, high endurance, resistance to many mineral toxins, and tool competence from childhood exposure. Their skeleton is broad-shouldered and dense, with hands built for tools rather than claws.
c.abilities: Grey-Folk abilities are natural darkvision, stonework intuition, endurance under poor air, disciplined route memory, and trained engineering. Any magic belongs to a person, school, or office unless a bloodline trait is explicitly identified.
c.bg: They are the principal interpreting species of the Throat: most routes, names, warnings, and taxonomic errors in the journal come through Grey-Folk observation. Treating guilds as species would corrupt the whole index.

c.g: The common Derro — present in the deepest reaches of the Untamed in groups whose coherence oscillates between military precision and complete chaos. They do not engage with grey-folk diplomatically, attack in coordinated waves, then sometimes stop mid-engagement and leave, apparently following an instruction no one else can hear.
c.tax: Derro of commoner-caste classification: no formal role designation, collective-psychosis baseline, operates in medium groups. The baseline Derro as encountered by most delvers.
c.ap: 1.1–1.2 m; chalk-white; mismatched leather-and-metal armour; short sword or crossbow; the crossbow bolts frequently coated in something unidentified.
c.eco: Common Derro ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Common Derro behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Common Derro include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of Common Derro are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, Common Derro matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.
c.note: Derro Savant role collapsed into the biological Derro species.

c.g: The Gorge Goblin. Distinguishable from surface goblins by paler skin, larger eyes, and a specific relationship with the passage-ecosystem: they maintain cleaning arrangements with the Beast-Bonded's bond-animals, removing ectoparasites in exchange for not being eaten. Whether the animals are aware of this arrangement is unclear. Widespread in underdark cave systems globally; the Throat population is well-documented but not exceptional.
c.tax: Cave-adapted Goblin with deep-environment morphology. Distinguished from surface forms by pale grey skin, enlarged eyes, and established ecological relationship with megafauna in the Fogreach.
c.ap: 100–110 cm; pale cave-grey; very large gold-yellow eyes; wiry; typically in groups of 4–10; dressed in patched equipment assembled from multiple sources.
c.eco: Gorge Goblin ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Gorge Goblin behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Gorge Goblin include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of Gorge Goblin are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, Gorge Goblin matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.

c.g: The standard surface goblin — the common relative the Gorge Goblin branched from. Found in forests, hills, and surface ruin-complexes worldwide, living in larger groups than their cave-adapted cousins and with more social complexity. Surface goblins are the baseline for all goblin-kind comparison; the Gorge Goblin represents what a surface goblin becomes after generations without surface access.
c.tax: Goblin of surface-habitat adaptation; green skin (standard surface pigmentation); compound social structure of 20–100 individuals; scavenger-opportunist ecology; not cave-adapted in vision or coloration.
c.ap: 90–110 cm; green skin (varies by region from bright to olive); yellow-gold eyes; prominent teeth; wiry build; functional scavenged equipment; lives in groups large enough to be a problem.
c.eco: Surface Goblin ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Surface Goblin behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.g: The Deep Hobgoblin Legionary. Operates in the Fogreach in war-bands of 8–20, holding territory against other groups with a systematic thoroughness that the grey-folk find simultaneously admirable and extremely inconvenient when the territory they hold is passage infrastructure.
c.tax: Hobgoblin of confirmed deep-adaptation and military-unit operational context. Distinguished from surface variants by pale grey-brown coloration and adaptation to total-darkness combat.
c.ap: 1.8 m; pale grey-brown; red eyes; heavy armour marked with unit insignia; great weapon and shield; moves in tight formation.
c.eco: Deep Hobgoblin Legionary ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.

c.g: The surface hobgoblin — the standard form from which the deep Hobgoblin Legionary descended. Surface hobgoblins are the baseline of goblinoid military organisation: formally structured, equipment-maintained, lawfully disciplined. What the Deep Hobgoblin does with dark-adaptation, the surface form does with surface terrain and sunlight.
c.tax: Hobgoblin of surface-habitat adaptation; surface-standard coloration (red-brown skin rather than deep-pale); same military social structure as deep variants; not cave-adapted.
c.ap: 1.7–1.9 m; red-brown skin; red eyes; standard military equipment maintained to the hobgoblin standard; moves in formation.
c.eco: Surface Hobgoblin ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Surface Hobgoblin behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.g: The Cave Bugbear. Its ability to remain completely motionless in a dark passage for hours before striking is the characteristic grey-folk encounter with this species: you do not notice it until it is done not noticing you.
c.tax: Bugbear of cave-adapted deep coloration and confirmed ambush-predation strategy in enclosed-passage context.
c.ap: 2–2.2 m; dark grey-brown with mottled integument; yellow eyes visible in lamplight; carries a heavy blunt weapon and does not make a sound before using it.
c.eco: Cave Bugbear ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Cave Bugbear behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.g: The surface bugbear — the standard form from which the Cave Bugbear descended. Larger than goblins and hobgoblins, ambush-specialized, and covered in a shaggy fur that breaks up their silhouette in surface forest environments the same way the Cave Bugbear's dark grey coloration works in passage environments.
c.tax: Bugbear of surface-habitat adaptation; shaggy fur as camouflage; same ambush-specialization ecology as cave variant; not cave-adapted in coloration.
c.ap: 1.9–2.2 m; dark brown shaggy fur; yellow eyes; moves silently for its size in surface woodland; carries a morningstar or similar heavy weapon.
c.eco: Surface Bugbear ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Surface Bugbear behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.g: The Gorge Orc. Cave-adapted over several generations, paler than surface kin, adapted to the dark, and considerably more pragmatic than surface orcs regarding what constitutes a fight worth having. The grey-folk Haulwrights have a formal trade agreement with the dominant clan in Tier I — ore in exchange for passage. Nobody mentions the formal trade agreement in polite company.
c.tax: Orcish phylum with cave-environment adaptation: reduced pigmentation, enlarged pupils, informal Stonesong familiarity. Distinguished from surface Orcs by pale grey-green skin and the existence of a trade agreement.
c.ap: 1.8–2.0 m; pale grey-green; large dark-adapted pupils; muscular; carries a combination of orcish tribal weapons and grey-folk manufactured tools.
c.eco: Gorge Orc ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.

c.g: The Surface Orc, included as nearest non-deep relative of the Gorge Orc. Not present in the Throat except as seasonal raiding parties in Tier I.

c.g: The standard Gnoll raider encountered in upper Gorge tiers — part of surface raiding bands that descend seasonally when surface prey is scarce. Their cackling war-cry carries through stone extremely well, which the grey-folk consider their only useful warning system.
c.tax: Gnoll of raiding-ecology with confirmed Gorge-penetration into Tier I. Distinguished from settled gnoll communities by full military kit and absence of pups.
c.ap: 2.0–2.1 m; spotted grey-brown fur; hyena head; armour assembled from multiple sources; glaive or halberd; laughs during combat.

c.g: The Gorge Troglodyte is the adult reptilian humanoid species occupying unmapped Tier III passages. Warrior and shaman are social roles within this species, not subspecies.
c.tax: Troglodyte-family Reptilian Humanoid with musk-gland defense, primitive tool culture, territorial cave ecology, and rough grey-black scales.
c.ap: Troglodytes are bipedal reptilian humanoids with hunched shoulders, long arms, clawed hands, blunt snouts, pebbled hide, and a heavy balancing tail. Their most reliable field mark is the oily musk gland complex along the back and neck, not weapons or shamanic dress.
c.eco: Troglodytes occupy damp caves, refuse margins, and warm crevices where smell and darkness favour them. They lay guarded clutches, eat meat, carrion, fish, fungus, eggs, and captured vermin, and foul lairs deliberately so rival humanoids avoid the approach.
c.beh: Their behaviour is clan-based, territorial, and scent-driven. Warriors, hunters, egg-guards, and shamans are social roles within one species; disputes are settled through dominance, musk display, ambush, and control of food stores.
c.traitsText: Traits include darkvision, claws, bite, musk defence, strong climbing hands, poor tolerance for bright open spaces, and low but practical tool intelligence. Skeletons show an upright reptilian humanoid pelvis and hand bones, not a drake or salamander body.
c.abilities: Innate abilities are musk stench, claw-and-bite fighting, darkvision, stealth in caves, and ambush coordination. Ritual magic belongs to individuals or cults, not to separate biological species.
c.bg: In the Throat, Troglodytes matter as a competing cave people whose musk-marked territories can close side passages and contaminate water or fungus stores.

c.g: The Murk Lizardfolk. Occupies the still-water lake margins of the Fogreach, building low-profile settlements on the shore that are almost invisible in the fog. Fishes the Blind Pale Fish shoals with practiced efficiency. Trades smoked fish and rendered Cave-Ray fat with the grey-folk through the Pall-Broker intermediary system.
c.tax: Lizardfolk of confirmed Murk-habitat residency with established grey-folk trade relations. Distinguished from other Lizardfolk by fog-navigation capacity and aquatic-primary hunting ecology.
c.ap: Murk Lizardfolk is a tall bipedal reptilian humanoid with a crocodilian or iguanid head, broad tail, clawed hands, scaled hide, and digitigrade feet suited to mud, water, and stone. Weapons and ornaments identify culture; the body is identified by tail mass, jaw shape, and limb posture.
c.eco: Murk Lizardfolk lives around water, marshy caverns, or wet ledges, feeding on fish, amphibians, insects, carrion, and meat taken in short ambushes. Eggs are laid in warm protected nests and guarded by kin groups when resources permit.
c.beh: Murk Lizardfolk behaviour is pragmatic and survival-centred. It values food, territory, kin, useful tools, and clear threat displays more than abstract law; alliances are possible when routes, hunting rights, or water access are respected.
c.traitsText: Traits include swimming strength, hold-breath capacity, natural armour, claw and bite attacks, darkvision or low-light adaptation, and a powerful tail for balance. The hand remains tool-capable, which separates it from draconid beasts.
c.abilities: Innate abilities are bite, claws, swimming, breath-holding, stealth near water, and hide toughness. Shamanic magic, spear drills, or boatcraft are learned culture, not species boundaries.
c.bg: In the Throat, Murk Lizardfolk is relevant wherever water corridors and humanoid territory overlap, especially where scouts might mistake a hunting party for animal predators.

c.g: Surface Lizardfolk — the nearest non-deep relatives of the Murk Lizardfolk. Not present in the Throat; included as phylogenetic context.
c.tax: Lizardfolk of surface-water habitat with established civilisation. Nearest non-deep relative of Lacertus profundus.

c.g: Black Kobolds are a stable kobold colour-line. Engineer, trapper, shaman, and warrior are roles that may occur in any colour-line.
c.tax: Kobold-family Reptilian Humanoid subspecies keyed to inherited draconic colour-line traits rather than profession.
c.ap: Black Kobold is a small bipedal reptilian humanoid, usually knee- to chest-high beside a grey-folk adult, with a narrow torso, digitigrade legs, clawed hands, flexible tail, short horns, and a dragonlike snout. This brood-line shows matte black scales, yellow eyes, flattened snout, webbed fingers; it is not a class, job, or caste, and shamans or warriors of the line remain the same subspecies.
c.eco: Black Kobold clans breed by guarded egg clutches in wet tunnels, sump traps, bone softened in caustic pits. They eat fungi, insects, small vertebrates, preserved meat, and whatever tribute or scavenged supplies the warren can secure; hatchlings are communal and learn tunnel routes before toolcraft.
c.beh: Black Kobold behaviour is communal, anxious, ingenious, and status-conscious. Individuals prefer traps, alarms, murder-holes, collapsible floors, and negotiated deference to open combat; reverence for black draconic ancestry shapes shrines, colours, and craft marks without making them dragons.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include small humanoid stature, scales of the black line, darkvision, pack coordination, nimble hands, trapcraft, sensitivity to bright open spaces, and resistance or practical tolerance associated with acid. Their skeleton has a humanoid pelvis and hand structure, not a drake or true-dragon frame.
c.abilities: Black Kobold innate abilities are warren movement, darkvision, coordinated ambush, quick tool use, and minor resistance or instinctive avoidance keyed to acid. Spellcasting, priesthood, mining, and warrior training are cultural roles, not separate biological entries.
c.bg: In the Throat, Black Kobold warrens matter because they turn marginal passages into engineered territory. Route records note alarm strings, false floors, egg chambers, shrine alcoves, and negotiated toll points rather than treating the population as random monsters.

c.g: A Githyanki patrol — descending into the Gorge tracking Intellect Devourers. If the Intellect Devourers are here, Mind Flayers are establishing. If Mind Flayers are establishing, the Githyanki will be here in force shortly. The grey-folk consider them allies by circumstance and neighbours by nobody's choice.
c.tax: Githyanki of standard military-patrol grade; silver sword (lich-queen gifted); psionic capacity; tracking Mind Flayer presence.
c.ap: 1.9 m; yellow-grey skin; angular features; red-black armour; silver longsword; moves with the efficiency of someone who has been fighting the same enemy for ten thousand years.
c.eco: Githyanki Warrior ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Githyanki Warrior behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Githyanki Warrior include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of Githyanki Warrior are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, Githyanki Warrior matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.
c.note: Warrior role removed from species name.

c.g: A Githzerai contemplative in the Gorge for reasons they have not shared. Githzerai do not explain themselves; they observe. What they are observing in the Gorge is the Nothing-Below, which they identified in their first hour of descent and have been sitting quietly at a moderate distance from ever since.
c.tax: Githzerai of contemplative-grade; psionic capacity manifest as absolute situational awareness; in Gorge for observational purpose only; not hostile.
c.ap: 1.8 m; yellow-grey skin; plain grey robes; sitting or standing with perfect stillness; the expression of someone who has seen something large and is deciding what it means.
c.eco: Githzerai Contemplative ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Githzerai Contemplative behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Githzerai Contemplative include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of Githzerai Contemplative are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, Githzerai Contemplative matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.

c.g: The Yuan-Ti Pureblood — appearing nearly humanoid, discoverable only by the forked tongue, the slit pupils, and the cold skin. The most recently arrived in the Gorge; their purpose is unknown to everyone who has identified one and no one who knows them has told the grey-folk what it is.
c.tax: Yuan-Ti of Pureblood grade: minimal serpent-trait expression; magic resistance at pureblood level; appears humanoid; primary infiltration-grade Yuan-Ti.
c.ap: Humanoid 1.6–1.8 m; forked tongue; slit pupils; skin slightly cool to touch; dress unremarkably; the tell is the forked tongue and the quality of stillness.
c.eco: Yuan-Ti Pureblood ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Yuan-Ti Pureblood behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Yuan-Ti Pureblood include upright gait, manipulative hands, language capacity, tool learning, social memory, and the sensory adaptations named in its appearance. Skeletal remains should be compared by pelvis, skull, teeth, limb proportions, and hand bones.
c.abilities: Innate abilities of Yuan-Ti Pureblood are limited to inherited senses, resistances, movement, lifespan, or minor magical traits. Combat training, priesthood, craft specialism, and spell study are cultural overlays unless the entry explicitly identifies a biological difference.
c.bg: In the Throat index, Yuan-Ti Pureblood matters as a people or comparative people: it affects routes through settlement, trade, conflict, ancestry, language, and ecological pressure rather than by monster-lair evidence alone.

c.g: The Yuan-Ti Halfblood — mid-transformation, too snake to pass as humanoid, too humanoid to pass as a Beast. Serves as the enforcement arm of Yuan-Ti operations; not subtle about it.
c.tax: Yuan-Ti of Halfblood grade: prominent serpent-trait expression (scales on torso, snake-head or snake-arms); magic resistance elevated over Pureblood; not designed for concealment.
c.ap: 1.7 m; human torso with snake lower body OR human body with snake head; scales visible; moves with snake-characteristic silence.
c.eco: Yuan-Ti Halfblood ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.
c.beh: Yuan-Ti Halfblood behaviour should be read as species or population tendency, not as class role. Soldiers, shamans, nobles, guild members, exiles, and artisans may appear within the population without becoming separate taxa.

c.g: The Yuan-Ti Isolate — a Pureblood or Halfblood operating entirely alone in the Gorge, with no apparent colonial affiliation and no evident mission that the Heralds have been able to reconstruct from their observation records. It has been moving through the Fogreach for approximately eight months, spending between two and fifteen days in each location before moving on. The Heralds' working hypothesis is scouting; their backup hypothesis is exile.
c.tax: Yuan-Ti of solitary operational type; distinguished from standard Pureblood and Halfblood colonial-affiliation members by confirmed lack of contact with any known colonial structure.
c.ap: Appears as a standard grey-folk from a distance; forked tongue; slit pupils; moves with systematic thoroughness suggesting a survey rather than a destination.
c.eco: Yuan-Ti Isolate ecology is social rather than purely predatory: food, reproduction, childhood, territory, tool access, and intergroup exchange determine where the population persists. Diet is omnivorous or culturally specialized according to the settlements and terrain described for this entry.

c.g: Large-bodied humanoid-structured beings 3–9 m tall, with limited language and innate elemental affinity — the deep Stone-Gaunts of the Throat appear only in the wildest reaches of Tier III and have never been successfully communicated with by grey-folk.
c.tax: Giants phylum revised so former proto/sub-phyla containers no longer interrupt the hierarchy. Proto-Giants is retained as an inferred orphan species; observed lineages now sit directly beneath the phylum as classes.
c.ap: 4–9 m humanoid shape; elemental-affinity coloration by type; move with deceptive speed for their size. Stone-Gaunts specifically: stone-textured skin, silent locomotion, faces worn featureless.

c.g: The theorized common ancestor of all giant-kind, prior to the True Giant and Giant-Kin divergence. Inferred from the shared giant-grade physiology across all descendant lineages: the bone density, the elemental affinity, the simple language, and the territorial social structure that appear in every branch from Storm Giants to Trolls. No specimen recovered and none expected.
c.tax: Species-level inferred stem ancestor for the Giant branch. It is retained as an orphan reconstruction rather than as a formal container, so observed classes can sit directly beneath the Giant phylum.
c.ap: Proto-Giant is described by the physical or metaphysical structures stated in its own entry: body plan, surface texture, movement, diagnostic marks, and remains likely to survive as tracks, bones, residue, spores, ash, or anchor traces.
c.eco: Proto-Giant occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.
c.beh: Proto-Giant behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Proto-Giant traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: fossil or inferred, theorized.
c.abilities: Proto-Giant abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description.
c.bg: The Throat records Proto-Giant for field relevance: distribution, danger, ecological role, fossil meaning, cultural contact, or comparison value. Status: fossil or inferred, theorized. Subordinate taxa: True Giants, Giant-Kin, Ettins.

c.g: The core giant lineage — Storm, Fire, Frost, Stone, and Hill Giants, unified by giant-grade physiology and innate elemental affinity. The variety in their forms reflects adaptation to wildly different environments over geological time rather than fundamental biological divergence.
c.tax: True Giant lineage: 3–9 m bipedal humanoid; giant-grade bone density; elemental affinity by type; simple language; territorial social structure; lifespan 200–400 years.
c.ap: Variable by family; all share the giant-grade scale and proportionate power. The stone quality of their skin and the elemental character of their hair and eyes reflects their elemental type.

c.g: Storm Giants — the apex True Giants; benevolent in temperament by giant standards.
c.tax: True Giant of maximum size and weather-magic; benevolent by giant standards.
c.ap: Storm Giants is described by the physical or metaphysical structures stated in its own entry: body plan, surface texture, movement, diagnostic marks, and remains likely to survive as tracks, bones, residue, spores, ash, or anchor traces.

c.g: The apex True Giant — storm giants inhabit mountain peaks and ocean depths in equal measure, spending centuries in either environment in a contemplative state that lesser beings mistake for inactivity. They are neutral in alignment by individual disposition rather than by indifference; they have witnessed enough history to have opinions about it and have concluded that the arc of history bends without their assistance. When a Storm Giant does intervene, the event tends to be recorded.
c.tax: Neutral True Giant of storm affinity; lightning and thunder capacity; ocean and mountain-peak habitat; 7–9 m; prophetic capacity through observation of cloud and weather patterns; maximum True Giant intelligence.
c.ap: 7–9 m tall; aqua-green skin; long white or silver hair; armoured in scale and plate of their own design; carries a javelin the size of a ship's mast; in their mountain or ocean habitat appears as a feature of the landscape until it moves.
c.eco: Storm Giant occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.

c.g: Giants of forge and flame — the most militaristic of the True Giant lineage and the ones most likely to be building something. Fire Giants inhabit volcanic mountain ranges and construct fortresses of black iron around active vents. Their society is caste-based, their smithwork is genuinely excellent, and their ambition is structural: they want kingdoms, not just territory. Not present in the Throat by confirmed record, though the Nadir's magma ocean is thermally compatible with their preferred environment.
c.tax: True Giant of fire affinity; black-iron skin; fire immunity; forge-craft as primary cultural technology; military-caste social structure; 4–5 m; evil-alignment tendency.
c.ap: 4–5 m; skin a deep iron-black with a slight heat-shimmer; hair and beard orange-red; armoured in black iron plate of their own manufacture; carries weapons of forge-quality that humanoid smiths would find instructive.

c.g: The forge-masters of the True Giant lineage — Fire Giants construct fortresses, weapons, and mechanical systems of genuine sophistication, channeling their fire affinity into craft rather than combat as a first resort. Their society is the most formally organized of the True Giants and the most likely to pursue long-term strategic objectives. Not present in the Throat by confirmed record.
c.tax: Lawful evil True Giant of fire affinity; fire immunity; master smithcraft as cultural technology; 4–5 m; military social hierarchy.
c.ap: 4–5 m; black iron-textured skin; orange-red hair; armoured in black iron plate of their own making; carries weapons of forge quality; the skin at joints and creases glows faintly with internal heat.

c.g: Giants of cold and conquest — Frost Giants inhabit arctic mountain ranges and glacial coasts, organized into loose clans united by strength and raiding tradition. They are the least architecturally inclined True Giant variety and the most directly aggressive: where Fire Giants build fortresses, Frost Giants prefer to live in the fortresses of others, particularly after the previous residents have been persuaded to leave. Not present in the Throat.
c.tax: True Giant of cold affinity; cold immunity; arctic habitat; raid-based economy; strength-hierarchy clan structure; 4–6 m; evil-alignment tendency.
c.ap: 4–6 m; skin blue-grey from cold; hair white or pale yellow; armoured in furs and bone-plate over hide; carries a greataxe or morningstar of crude but effective design; breath visible in any temperature.

c.g: Raiding giants of arctic mountain ranges — the Frost Giant clan system prizes martial achievement above all else and their raids are the primary means by which individuals establish status. They are not the most intelligent True Giants but they are among the most physically powerful, and in their arctic environment the cold that kills most things is the medium in which they thrive.
c.tax: Chaotic evil True Giant of cold affinity; cold immunity; arctic habitat; 4–6 m; clan-based social structure with raid-economy.

c.g: Surface stone giants — the nearest surface relatives of the Stone-Gaunts. Inhabit deep mountain ranges; have a developed culture including cave-art and stone-craft.
c.tax: True Giant of stone-affinity; stone-throwing as primary weapon; deep-mountain habitat; living-rock aesthetic culture. Distinguished from Stone-Gaunts by surface adaptation and communication capacity.
c.ap: 3–5 m; stone-grey skin; no mineral deposition; humanoid features; known to communicate with each other and occasionally with humanoids.

c.g: The deep Giants of the Throat — their origin unknown, their language unrecorded, their intentions toward grey-folk ranging from indifferent to territorial. Their relationship to specific crystal formations suggests a connection to the Living Stone.
c.tax: Stone Giant lineage with extreme deep-stone adaptation; mineral deposition creating stone-textured skin; Stonesong-resonance response in Crystal Gaunt variant. Territory defined by mineral formations rather than passage-space.
c.ap: Stone-grey to near-black textured skin; 4–6 m; face worn smooth; hair absent or replaced by mineral deposition; move silently despite size.

c.g: The Deep Stone Giant. Observed in crystal galleries with apparent relationship to specific large crystal formations — it positions itself near them in a way suggesting either guarding or worship, and the distinction may not matter.
c.tax: Stone-Gaunt with crystal-formation territorial behavior; no confirmed communication.
c.ap: 4–5 m; stone-grey textured skin; face features present but worn smooth; holds a crystal-composite object (not clearly manufactured); stands near large crystals for hours without moving.
c.eco: Deep Stone Giant occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.

c.g: The Crystal Giant. Distinguished by pronounced crystal deposition — quartz and calcite formations grow from its shoulders, skull, and knuckles. The Stonesong Heralds have recorded that it responds to specific Stonesong frequencies with a corresponding resonance in its crystals, which the Heralds consider the most important discovery of the last century and are keeping very quiet about.
c.tax: Stone-Gaunt with active crystal deposition and confirmed Stonesong-frequency resonance; the most significant cryptobiology finding in current Heralds records.
c.ap: 5–6 m; stone-grey with white quartz-crystal projections from shoulders, skull crown, and knuckles; the crystal projections ring faintly at Stonesong frequencies; face slightly more distinct than Gigas profundus.
c.eco: Crystal Giant occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.

c.g: The most numerous and least sophisticated True Giant — Hill Giants occupy hill country and forest margins, eating whatever is available and whatever is not available but slower than they are. Their intelligence is the minimum that qualifies as Giant rather than animal, and their social structure is a simple hierarchy of who can hit hardest. Not present in the Throat by any confirmed record, though Gorge Ogres share similar ecology and fill the same ecological role.
c.tax: True Giant of minimum intelligence and maximum appetite; hill and lowland forest habitat; simple strength-hierarchy group structure; 3–5 m; evil-alignment tendency.
c.ap: 3–5 m; heavyset; brown to grey-green skin; poor hygiene; carries a large tree-trunk or boulder as a preferred weapon; smells of what it has eaten, which is most things.
c.eco: Hill Giants occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.

c.g: The lesser giant lineage — Ogres, Trolls, Ettins and related forms representing an early divergence from the giant ancestral line that did not develop the full True Giant suite of elemental affinity and intelligence. They are physically giant-grade but intellectually below True Giant range, and their ecology reflects it: opportunistic rather than strategic.
c.tax: Giant-Kin body plan (2–4 m); below True Giant intelligence; no formal elemental affinity; opportunistic social structure; trolls distinguished by regeneration; ogres by moderate intelligence enabling simple agreements.
c.ap: Variable: Ogres 2–3 m, heavyset, humanoid; Trolls 3–4 m, gangly, green-grey; Ettins 4–5 m, two-headed.

c.g: Giant-Kin of moderate size and simple motivation — Ogres are the most commonly encountered non-Troll Giant-Kin in accessible underdark environments. Their intelligence is sufficient for simple agreement-following and tool-use, but not for strategic planning; they are dangerous as individual encounters and manageable as neighbours given adequate food supply. The Gorge Cave Ogre population in the mid-tiers is established enough that the Pall-Brokers maintain a formal food-for-passage contract system.
c.tax: Giant-Kin of sub-True-Giant intelligence; simple tool use; agreement-following behavior when motivation is clear; size sufficient for significant physical threat to humanoid-sized organisms; cave-adapted forms show pale coloration and larger eyes.
c.ap: 2–3 m; heavily built humanoid; brown to grey in surface forms, pale grey-brown in cave-adapted forms; large hands and feet; carries a simple heavy weapon (often a stalactite or large rock); smells powerfully of its diet.

c.g: The Cave Ogre — present in the middle tiers in the same ecological niche as surface ogres (large, violent, hungry, not particularly strategic) but adapted to the dark with the same simple effectiveness that characterises everything in the Throat. Cave Ogres are individually manageable; in groups of three or more they represent a significant resource-drain on Wall-Warden operations. The Pall-Brokers have observed that Cave Ogres respond to simple contract arrangements — food in exchange for not attacking things — and have brokered several such arrangements that have held for years. The arrangements collapse when the food supply is interrupted, which the Pall-Brokers consider a lesson in contract specificity.
c.tax: Cave-adapted Ogre; 2–3 m; sub-True-Giant intelligence but sufficient for simple agreement-following behaviour; distinguished from surface Ogres by pale skin, enlarged eyes, and the specific quality of the threat they represent in enclosed passage systems (large organisms in tight spaces are disproportionately effective).
c.ap: 2–3 m; humanoid; pale grey-brown skin from cave-adaptation; enlarged eyes; large hands; carries a club that is technically a stalactite it pulled from a passage ceiling; smells of the things it eats, which in the Gorge is mostly Vermin, Cave Stalkers, and whatever grey-folk provisions it has successfully contracted for.

c.g: Giant-Kin with rapid regeneration — trolls survive almost anything and regrow from almost nothing, the exceptions being acid and fire damage which suppresses the regeneration pathway. In the Gorge context this makes them effectively unkillable by most cave fauna and highly killable by the Forgemasters' acid-resistant work equipment and the occasional experimental application of Roil-vented fire. They are present throughout Tier II and III, individually or in small groups, and they are the primary reason the Wall-Wardens stock acid flasks as standard kit.
c.tax: Giant-Kin of rapid-regeneration body plan; 3–4 m; regenerates all non-acid, non-fire damage at a rate that makes conventional weapons non-viable as a primary response. Cave-adapted variants show reduced surface coloration and enlarged eyes.
c.ap: Trolls is described by the physical or metaphysical structures stated in its own entry: body plan, surface texture, movement, diagnostic marks, and remains likely to survive as tracks, bones, residue, spores, ash, or anchor traces.

c.g: The Cave Troll — the standard Gorge Troll, fully adapted to the dark over generations of deep-passage residency. Its regeneration makes it the most durable organism in the Fogreach passage system for anything short of the Wall-Horror apex predators. It is not aggressive by choice; it is aggressive by hunger, which is constant, and by territorial instinct, which is nearly as constant. The Wall-Wardens use acid-flask protocols; the grey-folk colloquially call this 'the troll solution', which has a different and coincidental meaning in the context of the Three Humours programme.
c.tax: Cave-adapted Troll of standard Gorge passage-ecology; distinguished from surface Trolls by pale grey-green skin (reduced pigmentation), enlarged eyes (low-light adaptation), and slightly reduced above-ground sun-sensitivity. Regeneration as standard Troll; acid and fire suppression as standard Troll.
c.ap: 3–4 m; gangly; pale grey-green skin from cave-adaptation; eyes enlarged and pale; long arms reaching below the knee when standing; perpetually wet-looking; smells of the things it has partially eaten and regenerated from eating.
c.eco: Cave Troll occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.

c.g: The Stone Troll — a Troll whose regeneration has incorporated the mineral environment so completely that it heals using local stone as a substrate, regenerating damaged tissue as a mineral-biological composite rather than purely biological material. This makes its regenerated tissue more durable than the original but significantly heavier; old Stone Trolls are visibly more stone than flesh. They move slowly. They are very difficult to kill even with acid, as the mineral composite tissue does not respond to acid the same way biological tissue does. The Forgemasters have noted this and are researching alternatives.
c.tax: Cave Troll with mineral-substrate regeneration pathway; regenerated tissue is mineral-biological composite; distinguished from standard Cave Troll by increased mass, reduced speed, and acid-partial-resistance in regenerated tissue sections. Older specimens may be 60–70% mineral-composite by mass.
c.ap: 3.5–4.5 m; much heavier than Cave Troll; skin in non-regenerated areas grey-green standard; regenerated areas visibly stone-grey with a rough mineral texture; moves with the deliberateness of something that weighs significantly more than it looks like it should; leaves mineral-dust footprints.

c.g: Two-headed giants — Giant-Kin of the ettin line, each head operating as a semi-independent personality. They do not agree with each other. This makes them simultaneously more dangerous in combat (each head can act independently, effectively never being surprised) and completely unable to maintain a strategic position for longer than the two heads can hold consensus, which is rarely long.
c.tax: Giant-Kin of two-headed body plan; each head has independent mental capacity and often conflicting motivation; 4–5 m; Giant-Kin grade intelligence (above Troll, below Hill Giant).
c.ap: 4–5 m; two heads on a single neck-divided torso; always arguing with itself; carries two weapons, one per hand, each chosen by a different head; smells of something decomposing.

c.g: A cave-adapted Ettin in the deep Fogreach. Its two heads have been arguing about territory with a goblin clan for eleven years. The dispute has become sufficiently complex that the Pall-Brokers maintain a mediation file. Neither head has agreed to the terms simultaneously; the terms change every time one head is convinced.
c.tax: Ettin of cave-adaptation; confirmed Fogreach residency; two-head conflict currently entangled in Pall-Broker mediation.
c.ap: 4.5 m; pale grey from cave-adaptation; right head angrier; left head marginally more reasonable; both heads' arguments audible from 50 m.

c.g: The Slow Ettin — an individual whose two heads have, for once, fully agreed on something, which in an Ettin produces an eerie calm. What they have agreed on is not apparent. The Pall-Brokers' mediation file notes that the previously documented Ettin dispute with the goblin clan was resolved three months ago; whether this is related is recorded as a question without an answer.
c.tax: Ettin in confirmed consensus-state between heads; distinguished by absence of inter-head argument and by consequent deliberate movement. The consensus state is apparently stable, which the Heralds find more alarming than the standard discord.
c.ap: As Biceptus profundus but both heads oriented in the same direction and both expressions identically calm; moves slowly and with clear purpose toward an unstated destination.

c.g: Dragons are the draconic kingdom of Primal: large-scaled or scale-derived vertebrate monsters whose descendants share reinforced jaws, heavy keratinous or mineralized integument, predatory cranial architecture, and elemental or quasi-elemental resonance. The kingdom includes three great living plans: true dragons, wyrms, and draconids. Those branches are related, but they are not interchangeable; a breath-bearing true dragon, a ground-bound axial wyrm, and a limb-specialized draconid must be identified by body plan before color, habitat, or reputation is considered.
c.tax: Phylum-level draconic life is defined by shared deep ancestry from the inferred primodragon, but the rank does not imply that every descendant has wings, breath magic, hoarding instinct, or human-level speech. True dragons, wyrms, and draconids are phyla because each fixes a different solution to the same ancestral draconic toolkit: six-limbed sovereign flight, axial burrowing enormity, or reduced/specialized predatory forms.
c.ap: At kingdom scale the reliable signs are draconic skull construction, enlarged recurved dentition, reinforced jaw hinges, scale or scute armor, clawed digits, and a tail built as a balancing or striking organ. The exact silhouette differs sharply: true dragons are six-limbed with independent wings, wyrms are long-bodied and ground-bound, and draconids include wing-forelimbed wyverns and terrestrial drakes.

c.g: Primodragon is the inferred species-level stem animal from which the draconic kingdom is reconstructed. It is not a domain, kingdom, or phylum; it is a hypothetical species placed high in the tree because its descendants are numerous and divergent.
c.tax: Inferred species directly subordinate to the Dragon kingdom. It marks the last common draconic condition before true dragons, wyrms, and draconids separated into distinct phyla.
c.ap: The reconstruction is not a modern true dragon. It is best modeled as a heavy-scaled quadrupedal predator with a long balancing tail, deep jaw muscles, short cranial horns or brow ridges, and no requirement for a separate powered wing pair.
c.eco: As an inferred stem animal, primodragon represents an egg-laying predatory ecology in warm stone, volcanic margins, or mineral-rich caverns. Its descendants preserve the pattern of defended nests, slow maturation, and chemically marked territory.
c.beh: Behavior is reconstructed as territorial, nest-defensive, and strongly site-faithful rather than wise or sovereign. Later true dragons develop the famous hoarding intellect; stem primodragon should be read as dangerous animal ancestry, not an ancient monarch.
c.traitsText: Reconstructed traits include heavy scales, clawed limbs, binocular predatory vision, mineral-tolerant digestion, tail balance, and a skull capable of sustaining extreme bite force. Wings, spellcasting, and moral color categories are absent from the reconstruction.
c.abilities: The likely innate ability was primitive elemental tolerance rather than a formal breath weapon: resistance to heat, mineral toxins, or pressure sufficient to let later branches specialize into fire, stone, storm, water, and venom expressions.
c.bg: This species exists to stop the catalogue from treating every draconic creature as a distorted true dragon. It anchors common descent without forcing drakes, wyrms, and true dragons to share the same adult anatomy.

c.g: True Dragons are the six-limbed sovereign phylum of the dragon kingdom. They are defined by four walking limbs, two independent wings, breath-channel anatomy, age-deep magic, and intelligence sufficient for language, memory, territory law, and long-term strategy.
c.tax: Class under Dragons containing the inferred protodragon species and the chromatic, gem, and metallic classes. True dragons are not drakes, wyrms, wyverns, kobolds, or demi-draconic hosts.
c.ap: The phylum silhouette is unmistakable: horned skull, long neck, four clawed legs, separate wings with elongated wing fingers, rib and sternum reinforcement for flight, and a heavy tail used for balance and combat.
c.eco: True dragons are long-lived egg layers with extended juvenile stages and lairs that mature with them. Their territories become ecological centers, storing treasure, corpses, heat, poison, sound, memory, or covenant depending on class and species.
c.beh: True dragons possess selfhood on the scale of dynasties. They plan, remember insults, establish lair law, recognize lineage, and often treat shorter-lived creatures as either tools, petitioners, prey, students, or oath-partners.
c.traitsText: Phylum traits include six limbs, powered flight, breath anatomy, high intelligence, keen senses, age categories, heavy scale armor, elemental resistance, and lair imprinting.
c.abilities: True dragons possess breath weapons, frightful presence, innate magic that deepens with age, and class-specific lair effects. These abilities must not be assigned to wyrms or draconids unless their own entries state them.
c.bg: In the Throat, a true dragon is a political and ecological event. Its presence changes routes, tribute systems, clan myths, and the behavior of every predator nearby.

c.g: Protodragon is the inferred species at the base of True Dragons. It represents the first draconic body plan with four walking limbs and a separate pair of powered wings.
c.tax: Inferred species directly subordinate to the True Dragons phylum. It anchors chromatic, gem, and metallic classes without implying that wyrms or draconids passed through the same six-limbed adult form.
c.ap: Reconstructed as a six-limbed draconic animal: four clawed walking limbs, two independent wing limbs, long neck, horned skull, heavy tail, and a sternum enlarged for flight muscle. This is where true-dragon flight enters the tree.
c.eco: Protodragon likely nested in elevated stone, hot cliffs, or cavern chimneys where flight and lair defense mattered. Its descendants preserve egg clutches, long juvenile periods, and age-linked growth.
c.beh: Behavior is reconstructed as territorial, intelligent, and increasingly hoard-like: not yet divided into chromatic domination, metallic covenant, or gem contemplation, but already more than a mere animal predator.
c.traitsText: Traits include six-limbed anatomy, powered flight, heavy scale armor, acute senses, long lifespan, high intelligence, and the first stable breath-channel structures in the throat and chest.
c.abilities: Abilities include primitive breath discharge, fright display, lair imprinting, and the first age-deep magical accumulation that later true dragons elaborate into named species powers.
c.bg: This inferred species is the key correction separating true dragons from wyrms and draconids. It prevents winged true-dragon anatomy from being projected backward onto all dragon relatives.

c.g: Chromatic true dragons are the destructive class of true dragons, organized around territorial conquest, solitary dominance, and a single overwhelming breath expression tied to color-line physiology.
c.tax: Order within True Dragons. Chromatics are separated from metallic and gem dragons by predatory territorial psychology, one primary elemental breath, and scale pigmentation that corresponds to inherited lair chemistry rather than ornament alone.
c.ap: Chromatic bodies are six-limbed true dragons: four walking limbs plus two independent wings. Their skulls are aggressive and overbuilt, with brow horns, nasal weaponry, or jaw projections that remain diagnostic even on skeletons.

c.g: The apex chromatic — arrogant, avaricious, and possessed of the most powerful fire breath in the chromatic lineage. Red Dragons settle in mountain peaks and volcanic regions, accumulating hoards of smelted metal and refined ore rather than raw stone, their fire-breath having pre-processed much of it. Their intelligence is high and their patience for anything less than absolute deference is nil. A Red Dragon that has decided it has been disrespected is a geological event.
c.tax: Chromatic True Dragon of fire breath; mountain and volcanic habitat; intelligence at the upper chromatic range; evil-alignment disposition; hoard composition skews toward refined metals and worked gold.
c.ap: Red Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are high nasal ridge, swept-back crown horns, deep furnace sternum, blackened wing membranes, long dorsal spines, and heat-cracked teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.

c.g: An adult Red Dragon in full territorial possession — the standard encounter for the species and the version for which the taxonomic entry was written. It has a hoard, a mountain, and a fire-breath capacity sufficient to make anyone else’s opinion about either irrelevant.
c.tax: Chromatic adult-grade; fire breath cone 9 m wide and 27 m long; fire immunity; territorial; intelligent; evil.
c.ap: Red Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are high nasal ridge, swept-back crown horns, deep furnace sternum, blackened wing membranes, long dorsal spines, and heat-cracked teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.
c.traitsText: Useful traits for Red Dragon include the six-limbed skeleton, armoured overlapping scales, crushing bite, taloned forefeet, tail sweep, darkvision, keen smell, high reasoning ability, and immunity or strong resistance to fire. Preserved remains are identified by high nasal ridge, swept-back crown horns, deep furnace sternum, blackened wing membranes, long dorsal spines, and heat-cracked teeth, plus the deep sternum and wing phalanges of true dragons.
c.abilities: Red Dragon uses a cone of fire supported by chest pressure, specialized throat sacs, frightful presence, flight, claw-and-bite combinations, tail strikes, and age-deep innate magic. Its lair effects should express fire and its own territorial psychology, not drake, wyvern, kobold, salamander, or metallic-dragon traits.
c.eco: Red Dragon lairs where volcanic heat, sulphur vents, baked stone, and exposed treasure halls make its fire adaptation useful. It is an apex predator and hoard-keeper, but its ecology is not merely feeding: prey, tribute, lair heat or chemistry, egg chambers, and controlled approach routes are arranged to support an adult that can hold territory for centuries.
c.beh: Red Dragon behaviour centres on tyranny and visible dominance. It recognizes individuals, remembers trespass, tests weakness before bargaining, and treats the lair as an extension of ownership. Any apparent negotiation should be read as a dominance exchange unless repeated evidence proves otherwise.
c.bg: In the Throat record, Red Dragon marks a specific chromatic-dragon hazard. Surveyors should map breath residue, approach geometry, hoard traffic, species-specific tracks, shed scales, and bones arranged by intelligence rather than scavenger chance.

c.g: The collector and controller of the chromatic line — Blue Dragons accumulate hoards organised with a precision their Tanar'ri cousins would find baffling. They are lightning-breathers of desert and arid terrain, and their territorial markings are the lightning-strike scars on rock faces surrounding their lairs. They communicate more readily than most chromatics and are marginally more likely to negotiate, though the terms will be heavily weighted in their favour and they will remember every clause.
c.tax: Chromatic True Dragon of lightning breath; desert and arid-terrain habitat; organised hoard; above-average chromatic intelligence; willing to negotiate but not to compromise.
c.ap: Blue Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are single tall nasal horn, broad ear-frills, ridged brow, narrow skull, long digging claws, and electric scarring along horn and teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.

c.g: An adult Blue Dragon in its desert territory — it has catalogued every item in its hoard, can recite the acquisition date and source of each, and will do so if given an opening. Its lightning breath is fast enough that most targets do not understand what happened until several seconds afterward.
c.tax: Chromatic adult-grade; lightning breath line 30 m; lightning immunity; territorial; collector psychology; evil.

c.g: The manipulator of the chromatic lineage — Green Dragons prefer forested terrain and the slow accumulation of influence over the immediate exercise of force. They are poisonous in breath and personality, patient enough to wait years to deliver a consequence, and sufficiently intelligent to hold a grudge across generations. They collect the confessions and secrets of those they deal with as a secondary hoard.
c.tax: Chromatic True Dragon of poison breath; forest habitat; manipulative social strategy; poison-cloud breath weapon; collects secrets and debts of obligation as a secondary hoard alongside material wealth.
c.ap: Green Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are hooked jaw, cheek spines, leaflike hornlets, long sinuous neck, narrow nostrils, and stained tooth grooves. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.

c.g: An adult Green Dragon in its forest territory — it knows exactly what each creature in its domain is doing and has probable cause to believe it knows what they are thinking. Its poison breath is secondary to its capacity for sustained manipulation; the breath is for when manipulation fails, which is rare.
c.tax: Chromatic adult-grade; poison-cloud breath; poison immunity; manipulative psychology; forest habitat; evil.

c.g: The most treacherous of the chromatic dragons and the most at home in the dark — Black Dragons inhabit swamps, underground water systems, and ruin-fields where standing water pools over collapsed masonry. Their acid breath dissolves bone, armour, and stone with equal indifference. They are not particularly patient and not particularly subtle, but they are long-lived enough to have made every mistake once and learned from it.
c.tax: Chromatic True Dragon of acid breath; wetland and underground-adjacent habitat; treacherous psychology; acid dissolves non-magical metal and bone; notably dishonest even by chromatic standards.
c.ap: Black Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are forward-curving horns, crocodilian skull, exposed cheekbone ridges, ragged wing edges, webbed talons, and acid-pitted teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.

c.g: An adult Black Dragon in its swamp territory — it is perpetually annoyed, perpetually treacherous, and acid-drips constantly, which has the effect of making everything around it slowly worse over time. This is both an ecological fact and an accurate character description.
c.tax: Chromatic adult-grade; acid breath line 27 m; acid immunity; treacherous psychology; wetland habitat; evil.
c.ap: Black Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. c.g: The least intelligent and most instinct-driven of the chromatic dragons — White Dragons survive by ferocity rather than strategy, inhabiting arctic and deep-cold environments where nothing else wants to go and where their cold breath is genuinely useful. They are not stupid, merely immediate: they do not plan campaigns; they hunt, eat, and hoard in that order. A White Dragon's hoard is often frozen solid.
c.tax: Chromatic True Dragon of cold breath; arctic and glacial habitat; minimum chromatic intelligence; instinct-dominant behaviour; hoard often cryogenically preserved in ice.
c.ap: White Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are low wedge skull, backward-swept crest, short heavy horns, compact chest, ice-hook claws, and serrated frost-shearing teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.

c.g: An adult White Dragon in its arctic territory — it is pursuing prey it was tracking forty days ago with the same unbroken attention it brought to the first day, which is as close to intelligence as this species reliably gets. It is not strategic. It does not need to be.
c.tax: Chromatic adult-grade; cold-breath cone; cold immunity; instinct-dominant; arctic habitat; evil.
c.ap: White Dragon is a six-limbed true dragon: four clawed walking legs, two separate shoulder wings, a long neck, horned skull, deep breath-bearing chest, and heavy counterbalancing tail. Its species marks are low wedge skull, backward-swept crest, short heavy horns, compact chest, ice-hook claws, and serrated frost-shearing teeth. Field identification must use skull profile, horn cores, wing-finger proportions, tooth wear, gait width, and breath residue, not scale colour alone.
c.eco: White Dragon lairs where ice caves, hoarfrost ledges, glacial cracks, and freezer-still caverns make its cold adaptation useful. It is an apex predator and hoard-keeper, but its ecology is not merely feeding: prey, tribute, lair heat or chemistry, egg chambers, and controlled approach routes are arranged to support an adult that can hold territory for centuries.
c.beh: White Dragon behaviour centres on immediate hunger, memory of insult, and direct predation. It recognizes individuals, remembers trespass, tests weakness before bargaining, and treats the lair as an extension of ownership. Any apparent negotiation should be read as a dominance exchange unless repeated evidence proves otherwise.
c.traitsText: Useful traits for White Dragon include the six-limbed skeleton, armoured overlapping scales, crushing bite, taloned forefeet, tail sweep, darkvision, keen smell, high reasoning ability, and immunity or strong resistance to cold.
