c.eco: They hold territories as custodians. A metallic lair often preserves records, water, seed stores, oaths, rescued creatures, or negotiated boundaries; even when dangerous, it is usually managed rather than merely dominated.
c.beh: Metallic behavior is conversational, testing, and covenant-minded. They may deceive through shapechanging, but usually to examine character, enforce bargains, or protect a charge rather than to indulge predatory ambush.
c.traitsText: Class traits include six-limbed anatomy, high intelligence, reflective armor, strong memory, elemental resistance, language aptitude, and a tendency to form long relationships with mortal societies.
c.abilities: Metallics possess dual breath modes, innate magic, lair protection, and frequent polymorphic ability. Their powers are not interchangeable with chromatic conquest breaths or gem psionics.
c.bg: Metallic classification matters in the Gorge because a metallic dragon may be a patron, judge, archive-keeper, or hidden treaty power, not merely a monster occupying a cavern.

c.g: Wyrms are the axial draconic phylum: long-bodied relatives of dragons that specialize in pressure, burrowing, water, storm, coil, or terrain control rather than the six-limbed flight and spell-sovereignty of true dragons.
c.tax: Class under Dragons containing the inferred protowyrm species and the true wyrm, lung, and lindworm classes. Wyrms are closer to primodragon body ancestry than to chromatic dragons and should not be nested under chromatic lines.
c.ap: The common wyrm silhouette is long, low, and axial: elongated trunk, heavy skull, powerful tail, reduced or bracing limbs, and no independent powered wing pair. Species may add horns, fins, whiskers, manes, scutes, or sensory ridges.
c.eco: Wyrms dominate routes rather than skies. They occupy tunnels, rivers, fault lines, marsh roads, cloud channels, or root systems and often become hazards of place as much as animals.
c.beh: Wyrm behavior is patient, territorial, and site-bound. They wait, listen, coil, sleep, and remember pressure or water patterns across spans that make ordinary patrol behavior look frantic.
c.traitsText: Phylum traits include elongated axial musculature, strong bite, ground or water adaptation, tremor or pressure sense, mineralized or slick armor, and branch-specific resistance to heat, cold, drowning, crushing, venom, or weather.
c.abilities: Wyrm abilities are environmental: collapse, constriction, flood, fog, scalding vapor, tremor, venom, weather-riding, or pressure surge. They should not automatically receive true-dragon spellcasting or wing flight.
c.bg: The corrected wyrm phylum solves the earlier problem of deep wyrms being treated as chromatic dragons. Their ancestry is draconic, but their body plan is their own.

c.g: Protowyrm is the inferred species at the base of the wyrm phylum, representing the first draconic line to specialize into long-bodied, ground-bound, pressure-sensitive forms.
c.tax: Inferred species directly subordinate to Wyrms. It is the common reconstructed source for true wyrms, lung dragons, and lindworms, not a chromatic dragon ancestor.
c.ap: Reconstructed as a long-bodied draconic animal with a deep skull, heavy axial muscles, short stabilizing limbs, armored scutes, and no independent powered wings. The body reads as a tunnel predator rather than a flyer.
c.eco: Protowyrm ecology was probably burrow, riverbank, or fissure-based. It persisted by sensing vibration, guarding long territories, and taking large prey through ambush rather than aerial pursuit.
c.beh: Behavior is reconstructed as stillness, sudden force, and route memory. It likely recognized paths and pressure changes more than social rank or treasure value.
c.traitsText: Traits include elongation, tremor sense, pressure tolerance, mineralized scales, short bracing limbs, and a bite adapted to gripping prey in confined spaces.
c.abilities: Abilities include primitive tremor pulse, constrictive pressure, environmental tolerance, and chemical or thermal exhalation at a level below later true wyrm specialization.
c.bg: This inferred species keeps wyrms close to primodragon ancestry while avoiding the false implication that they descended from chromatic or metallic true dragons.

c.g: True Wyrms are the heavy axial class of the wyrm phylum: long-bodied draconic predators whose power lies in mass, pressure, burrowing, heat, water, stone, or vapor rather than sovereign flight.
c.tax: Order within Wyrms. True wyrms differ from lung dragons by lacking the weather-serpentine ritual body, and from lindworms by retaining more massive bracing limbs and route-scale subterranean ecology.
c.ap: A true wyrm has an elongated trunk, long tail, deep rib column, heavy skull, and short but powerful bracing limbs. It has no separate powered wing pair; any fin, ridge, or membrane is used for steering, display, pressure sensing, or heat shedding.
c.eco: True wyrms occupy tunnels, river throats, magma margins, mineral faults, and ancient lowland routes. They sleep for long intervals, then feed in enormous episodes that alter migration and traffic patterns.
c.beh: Their behavior is patient, geological, and ambush-oriented. A true wyrm may ignore a settlement for years and then treat an entire passage, bridge, or watercourse as prey territory.
c.traitsText: Traits include axial mass, pressure sense, tremor perception, mineralized scutes, powerful bite, bracing claws, and resistance to crushing, heat, cold, or saturation depending on lineage.
c.abilities: True wyrms express draconic power through tunnel collapse, mineral gas, scalding vapor, stone pressure, tremor pulses, or engulfing motion rather than true-dragon spellcraft.
c.bg: True wyrms explain the draconic hazards of the deep that are too immense and ancient to be drakes but structurally wrong for true dragons.

c.g: Deep Wyrms are the Gorge-present subterranean branch of Wyrms: long-bodied draconic organisms adapted to pressure, mineral heat, crystal resonance, fog chemistry, and old tunnel systems. They are not chromatic dragons, not drakes, and not merely wingless true dragons; they are an axial wyrm radiation whose closest parent is the Wyrm line beneath Protowyrm.
c.tax: Wyrm phylum defined by lower-Throat habitat, elongated axial anatomy, four short bracing limbs in most confirmed forms, reduced or absent flight structures, mineralized scale armour, and breath or resonance organs tied to stone, heat, fog, crystal, or pressure. Deep Wyrms are nested inside Wyrms and ultimately Protowyrm, not inside the chromatic or metallic true-dragon orders.
c.ap: A Deep Wyrm presents as a living tunnel-engine: long skull, broad jaw hinges, extended ribcage, low bracing limbs, heavy tail, armored belly plates, and scales infiltrated by ore or crystal. Specimens may carry shoulder sails, heat fins, or resonant membranes, but these are not the separate powered wing pair of true dragons. Bones should show reinforced axial ribs and limb girdles rather than true-dragon wing phalanges.

c.g: Magma Wyrms are deep wyrms specialized for magma heat, sulphur gas, and ore-vein pressure. They are not chromatic dragon families; their ancestry is basal draconic and their body plan follows the Protodragon-derived wyrm line rather than the winged true-dragon colour orders.
c.tax: Deep-wyrm family defined by magma heat, sulphur gas, and ore-vein pressure; long subterranean body; reduced or absent flight membranes; mineralized scale armour; and breath chemistry or resonance specific to the family habitat.
c.ap: Magma Wyrms have elongated draconic bodies with four short bracing limbs, heavy tails, extended ribcages, and no separate powered wing pair. Diagnostic marks include furnace sacs, basaltic scales, heat pits along the jaw, short bracing legs, and shoulder heat-sails. Fossil or shed material should be checked for axial length, mineral deposition, and rib resonance rather than true-dragon wing phalanges.

c.g: The Nadir Wyrm. Confirmed dragon of the Throat, in the deepest reaches of the Untamed. It has been there, by Stonesong record, longer than the Works have existed. The grey-folk consensus is that it knows they are there and has decided they are not interesting yet. The grey-folk very much hope they remain not interesting.
c.tax: Magma Wyrm species of the Nadir margin; ore-rock grey scales; blue-hot sulphur and mineral-gas exhalation; basal deep-wyrm body plan; no evidence of true-dragon wing flight or chromatic ancestry.
c.ap: The Nadir Wyrm is an immense basal draconic wyrm, not a flying chromatic dragon. It has a long tunnel-filling trunk, four columnar bracing legs set low on the body, a crocodilian horned skull, furnace-expanded throat and sternum, ore-rock scales, heat-sail folds along the shoulders, and a tail thick enough to block a passage. The skeleton should show extended ribs and reinforced limb girdles, but not a separate powered wing pair.
c.note: Phylogenetic placement debated. Stonesong records suggest residence predating the Works by centuries. The Forgemasters have analyzed its breath weapon and declined to publish.
c.eco: The Nadir Wyrm occupies geothermal throat passages where sulphur gas, ore veins, and radiant heat can sustain its slow metabolism. It likely feeds rarely on megafauna, mineral-rich carrion, and magical heat sources, then returns to long intervals of furnace-still rest. Reproduction is unconfirmed; if eggs exist, they would need extreme heat and mineral saturation.
c.beh: The Nadir Wyrm behaves like a territorial geological intelligence. It ignores small traffic until vibration, heat theft, mining, or repeated trespass crosses a threshold, then responds with route-scale violence rather than pursuit. Grey-folk survival depends less on hiding from its eyes than on never becoming interesting to its senses.
c.traitsText: Traits include immense size, ore-mineral scales, heat immunity or near-immunity, tremor and heat sense, crushing jaws, bracing limbs, slow metabolism, and a furnace chest. Its shoulder sails are heat and pressure structures, not flight wings, and should not be used to place it among chromatic true dragons.
c.abilities: The Nadir Wyrm exhales blue-hot sulphurous mineral gas, heats stone by proximity, senses vibration through ore veins, and can brace its body against tunnel walls to crush or seal a route. Any magic it possesses is geothermal and mineral, not red-dragon fire sorcery.
c.bg: In the Throat, the Nadir Wyrm is a reason the deepest routes remain political, religious, and engineering problems rather than simple maps. Its existence marks the Nadir as occupied by an organism older than the Works and not governed by chromatic dragon assumptions.

c.eco: Magma Wyrms lair in deep structures where magma heat, sulphur gas, and ore-vein pressure can be used for shelter, sensing, and defence. Feeding is infrequent and heavy: large prey, mineral-rich carrion, geothermal microbial mats, or magical residue may all matter depending on specimen, but ordinary surface-dragon hunting cycles should not be assumed.
c.beh: Magma Wyrms respond first to pressure, heat, vibration, and trespass. Some are sapient enough to recognize routes or bargains, but their habits are geological in timescale: sleeping across passages, waking during fault movement, or treating a relay chamber as a body part.
c.traitsText: Traits include deep pressure tolerance, slow metabolism, mineralized scales, vibration sense, powerful bracing limbs, crushing jaws, and family-specific breath or resonance. The absence of true wings is diagnostic and should not be explained as recent limb loss without evidence.
c.abilities: Magma Wyrms use family-specific exhalation or resonance linked to magma heat, sulphur gas, and ore-vein pressure, along with crushing bite, body constriction against tunnel walls, tremor sensing, and stasis-like endurance. They do not inherit chromatic colour psychology or metallic polymorphing.
c.bg: In the Throat, Magma Wyrms are mapped as living geological hazards. A route may be safe for generations and become fatal only when heat, sound, or excavation reaches the wyrm's threshold.

c.g: Crystal Wyrms are deep wyrms specialized for crystal resonance, brittle light, and mineral harmonics. They are not chromatic dragon families; their ancestry is basal draconic and their body plan follows the Protodragon-derived wyrm line rather than the winged true-dragon colour orders.
c.tax: Deep-wyrm family defined by crystal resonance, brittle light, and mineral harmonics; long subterranean body; reduced or absent flight membranes; mineralized scale armour; and breath chemistry or resonance specific to the family habitat.
c.ap: Crystal Wyrms have elongated draconic bodies with four short bracing limbs, heavy tails, extended ribcages, and no separate powered wing pair. Diagnostic marks include faceted scale plates, resonating ribs, chisel-like claws, prism growths along the spine, and a skull that rings under Stonesong contact. Fossil or shed material should be checked for axial length, mineral deposition, and rib resonance rather than true-dragon wing phalanges.

c.g: The Deep Crystal Wyrm — confirmed single individual in the deepest crystal gallery of the Reaching. Known to the Stonesong Heralds for two hundred years and never once directly observed; all data is resonance-record. The Heralds' primary concern is that the Stonesong relay node installed adjacent to its territory eighteen years ago has begun producing an additional sub-harmonic that was not in the installation spec. The sub-harmonic carries structured information content by frequency analysis. The Heralds have not told the node's maintenance team what this means.
c.tax: Crystal Wyrm species known from Stonesong resonance in the Reaching; basal deep-wyrm anatomy; no confirmed visual observation; distinguished by persistent harmonic signature rather than true-dragon colour or wing marks.
c.ap: The Deep Crystal Wyrm is reconstructed as a long-bodied basal wyrm with four climbing/bracing limbs, faceted crystal armour, an elongated resonating ribcage, prism growths along the spine, and a wedge skull built to transmit vibration. It should not be drawn with full true-dragon wings; any lateral membranes would be tension surfaces for resonance, not flight.
c.eco: It inhabits a deep crystal gallery where sound, light, and mineral growth carry information. Feeding may involve mineral-rich prey, crystal-associated organisms, or magical resonance rather than ordinary hunting. Its life stages are unknown, but two centuries of stable signature imply very slow metabolism and strong site fidelity.
c.beh: The specimen appears to communicate or at least respond through sub-harmonics in the Stonesong relay. It has not presented itself visually, which may indicate caution, immense age, or that direct sight is irrelevant to its sensory world. Disturbing the relay may be interpreted as touching the body.
c.traitsText: Traits include crystal-armoured scales, resonance-sensitive ribs, vibration sense, slow metabolism, climbing claws, and an ability to hide as mineral architecture. Bones or casts should show rib and skull adaptations for sound transmission rather than the wing shoulder of true dragons.
c.abilities: The Deep Crystal Wyrm produces resonance pulses capable of travelling through crystal, distorting Stonesong signals, and possibly cracking brittle stone. Its breath or exhalation is best treated as harmonic pressure and mineral dust, not chromatic elemental breath.
c.bg: In the Throat record, this wyrm matters because it may be using the Herald relay network or being used by it. The taxonomic correction matters: it is a deep communication hazard, not a chromatic dragon hiding in a cave.

c.eco: Crystal Wyrms lair in deep structures where crystal resonance, brittle light, and mineral harmonics can be used for shelter, sensing, and defence. Feeding is infrequent and heavy: large prey, mineral-rich carrion, geothermal microbial mats, or magical residue may all matter depending on specimen, but ordinary surface-dragon hunting cycles should not be assumed.
c.beh: Crystal Wyrms respond first to pressure, heat, vibration, and trespass. Some are sapient enough to recognize routes or bargains, but their habits are geological in timescale: sleeping across passages, waking during fault movement, or treating a relay chamber as a body part.
c.traitsText: Traits include deep pressure tolerance, slow metabolism, mineralized scales, vibration sense, powerful bracing limbs, crushing jaws, and family-specific breath or resonance. The absence of true wings is diagnostic and should not be explained as recent limb loss without evidence.
c.abilities: Crystal Wyrms use family-specific exhalation or resonance linked to crystal resonance, brittle light, and mineral harmonics, along with crushing bite, body constriction against tunnel walls, tremor sensing, and stasis-like endurance. They do not inherit chromatic colour psychology or metallic polymorphing.
c.bg: In the Throat, Crystal Wyrms are mapped as living geological hazards. A route may be safe for generations and become fatal only when heat, sound, or excavation reaches the wyrm's threshold.

c.g: Fog Wyrms are deep wyrms specialized for mineral fog, electrochemical gradients, and damp acoustic cover. They are not chromatic dragon families; their ancestry is basal draconic and their body plan follows the Protodragon-derived wyrm line rather than the winged true-dragon colour orders.
c.tax: Deep-wyrm family defined by mineral fog, electrochemical gradients, and damp acoustic cover; long subterranean body; reduced or absent flight membranes; mineralized scale armour; and breath chemistry or resonance specific to the family habitat.
c.ap: Fog Wyrms have elongated draconic bodies with four short bracing limbs, heavy tails, extended ribcages, and no separate powered wing pair. Diagnostic marks include translucent grey scales, broad sensory whiskers or tendrils, low heat signature, paddle-like bracing limbs, and nostrils adapted to tasting fog. Fossil or shed material should be checked for axial length, mineral deposition, and rib resonance rather than true-dragon wing phalanges.

c.g: The Murk Wyrm — the secondary signature in the Beast-Bonded's Leviathan tracking data. Whether it is following the Leviathan, accompanying it, or simply occupying the same deep Murk passages for unrelated reasons, the Beast-Bonded have chosen not to investigate. Their standing instruction to junior members is: 'there are two things in the deep Murk, and you have found the smaller one. Be grateful.'
c.tax: Fog Wyrm species of the Murk; basal deep-wyrm branch; identified by electrochemical secondary signature in Leviathan tracking data; no placement within chromatic dragons supported.
c.ap: The Murk Wyrm is likely long and low, with four paddle-bracing limbs, translucent grey mineral scales, broad sensory tendrils around the jaws, reduced eyes, and a tail flattened for steering through dense wet fog. It lacks the separate shoulder wings of true dragons; in the Murk, flight would be useless compared with pressure-sensing and silent wall-bracing movement.
c.eco: It occupies wet mineral fog where ordinary sight and heat cues fail. Its feeding probably involves ambush through electrochemical gradients: stunned fish, Murk megafauna, lost travellers, or carcasses located by charge and pressure. Brooding is unconfirmed and may occur in sealed fog pockets or submerged ledges.
c.beh: The Murk Wyrm appears in tracking records as a secondary signature, suggesting it shadows the Leviathan, follows the same prey corridors, or uses the larger creature's passage to mask its own movement. Its behaviour should be treated as deliberate but not necessarily social.
c.traitsText: Traits include low heat signature, electrochemical sense, fog tasting, pressure hearing, translucent mineralized scales, bracing limbs, and corrosion-resistant tissues. These are Murk adaptations, not chromatic colour traits.
c.abilities: The Murk Wyrm exhales choking mineral fog or corrosive vapour, disrupts electric and acoustic tracking, and moves silently by bracing against walls and waterlogged stone. Its danger is disappearance and proximity, not aerial dragon assault.
c.bg: For Throat navigation, the Murk Wyrm turns Leviathan routes into double hazards. Beast-Bonded records must distinguish primary displacement from this secondary electrochemical hunter.

c.eco: Fog Wyrms lair in deep structures where mineral fog, electrochemical gradients, and damp acoustic cover can be used for shelter, sensing, and defence. Feeding is infrequent and heavy: large prey, mineral-rich carrion, geothermal microbial mats, or magical residue may all matter depending on specimen, but ordinary surface-dragon hunting cycles should not be assumed.
c.beh: Fog Wyrms respond first to pressure, heat, vibration, and trespass. Some are sapient enough to recognize routes or bargains, but their habits are geological in timescale: sleeping across passages, waking during fault movement, or treating a relay chamber as a body part.
c.traitsText: Traits include deep pressure tolerance, slow metabolism, mineralized scales, vibration sense, powerful bracing limbs, crushing jaws, and family-specific breath or resonance. The absence of true wings is diagnostic and should not be explained as recent limb loss without evidence.
c.abilities: Fog Wyrms use family-specific exhalation or resonance linked to mineral fog, electrochemical gradients, and damp acoustic cover, along with crushing bite, body constriction against tunnel walls, tremor sensing, and stasis-like endurance. They do not inherit chromatic colour psychology or metallic polymorphing.
c.bg: In the Throat, Fog Wyrms are mapped as living geological hazards. A route may be safe for generations and become fatal only when heat, sound, or excavation reaches the wyrm's threshold.

c.g: Stone Wyrms are deep wyrms specialized for stone pressure, seismic resonance, and geological stasis. They are not chromatic dragon families; their ancestry is basal draconic and their body plan follows the Protodragon-derived wyrm line rather than the winged true-dragon colour orders.
c.tax: Deep-wyrm family defined by stone pressure, seismic resonance, and geological stasis; long subterranean body; reduced or absent flight membranes; mineralized scale armour; and breath chemistry or resonance specific to the family habitat.
c.ap: Stone Wyrms have elongated draconic bodies with four short bracing limbs, heavy tails, extended ribcages, and no separate powered wing pair. Diagnostic marks include sediment-cemented scales, pillar-like ribs, buried limbs, sealed eyes, and a skull almost indistinguishable from carved passage stone. Fossil or shed material should be checked for axial length, mineral deposition, and rib resonance rather than true-dragon wing phalanges.

c.g: One of two confirmed Stone Wyrm specimens in the Heralds' institutional record, occupying the eastern wall of Relay Passage 7, Tier II — a 14-metre section of passage wall that has been the highest-fidelity signal segment in the entire Stonesong network for the full length of recorded relay history. The Heralds know what it is. The relay-maintenance Haulwrights do not. The Heralds have been not telling the Haulwrights for six generations, because explaining it would require explaining the other relay node, which is also a Stone Wyrm, which would require explaining why they have been routing Stonesong signals through two sleeping ancient dragons for two centuries, which is a conversation the Heralds' institutional culture has collectively decided it is not ready to have. The Saxum lapis specimen has been in confirmed stasis for the entirety of Heralds records, and by Stonesong resonance analysis, for approximately two to three million years prior. The thermal signature of its surrounding stone has not changed by any measurable amount in six generations of monitoring. The relay signal it conducts has never degraded, never fluctuated, and never produced any output that could be interpreted as a response to the signal passing through it. Twice in the record a Herald has included, in their monitoring log, the notation 'it is dreaming something' without elaboration. Both entries were subsequently struck through by the senior archivist of their respective eras. The original text remains legible.
c.tax: Stone Wyrm of confirmed relay-integration; Relay Passage 7, Tier II; thermal anomaly stable over six generations of measurement; Stonesong conduction of maximum fidelity; stasis duration estimated 2–3 million years by resonance-depth analysis. Distinguished from the second confirmed specimen (see Saxum eternum) by passage-location and by the fact that the Haulwrights' maintenance schedule has never required work on its segment, which the Haulwrights attribute to good stone quality and the Heralds attribute to something else entirely.
c.ap: The First Relay is a Stone Wyrm body incorporated into passage architecture: elongated trunk sealed into wall or ceiling, ribs fused with host stone, four limbs buried as buttresses, head and jaw lines visible only as impossible symmetry, and no functional true-dragon wing pair. What looks like vaulting or carved support may be mineralized scale and bone.
c.eco: The First Relay survives in stone-stasis, drawing little or no ordinary food while conducting heat, pressure, and Stonesong resonance through its mineralized body. Its lifecycle is measured by geological contact rather than observed breeding; whether it can wake, reproduce, or die naturally remains a dangerous open question.
c.beh: The First Relay behaves, if the word applies, through resonance response. It does not patrol or hoard like a true dragon; it conducts, dampens, amplifies, and possibly listens. Work crews interacting with the relay may be touching a sleeping organism rather than a structure.
c.traitsText: Traits include stone-fused scales, extreme stasis, vibration conduction, pressure tolerance, hidden bracing limbs, and skeletal indistinguishability from architecture. The absence of wing phalanges is important evidence for deep-wyrm placement rather than chromatic true-dragon ancestry.
c.abilities: The First Relay can conduct Stonesong with abnormal fidelity, store tremor information, resist excavation, and perhaps wake through seismic or magical thresholds. Its power is relay-scale stone resonance, not ordinary breath combat.
c.bg: In the Throat record, The First Relay is both infrastructure and organism. The Heralds' network may depend on the body of a living basal wyrm, making maintenance, ethics, and emergency procedures inseparable.

c.g: The second confirmed Stone Wyrm specimen in the Heralds' records — occupying the ceiling vault of Relay Chamber 3, Tier III, which was incorporated into the Stonesong network not because the Heralds chose the location for engineering reasons, but because the resonance anomaly was already there when they surveyed the passage. The supervising Herald of that survey made a note in the margin of the survey record: 'the anomaly is approximately 20 metres long, oriented east-west, and warm. Do not tell anyone.' The survey record was classified. The relay chamber was built around it. The classification has been maintained ever since. Saxum eternum is estimated to have been in its current location for longer than Saxum lapis — the Stonesong resonance-depth signature suggests 4–5 million years, which would predate the formation of the geological layer it currently occupies, implying either that the resonance dating methodology is flawed or that the Stone Wyrm has been here longer than the rock it is in. The Heralds have not resolved this. They have added it to the list of things they are not resolving.
c.tax: Stone Wyrm of maximum confirmed age-estimate; Relay Chamber 3, Tier III; stasis duration estimated 4–5 million years by resonance-depth analysis, with the complication that this exceeds the estimated geological age of the host formation; Stonesong conduction as per family description; 20-metre body-length estimate from thermal-anomaly mapping. The oldest confirmed living Dragon in the Throat and possibly in the material plane. Possibly.
c.ap: The Vault is a Stone Wyrm body incorporated into passage architecture: elongated trunk sealed into wall or ceiling, ribs fused with host stone, four limbs buried as buttresses, head and jaw lines visible only as impossible symmetry, and no functional true-dragon wing pair. What looks like vaulting or carved support may be mineralized scale and bone.
c.note: The survey record that first documented this specimen is classified at the highest Heralds archive tier. The supervising Herald's personal diary, donated to the archive after their death, contains a single relevant entry: 'I put my hand flat against the ceiling of Chamber 3 this morning, where it is warm. I held it there for a long time. I do not know if it is aware of me. I do not know which answer I am hoping for.'
c.eco: The Vault survives in stone-stasis, drawing little or no ordinary food while conducting heat, pressure, and Stonesong resonance through its mineralized body. Its lifecycle is measured by geological contact rather than observed breeding; whether it can wake, reproduce, or die naturally remains a dangerous open question.
c.beh: The Vault behaves, if the word applies, through resonance response. It does not patrol or hoard like a true dragon; it conducts, dampens, amplifies, and possibly listens. Work crews interacting with the relay may be touching a sleeping organism rather than a structure.
c.traitsText: Traits include stone-fused scales, extreme stasis, vibration conduction, pressure tolerance, hidden bracing limbs, and skeletal indistinguishability from architecture. The absence of wing phalanges is important evidence for deep-wyrm placement rather than chromatic true-dragon ancestry.
c.abilities: The Vault can conduct Stonesong with abnormal fidelity, store tremor information, resist excavation, and perhaps wake through seismic or magical thresholds. Its power is relay-scale stone resonance, not ordinary breath combat.
c.bg: In the Throat record, The Vault is both infrastructure and organism. The Heralds' network may depend on the body of a living basal wyrm, making maintenance, ethics, and emergency procedures inseparable.

c.eco: Stone Wyrms lair in deep structures where stone pressure, seismic resonance, and geological stasis can be used for shelter, sensing, and defence. Feeding is infrequent and heavy: large prey, mineral-rich carrion, geothermal microbial mats, or magical residue may all matter depending on specimen, but ordinary surface-dragon hunting cycles should not be assumed.
c.beh: Stone Wyrms respond first to pressure, heat, vibration, and trespass. Some are sapient enough to recognize routes or bargains, but their habits are geological in timescale: sleeping across passages, waking during fault movement, or treating a relay chamber as a body part.
c.traitsText: Traits include deep pressure tolerance, slow metabolism, mineralized scales, vibration sense, powerful bracing limbs, crushing jaws, and family-specific breath or resonance. The absence of true wings is diagnostic and should not be explained as recent limb loss without evidence.
c.abilities: Stone Wyrms use family-specific exhalation or resonance linked to stone pressure, seismic resonance, and geological stasis, along with crushing bite, body constriction against tunnel walls, tremor sensing, and stasis-like endurance. They do not inherit chromatic colour psychology or metallic polymorphing.
c.bg: In the Throat, Stone Wyrms are mapped as living geological hazards. A route may be safe for generations and become fatal only when heat, sound, or excavation reaches the wyrm's threshold.

c.eco: Deep Wyrms inhabit route-scale ecological niches: magma throats, crystal galleries, mineral fog, relay stone, pressure faults, and chambers where the rock itself carries food, heat, or vibration. Their lifecycles are slow and poorly observed; eggs, brooding chambers, or stasis-molts are inferred from shed scale fields and sealed mineral nests rather than from direct grey-folk observation.
c.beh: Deep Wyrm behavior is usually threshold-based. A specimen may sleep across a passage for decades, ignore harmless traffic, then wake violently when excavation, heat theft, tremor, blood, or spell resonance crosses its tolerance. They are less socially theatrical than true dragons and less merely bestial than drakes; many behave like geological intelligences with animal appetites.
c.traitsText: Deep Wyrm traits include immense axial strength, tunnel-bracing limbs, vibration sense, mineralized scales, slow metabolism, pressure tolerance, crushing jaws, and breath chemistry or resonance tied to the local family. The absence of true wings is diagnostic and should be treated as inherited wyrm anatomy, not as unexplained limb loss from a winged ancestor.
c.abilities: Deep Wyrm abilities arise from the body plan and habitat: mineral-gas exhalation, resonance shock, corrosive fog, seismic pressure, stone-stasis, thermal endurance, route sealing, and tremor reading. These powers are wyrm-specific expressions of Proto-Wyrm inheritance, not chromatic colour magic or metallic covenant magic.
c.bg: Deep Wyrms matter to the Throat because they can be mistaken for geography until they move. They explain passages that breathe, relay walls that answer, fog corridors that hunt, and stone chambers no surveyor should strike with a hammer.

c.g: Lowland Wyrms are the non-Gorge sister radiation to Deep Wyrms within the Wyrm line. They occupy river valleys, marsh shelves, buried ruins, and cold hill country rather than the pressure, heat, and mineral chemistry of the lower Throat.
c.tax: Wyrm family outside the confirmed Gorge record, distinguished by long axial body, reduced or absent powered wings, four low limbs or limb-spurs, heavy skull, and lairs cut through wet soil, peat, ice, or loose stone rather than deep mineral faults.
c.ap: A Lowland Wyrm is long and ground-bound, with a wedge-shaped skull, laterally-set eyes, thick neck, four squat digging limbs, belly scutes, and a tail used for swimming, bracing, or sweeping. It lacks true-dragon wing bones and should be identified by tunnel track, belly drag, paired claw trenches, and shed scale rows.
c.eco: Lowland Wyrms feed more regularly than Deep Wyrms because their habitats produce seasonal prey: deer, cattle, marsh birds, fish runs, carrion, and travellers. They lay clutches in mud-warm banks or ruin cellars and guard the nest by coiling through the access tunnels rather than perching over it like a true dragon.
c.beh: They are territorial ambush predators with enough memory to recognize villages, ferry crossings, or toll routes. A Lowland Wyrm may become a local terror not because it craves tribute, but because roads and herds repeatedly enter the same feeding geometry.
c.traitsText: Traits include amphibious endurance, tunnel sense in soft ground, belly armour, powerful constricting trunk, broad jaws, and resistance to cold, rot, or swamp gas depending on habitat. Their intelligence is usually lower than a true dragon but higher than a crocodile or serpent.
c.abilities: Common abilities include mud-burrowing, drowning coils, venomous or septic bite, marsh-fog exhalation, and the power to collapse wet banks or ruins by bracing and twisting through them. These are physical wyrm abilities, not the spellcasting and hoard-covenant traditions of true dragons.
c.bg: Lowland Wyrms are included for comparison because they show what the Wyrm body plan looks like without the Throat exaggerating it into mineral stasis, crystal resonance, or Nadir heat. They help prevent Deep Wyrms from being misread as degenerate chromatic dragons.

c.g: Lung are the serpentine and weather-bound class of wyrms. They retain draconic ancestry but express it through long flexible bodies, atmospheric command, watercourse guardianship, and ritual movement rather than six-limbed flight.
c.tax: Order within Wyrms. Lung are separated from true wyrms by a lighter serpentine body and from lindworms by stronger weather, river, and cloud associations.
c.ap: A lung has a long antlered or whiskered head, sinuous trunk, reduced or absent walking limbs depending on species, mane or tendril growth, and no batlike true-dragon wing pair. Flight, where present, is supernatural buoyancy or cloud-riding rather than sternum-driven wing flight.
c.eco: Lung ecology follows rivers, rain shafts, mist chimneys, high cisterns, and storm paths. They feed less like ambush beasts and more like territorial spirits embodied in draconic flesh.
c.beh: They are ceremonial, proud, and bound to weather or water etiquette. A lung may punish pollution, oath-breaking, or disruption of a river with the same seriousness a chromatic gives to treasure theft.
c.traitsText: Traits include serpentine flexibility, weather sense, water affinity, whisker or horn sensory organs, and unusual buoyancy or cloud-walking in some species.
c.abilities: Abilities include rain-calling, mist riding, river command, thunder voice, and blessing or withholding water. These are lung traits, not chromatic breath traits.
c.bg: Lung placement under Wyrms keeps serpentine dragons near axial draconic ancestry without forcing them into western true-dragon anatomy.

c.g: Lindworms are the reduced-limb or ground-serpent class of wyrms: draconic predators built for coils, banks, roots, ruins, and low tunnels rather than sovereign flight or storm ceremony.
c.tax: Order within Wyrms. Lindworms are closer to protowyrm body logic than to true dragons: elongated, ground-bound, often with two forelimbs or reduced limbs, and reliant on bite, coil, venom, or constriction.
c.ap: A lindworm presents as a long armored serpent-dragon with a wedge skull, heavy neck, powerful tail, and reduced limb set. It lacks the four-leg/two-wing true-dragon plan and should not be mistaken for a juvenile dragon.
c.eco: Lindworms inhabit banks, marsh margins, root caves, ruins, and old road cuts where a long body can disappear into cover. They take livestock, travelers, fish, and carrion depending on habitat.
c.beh: They are more animal-direct than true dragons: jealous, territorial, ambush-prone, and capable of fixation on a ford, bridge, tree, or hoard-like refuse pile.
c.traitsText: Traits include low posture, strong coil muscles, venom or septic bite in many species, concealed movement, and excellent vibration sense through the jaw and belly scales.
c.abilities: Abilities include constriction, venom, sudden lunging bite, bank collapse, and in some species a curse-like blight around lairs. They do not possess true-dragon flight or class magic by default.
c.bg: Lindworms fill the ecological gap between mythic serpent and draconic monster, explaining many dragon reports that lack wings, foreleg symmetry, or breath anatomy.

c.g: Draconids are the specialized-predator phylum of the dragon kingdom. They share draconic ancestry but not the six-limbed true-dragon body or the long axial wyrm body.
c.tax: Class under Dragons containing the inferred protodraconid species and the wyvern and drake classes. Pseudodragons are retained as a minor draconid class pending later review because they are small winged draconic animals, not true-dragon juveniles.
c.ap: Draconids are identified by draconic heads, scales, claws, and tails combined with non-true-dragon limb plans: wyverns modify forelimbs into wings, drakes remain terrestrial quadrupeds, and small pseudodragons miniaturize the plan.

c.g: Protodraconid is the inferred species at the base of Draconids, representing the branch that reduced or specialized the ancestral draconic body into predatory forms such as wyverns and drakes.
c.tax: Inferred species directly subordinate to the Draconids phylum. It is not Protodragon and not Protowyrm; it anchors the draconid solution to draconic ancestry.
c.ap: Reconstructed as a scaled predatory animal with a strong skull, long tail, four primary limbs, and variable forelimb potential: one descendant line modifies the forelimbs into wings, while another keeps terrestrial running limbs.
c.eco: It likely occupied cliff bases, broken stone, and warm hunting corridors where agility mattered more than huge size or age magic. Egg-laying and den defense are the safest reconstructions.
c.beh: Behavior was probably animal-cunning, territorial, and nest-defensive. It may have shown pack tolerance in rich hunting grounds but not true-dragon diplomacy.
c.traitsText: Traits include scale armor, powerful jaws, claws, tail balance, elemental hardiness, rapid juvenile development compared with true dragons, and strong sensory attention to movement and heat.
c.abilities: Abilities include bite, claw, tail strike, short bursts of speed, climbing or leaping, and primitive elemental resistance. Powered wing-forelimbs and drake ground specialization develop after this point.
c.bg: This inferred species prevents draconids from being described as degenerate true dragons. They are a sister phylum with their own body-plan history.

c.g: Wyvern are the wing-forelimb class of draconids. Their flight surfaces are modified forelimbs, so they do not have the true-dragon arrangement of four legs plus a separate wing pair.
c.tax: Order within Draconids. Wyverns are distinguished by two hind legs, two wing-forelimbs, a long balancing tail, and predatory animal ecology rather than true-dragon speech or age magic.
c.ap: A wyvern stands on two hind legs when grounded and folds its wing-forelimbs like hooked crutches. The skull is narrow, the neck sinuous, and the tail often bears a stinger, blade, or venom structure.
c.eco: Wyverns nest on ledges, chimneys, cliffs, and high cavern shelves. They hunt by dropping, raking, carrying prey, and retreating to inaccessible perches.
c.beh: They are dangerous but not sovereign. Wyvern behavior centers on nest defense, hunger, mate competition, and control of launch points rather than treasure law or diplomacy.
c.traitsText: Traits include wing-forelimbs, two hind legs, hooked wing claws, strong launch muscles, keen sight, and often venom-bearing tail anatomy.
c.abilities: Abilities include diving strike, carry-off, tail venom or impalement, wing buffet, and cliff-launch ambush. They do not possess true-dragon breath by class default.
c.bg: Wyvern placement under Draconids prevents them from being mistaken for lesser true dragons or for wyrms that somehow retained wings.

c.g: Drake is the terrestrial predatory class of draconids. Drakes keep the scaled skull, claws, tail, and elemental hardiness of dragon relatives but express them in a four-limbed ground body.
c.tax: Order within Draconids. Drakes are separated from wyverns by lacking wing-forelimbs and from true dragons by lacking the independent wing pair, age-deep magic, and sovereign speech typical of true dragons.
c.ap: A drake is a four-legged, low-shouldered draconic predator with heavy foreclaws, a wedge or horned skull, a muscular tail, and armored scutes along the back. It runs, climbs, digs, or swims depending on species; it does not fly as a true dragon.

c.g: A terrestrial drake adapted to caves and broken stone slopes.
c.tax: Drake-family draconid with no functional wings, weak mineral-acid breath, and pack hunting behaviour.
c.ap: Cave Drake is a drake, not a true dragon: it has four limbs total, no separate wing pair, a long reptilian head, heavy tail, low shoulders, and a ground-running gait. Its field marks are four powerful limbs, low chest, stone-grey scales, blunt digging claws, vestigial shoulder knobs instead of wings; tracks show paired forefeet and hindfeet only, never wing-finger prints or the launch scars left by true dragons.
c.eco: Cave Drake lives by ambushes by vibration and scent in low galleries, swallowing goats, goblins, and unattended pack animals whole. It breeds as a clutch-laying cave reptile, caches kills near warm or hidden stone, and competes with large beasts rather than with intelligent hoard-keeping dragons.
c.beh: Cave Drake is cunning in the manner of an ambush predator. It memorizes routes, returns to successful kill sites, avoids adult true dragons, and displays with hisses, tail blows, body inflation, and territorial scent rather than speech, negotiation, or hoard politics.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include four-limbed draconid anatomy, strong jaw muscles, armoured scales, scent tracking, heat or dark adaptation, and resistance associated with blind-dark pursuit. The absence of a separate wing skeleton is the quickest skeletal distinction from true dragons.
c.abilities: Cave Drake abilities are physical and instinctive: bite, claw, tail slam, sudden rush, climbing or swimming as anatomy permits, and a limited elemental discharge or environmental tolerance tied to blind-dark pursuit. It has no age-deep sorcery, frightful presence, or true-dragon lair magic unless separately trained by an outside force.
c.bg: In the Throat, Cave Drake matters as a corridor predator. Its lair can block trade or scouting routes, but the evidence is beastlike—drag marks, cached bones, scat, eggshells, and shed scutes—rather than tribute patterns or deliberate draconic warnings.

c.g: A heat-adapted drake of volcanic galleries and forge-waste caverns.
c.tax: Drake-family draconid with heat resistance and weak fire breath, not a juvenile red dragon.
c.ap: Ember Drake is a drake, not a true dragon: it has four limbs total, no separate wing pair, a long reptilian head, heavy tail, low shoulders, and a ground-running gait. Its field marks are four-limbed, barrel-chested draconid with coal-dark back plates, ember vents at the nostrils, and claws heat-glazed at the tips; tracks show paired forefeet and hindfeet only, never wing-finger prints or the launch scars left by true dragons.
c.eco: Ember Drake lives by nests in warm rubble near vents, cooking carrion before feeding and driving rivals away with heat displays. It breeds as a clutch-laying cave reptile, caches kills near warm or hidden stone, and competes with large beasts rather than with intelligent hoard-keeping dragons.
c.beh: Ember Drake is cunning in the manner of an ambush predator. It memorizes routes, returns to successful kill sites, avoids adult true dragons, and displays with hisses, tail blows, body inflation, and territorial scent rather than speech, negotiation, or hoard politics.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include four-limbed draconid anatomy, strong jaw muscles, armoured scales, scent tracking, heat or dark adaptation, and resistance associated with heat and bite. The absence of a separate wing skeleton is the quickest skeletal distinction from true dragons.
c.abilities: Ember Drake abilities are physical and instinctive: bite, claw, tail slam, sudden rush, climbing or swimming as anatomy permits, and a limited elemental discharge or environmental tolerance tied to heat and bite. It has no age-deep sorcery, frightful presence, or true-dragon lair magic unless separately trained by an outside force.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ember Drake matters as a corridor predator. Its lair can block trade or scouting routes, but the evidence is beastlike—drag marks, cached bones, scat, eggshells, and shed scutes—rather than tribute patterns or deliberate draconic warnings.

c.g: A sump and fog-passage drake with amphibious habits.
c.tax: Drake-family draconid with swamp adaptation, weak poison or acid breath, and broad swimming tail.
c.ap: Murk Drake is a drake, not a true dragon: it has four limbs total, no separate wing pair, a long reptilian head, heavy tail, low shoulders, and a ground-running gait. Its field marks are long-bodied four-limbed draconid with webbed feet, lateral tail fin, low eyes, and slime-dark scale plates; tracks show paired forefeet and hindfeet only, never wing-finger prints or the launch scars left by true dragons.
c.eco: Murk Drake lives by waits half-submerged in black fog pools, striking at silhouettes and dragging prey into mineral mud. It breeds as a clutch-laying cave reptile, caches kills near warm or hidden stone, and competes with large beasts rather than with intelligent hoard-keeping dragons.
c.beh: Murk Drake is cunning in the manner of an ambush predator. It memorizes routes, returns to successful kill sites, avoids adult true dragons, and displays with hisses, tail blows, body inflation, and territorial scent rather than speech, negotiation, or hoard politics.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include four-limbed draconid anatomy, strong jaw muscles, armoured scales, scent tracking, heat or dark adaptation, and resistance associated with fog and water-edge hunting. The absence of a separate wing skeleton is the quickest skeletal distinction from true dragons.
c.abilities: Murk Drake abilities are physical and instinctive: bite, claw, tail slam, sudden rush, climbing or swimming as anatomy permits, and a limited elemental discharge or environmental tolerance tied to fog and water-edge hunting. It has no age-deep sorcery, frightful presence, or true-dragon lair magic unless separately trained by an outside force.
c.bg: In the Throat, Murk Drake matters as a corridor predator. Its lair can block trade or scouting routes, but the evidence is beastlike—drag marks, cached bones, scat, eggshells, and shed scutes—rather than tribute patterns or deliberate draconic warnings.

c.eco: Drakes fill large predator niches in caves, lava margins, wetlands, and rubble fields. They nest in dens, guard eggs, and often shape local prey behavior without dominating whole cultures.
c.beh: Drakes are cunning animals or near-sapient predators rather than ancient monarchs. They can learn routes, ambush points, and handlers, but their motives remain food, territory, heat, mates, and nest safety.
c.traitsText: Traits include quadrupedal gait, powerful bite, claw traction, scale armor, branch-specific elemental tolerance, strong scent tracking, and lair fidelity.
c.abilities: Abilities include pounce, crushing bite, claw rake, tail sweep, burrow or climb strength, and in some species minor elemental exhalation or resistance. They do not receive true-dragon spellcasting by ancestry alone.
c.bg: Drakes are common sources of false dragon reports in the Throat. Correct classification tells a traveler to prepare for an animal predator, not a negotiating ancient.

c.g: Pseudodragons are retained as a minor draconid class for small, winged, highly intelligent draconic animals. They are not true-dragon juveniles and not ordinary drakes, but their existing catalogue entries are preserved here for continuity.
c.tax: Order within Draconids, retained from earlier catalogues as a small-bodied draconid branch. It may be revised later if the field model treats pseudodragons as familiar spirits or demi-draconic transformations instead.
c.ap: A pseudodragon has a compact cat-sized to fox-sized draconic body, delicate wings, a long tail, fine scales, and expressive cranial horns. Its proportions are miniature, not juvenile true-dragon proportions.

c.g: The small dragon — cat-sized, surprisingly intelligent, telepathic within 60 m, and equipped with a venomous barbed tail-sting that produces sleep rather than tissue damage. They form familiar-bonds with spellcasters with an enthusiasm that suggests they find the arrangement as useful as the spellcaster does. Whether this is affection or a long-term strategy has not been determined.
c.tax: Draconid of miniature body plan; telepathic capacity; venomous tail-sting (sleep toxin); domesticable through familiar-bond; distinguished from all True Dragons by size, absence of fire breath, and the specific way they expect to be fed treats.
c.ap: 30–60 cm body; dragon body plan miniaturized; barbed tail; large amber eyes; colours ranging through red-brown to green; the tail-sting is prominent and they are not shy about displaying it.
c.eco: Pseudodragon occupies draconid ecology appropriate to its own body plan: nesting dens or ledges, egg clutches, meat-heavy diet, territorial routes, and local predator pressure. It does not generate true-dragon lair dominion unless this specific entry says so.
c.beh: Pseudodragon behaves as its draconid body permits: ambush, den defence, territorial display, pursuit, retreat, and learned route memory. It may be clever, but unless this exact entry says otherwise it does not negotiate or scheme as a true dragon.
c.traitsText: Pseudodragon traits are the anatomical and inherited marks named for this species: limb plan, skull, scales, senses, breath structures, intelligence grade, resistances, and lifespan. Do not use traits from sibling draconic branches as shorthand. Recorded flags: context-only.
c.abilities: Pseudodragon abilities are draconid-specific: natural weapons, movement mode, venom, minor breath, telepathy, or other powers actually named for this family or species. Text indicates: breath, fire, venom, telepath, spell, sleep. True-dragon sorcery is excluded unless explicitly stated.
c.bg: The Throat records Pseudodragon to keep draconic categories operational in the field. It currently functions as context-only. This prevents scouts from confusing true dragons, draconid beasts, and humanoid dragon-kin.

c.eco: They occupy niches of small aerial predator, familiar companion, or magical commensal depending on region. Their nests are hidden in shelves, libraries, warm cracks, or defended camps.
c.beh: Pseudodragons are curious, selective, and socially discriminating. They bond, prank, scout, and warn rather than rule territory like large dragons.
c.traitsText: Traits include small size, flight, fine manipulation by mouth and claws, keen senses, telepathic or empathic contact in many accounts, and a venomous or sleep-inducing tail in some lines.
c.abilities: Abilities include stealth flight, stinging tail, empathic communication, and familiar-like bonding. They do not possess true-dragon age magic or major breath weapons.
c.bg: This class is marked for possible future review, but keeping it under Draconids is safer than filing it with true dragons or fey without stronger evidence.

c.eco: They occupy predator niches rather than sovereign lair kingdoms. Their eggs, dens, flight ledges, and hunting routes matter locally, but they rarely reshape whole cultures unless domesticated, cursed, or unusually numerous.
c.beh: Draconid behavior is immediate and ecological: hunt, nest, guard, display, flee, or bond. Their intelligence varies by class, but their branch should not be given true-dragon politics by default.
c.traitsText: Phylum traits include scale armor, strong jaws, clawed limbs, tail balance, elemental hardiness, and faster maturation than true dragons. Wing structure and sapience vary by class.
c.abilities: Draconid abilities include flight-forelimb strikes, venom, pounce, burrowing, climbing, heat tolerance, minor exhalations, or familiar-like communication depending on class. Major true-dragon breath and spellcasting are absent unless a species states otherwise.
c.bg: Draconids are important to the Throat because they produce many dragon-like encounters without the strategic consequences of a true dragon or the route-scale hazard of a wyrm.

c.eco: Dragons occupy apex or near-apex ecological positions and alter the zones around them through lair chemistry, prey depletion, mineral accumulation, heat, cold, storm pressure, or fear-driven migration by lesser creatures. Their reproduction is egg-based or egg-derived in the living branches, but stem species in the catalogue are reconstructions rather than breeding populations.
c.beh: Dragon behavior should be read branch by branch. True dragons develop sovereign territorial psychology and long memory; wyrms tend toward route-scale ambush, geological torpor, and pressure sensing; draconids show more animal predation, pack behavior, or nest defense. A dragon entry should never borrow behavior from a cousin branch merely because both are draconic.
c.traitsText: Shared kingdom traits include hardened integument, powerful jaws, clawed limbs, long sensory memory, resistance to ordinary environmental extremes, and a tendency for the lair to record the animal’s element or chemistry. Flight, speech, spellcraft, and breath weapons are branch traits, not kingdom guarantees.
c.abilities: At kingdom level the inherited ability is elemental resonance: the body stores, channels, resists, or expresses a powerful environmental principle. True dragons express this as breath and age-deep magic; wyrms express it as pressure, heat, stone, vapor, or water-body hazard; draconids express it through venom, strength, stealth, flight-forelimbs, or localized elemental resistance depending on class.
c.bg: The Throat requires a strict dragon taxonomy because unrelated draconic branches can share scale, heat, hoards, and cave lairs. Misidentifying a wyrm as a chromatic dragon, or a drake as a young true dragon, leads to the wrong assumptions about wings, speech, spellcraft, breath, and safe approach distance.

c.g: Deliberately manufactured beings given programmatic behavior. The oldest known Constructs in the Throat predate the current clan system and no longer have a manufacturer who can answer questions about them. They continue doing what they were made to do with the patience of things that do not get tired, which is what they are.
c.tax: Artificially manufactured; behavior follows a program rather than instinct or learning; no biological metabolism; immunity to biological conditions; does not age; sustained by the animating principle of its construction. Classification as a 'kingdom' is an organizational convenience — Constructs do not evolve; they are made.
c.ap: Black adamantine structural elements with mithril wire inlay; Stonesong-crystal power cells visible as glowing inclusions; no attempt at biological likeness in any grey-folk Construct design.
c.note: Constructs do not have a phylogenetic common ancestor — they are manufactured, not evolved. The 'kingdom' classification is an organizational tool to indicate that Constructs are a distinct ontological category of being, not a shorthand for a monophyletic group.

c.g: Constructs manufactured by the grey-folk — most utilitarian, but the oldest have accumulated enough operational history to behave in ways their makers would not have specified.
c.tax: Constructs animated by Stonesong resonance stored in mithril-crystal power cells; programmatic behavior; occasional behavioral drift in very old units.
c.ap: Black adamantine structural elements with mithril wire inlay; Stonesong-crystal power cells visible as glowing inclusions.

c.g: Constructs built to relay or process Stonesong signals in passages too dangerous for grey-folk. They have been doing their job since before anyone now alive can remember their commissioning.
c.tax: Grey-Wrought of Stonesong-relay function; passage-positioned; minimal behavioral range.
c.ap: 2 m humanoid shape; black adamantine; six sensor-crystals in place of a head, glowing blue when processing; mithril wire inlay visible along arms and torso.

c.g: The Relay Golem. When the Heralds tried to retire the oldest known unit, it continued standing and transmitting on its original frequency, which the replacement unit was forced to accommodate.
c.tax: Stonesong Automaton of relay function; oldest operational unit pre-dates known commissioning records.
c.ap: 2 m; black adamantine; six blue sensor-crystals; mithril inlay; stands with arms slightly extended to maximize signal capture; completely motionless between transmissions.
c.eco: Relay Golem occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.
c.beh: Relay Golem behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Relay Golem traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: Gorge-present. Trait tags: mineral-adapted tissues, electroreception.
c.abilities: Relay Golem abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description. Text indicates: stone.
c.bg: The Throat records Relay Golem for field relevance: distribution, danger, ecological role, fossil meaning, cultural contact, or comparison value. Status: Gorge-present.

c.g: The Haulwork Engine. Every physical component has been replaced at least once across the generations. It is the Ship of Theseus of the Works, and the Haulwrights consider this a matter of theological significance.
c.tax: Stonesong Automaton of loading-mechanism function; all original components replaced over generations; continuity of identity debated.
c.ap: 3 m articulated arm-and-platform shape; multiple repair-generations of components visible as color and material variations; moves efficiently; ignores everything not classified as load or obstacle.
c.eco: Haulwork Engine occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.
c.beh: Haulwork Engine behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Haulwork Engine traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: Gorge-present. Trait tags: mineral-adapted tissues.
c.abilities: Haulwork Engine abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description. Text indicates: stone.
c.bg: The Throat records Haulwork Engine for field relevance: distribution, danger, ecological role, fossil meaning, cultural contact, or comparison value. Status: Gorge-present.

c.beh: Stonesong Automata behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Stonesong Automata traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: Gorge-present. Trait tags: mineral-adapted tissues, electroreception.
c.abilities: Stonesong Automata abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description. Text indicates: stone.
c.bg: The Throat records Stonesong Automata for field relevance: distribution, danger, ecological role, fossil meaning, cultural contact, or comparison value. Status: Gorge-present. Subordinate taxa: Relay Golem, Haulwork Engine.

c.g: Large Constructs of unknown original purpose found in Tier III — too large for relay function, too heavily built for cargo handling, with structural elements suggesting a combat function that no grey-folk Construct doctrine acknowledges manufacturing. The Heralds cannot account for them. The Haulwrights call them the Old Ones and give them a wide berth.
c.tax: Grey-Wrought Constructs of combat-adapted design; larger than any known commissioned model; design generation predates current Construct doctrine. Original manufacturer unknown.
c.ap: 4–5 m tall; black adamantine structure heavier than relay-Construct design; limbs built for impact rather than manipulation; Stonesong-crystal power cells are an older generation than any current design.

c.g: The Old War-Frame — the subset that is still operational. Following a patrol route around a section of Tier III passage that nothing else occupies. The route is approximately 3 kilometers long and it has been walking it, according to Stonesong records, since the relay network was first extended that deep.
c.tax: War-Frame in operational state; patrol-programming still active; route-following behavior; non-hostile to grey-folk unless the patrol route is blocked.
c.ap: 4–5 m; black adamantine; Stonesong-crystal cells glowing a blue slightly different from standard relay-Construct blue — older pigment. Moves at a deliberate walking pace. Does not stop.
c.eco: Old War-Frame occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.
c.beh: Old War-Frame behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Old War-Frame traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: Gorge-present. Trait tags: mineral-adapted tissues, electroreception.
c.abilities: Old War-Frame abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description. Text indicates: stone.
c.bg: The Throat records Old War-Frame for field relevance: distribution, danger, ecological role, fossil meaning, cultural contact, or comparison value. Status: Gorge-present.

c.eco: Inner Primordials occupies the niche implied by its own origin, anatomy, and recorded habitat. For field use, note diet or energy source, reproduction or formation, shelter, lifecycle, and what evidence remains after it passes.
c.beh: Inner Primordials behaviour should be read from its own body and origin: how it moves, feeds, responds to threat, treats kin or rivals, and repeats habits in the field.
c.traitsText: Inner Primordials traits include the size, senses, resistances, movement, intelligence, vulnerabilities, and diagnostic field marks belonging to this entry alone. Recorded flags: fossil or inferred, theorized.
c.abilities: Inner Primordials abilities are the natural, supernatural, magical, psionic, or extraordinary capacities actually supported by its own description. Text indicates: fire, stone.
c.bg: This domain anchors most life encountered in the Throat: its stone, water, fungus, beast, humanoid, dragon, elemental, fey, and unlife phenomena all depend on Inner-Primordial inheritance rather than Outer-Planar alignment or Far-Realm intrusion.
c.note: Promoted from Kingdom to Domain. Elementals, Unlife, Fey, and Proto-Material are intentionally retained beneath it.

c.g: Outer Primordial Life is the domain of alignment-essence and planar personhood. It contains the kingdom-rank branches Fiends, Celestials, and Outsiders, plus the inferred stem species Proto-Outsiders. Bodies in this domain are expressions of moral, metaphysical, or planar condition rather than ordinary material descent.
c.tax: Domain-rank branch for life descended from or constituted by Outer-Primordial dissolution: planar organisms whose anatomy, death, reproduction, and powers are shaped by alignment, soul-contract, radiance, infernal law, abyssal entropy, vestigial outer-plane pressure, or related substrates. It is parallel to Inner Primordial Life, Far-Realm Life, and Mutagenic Transformations.
c.ap: The domain has no single anatomy. Its members may be horned fiends, luminous celestials, animal-headed guardians, shadow-planar predators, or other bodies whose physical form is secondary to planar essence. Individual kingdoms and species must supply the diagnostic anatomy.

c.g: Proto-Outsiders are the inferred stem species of outer-planar life: the first stable beings whose bodies were organized by planar essence rather than by material ecology or inner-elemental pressure.
c.tax: Inferred species directly subordinate to Outer Primordial Life. It is a stem species rather than a domain, despite its ancestral position in the tree.
c.ap: No specimen is recoverable. Reconstructions give it a body less important than its planar signature: a self-bounded presence able to survive outside the material biosphere.
c.eco: It is inferred as the ancestor condition from which alignment-bound fiends, celestials, and other outsider forms diverged.
c.beh: Behavior cannot be reconstructed beyond planar agency: persistence, self-direction, and capacity to cross cosmological boundaries.
c.traitsText: Planar essence, native extraplanar stability, resistance to ordinary environmental death, and the seed of later alignment or planar affinities.
c.abilities: Innate planar transit, resistance to mundane harm, and essence-based self-maintenance are inferred, not observed.
c.bg: The entry prevents Fiends and Celestials from being mistaken for material organisms while also avoiding the old error of making the stem condition a high-rank domain.

c.g: Beings of the Lower Planes for whom evil is ontological — constituted from evil the way stone is constituted from mineral. All share planar-travel capacity, immunity to non-magical fire and poison, and evil alignment as a physical property rather than a moral choice.
c.tax: Kingdom-rank lower-planar origin (Nine Hells, Abyss, Gehenna, or equivalent); evil alignment as constitutive property; immunity to non-magical fire and poison; native planar travel. Distinguished from Aberrations by alignment-essence origin; from Undead by living status.
c.ap: Bipedal to quadrupedal, 1.5–4 m tall; invariably horned, clawed, or scaled; always warm; always dangerous. Surface appearance varies by alignment-subtype but invariably signals threat.

c.g: The theorized common ancestor of all fiend-kind, prior to the divergence into lawful (Baatezu), chaotic (Tanar'ri), and neutral (Yugoloth) evil lines. No specimen. Inferred from the shared alignment-essence and planar-travel capacity across all three phyla.
c.tax: Inferred: evil alignment as constitutive property; planar travel as natural capacity; fire and poison resistance. Precise form unknown.
c.ap: Unknown. Inferred to have been roughly bipedal with overt predatory morphology. The alignment-specific physical traits (Baatezu formality, Tanar'ri violence, Yugoloth adaptability) are post-divergence.
c.eco: Proto-Fiend persists where its lower-planar appetite has purchase: souls, fear, contracts, blood, ruin, corruption, or violence. It does not simply fill an animal niche; it engineers conditions that feed its nature.
c.beh: Proto-Fiend behaviour follows its fiendish specialization: bargaining, predation, temptation, command, ambush, or mercenary service as described by the entry. It recognizes advantage and retreats from losses that do not feed its purpose.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include evil-aligned aura, unnatural heat or cold, horns or claws where present, resistance to mundane harm, planar scent, and the specific body marks named in appearance.
c.abilities: Proto-Fiend abilities may include teleportation, poison, fire, fear, disguise, contract magic, life-drain, or summoning only where the entry or lineage indicates that specialization.
c.bg: In the Throat, Proto-Fiend matters when local suffering or bargains provide a stable foothold, turning social conflict into a biological and metaphysical hazard.

c.g: Devils of the Nine Hells — lawful evil outsiders whose nature is contract, hierarchy, and the long calculation of souls. The most organized fiends.
c.tax: Lawful evil outsider; Nine Hells origin; strict hierarchy; nature is exploitative by constitution. Distinguished from Tanar'ri by formal lawful organization and soul-contract mechanics.
c.ap: Generally bipedal; heavily armed or armored appearance; hot-coloration — reds and blacks. Features convey authority as much as menace: horns, wings, formal bearing.
c.eco: Baatezu persists where its lower-planar appetite has purchase: souls, fear, contracts, blood, ruin, corruption, or violence. It does not simply fill an animal niche; it engineers conditions that feed its nature.
c.beh: Baatezu behaviour follows its fiendish specialization: bargaining, predation, temptation, command, ambush, or mercenary service as described by the entry. It recognizes advantage and retreats from losses that do not feed its purpose.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include evil-aligned aura, unnatural heat or cold, horns or claws where present, resistance to mundane harm, planar scent, and the specific body marks named in appearance.
c.abilities: Baatezu abilities may include teleportation, poison, fire, fear, disguise, contract magic, life-drain, or summoning only where the entry or lineage indicates that specialization.
c.bg: In the Throat, Baatezu matters when local suffering or bargains provide a stable foothold, turning social conflict into a biological and metaphysical hazard.

c.g: Demons of the Abyss — chaotic evil outsiders whose nature is entropy and hunger organized into will. Among the most numerous and varied of fiend-kind. The Shadow-Bound of the Throat are a Tanar'ri-lineage subfamily adapted to the deep.
c.tax: Chaotic evil outsider; Abyss-origin; no formal hierarchy; nature is destructive by constitution rather than policy. Distinguished from Baatezu by the absence of lawful organization.
c.ap: Wildly variable across the Abyss; bipedal to many-limbed; always marked by obvious violence in their morphology. Deep-adapted forms take on geological characteristics.

c.g: Tanar'ri-lineage fiends drawn to the Throat by a resonance in the deep — possibly the sealed entity at the Nadir, possibly the Roil mirroring Abyssal fires. Extended residence has produced geological adaptations: calcified scales, ore-vein skin patterning, blood toxic with dissolved iron.
c.tax: Tanar'ri morphology overlaid with deep-stone mineral deposition in outer tissues. The geological fingerprint — not the alignment subtype — is the classification criterion.
c.ap: Dark stone-grey to iron-black bipeds 2–3 m tall. Horns calcified to stalactite composition. Skin patterned in ore-vein geometry that glows faintly at the joints. Always warm to the touch.

c.g: Shadow-Bound that migrated toward the Nadir — showing the most extreme geological adaptation. They move slowly, grind when they walk, and leave mineral deposits wherever they rest.
c.tax: Shadow-Bound with active mineral deposition sufficient to require grinding locomotion; scales partially or fully replaced by calcite.
c.ap: 3–4 m tall; heavily calcified; every step produces a grinding sound; eyes deep amber; breath superheated mineral-gas. At rest, indistinguishable from a large mineral formation in dim light.

c.g: The Ore-Devil. Mineral deposition has progressed so far it moves like stone architecture finding a gait. Hoards ore specimens compulsively, original planar greed redirected by deep-stone influence. Capable of contract-speech if you can hold its attention from the ore.
c.tax: Nadir-Drawn with calcification progressed to full plate; original scale visible only at joint-gaps.
c.ap: 3 m; plated in calcite over original scale; ore-vein geometry glows red at joints; carries an adamantine pick it found, not forged. When still, appears as a worked stone pillar.
c.eco: Ore-Devil persists where its lower-planar appetite has purchase: souls, fear, contracts, blood, ruin, corruption, or violence. It does not simply fill an animal niche; it engineers conditions that feed its nature.
c.beh: Ore-Devil behaviour follows its fiendish specialization: bargaining, predation, temptation, command, ambush, or mercenary service as described by the entry. It recognizes advantage and retreats from losses that do not feed its purpose.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include evil-aligned aura, unnatural heat or cold, horns or claws where present, resistance to mundane harm, planar scent, and the specific body marks named in appearance.
c.abilities: Ore-Devil abilities may include teleportation, poison, fire, fear, disguise, contract magic, life-drain, or summoning only where the entry or lineage indicates that specialization.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ore-Devil matters when local suffering or bargains provide a stable foothold, turning social conflict into a biological and metaphysical hazard.

c.g: The Deep Devil. Less mineralized than D. saxum, closer to original fiendish nature, more capable of manipulation. Writes contracts in Quick-water-dissolved mineral on cured stone-hide.
c.tax: Nadir-Drawn retaining scale-over-calcite rather than full calcite replacement; mobile and communicative.
c.ap: 2.5 m; iron-grey scale still visible; horns curve inward like mine-arch supports; carries a ledger. Prefers seals to violence, mostly.
c.eco: Deep Devil persists where its lower-planar appetite has purchase: souls, fear, contracts, blood, ruin, corruption, or violence. It does not simply fill an animal niche; it engineers conditions that feed its nature.
c.beh: Deep Devil behaviour follows its fiendish specialization: bargaining, predation, temptation, command, ambush, or mercenary service as described by the entry. It recognizes advantage and retreats from losses that do not feed its purpose.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include evil-aligned aura, unnatural heat or cold, horns or claws where present, resistance to mundane harm, planar scent, and the specific body marks named in appearance.
c.abilities: Deep Devil abilities may include teleportation, poison, fire, fear, disguise, contract magic, life-drain, or summoning only where the entry or lineage indicates that specialization.
c.bg: In the Throat, Deep Devil matters when local suffering or bargains provide a stable foothold, turning social conflict into a biological and metaphysical hazard.

c.g: Shadow-Bound that settled in the Fogreach, feeding on the perpetual war-grief of the four frontier clans. Less geologically adapted; more socially dangerous. Appear neutral, offer navigational help, and gradually tilt every interaction toward renewed conflict.
c.tax: Shadow-Bound without significant mineral deposition; Fogreach-dwelling; feeds on grief-derived emotional resonance.
c.ap: 2 m tall; dark mist-grey; outline blurs at edges as if partly fog; move with unnatural speed in the black fog.

c.g: The Fog-Daemon. Feeds on war-grief. Appears as a tall grey-folk with features slightly too regular. Fog converges on it; the Pall-Brokers have standing orders to report sightings and refuse negotiation.
c.tax: Fog-Fiend in grey-folk disguise; identifiable by fog-convergence behavior and copper-yellow luminous eyes.
c.ap: Appears as a 1.9 m grey-folk until close; features uncanny-regular; copper-yellow luminous eyes; surrounding fog drifts inward toward it.
c.eco: Fog-Daemon persists where its lower-planar appetite has purchase: souls, fear, contracts, blood, ruin, corruption, or violence. It does not simply fill an animal niche; it engineers conditions that feed its nature.
c.beh: Fog-Daemon behaviour follows its fiendish specialization: bargaining, predation, temptation, command, ambush, or mercenary service as described by the entry. It recognizes advantage and retreats from losses that do not feed its purpose.
