c.tax: Class within the Lycanthrope phylum defined by balanced or situational behavior: solitary range defense, opportunistic feeding, mating cycles, and local ecology determine risk more than ideology.
c.ap: Hybrid anatomy is optimized for movement and survival: padded feet, tusks, claws, flexible spines, bristles, or climbing limbs depending on beast-template.
c.eco: Neutral strains maintain ranges, dens, wallows, or hunting loops. They may infect accidentally during territorial fights or feeding, and outbreaks often remain local unless settlement contact is frequent.
c.beh: Pragmatic, wary, reactive, or irritable. They can be bargained with when the host remains lucid, but trigger states replace negotiation with instinct.
c.traitsText: Shared traits include shapeshifting, regeneration, silver vulnerability, strong senses, and template-specific locomotion such as climbing, charging, or mud ambush.
c.abilities: Abilities include beast and hybrid transformation, rapid healing, template attacks, scent or vibration tracking, and curse transmission through deep wounds.
c.bg: Neutral Lycanthropes are common in field reports because they occupy ecological niches rather than moral roles: a route may be safe for months, then lethal during a trigger season.

c.g: Boar-template lycanthropes defined by tusks, rage surges, and route-breaking charges.
c.tax: Genus of Lycanthropes with suid template: heavy skull, tusked lower jaw, bristled hide, short powerful neck, and infectious gore wounds.
c.ap: Hybrid forms are barrel-chested with bristled shoulders, wedge-shaped heads, protruding tusks, and hoof-thickened nails. Their skeletons show reinforced cervical vertebrae and dense frontal bone.
c.eco: They root through fungus beds, stored tubers, carrion pits, and refuse mounds. Territory is marked by churned mud, gouged walls, and broken barriers.
c.beh: They are irritable, direct, and hard to intimidate. A weresuid often answers fear by charging the sound, not by fleeing from it.
c.traitsText: Core traits include charge impact, tusk wounds, stubborn endurance, disease tolerance, regeneration, silver vulnerability, and blood-transmitted curse risk.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, trampling charge, tusk-gore infection, fast healing, and resistance to pain or fear while enraged.
c.bg: In the Throat, weresuids are feared less for stealth than for destroying gates, barricades, and careful passage engineering in a single triggered rush.

c.g: A deep-route boar strain whose bristles darken with iron dust and dried blood.
c.tax: Weresuid subspecies with boar template, heavy tusks, and strong charge reflex.
c.ap: Broad hybrid with black iron-stiff bristles, red-rimmed eyes, tusks that hook upward, and shoulders scarred from ramming doors and stonework.
c.eco: Feeds on roots, fungus beds, carrion, and stored rations. It turns occupied routes into ploughed wreckage while searching for food or enemies.
c.beh: Short-tempered and challenge-driven. It charges light, noise, blood smell, or insult with little delay.
c.traitsText: Large size, tusk gore, barrier-breaking strength, regeneration, silver vulnerability, and rage-triggered pain suppression.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, trampling charge, infectious tusk wounds, and rapid healing from blunt trauma.
c.bg: Iron-Bristle Wereboars are a civil-engineering hazard as much as a predatory one; one carrier can ruin a barricade network.

c.g: A wet-cavern boar strain adapted to muck, reed beds, and flooded side passages.
c.tax: Weresuid subspecies with marsh-boar template, mud concealment, and contaminated gore wounds.
c.ap: Low, heavy hybrid with mud-caked bristles, wide splayed feet, yellow tusks, and nostrils that close while submerged.
c.eco: Wallows in flooded sumps and fungus marshes. Its feeding churns water black, spreads parasites, and hides tracks beneath mud.
c.beh: Ambushes from mud or shallow water, then follows wounded prey by blood scent and splashing vibration.
c.traitsText: Large size, mud camouflage, breath control, tusk gore, regeneration, silver vulnerability, and high infection risk from dirty wounds.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, submerged ambush, gore-charge, blood-scent pursuit through water, and rapid closure of non-silver wounds.
c.bg: Mire-Tusk Wereboars make wet routes unsafe because a victim may escape the first attack only to carry the curse back to a settlement.

c.g: Neutral cat-template lycanthropes shaped by stealth, solitary territory, and ambush rather than pack infection or moral compulsion. Most are self-interested survivors whose danger depends on hunger, intrusion, and whether the host enjoys the hunt.
c.tax: Genus of Neutral Lycanthropes defined by felid templates: retractile claws, flexible spine, padded feet, slit-pupil eyeshine, silent movement, and pounce-oriented hybrid anatomy.
c.ap: Hybrid forms are lean, low-shouldered, and long-tailed with short muzzles, whisker pits, mobile ears, retractile claw sheaths, and a spine capable of deep flexion. Tracks are soft four-pad impressions with claw marks absent unless the creature was sprinting or climbing.
c.eco: Werefelids hunt small livestock, cave birds, fish, bats, and isolated travelers, but many also live as thieves, scouts, or solitary den-holders. Transmission is sporadic because they prefer clean kills or avoidance over uncontrolled mauling.
c.beh: They are private, territorial, curious, and prone to testing boundaries. A werefelid may bargain, stalk, or simply watch for days before deciding whether a trespass is worth violence.
c.traitsText: Traits include silent movement, night vision, climbing, balance, springing strength, retractile claws, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and lower social contagion than canid strains.
c.abilities: They shapeshift, climb nearly sheer rough stone, fall safely from surprising heights, pounce from concealment, and heal ordinary wounds. Their curse spreads through deep claw rakes or bites, but less reliably than werewolf infection.
c.bg: Werefelids explain many ambiguous Throat reports of a person seen in a settlement one day and a large silent cat glimpsed above the same passage the next.

c.g: A black-furred ambush strain adapted to shadowed ledges and abandoned lift-shafts.
c.tax: Species of Werefelid with panther template, climbing ambush, and solitary range defense.
c.ap: Large black hybrid with green eyeshine, long tail, padded feet, retractile claws, and shoulders built for vertical pounces from ledges.
c.eco: Hunts bats, goats, tunnel deer, and lone travelers. It caches kills above floor level where crawlers and oozes cannot easily reach them.
c.beh: Silent, patient, and territorial. It usually retreats from groups but may follow a single marked intruder for hours.
c.traitsText: Large stealth predator, excellent night vision, climbing claws, balance tail, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and solitary infection pattern.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, vertical pounce, silent climbing, dark ambush, and non-silver wound regeneration.
c.bg: Cave Werepanthers matter to route-makers because they turn vertical space into hunting ground; looking along the floor is not enough.

c.g: A broad-headed lion-template strain found near warm vents and old clan halls, where it behaves more like a territorial monarch than a wandering hunter.
c.tax: Species of Werefelid with lion template, pride-like dominance behavior, and resonant roar.
c.ap: Tall hybrid with tawny hide, rust-colored mane, heavy forequarters, broad muzzle, tufted tail, and clawed hands large enough to palm a helmet.
c.eco: Claims warm stone, high platforms, and prey-rich halls. It tolerates followers but not rivals, and its feeding territories often overlap abandoned fortifications.
c.beh: Proud, direct, and status-conscious. It warns with posture and roar before violence, but once challenged it pursues until dominance is settled.
c.traitsText: Large size, roar intimidation, night vision, claws, burst speed, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and strong territorial memory.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, stunning roar in enclosed chambers, clawed pounce, dominance scent-marking, and regeneration from ordinary injuries.
c.bg: Rust-Mane Werelions appear in grey-folk stories as false kings of abandoned halls, which is behaviorally accurate even when the politics are exaggerated.

c.g: Lycanthropic strains whose curse-expression reliably amplifies predation, domination, infestation, cruelty, or deliberate transmission. These are the strains most likely to turn a hidden carrier into the seed of an outbreak.
c.tax: Class within the Lycanthrope phylum defined by harmful curse pressure across hosts. Evil lycanthropy is classified by repeated strain behavior: pack predation, colony spread, coercive infection, sabotage, or enjoyment of the transformed hunt.
c.ap: Hybrid bodies emphasize pursuit, biting, concealment, gnawing, or infectious injury. They often have muzzles or incisors built to deliver curse-bearing wounds efficiently.
c.eco: They spread through packs, nests, ambush territories, drains, war corridors, or social infiltration. Infection is not merely accidental; many carriers use it to make allies, servants, or disposable cover.
c.beh: Predatory, secretive, coercive, and socially dangerous. Some retain charm or ordinary speech specifically to get close enough to infect others.
c.traitsText: Shared traits include strong transmissibility, living metabolism, shapeshifting, regeneration, silver vulnerability, heightened senses, and a tendency to form packs or hidden colonies.
c.abilities: Abilities include forced transformation, infectious bite or claw, pack calls, vermin or predator coordination, stealth entry, and rapid non-silver healing.
c.bg: Evil Lycanthropes are the primary reason settlements treat unknown bite wounds as civic emergencies rather than private medical problems.

c.g: Canid lycanthropes built around pursuit, pack coordination, and bite-transmission. They are the most socially contagious lycanthropes because infected hosts tend to seek or make packs.
c.tax: Genus of Lycanthropes defined by wolf, jackal, or hound templates: elongated muzzle, carnassial teeth, digitigrade hybrid stance, pack vocalization, and high bite-vector reliability.
c.ap: Hybrid forms have long muzzles, triangular ears, heavy neck muscles, clawed but still hand-like forelimbs, digitigrade hind limbs, and a balancing tail. Tracks show a confusing alternation between boot-like heel pressure and four-toed canid pads.
c.eco: Werecanids hunt in circuits, revisit kill sites, and establish scent-marked corridors. Infected hosts often form small packs around the oldest or most controlled carrier, producing social reservoirs rather than solitary outbreaks.
c.beh: They are cooperative, territorial, and pursuit-oriented. A werecanid may retain ordinary speech in hybrid form, but body language shifts toward dominance display, flank movement, and group encirclement.
c.traitsText: Core traits are scent tracking, endurance pursuit, pack signaling, fast wound closure, silver vulnerability, and bite-transmitted curse. They are less massive than bear or boar strains but more efficient at spreading.
c.abilities: They shift between host, wolf/jackal/hound, and hybrid forms; howl to coordinate or panic prey; track by blood-scent through mineral dust; and transmit the strain through deep bite wounds.
c.bg: Werecanids are the form most feared near grey-folk corridors because one hidden carrier can turn a watch patrol into a pack before the next census.

c.g: The common deep-passage werewolf strain, adapted to echoing tunnels and scent trails laid over wet stone.
c.tax: Werecanid subspecies with wolf template, pack contagion, and strong lunar/deep-cycle response.
c.ap: Tall hybrid with grey-black fur, long wolf skull, amber eyeshine, enlarged chest, and forearms long enough for knuckle-assisted sprinting in low passages.
c.eco: Hunts by driving prey toward ledges, choke points, or old doorways. Packs den above warm stone or near livestock caverns where prey returns predictably.
c.beh: Dominance is established by eye contact, throat exposure, and restraint after victory. Newly infected hosts often remember the chase more clearly than the kill.
c.traitsText: Large hybrid, keen smell, pack coordination, climbing scrabble on stone, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and bite transmission.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, blood-scent tracking, fear-howl in enclosed spaces, and regeneration except from silvered or sanctified wounds.
c.bg: The Throat Werewolf is the default lycanthrope concern for inhabited routes because it can pass as a person by day and exploit patrol schedules by night.

c.g: A smaller scavenger-predator strain associated with fogbound battlefields and corpse roads.
c.tax: Werecanid subspecies with jackal template, carrion tolerance, and ambush-scavenger behavior.
c.ap: Lean hybrid with narrow muzzle, large ears, reddish-grey coat, low shoulders, and a laugh-like yipping call that carries strangely through fog.
c.eco: Feeds from kills made by others as often as from its own hunts. It follows armies, feud routes, and plague carts, turning wounded survivors into carriers.
c.beh: Clever, evasive, and opportunistic. Fogreach Werejackals avoid fair fights, harry the exhausted, and retreat into mist or rubble after a single infectious bite.
c.traitsText: Medium hybrid, acute hearing, carrion tolerance, stealth in fog, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and bite transmission.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, corpse-scenting, deceptive pack calls, sudden hamstringing bites, and accelerated healing unless struck with silver.
c.bg: This strain is dangerous in the Fogreach because casualties are common and verification of bodies is difficult.

c.g: Evil-aligned rodent-template lycanthropes organized around infestation, gnawing entry, filth tolerance, and hidden transmission. They are less physically imposing than wolf or bear strains but far more difficult to purge from a settlement. Ratweres are excluded from this genus: a wererat is a humanoid host altered toward rat traits, while a ratwere is a rat-origin inverted therianthrope under Dire Pathways.
c.tax: Genus of Evil Lycanthropes defined by rat, mole-rat, or giant-rodent templates: chisel incisors, narrow skull, flexible ribs, clawed digging hands, disease tolerance, and communal nesting. Ratweres are excluded from this genus: a wererat is a humanoid host altered toward rat traits, while a ratwere is a rat-origin inverted therianthrope under Dire Pathways.
c.ap: Hybrid forms are hunched and narrow with long incisors, whiskered muzzles, hairless or patchy tails, grasping forepaws, and shoulders able to compress through gaps smaller than the torso appears to allow. Gnaw marks on wood, leather, and bone are diagnostic.
c.eco: Wererodents live in drains, food stores, refuse walls, and the warm undersides of settlements. They feed on grain, carrion, leather, fungus, eggs, and the dead, spreading curse and disease through bites that are easy to dismiss as vermin wounds.
c.beh: Secretive, resentful, and opportunistic. They prefer blackmail, hoarding, sabotage, and swarm support over open combat, and often maintain human identities for years while expanding a hidden nest.
c.traitsText: Traits include compression through gaps, gnawing incisors, disease tolerance, smell, rapid breeding among infected hosts, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and high urban contagion risk.
c.abilities: They shapeshift, squeeze through cracks, gnaw locks and supports, command or attract ordinary vermin, infect through bites, and heal non-silver wounds quickly enough to survive purges.
c.bg: Wererodents are an evil lycanthropic class concern because they weaponize settlement infrastructure: food, waste, drains, and trust become parts of the outbreak. Ratweres are excluded from this genus: a wererat is a humanoid host altered toward rat traits, while a ratwere is a rat-origin inverted therianthrope under Dire Pathways.

c.g: A settlement-infiltrating rat strain that nests in culverts, cistern walls, and refuse shafts.
c.tax: Species of Wererodent with rat template, hidden colony behavior, and high bite-transmission reliability. It should not be confused with a ratwere, which begins as a rat-line creature and acquires a humanoid mask rather than beginning as a humanoid cursed toward rat form.
c.ap: Small hunched hybrid with grey-brown fur, naked tail, sharp incisors, pink hands, wet black eyes, and feet that leave five-toed prints beside tail drag marks.
c.eco: Feeds on refuse, grain, corpses, and stolen food. Colonies gnaw false passages through wattle, fungal brick, and old timber supports.
c.beh: Cowardly in the open and ruthless in the dark. It prefers ambush, numbers, traps, and threats against sleeping households.
c.traitsText: Small size, squeeze movement, gnawing, disease tolerance, vermin empathy, rapid healing, silver vulnerability, and infectious bite.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, rat-swarm concealment, lock-gnawing, bite infection, and rapid recovery from ordinary cuts.
c.bg: Drain Wererats are among the most politically dangerous lycanthropes because discovery often reveals that the outbreak has already entered food stores and guard barracks.

c.g: A blind or half-blind burrowing strain from chalky lower tunnels and collapsed mine spoil.
c.tax: Species of Wererodent with mole-rat template, burrowing locomotion, and low-light or no-light adaptation. It should not be confused with a ratwere, which begins as a rat-line creature and acquires a humanoid mask rather than beginning as a humanoid cursed toward rat form.
c.ap: Pale hairless hybrid with tiny eyes, long whiskers, shovel-claws, protruding incisors, and a blunt head carried low to the ground.
c.eco: Tunnels through soft mineral seams, refuse banks, and old grave fill. It stores roots, bones, and shiny metal scraps in chambered nests.
c.beh: Nervous, communal, and vicious when cornered. It attacks ankles and hands first, then retreats through burrows larger pursuers cannot enter.
c.traitsText: Small-to-medium size, tremor sense, digging claws, compression, disease tolerance, regeneration, silver vulnerability, and bite transmission.
c.abilities: Shapeshifting, rapid burrowing in soft stone or fill, tremor tracking, infectious bite, and non-silver wound healing.
c.bg: Pale-Mole Wererats matter in the Throat because they can undermine floors and walls while remaining almost invisible to surface-oriented patrols.

c.g: Inverted Therianthropes are beast-origin lycanthropic inversions: animal lines that acquire humanoid mimicry or humanoid transformation. They belong under the Lycanthropes kingdom because they are part of the same curse ecology, but their direction is reversed: beast first, person-mask second.
c.tax: Phylum under Lycanthropes. Distinguished from ordinary lycanthropes because the stable ancestral host is an animal lineage rather than a humanoid or giant infected with a beast-form curse. Ratweres belong here.
c.ap: Bodies show the animal skeleton beneath the disguise: too-long incisors, digitigrade weight shifts, flexible ribs, whisker pits, tail-base scars, reflective eyes, and hands that seem learned rather than born.
c.eco: They persist through breeding populations of altered animals, contaminated nests, and inherited mimicry rather than ordinary lycanthropic bite chains. Some can transmit disease or lesser dire traits, but not full humanoid lycanthropy.
c.beh: They are secretive, scavenging, opportunistic, and socially parasitic. Their humanoid behavior is often convincing at a distance but fails under scrutiny of manners, scent, food habits, and fear responses.
c.traitsText: Traits include animal-origin scent, mimicry intelligence, disease tolerance, climbing or squeezing ability, and skeletal tells that betray the disguise.
c.abilities: Abilities include humanoid shape assumption, voice imitation, swarm command, plague carrying, sudden reversion to animal gait, and ambush coordination with unaltered kin.
c.bg: This phylum exists to prevent ratweres from being misclassified as wererats while still placing them within lycanthropic taxonomy. A wererat is humanoid-to-rat; a ratwere is rat-to-humanoid.

c.g: Ratweres are inverted rodent therianthropes: rat-origin predators that assume or counterfeit humanoid form to enter stores, camps, guild tunnels, and wounded settlements.
c.tax: Genus under Inverted Therianthropes, Dire Pathways. Ratweres are not Wererodents under Lycanthropes because they are not humanoid hosts carrying a rat curse; their baseline ancestry is ratlike and the humanoid form is the acquired mask.
c.ap: A ratwere in false humanoid form is narrow-shouldered, quick-eyed, and overcareful with its hands. Close inspection reveals forward incisors, whisker pores along the cheeks, a tail-base tuck, digitigrade pauses before running, and claw nails that never sit correctly in gloves.
c.eco: They breed in hidden nests like altered rats, using humanoid disguise to steal food, map pantry routes, spread disease, and select isolated victims. Young first learn scent trails and only later learn the person-mask.
c.beh: Ratweres are cowardly when alone, cruel in numbers, and excellent at exploiting domestic trust. They prefer kitchens, infirmaries, granaries, refuse shafts, and treaty camps where ordinary people explain away odd behavior.
c.traitsText: Traits include small flexible bones, disease tolerance, darkvision, scent memory, swarm affinity, lock-gap squeezing, and a permanent musk that dogs, cave-cats, and trained grey-folk can detect.
c.abilities: Abilities include rat-to-humanoid shifting, voice mimicry, plague bite, swarm command, squeeze escape, and sudden quadrupedal sprinting when the disguise is abandoned.
c.bg: Ratweres matter in the Throat because they can infiltrate clan logistics without triggering the same precautions used for wererats. Their correct placement under Dire Pathways changes how scouts inspect tracks, bedding, grain sacks, and bite marks.

c.g: The common ratwere is the pantry-and-refuse form most often mistaken for a filthy traveller, apprentice, or half-starved tunnel vagrant.
c.tax: Species of Ratwere. It is defined by rat-origin ancestry, stable humanoid mimicry, plague-carrying bite, and social scavenging around settlements.
c.ap: In humanoid guise it is short, wiry, twitch-handed, and narrow-faced; in rat form it is too large for a true rat and carries humanlike shoulder rotation. The skull has enlarged incisors, broad zygomatic arches, and a jaw hinge capable of both gnawing and crude speech.
c.eco: It nests in wall gaps, cistern voids, refuse shafts, and abandoned bedrolls. It eats grain, grease, carrion, stored cheese, candle fat, and the soft parts of unattended corpses.
c.beh: It avoids fair fights, feigns illness, begs convincingly, and attacks sleepers or wounded people. Several common ratweres will take turns maintaining a single humanoid identity if it grants access to food stores.
c.traitsText: Traits include disease resistance, strong smell memory, climbing claws, narrow-rib squeezing, darkvision, and a weak but persistent humanoid vocabulary.
c.abilities: Abilities include humanoid disguise, plague bite, rat-swarm calling, squeeze escape, and voice repetition good enough to imitate simple phrases through a door.
c.bg: In the Throat, common ratweres are logistics predators. They can ruin a clan storehouse or expedition camp before anyone realizes the problem is a transforming animal rather than theft.

c.g: The fogreach ratwere is a pale, damp-adapted ratwere line that uses fog, grief-camps, and war corridors to pass as refugees or corpse-pickers.
c.tax: Species of Ratwere. Distinguished from the common ratwere by pale waterproof fur, fog navigation, stronger corpse-feeding habits, and better mimicry of coughing or wounded speech.
c.ap: Its false humanoid form has wet hair, grey lips, long fingers, and a permanent shiver. Rat form is pale-bellied with translucent ears, flattened claws for slick stone, and eyes that reflect dull green in lantern fog.
c.eco: It dens in collapsed drains, battlefield culverts, wet granaries, and fog-soaked rubble. Diet includes moldy grain, drowned insects, corpse fat, and blood diluted in standing water.
c.beh: It is more patient than the common ratwere, often following a group for days before entering camp as a survivor. It remembers voices heard through fog and repeats pleas at exactly the wrong distance.
c.traitsText: Traits include fog navigation, wet-fur insulation, disease tolerance, strong hearing, low-light eyeshine, and a scent masked by mildew and corpse-water.
c.abilities: Abilities include humanoid disguise, mimicry of coughs and calls, plague bite, fog-silent movement, swarm calling from drains, and rapid escape through flooded cracks.
c.bg: Fogreach ratweres are especially dangerous because their disguise overlaps with genuine refugees and wounded scouts. The correct field test is spoor and scent, not apparent need.

c.g: Lycanthropes are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.tax: Lycanthropes are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.ap: Lycanthropes are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.eco: Lycanthropes are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.

c.abilities: Lycanthropes are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.

c.g: The dead reorganized into active beings: not a living kingdom, but a taxonomic kingdom of post-life forms. Undead are defined by an animating force attached to death, corpse, spirit, shadow, hunger, oath, phylactery, curse, or unfinished identity.
c.tax: Member of Proto-Undead. The kingdom is divided first by operating body: Corporeal Undead retain a material vessel, while Incorporeal Undead act primarily as spirit, shadow, memory, or negative-energy presence. This prevents ghosts from being treated as wraiths and prevents corpse strains from being confused with haunting vectors.
c.ap: Undead appearance depends on whether the body remains. Corporeal forms can be measured by bone, flesh, embalming, crystal, bite, and gait; incorporeal forms are read by silhouette, temperature, light behavior, voice, and the absence of tracks. No single undead body plan should be assumed across both subfamilies.
c.note: Zombies, skeletons, liches, vampires, and similar Undead are each specific CURSE STRAINS applied to living creatures, not phylogenetic lineages. A 'zombie' is not a species — it is a Grey-Folk (or dwarf, or deer) carrying the Zombie Strain curse. The curse ancestor is theorized as the 'Protozombie' from which all zombie-type strains descend. Similarly, lichdom, vampirism, and ghoulification are distinct curse strains. Undead in the Throat (Stone-Walkers, Fog Remnants) appear to be animated by elemental positive-or-neutral energy rather than classic negative energy — their classification as Undead rather than Elemental-Constructs is currently disputed.

c.g: The post-mortal mutagenic pathway: a transmissible metaphysical program that acts on a dead or dying host and causes death to keep operating. It is not an organism and has no body when isolated; it behaves like a curse-virus whose substrate is corpse, spirit, shadow, blood, oath, name, grave, or phylactery.
c.tax: Member of the Mutagenic Transformations lineage. Proto-Undead is not descended from living taxa and should not be placed among beasts, humanoids, aberrations, or planar organisms merely because it uses their remains. It is the root pathway whose strains produce corporeal and incorporeal undead forms by altering the post-life state of a host.
c.ap: Alone it has no anatomy. It becomes visible only through altered host matter: preserved corpse posture, self-moving bones, blood without circulation, a shadow that acts independently, a voice without lungs, a grave that behaves like an organ, or a phylactery that replaces the body as the continuity structure.
c.eco: Proto-Undead spreads through death-events rather than breeding. Battlefields, sealed tombs, oath-sites, plague pits, sacrificial altars, hungry ghosts, and necromantic laboratories are its ecological reservoirs. A living population is not required; any suitable death under sufficient metaphysical pressure can become the next host-event.
c.beh: The pathway has no personality of its own, but it imprints compulsions into its products. Corporeal strains tend to repeat bodily hunger, labor, guarding, or command. Incorporeal strains tend to repeat unfinished obligation, grief, hatred, territorial attachment, fear, or the last logic of the death that produced them.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits are procedural rather than anatomical: host condition, death trigger, retained memory, anchor object, contagion route, daylight response, burial dependence, and release condition. These are the taxonomic characters that separate one undead strain from another.
c.abilities: The proto-pathway authorizes post-mortal abilities but does not perform them unaided. Specific strains produce animation, life drain, paralysis, fear, possession, disease, shadow propagation, phylactery return, or haunting according to the host substrate and the vector involved.
c.bg: In the Throat this pathway explains why the same corpse species can produce several different undead results. Grey-folk, dwarf, lizardfolk, beast, or dragon remains should be classified first by the active death-strain, then by the original host only where that host changes the resulting form.

c.g: Undead whose animating force remains bound to a body, corpse, skeleton, preserved organ system, or draconic carcass. Their threat can be read from anatomy because the dead matter still constrains movement, feeding, durability, and spoor.
c.tax: Phylum of the Undead kingdom containing post-mortal transformations that keep a body, corpse, skeleton, preserved flesh, or bound draconic frame as the operating vessel. It is separated from Incorporeal Undead by the continuing importance of anatomy, remains, and physical containment.
c.ap: A corporeal undead has a silhouette that can be drawn: bipedal corpse, articulated skeleton, embalmed body, crystal-infiltrated cadaver, or preserved spell-bearing remains. Field identification should note joints, skull, hands, bite marks, embalming, mineral growth, eye glow, gait, and whether the body casts an ordinary shadow.
c.eco: They do not reproduce as animals do. New specimens form when a corpse is infected, cursed, deliberately raised, embalmed under necromancy, preserved by mineral saturation, or occupied by a will that refuses dissolution. Their diet may be flesh, blood, life-force, memory, heat, or simply the negative-energy maintenance required to keep dead tissue active.
c.beh: Corporeal undead behave according to the state of the body and the intelligence of the animating will. Skeletons repeat commands with mechanical precision, ghouls hunt as carrion packs, vampires maintain predatory society, liches plan across centuries, and Gorge walkers follow the mineral or fog conditions that made them.
c.traitsText: Shared traits include immunity to ordinary fatigue, poison, disease, suffocation, and morale collapse; vulnerability to body destruction, sanctified force, turning rites, sunlight or fire where specified; and physical evidence such as dragging feet, scraped knuckles, grave-salt, tooth damage, embalming resins, or mineralized marrow.
c.abilities: Corporeal undead express power through the retained body: claws, bites, disease, paralysis, blood drain, command of lesser dead, spell retention, phylactery anchoring, fear aura, corpse persistence, or mineralized resilience. These abilities should not be copied to incorporeal dead unless the specific entry states a shared curse.
c.bg: In the Throat they are practical hazards because bodies are plentiful and retrieval is difficult. Collapsed expeditions, drowned chambers, battlefields in Fogreach, and sealed tombs can all become undead nurseries if the corpse is left where curse, mineral pressure, or necromancy can use it.

c.g: The specific undead phenomena of the Throat — animated by the Living Stone network's elemental energy or by fog-dissolution, rather than by classic negative energy. Their classification as 'Undead' rather than 'Elemental Constructs' is disputed by the Stonesong Heralds.
c.tax: Grey-folk individuals whose death was followed by environmental animation. Animating force is believed to be the Georeticula network (Living Stone) or the fog-medium of the Murk. Whether this constitutes negative-energy undeath or a novel form of elemental animation is unresolved.
c.ap: Stone-Walkers: grey-folk proportions in smooth stone-grey mineral deposition, walking old routes. Fog Remnants: coherent distortions in the fog, speaking in a voice slightly out of sync with their apparent location.
c.note: Classification as Undead is provisional. The Stonesong Heralds maintain an ongoing investigation into whether the animating force of Stone-Walkers is negative energy, Georeticula elemental energy, or a novel third category. Fog Remnants are even less clear — dissolution into the Murk fog may be a form of Fey transition rather than undeath.

c.g: The Stone-Walker. Walks old shift-routes. Repeats functional actions. Occasionally speaks the names of people who died alongside it, in its original voice, which is the worst part.
c.tax: Grey-folk preserved by mineral deposition and animated by Living Stone; walks original shift-routes; speaks fragments of original speech.
c.ap: 1.4–1.7 m; grey-folk proportions; skin smooth stone-grey; features of specific individual preserved but mineral-textured; walks with the gait of its living self; eyes do not track moving objects but orient toward familiar sounds.
c.eco: Stone-Walker ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Stone-Walker behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Stone-Walker include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Stone-Walker abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Stone-Walker is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.g: The Crystal Dead. A Stone-Walker whose mineral replacement continued into full crystal growth. The entire figure is quartz or calcite, translucent in the right light, and rings faintly when the Stonesong passes through it. Stonesong pilots have mistaken it for a relay-tower, briefly.
c.tax: Stone-Walker with full-crystal mineral replacement; Stonesong-resonant; translucent quartz or calcite exterior.
c.ap: 1.4–1.7 m; entirely crystal-composite; translucent white; original grey-folk figure visible as shadowed form inside the crystal exterior; moves with a faint ringing sound.
c.eco: Crystal Dead ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Crystal Dead behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Crystal Dead include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Crystal Dead abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Crystal Dead is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.g: The Fog Remnant. A former Mist-Touched who dissolved into the fog entirely. They navigate; they speak; they remember. They are not alive, but the distinction feels less important the longer you talk to one. The Pall-Brokers conduct the funeral when a Mist-Touched begins to thin, but Fog Remnants do not attend.
c.tax: Mist-Touched grey-folk at full fog-dissolution; retains personality and memory without biological substrate. Source of animation is the Murk fog-medium rather than either positive or negative energy.
c.ap: Visible as a slightly denser patch of fog approximately the shape of a standing grey-folk; voice present but coming from very slightly the wrong direction; fog in their immediate area moves slightly against the ambient current, as if something inside is breathing.
c.eco: Fog Remnant ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Fog Remnant behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Fog Remnant include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Fog Remnant abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Fog Remnant is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.eco: Gorge Undead ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Gorge Undead behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Gorge Undead include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Gorge Undead abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Gorge Undead is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.g: The zombie curse — a magical virus that reanimates a dead creature's body with simple directives (attack living, spread infection). The Protozombie is the theorized first zombie from which all zombie strains descended; the diverse zombie types today (fast zombies, plague zombies, drowned zombies, etc.) are strain variants.
c.tax: Zombie Strain produces a creature with DUAL ANCESTRY: its living lineage ancestor AND the Protozombie curse ancestor. It is simultaneously a Grey-Folk (or whatever the living ancestor was) and an Undead. Classified as a curse vector rather than a species because the 'zombie' is defined by the curse, not by a morphological lineage.
c.ap: Zombie Strain is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Protozombie—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.
c.note: The Protozombie is theorized as the first application of the zombie curse — possibly a deliberate magical experiment, possibly a spontaneous negative-energy phenomenon. All zombie strains (plague, drowned, fast, slow) are variants of this original curse.

c.g: The theorized first zombie — the original application of the zombie curse to a living creature, from which all zombie strains descend. Its living lineage ancestor is unknown. Its existence is inferred from the shared properties of all zombie strains.
c.tax: Inferred: negative energy animation; directives of attack and spread; no language; decay of living form. The zombie curse ancestor.
c.ap: Unknown. Inferred to be the first creature reanimated by negative energy with zombie-strain characteristics.
c.eco: Protozombie ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Protozombie behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Protozombie include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Protozombie abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Protozombie is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.eco: Zombie Strain spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Zombie Strain has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Zombie Strain are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Zombie Strain grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Zombie Strain as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: The voluntary transformation pathway to Lichdom — a Lich is a spellcaster who has bound their soul to a phylactery object, converting themselves to undeath voluntarily. Distinct from Zombie Strain in being deliberate, intelligence-preserving, and power-amplifying.
c.tax: Lichdom produces an Undead creature with DUAL ANCESTRY: its living lineage (spellcaster of any humanoid type) AND the lichdom pathway. Distinguished from zombie strain by: voluntary transformation, intelligence fully preserved, magical power enhanced rather than destroyed.
c.ap: Lichdom is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—the listed undead forms—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.
c.eco: Lichdom spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Lichdom has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Lichdom are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Lichdom grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Lichdom as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: The vampirism curse — passed by bite, creating an intelligent Undead predator that feeds on blood and controls minds. Distinguished from zombie strain by full intelligence preservation and voluntary predation.
c.tax: Vampirism produces an Undead with DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage AND vampirism curse vector. Distinguished by preserved intelligence, blood-feeding ecology, and sunlight vulnerability.
c.ap: Vampirism is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—the listed undead forms—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.
c.eco: Vampirism spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Vampirism has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Vampirism are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Vampirism grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Vampirism as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: The voluntary transformation pathway by which a dragon chooses lichdom, binding their soul to a phylactery gem and converting themselves to undeath. Distinguished from Shadow Dragons by being fully voluntary, intelligence-preserving, and requiring no Shadowfell exposure.
c.tax: Dragon undeath of voluntary type: DUAL ANCESTRY — original Dragon phylum AND undeath-curse vector (specifically Lichdom pathway adapted to draconic physiology). Distinguished from Shadow Dragons by: voluntary transformation, fully preserved intelligence and magic, no planar requirement, phylactery as external soul vessel.
c.ap: Dracolich Pathway is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—the listed undead forms—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.
c.eco: Dracolich Pathway spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Dracolich Pathway has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Dracolich Pathway are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Dracolich Pathway grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Dracolich Pathway as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: Shadow Dragons are listed in the Dragons kingdom under Planar Dragons but are correctly classified here as undeath mutations. They are not planar-adapted dragons but undead dragons produced by Shadowfell immersion combined with negative-energy displacement. See 'Shadow Dragon' under Dragons → Primadraconis → Protodraconis → True Dragons → Planar Dragons.
c.tax: Cross-reference only. Full entry under Dragons kingdom.
c.ap: As original Dragon lineage but darkened: near-black scales regardless of original coloration; cold aura; pale eye-light points; shadow-form aura.
c.note: This entry exists to note that Shadow Dragons belong to this transformation category despite their listing in the Dragon taxonomy tree.
c.eco: Shadow Dragon (see Dragons kingdom) ecology is necrological: formation, anchor, corpse supply, ritual maintenance, and exposure to living victims replace birth, feeding, and breeding.
c.beh: Shadow Dragon (see Dragons kingdom) behaviour depends on retained mind and binding. Some guard, some hunt, some repeat a death-memory, and some obey a maker, but each record should name the actual compulsion.
c.traitsText: Traits of Shadow Dragon (see Dragons kingdom) include absence of normal metabolism, resistance to fatigue and poison, dark perception, and the bodily or incorporeal signs described in the appearance record.
c.abilities: Shadow Dragon (see Dragons kingdom) abilities are those of its own undead condition: animation, fear, drain, disease, possession, or resilience only as the entry specifies.
c.bg: In the Throat, Shadow Dragon (see Dragons kingdom) is a hazard category for old deaths, sealed chambers, failed rites, and route histories that remain active.

c.g: A distinct undeath curse from the Zombie Strain — where the Zombie Strain reanimates dead tissue wholesale, the Skeletonization Curse strips biological matter and animates only the skeletal structure. The result is durable and resilient in ways that Zombies are not (no tissue to damage, no flesh to destroy) but correspondingly limited (no sensory apparatus, minimal problem-solving). Both curses may originate from the same negative-energy event but they behave as entirely separate viral lineages.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Skeletonization Curse vector. Distinguished from Zombie Strain by the stripping of biological tissue and animation of skeletal structure only; distinguished from Wight transformation by the absence of retained sentience. The Skeleton is a mechanical negative-energy automaton, not a reduced personality.
c.ap: Skeletonization Curse is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Skeleton—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.

c.g: The most common Undead in the material plane — a humanoid skeleton animated by negative energy, retaining no personality or memory, following simple directives or the compulsion of the curse. In the Gorge, Skeletons appear in the oldest worked passages where grey-folk died and were not recovered, animated by the ambient negative energy of the deepening Nadir. The Stonesong Heralds classify each by the passage where it was found and the approximate generation of its original life, using bone-mineral composition analysis.
c.tax: Skeletonization Curse product of humanoid lineage; no biological tissue; no personality retention; simple directive-following behavior; immune to all biological conditions; cold immunity from negative energy composition.
c.ap: Skeleton appears as complete articulated skeleton with missing soft tissue, weapon-worn joints, and curse-light in the sockets. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Skeleton does not reproduce biologically; it forms through animated bare bone. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Skeleton behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Skeleton ignores pain, resists piercing damage poorly compared with bludgeoning force, and follows simple commands without fatigue. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Skeleton matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.eco: Skeletonization Curse spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Skeletonization Curse has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Skeletonization Curse are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Skeletonization Curse grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Skeletonization Curse as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: The Ghoulification Curse reanimates dead tissue with a specific behavioural curse: compulsive consumption of flesh, particularly the recently dead. Unlike Zombie Strain, Ghoulification partially preserves the cognitive function of the progenitor — Ghouls problem-solve, remember, and plan at a basic level. Ghasts are the result of Ghoulification applied to an already-powerful individual or of the curse reaching a secondary progression; they retain more cognition and produce a paralysing stench as an additional curse-expression.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Ghoulification Curse vector. Produces two grades: Ghoul (basic curse expression, partially-preserved cognition, paralysing touch) and Ghast (secondary progression, stronger curse expression, paralysing stench, greater strength). Both grades retain enough personality for basic deception.
c.ap: Ghoulification Curse is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Ghoul, Ghast—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.

c.g: A Ghoul in the deep passage system — following the scent of the dead or recently injured. In the Gorge they are most numerous near the Catarium, the Pall-Brokers' interment facility, despite the Brokers' considerable efforts to prevent the Ghoulification Curse from operating in their records. The Pall-Brokers have a standing contract with a group of Hound Archons for Catarium perimeter management that they consider the best investment the clan has ever made.
c.tax: Ghoulification Curse product of humanoid lineage; decomposing tissue over intact skeleton; paralysing touch (non-undead paralysis); flesh-compulsion behavior; basic problem-solving retained.
c.ap: Ghoul appears as gaunt grey corpse with distended jaw, long tongue, black nails, and carrion-stained teeth. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Ghoul does not reproduce biologically; it forms through corpse-hunger transformation. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Ghoul behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Ghoul paralyzes living prey by claw or bite, tracks warm blood by smell, and spreads ghoul-hunger through contaminated feeding wounds. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ghoul matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.g: A Ghast — the secondary progression of Ghoulification, producing a larger and more cognitively capable Undead with a paralysing stench that operates independently of physical contact. Ghasts in the Throat occur where the Ghoulification Curse has had time to progress or where the original subject was exceptional. The Pall-Brokers document their locations with the same care they devote to the Fog Remnants, on the theory that something that was a person deserves to have its location recorded even after it has become something terrible.
c.tax: Ghoulification Curse of secondary-progression grade; larger and stronger than Ghoul; paralysing stench rather than touch as primary incapacitation; greater cognitive retention including basic deception capacity.
c.ap: Ghast appears as larger ghoul-form with stronger musculature, corpse-oil sheen, and a stench that makes breath discipline difficult. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Ghast does not reproduce biologically; it forms through greater carrion hunger. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Ghast behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.

c.abilities: Ghast paralyzes like a ghoul, emits supernatural carrion reek, commands lesser ghouls by hunger dominance, and resists turning more stubbornly. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ghast matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.g: The Wight Transformation produces the most cognitively intact of the common Undead — a Wight retains full personality, memory, and motivation, reorganized around the negative energy that now animates it. This makes Wights the most dangerous common Undead: they pursue their pre-death goals with their pre-death intelligence and the additional capacity to drain life energy from anything they touch. Wights arise from negative-energy environments, specific cursed deaths, or the deaths of particularly strong-willed individuals in locations saturated with negative energy.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Wight Transformation vector. Full personality retention distinguishes from Skeleton and Ghoul; life-drain capacity distinguishes from all non-undead; the Wight's goals are continuous with its pre-death life but are pursued with the impatience of something that has already died once. Creatures slain by a Wight's life-drain arise as Wights themselves.
c.ap: Wight Transformation is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Wight—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.

c.g: A Wight in the Throat — pursuing whatever it was pursuing when it died, with its full intelligence and its full personality, both now organized around the negative energy that has replaced its positive-energy vital force. The Pall-Brokers maintain a specific record type for Wight sightings that includes the individual's pre-death identity, clan affiliation, and last known objective, on the theory that knowing what a Wight wants is the first step in predicting what it will do.
c.tax: Wight of confirmed Gorge-passage residency; full personality retention; life-drain capacity; pre-death objective continuity. Distinguished from Stone-Walkers by the retention of full cognition (Stone-Walkers repeat only fragments).
c.ap: Wight appears as dry intact corpse with sunken eyes, weapon memory in the hands, and grave-cold breath. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Wight does not reproduce biologically; it forms through willful corpse bound to malice. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Wight behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Wight drains life with its touch, raises slain victims as lesser undead, and retains enough intelligence to ambush, retreat, and hold grudges. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Wight matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.g: Preservation-based Undead — the Mummification Curse binds a dead body to service through ritual preservation and a divine or magical curse laid on the preserved form. Unlike other undeath curses, Mummification is typically intentional and deliberate: someone created the Mummy, which means someone wanted the Mummy to exist and do something specific. What the Mummy does, specifically, is guard the thing it was placed to guard and destroy with extreme prejudice anything that violates that guardianship. In the Gorge context, the Stonesong Heralds have records of deliberately-placed Mummy guards in at least two sealed archive chambers whose locations are not disclosed.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Mummification Curse laid deliberately by a third party (priest, wizard, or ritual specialist). Preserved body rather than decomposed tissue; divine or magical curse as animating principle rather than pure negative energy; life-drain through touch; curse transmission to those it strikes; rot-based attack available. Distinguished from other Undead by the deliberate creation and guardian-mandate behavior.
c.ap: Mummification Curse is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Ancient Mummy—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.

c.g: A deliberate guardian Mummy placed in a sealed archive chamber by the Stonesong Heralds at some point in their institutional history — the creation record is itself in the sealed archive, which creates a documentation problem the Heralds have added to the list of things they are not resolving. The chamber's location is known to the senior Herald archivist; the purpose of the archive is known to no one currently alive; the Mummy's mandate is to prevent access, which it has done successfully.
c.tax: Mummification Curse product placed as deliberate guardian; mandate is access-prevention to a specific sealed archive; activation condition is approach within 30 m; mandate-bound rather than autonomous.
c.ap: Ancient Mummy appears as desiccated wrapped body with resin-black seams, preserved facial structure, ceremonial binding knots, and dustless posture. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Ancient Mummy does not reproduce biologically; it forms through ritual preservation and mandate. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Ancient Mummy behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Ancient Mummy spreads mummy rot, fixes intruders with dread, endures mundane weapons, and obeys the tomb mandate until the binding rite is broken. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ancient Mummy matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.abilities: Mummification Curse grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Mummification Curse as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: Undead whose animating force has loosened or abandoned the body. They are not corpse-animals; they are persistence, malice, memory, grief, or shadow held in a shape that can move through the world without normal flesh.
c.tax: Phylum of the Undead kingdom containing post-mortal transformations that operate chiefly as spirits, shadows, memory-forms, or negative-presence fields rather than as walking bodies. It is separated from Corporeal Undead because the original flesh is absent, incidental, or no longer the main vessel.
c.ap: An incorporeal undead is identified by absence as much as shape: no foot pressure, no breath fog unless it chooses to mimic it, disturbed dust without tracks, lamplight dimming through the torso, faces that lag behind movement, or a shadow that moves against the flame. Bones found nearby are remains, not the creature’s operating body.
c.eco: They form from death events rather than reproduction: betrayal, burial without rites, catastrophic fear, deliberate shadow-curse, or a will unable to leave a place. Their feeding is usually emotional, vital, or thermal: warmth, courage, memory, hope, or life-force, depending on the vector.
c.beh: Incorporeal undead behave according to the obsession that holds them together. Ghosts may warn, repeat, bargain, grieve, haunt, or protect; wraiths and specters stalk, isolate, and extinguish. A field team should identify motive before assuming hostility, because the wrong rite can strengthen the wrong dead.
c.traitsText: Shared traits include resistance to mundane weapons, passage through cracks or walls, silence or unnatural echo, chill fields, and sensory traces detectable by animals before people. They are vulnerable to consecration, names, unresolved rites, sunlight, silver, radiant force, or anchor destruction according to the entry.
c.abilities: Their abilities are non-anatomical: life drain, possession, terror, aging, memory replay, shadow spawning, dream intrusion, cold aura, or compulsion tied to a place or death-object. Ghost powers are identity-shaped; wraith powers are hunger-shaped.
c.bg: The Throat produces incorporeal undead where bodies cannot be recovered and stories cannot be finished: bridge falls, fog massacres, sealed galleries, and Nadir-adjacent deaths. The Pall-Brokers treat these entries as navigational warnings as much as metaphysical taxonomy.

c.g: The Wraith-Formation produces incorporeal Undead — beings of negative energy and retained consciousness with no physical body remaining. Wraiths form from violent or despair-driven deaths in negative-energy environments, or from deaths where the will to continue was stronger than any available positive energy to anchor it. They are incorporeal, passing through non-magical physical matter, and drain the vital force of living creatures on contact. Specters are lesser Wraiths. Ghosts are treated separately because they may retain any alignment and are defined by haunting anchors rather than evil negative-energy predation.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Wraith-Formation vector. Incorporeal state distinguishes from all other common Undead; negative-energy composition without physical substrate; three grades: full Wraith (free-roaming, powerful life-drain), Specter (lesser, weaker drain). Sunlight destroys Specters; harms but does not destroy Wraiths.
c.ap: Wraith-Formation is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Wraith, Specter—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.

c.g: A free-roaming Wraith of the deep passage system — the full expression of Wraith-Formation. In the Gorge they arise in the highest-death zones: the active clan conflict areas of the deep Fogreach, the old massacre sites of the Works expansion period, and the Cenotaph approach route. Their life-drain is immediate on contact and creates Specters from its victims rather than full Wraiths, in the specific cruel arithmetic of negative-energy propagation.
c.tax: Wraith of free-roaming grade; full life-drain capacity producing Specter-grade victims; passage through non-magical matter; destroyed by positive energy; harms living creatures by proximity as well as contact.
c.ap: Wraith appears as man-shaped absence with a hooded head, clawed shadow-hands, no visible feet, and darkness thickening around the rib position. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Wraith does not reproduce biologically; it forms through predatory negative-energy hunger. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Wraith behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Wraith drains life, creates lesser undead from victims, slips through cracks as living shadow, and is weakened by bright consecrated light. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Wraith matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.g: A Specter — a Wraith of lesser grade, typically arising from a Wraith's life-drain rather than from a direct death-with-negative-energy event. Specters retain less of the original personality and less life-drain capacity than full Wraiths. They flicker rather than flowing; they are bound to the general area of their formation rather than free-roaming; and they are destroyed by sunlight, which is unavailable in the Gorge but does not make them pleasant.
c.tax: Wraith-grade Undead of lesser expression; typically created by a Wraith's life-drain of a living creature; weaker than full Wraith; flickering rather than flowing incorporeal form; cannot move more than 90 m from formation site.
c.ap: Specter appears as torn humanoid outline with flayed light at the edges and hands that look longer than the original bones. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Specter does not reproduce biologically; it forms through violent death residue. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Specter behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Specter flies, passes through walls, drains vitality by touch, and repeats the circumstances of its death against intruders. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Specter matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.
