c.abilities: Wraith-Formation grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Wraith-Formation as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: The Ghost-Formation vector produces anchor-bound incorporeal undead without requiring evil alignment. It is grouped near Wraith-Formation for field convenience but kept taxonomically separate.
c.tax: Incorporeal undead formation driven by emotional, moral, or oath-bound anchor rather than predatory negative-energy identity.
c.ap: Ghost-Formation is a transformation pathway rather than a body by itself. Physical signs appear in its products—Ghost—through corpse preservation, shadow loss, hunger marks, anchor objects, altered bones, or curse residue.
c.eco: Ghost-Formation spreads or persists when its required death condition, ritual, bite, oath, burial state, or necromantic pressure is present. It has no breeding population; the lifecycle is infection, binding, transformation, maintenance, and release or destruction.
c.beh: Ghost-Formation has no personality apart from the undead it produces. The vector determines compulsions such as hunger, guarding, domination, predation, repetition, or anchor-return, while individual minds may survive only where the pathway allows.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits of Ghost-Formation are procedural: formation trigger, corpse substrate, retained mind, anchor object, contagion route, and removal condition. These are more important than superficial humanoid shape.
c.abilities: Ghost-Formation grants or produces powers through the resulting undead form: animation, drain, curse, regeneration, possession, fear, or disease only when that pathway specifically generates them.
c.bg: The Throat records Ghost-Formation as a vector so field workers can prevent recurrence rather than merely destroy the current body.

c.g: A Ghost is an incorporeal undead person anchored by unresolved identity, duty, trauma, love, oath, or place. Unlike Wraiths, Ghosts may be benign, hostile, confused, protective, or any alignment consistent with the retained self.
c.tax: DUAL ANCESTRY: living lineage ancestor + Ghost-Formation vector. Incorporeal undead defined by anchor, retained identity, and variable alignment. Distinguished from Wraiths by not being inherently evil and by resolution of the anchor rather than destruction alone.
c.ap: Ghost appears as recognizable image of the dead individual, translucent edges, intact face, and clothing or wounds matching the moment most central to the haunting. Identification should record anchor, corpse condition, light behaviour, tracks or lack of tracks, and whether the mind retained is personal, predatory, or merely commanded.
c.eco: Ghost does not reproduce biologically; it forms through anchor-bound personal persistence. Its “habitat” is the place, corpse, oath, hunger, tomb, or violence that sustains it, and removal requires addressing that condition as much as damaging the body.
c.beh: Ghost behaviour follows the sustaining condition: hunger seeks bodies, guardians hold thresholds, oath-bound dead repeat duties, and anchor-bound spirits return to the same memory. Intelligence ranges from automatic to fully personal depending on the form.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include the specific corpse or spirit body described here, lack of normal metabolism, immunity to ordinary exhaustion, dark-adapted perception, and vulnerability to consecration, disruption, or anchor-breaking appropriate to the form.
c.abilities: Ghost manifests, passes through matter, influences objects, possesses the living in moments of weakness, and reforms until the anchor is resolved. These powers belong to this undead form and should not be merged with ghosts, wraiths, vampires, skeletons, or other undead unless the entry names a hybrid pathway.
c.bg: In the Throat, Ghost matters because it preserves danger after death and can turn old passages into active hazards. Pall-Brokers classify it by formation, anchor, body, and release condition so response teams do not treat every dead thing alike.

c.eco: Undead do not breed in the ordinary sense. They arise from death under a cause: infection, necromancy, curse, predation, failed burial, phylactery, grief, shadow exposure, or Nadir pressure. Their ecology is therefore tied to battlefields, tombs, sealed passages, failed expeditions, and communities that cannot retrieve their dead.
c.beh: Behavior ranges from mindless repetition to full personhood. The useful field question is not “what class was it?” but “what sustains it?” Hunger, command, oath, grief, territorial memory, blood need, or deliberate lich-will determines how it hunts, speaks, bargains, or returns.
c.traitsText: Shared undead traits include immunity to many living frailties, disrupted relation to breath and sleep, resistance to poison and disease, and vulnerability to rites, radiant force, body destruction, anchor destruction, sunlight, or naming depending on the branch. Corporeal and incorporeal evidence must be recorded separately.
c.abilities: Undead abilities are branch-specific. Corporeal dead use bodies, bites, claws, disease, blood, preserved spellcraft, or phylacteries; incorporeal dead use fear, possession, life drain, haunting, cold, and memory. A ghost’s haunting powers should not be copied from a wraith’s evil hunger.
c.bg: The Throat is unusually favourable to undead because it hides bodies, preserves remains in mineral conditions, traps spirits in fog and stone, and allows entire expeditions to vanish without rites. This kingdom records the active consequences of unrecovered death.

c.g: A glamour-and-nature mutagenic pathway that rewrites living hosts toward fey logic without making them true Fey by ancestry. It behaves like a story-virus: promises, names, seasons, hospitality breaches, revel exposure, and wild thresholds become mechanisms of physiological change.
c.tax: Member of Mutagenic Transformations, separate from the true Fey kingdom and its biological/metaphysical descendants. Proto-Fey Transformation is a pathway imposed on other organisms; it creates fey-touched hosts, changelings, briar-bound bodies, oath-marked bloodlines, and seasonal morphs rather than standalone fey species.
c.ap: Alone it is invisible. In hosts it appears as asymmetrical beauty, impossible eye colors, leaf-vein capillaries, glassy teeth, thorn growth at scars, altered shadows, pointed or softened ears, too-long fingers, seasonal hair changes, and name-sensitive marks on skin or bone.
c.eco: The pathway spreads through bargains, true-name capture, fairy food, revel abduction, enchanted wounds, inherited oath-debt, and repeated residence at thresholds where ordinary ecology overlaps fey rule. It does not breed outside hosts; it makes host lineages narratively contagious.
c.beh: It imposes etiquette reflexes, taboo avoidance, bargain hunger, seasonal moods, playful cruelty, hospitality compulsion, and fascination with names or debts. The host remains alive and may remain morally ordinary, but the body begins answering fey rules before human rules.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include glamour sensitivity, iron aversion, name vulnerability, seasonal cycling, altered fertility, charm susceptibility or charm talent, and body changes tied to promise, mood, or place rather than ordinary heredity.
c.abilities: Common expressions are minor glamour, charm, vanishing at thresholds, oath-binding, dream intrusion, growth of thorns or flowers from wounds, and altered luck around hospitality. These are not inherited from true Fey unless the entry specifically records fey ancestry.
c.bg: In the Throat this pathway explains miners, guides, or children who are not Fey but begin obeying Feywild rules after contact with deep courts, crystal sprites, fog revels, or old bargains preserved in stone.

c.g: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.tax: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.ap: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.eco: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.beh: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.traitsText: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.abilities: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.bg: Feytouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.

c.g: A planar mutagenic pathway that rewrites hosts with the physics, ethics, or elemental pressures of another plane. It is not a fiend, celestial, elemental, or construct by itself; it is the transmissible condition by which a local body becomes hell-touched, abyss-scarred, radiant, shadowed, earthen, burning, wind-hollow, or otherwise planarly overwritten.
c.tax: Member of Mutagenic Transformations. Proto-Planar differs from true planar ancestry because it acts on pre-existing hosts rather than producing native outsiders. It belongs beside Proto-Lycanthrope, Proto-Undead, and Proto-Fey as a mutagenic evolution route that changes the carrier’s traits and abilities without being a standalone organism.
c.ap: In isolation it has no body. In hosts it produces planar residues: ember veins, frost-rimed breath, stone-thick skin, luminous blood, sulfur sweat, shadow leakage, gravity errors, feather or horn buds, eyes like portals, and wounds that heal into sigils or elemental seams.
c.eco: It spreads through planar breaches, possession scars, elemental storms, infernal contracts, celestial overexposure, Shadowfell saturation, portal dust, summoning failures, and long residence near unstable planar seams. The host is the habitat, and the plane supplies the mutagenic pressure.
c.beh: Behavioral effects follow the plane involved: lawful compulsion, predatory chaos, radiant protectiveness, despair, hunger for heat, fascination with open sky, stone-still patience, or aversion to running water. These are pressures, not proof that the host has become a true outsider.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits are planar residue, altered resistances, environmental dependence, aura shift, changed sensory range, and body modifications aligned to one plane. The original species remains taxonomically relevant because it determines what the pathway can successfully rewrite.
c.abilities: Expressions include elemental resistance, hazardous touch, minor teleport or threshold sense, aura pressure, shadow stepping, radiant flare, heat venting, stone grip, or planar speech fragments. Full outsider powers require true outsider ancestry or a much stronger transformation entry.
c.bg: In the Throat, Proto-Planar explains local beings altered by Roil heat, sealed portals, fiendish contracts, celestial relics, Shadowfell seepage, or elemental pressure without misclassifying them as native members of those kingdoms.

c.g: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.tax: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.ap: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.eco: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.

c.abilities: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.
c.bg: Planetouched are mutagenic kingdoms: transmissible or exposure-driven pathways that are not standalone organisms when isolated, but which rewrite the bodies, instincts, senses, resistances, and abilities of host organisms.

c.g: Fiendish Pathways are mutagenic routes by which a living host is rewritten toward Lower-Planar physiology without becoming a true fiend by ancestry. The pathway produces horns, sulfurous blood, heat-tolerance, predatory appetite, contract-instinct, abyssal frenzy, or other fiend-coloured traits in a body that still began as something else.
c.tax: Kingdom of mutagenic transformations subordinate to the Mutagenic Transformations domain. It differs from the Fiends kingdom under Outer Primordial Life because the affected organism is a transformed host, not a native lower-planar organism. Proto-Fiendtouched is the inferred species-level pathway beneath this kingdom.
c.ap: The pathway itself has no free-standing body. Hosts show asymmetrical horn buds, darkened sclera, heat-crazed skin, cloven or taloned extremities, barbed tails, sulfur sweat, fang crowding, infernal script scars, or abyssal overgrowth at healed wounds depending on the exposure source.
c.eco: Its ecology is infection and exposure ecology: infernal contracts, demon ichor, Lower-Plane portal ash, cursed weapons, long residence in fiend-haunted tunnels, and hereditary pact contamination can all transmit or awaken the pathway. It does not breed alone; it persists by occupying host lines and social bargains.
c.beh: Fiendish hosts tend toward appetite, hierarchy, cruelty, bargaining compulsion, territorial dominance, or destructive impulse according to the fiendish pressure involved. These are mutagenic tendencies rather than proof of true fiendish ancestry, and individuals may resist them with varying success.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include heat or poison tolerance, sulfurous scent, altered eyeshine, horn or claw growth, social fixation on debts or dominance, and wounds that seal with dark glassy tissue. The host species remains important because it determines the final shape of the mutation.
c.abilities: Common expressions include minor fire resistance, venom resistance, intimidation aura, claw or bite enhancement, pact-speech, rage surges, or limited curse-carrying touch. Full fiend powers such as native planar travel belong to true Fiends unless specifically recorded for a transformed host.
c.bg: The Throat records this kingdom because fiendish contamination is common around Shadow-Bound territories, sealed bargains, and old conflict routes. It prevents a horned grey-folk, beast, or kobold from being misfiled as a fiend when the body is actually a local host carrying a Lower-Plane mutagen.

c.g: Proto-Fiendtouched is the inferred species-level Lower-Plane mutagenic pathway from which infernal, abyssal, and mercenary-fiend host alterations are classified.
c.tax: Inferred species under Fiendish Pathways. It is not a fiend and not a phylum; it is the reconstructed transmissible condition that explains multiple host expressions.
c.ap: No independent anatomy is known. In hosts it appears as horns, fangs, claw thickening, sulfurous blood, ember veins, contract scars, or abyssal lesions.
c.eco: It persists through contracts, ichor exposure, hereditary pact marks, cursed bites, ritual wounds, and portal residues rather than through ordinary reproduction.
c.beh: It pushes hosts toward dominance, predation, legalistic bargains, compulsive temptation, or violent appetite according to the Lower-Plane source.
c.traitsText: Stable field signs include altered heat tolerance, fiendish scent, infernal or abyssal scar geometry, and host traits that intensify under anger or fear.
c.abilities: The pathway grants host-scaled fiendish expressions such as claw hardening, heat resistance, curse-touch, fear-scent, or pact-speech fragments.
c.bg: This entry anchors fiendish transformations so they are not confused with true Fiends from Outer Primordial Life.

c.g: Infernal Mutagenesis covers lawful Lower-Plane transformations caused by contracts, debt marks, oath-binding, and hell-forged contamination.
c.tax: Phylum under Fiendish Pathways; distinguished by contractual transmission, hierarchical instincts, and body changes that resemble devils without granting true devil ancestry.
c.ap: Hosts develop measured, symmetrical marks: paired horns, red-black heat fissures, contract-script scars, barbed tails, talon nails, and metallic-smelling blood.
c.eco: It spreads through pact inheritance, signed blood, hell-iron wounds, notarized curses, and service in infernal places. Transmission is often conditional on consent, debt, or broken law.
c.beh: Affected hosts become exacting, punitive, debt-aware, and unusually calm under coercion. Their aggression often follows rules, bargains, or perceived ownership.
c.traitsText: Traits include fire tolerance, legalistic memory, horn growth, barbed natural weapons, and resistance to ordinary intimidation.
c.abilities: Abilities include pact-binding speech, minor hellfire discharge, fear pressure, claw hardening, and curse clauses that trigger under broken agreements.
c.bg: Infernal mutagenesis matters in the Throat wherever sealed bargains and contract-devils have influenced clans, mercenary bands, or desperate expeditions.

c.g: Abyssal Mutagenesis covers chaotic Lower-Plane transformations caused by demon ichor, abyssal heat, hunger curses, and exposure to entities that alter flesh by proximity.
c.tax: Phylum under Fiendish Pathways; distinguished by unstable morphology, violent appetite, rapid mutation, and transformation without lawful contract structure.
c.ap: Hosts grow uneven horns, extra teeth, blistered scale patches, knotted muscle, mismatched eyes, claws that split the fingers, or mouths that scar open wider during feeding.
c.eco: It spreads through contaminated wounds, demon blood, cursed feasts, battlefields soaked in abyssal residue, and long residence near chaotic fiendish manifestations.
c.beh: Affected hosts become impulsive, hunger-driven, territorial, and unpredictable. Rage episodes are common, but cunning may remain intact in older carriers.
c.traitsText: Traits include pain tolerance, rapid scarring, predatory scent, poor wound symmetry, and bursts of strength that exhaust the host afterward.
c.abilities: Abilities include frenzy, toxic bite, heat venting, fear howl, flesh warping, and short-lived regenerative surges after feeding.
c.bg: Abyssal mutagenesis is relevant in the Gorge because Tanar'ri-lineage presences and war-grief corridors create repeated opportunities for flesh contamination.

c.g: Celestial Pathways are mutagenic routes by which a mortal or local organism is overprinted by Upper-Planar radiance, oath, mercy, judgment, or protective instinct. The result is not a true Celestial; it is a host whose body has begun answering celestial physics.
c.tax: Kingdom under Mutagenic Transformations. It differs from the Celestials kingdom under Outer Primordial Life because transformed hosts retain their original ancestry while acquiring radiant traits, vow-bound behavior, or sanctified resistances.
c.ap: The pathway has no isolated anatomy. Hosts may show luminous irises, feather or halo scars, silvered blood, symmetrical healing marks, voice harmonics, softened shadows, radiant birthmarks, or wounds that close as pale seams rather than red scar tissue.
c.eco: It spreads through relic exposure, angelic blood, holy oaths, divine intervention, sanctified springs, upper-planar light leaks, hereditary blessings, or long service under celestial compulsion. It persists through vows and bloodlines, not free-living reproduction.
c.beh: Hosts often develop protective compulsion, truth aversion to deliberate cruelty, heightened guilt response, courage under fear, and a tendency to interpose their bodies between danger and innocents. These pressures can be burdensome rather than pleasant.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include radiant eyeshine, disease resistance, fear resistance, oath sensitivity, pain response altered by protection, and aversion to desecrated places.
c.abilities: Abilities include healing warmth, protective aura, radiant flare, truth-sense fragments, fear suppression, sanctified touch, or the capacity to harm undead or fiendish tissue more cleanly than mundane force would allow.
c.bg: The Throat includes this kingdom to classify blessed, oath-marked, or relic-altered hosts without misfiling them as true Celestials. It also explains why certain non-celestial defenders survive conditions that should break ordinary bodies.

c.g: Proto-Celestialtouched is the inferred species-level Upper-Planar mutagenic pathway that produces radiant, oath-bound, or sanctified hosts.
c.tax: Inferred species under Celestial Pathways. It is a host-altering condition rather than a native Celestial organism.
c.ap: No free-standing body is known; in hosts it appears as luminous eyes, pale scar seams, featherlike marks, warm blood, and softened or golden shadows.
c.eco: It persists through relics, vows, blessings, celestial wounds, sacred water, hereditary divine debts, and exposure to Upper-Planar radiance.
c.beh: The pathway presses hosts toward protection, truth, mercy, discipline, or judgment depending on the celestial source and the host’s own nature.
c.traitsText: Traits include fear resistance, disease resistance, aura warmth, truth sensitivity, and radiance-reactive blood or bone.
c.abilities: Common abilities include minor healing, radiant flare, protective interposition, undead repulsion, and vow-bound endurance.
c.bg: This entry anchors celestial transformations so they remain distinct from true Celestials and from ordinary pious culture.

c.g: Radiant Mutagenesis covers bodily overprinting by holy light, relic charge, and upper-planar healing pressure.
c.tax: Phylum under Celestial Pathways; distinguished by visible radiance, sanctified repair, and anti-undead or anti-fiend expression.
c.ap: Hosts show golden eyeshine, silver scar tissue, unusually clean bone knitting, pale heat at wounds, and sometimes featherlike growths along the shoulders or forearms.
c.eco: It spreads through relic wounds, healing miracles, sacred springs, exposure to celestial weapons, or inherited blessing lines.
c.beh: Affected hosts become protective and difficult to intimidate, though not automatically kind or wise. The mutation favors courage and intervention.
c.traitsText: Traits include radiant tolerance, disease resistance, fear resistance, clean wound closure, and hostile reactions from undead tissue nearby.
c.abilities: Abilities include healing touch, radiant pulse, sanctified strike, and temporary resistance to necrotic or fiendish corruption.
c.bg: Radiant mutagenesis explains blessed defenders and relic-survivors in the Throat without promoting them to celestial ancestry.

c.g: Oathbound Mutagenesis covers celestial alteration through vows, judgment, mercy debts, and binding promises made under Upper-Planar attention.
c.tax: Phylum under Celestial Pathways; distinguished by behavioral law and metaphysical enforcement rather than obvious radiance alone.
c.ap: Hosts may look mostly unchanged except for vow-marks on the tongue, palms, sternum, or brow. Broken promises darken these marks; fulfilled ones brighten them.
c.eco: It spreads through sacred oaths, divine contracts, inherited vows, named guardianship, and bargains made with celestial witnesses.
c.beh: Affected hosts become reliable to the point of self-harm, unable to ignore certain calls for aid, and physically distressed by oathbreaking.
c.traitsText: Traits include vow sensitivity, supernatural endurance while fulfilling sworn duties, and vulnerability when acting against the oath structure.
c.abilities: Abilities include oath-sense, warding speech, protective compulsion, fear suppression, and brief surges of strength when defending the oath’s subject.
c.bg: This pathway is important for Throat societies whose defenders are bound by old vows that continue to alter descendants long after the original event.

c.g: Dire Pathways enlarge, intensify, and weaponize beast lines without necessarily producing a humanoid disguise. Inverted therianthropes were formerly placed here because of their animal origin, but are now treated as a lycanthropic phylum because their defining feature is therianthropic transformation rather than mere dire escalation.
c.tax: Kingdom under Mutagenic Transformations. Defined by dire growth, predatory intensification, and inherited beast mutation; excludes Inverted Therianthropes, which are classified under Lycanthropes.
c.ap: The pathway has no independent body. Hosts show oversized skulls, reinforced jaws, enlarged claws, thickened shoulders, bristled hides, exposed tusk roots, red or green eyeshine, accelerated growth rings in bone, and sometimes hands or vocal structures that imitate humanoids.
c.eco: It spreads through old breeding curses, Nadir pressure, predator-line intensification, magical famine, deliberate war-beast selection, contaminated dens, and hereditary animal strains. It reproduces through altered host lineages more often than through bite-infection.
c.beh: Dire hosts become territorial, fearless, hungry, and cunning beyond ordinary animal limits. In inverted forms such as ratweres, the animal learns to wear a humanoid outline as a lure rather than a curse suffered by a humanoid victim.
c.traitsText: Diagnostic traits include size inflation, bone thickening, scent dominance, unusual intelligence, hybrid vocal anatomy, and inherited aggression. The original animal lineage remains visible in skull, teeth, gait, and spoor.
c.abilities: Abilities include unnatural growth, disease carrying, pack command, ambush mimicry, enhanced scent, powerful bite, resistance to pain, and in inverted therianthropes the ability to assume or counterfeit humanoid form.
c.bg: The Throat needs this kingdom because not every beast-man shape is a lycanthrope. Ratweres, dire rats, and war-bred cave predators should not be filed as humanoid hosts with animal curses when their ancestry and transmission run from beast toward humanoid or monstrous expression.

c.g: Proto-Dire is the inferred species-level mutagenic pathway by which ordinary animal bodies are enlarged, hardened, made cleverer, or pushed toward humanoid imitation.
c.tax: Inferred species under Dire Pathways. It is not a beast species by itself; it is the reconstructed pressure that explains dire beasts, oversized predatory strains, and inverted therianthropes.
c.ap: No isolated anatomy exists. In hosts it appears as overgrown teeth, thickened skull sutures, shortened fear response, enlarged scent bulbs, heavy forequarters, and accelerated bone deposition.
c.eco: It persists through dens, bloodlines, magical famine, war-beast selection, Nadir pressure, predator curses, and contaminated carrion chains.
c.beh: The pathway favors aggression, territorial certainty, pack dominance, and predatory problem-solving. It can also produce unnerving patience in ambush species.
c.traitsText: Traits include size escalation, pain tolerance, stronger bite mechanics, unusually bold behavior, and inherited hostility toward competitors.
c.abilities: Abilities include growth surges, intimidation, scent tracking, disease retention, pack coordination, and partial form mimicry in advanced lines.
c.bg: This entry anchors dire transformations separately from Lycanthropes so animal-origin reversals such as ratweres have a correct home.

c.g: Dire Beasts are animal lineages enlarged and intensified by the dire pathway without acquiring a stable humanoid disguise.
c.tax: Phylum under Dire Pathways; distinguished by animal ancestry, reproductive host lines, and intensified size or aggression rather than humanoid host infection.
c.ap: Bodies retain their animal plan but exaggerate load-bearing bone, jaw mass, claw curvature, shoulder height, hide thickness, and eye shine.
c.eco: They persist by breeding true within altered dens or bloodlines. Scarcity, magical pressure, and predator competition select for the largest and boldest offspring.
c.beh: Dire beasts behave like apex animals with too little fear and too much memory. They learn routes, recognize weapons, and punish repeated intrusion.
c.traitsText: Traits include great size, heavy bone, enhanced smell, pain tolerance, territorial boldness, and strong disease or parasite tolerance.
c.abilities: Abilities include crushing bite, charge, pack-leading, scent pursuit, and survival after injuries that would disable ordinary animals.
c.bg: Dire beasts are common enough in Throat stories that they need separation from lycanthropic curse carriers and ordinary Beasts.

c.g: Demi-Creatures are stable hybrid or inheritance-bearing creatures whose bodies combine a primary host ancestry with a second lineage that has become taxologically consequential. Half-elves, half-dragons, half-fiends, and similar forms belong here when the inherited condition reliably changes anatomy, senses, lifespan, resistance, social reproduction, or innate powers.
c.tax: Kingdom under Mutagenic Transformations. Distinguished from ordinary mixed ancestry by stable, diagnosable trait inheritance; distinguished from Planetouched and Feytouched by direct lineage blending rather than planar exposure alone; distinguished from Lycanthropes by heredity rather than cyclical beast transformation.
c.ap: Demi-creature anatomy shows blended diagnostic structures: ears, eyes, teeth, horns, scales, breath organs, bone density, blood temperature, skin texture, or aura all carry signs of the secondary lineage. The body remains a body, not merely a temporary magical disguise.
c.eco: They persist through ancestry, magical gestation, ritual grafting of bloodlines, divine or draconic paternity, clan endogamy, and rare true-breeding communities. Some lines are first-generation only; others stabilize into populations.
c.beh: Behavior depends on both inherited instincts and host culture. A half-elf may negotiate between lifespans and kinship systems; a half-dragon may express territoriality, hoarding impulse, or elemental confidence even when raised outside dragon society.
c.traitsText: Traits include mixed morphology, altered senses, hybrid lifespan, inherited resistances, partial access to lineage-specific instincts, and social ambiguity. Field identification must separate culture from body: a person raised by elves is not a half-elf unless the physiology agrees.
c.abilities: Abilities may include darkvision, charm resistance, breath weapons, elemental resistance, wings, claws, inherited languages, aura expression, or limited magic depending on the contributing lineage. Abilities must be assigned by exact demi-line, not borrowed from all hybrids.
c.bg: This kingdom gives half-elves and half-dragons a correct home without misplacing them as normal Humanoids, true Dragons, Planetouched, or cultural castes. It also records hybrid lineages even when local grey-folk would not know the origin story.

c.g: Proto-Demi is the inferred species-level reconstruction of the first stable demi-creature condition: a host body capable of carrying a second lineage as inheritable anatomy rather than temporary magic.
c.tax: Inferred Species directly under Demi-Creatures. It is not a Domain or Kingdom; it is a reconstructed stem condition explaining stable half-lineages.
c.ap: Reconstructed anatomy would show mosaic inheritance: ordinary host skeleton with secondary-lineage changes in skull, blood, senses, skin, lifespan, or organ function.
c.eco: Its ecology is inferred from later demi-lines: persistence requires reproductive compatibility, magical mediation, or ritual stabilization strong enough to pass traits to descendants.
c.beh: Behavior cannot be reconstructed as a personality, but the condition likely produced identity strain between host society and inherited instincts.
c.traitsText: Traits include mixed inheritance, partial trait dominance, variable fertility, and stable enough physiology to produce recognizable descendants.
c.abilities: Abilities are inferred as partial inheritance rather than full powers: resistance, altered senses, minor innate magic, or body-plan additions depending on lineage.
c.bg: Proto-Demi exists as a bookkeeping species so demi-creatures need not be treated as ad-hoc exceptions.

c.g: Mortal Demi-Lineages are hybrids between closely compatible mortal peoples, especially humanoid or giant-adjacent lines whose offspring remain socially and biologically mortal.
c.tax: Phylum under Demi-Creatures for stable mortal-to-mortal hybrid lineages.
c.ap: Bodies are mostly host-shaped but show visible mixed inheritance in ears, eyes, facial bones, hair texture, stature, lifespan signs, or sensory organs.
c.eco: They persist through ordinary reproduction when fertility is stable and through border communities where multiple peoples intermarry.
c.beh: They often behave as cultural intermediaries, though personality remains individual rather than taxonomic.
c.traitsText: Traits include hybrid lifespan, mixed senses, social bilingualism when raised in both cultures, and partial resistance or aptitude inherited from parent lines.
c.abilities: Abilities are usually modest: darkvision, charm resistance, sleep resistance, unusual endurance, or inherited sensory acuity.
c.bg: They matter because the catalogue separates biological hybridization from guild, clan, or adoption categories.

c.g: Half-Elves are mortal demi-creatures combining human or similar mortal ancestry with elven ancestry.
c.tax: Genus of Mortal Demi-Lineages defined by elven-mortal physiological inheritance.
c.ap: They have mostly human-compatible bodies with elven ear structure, fine facial bones, reflective low-light eyes, lighter sleep requirements, and slower aging.
c.eco: They reproduce variably depending on parent community and magical history; some lines remain first-generation while others stabilize in border settlements.
c.beh: They tend to learn codes, languages, and manners across communities because their bodies make them visible in both.
c.traitsText: Traits include low-light sight, charm resistance, partial elven trance, extended lifespan, and mixed social scent markers.
c.abilities: Abilities include keen perception, resistance to magical sleep, partial trance recovery, and inherited aptitude for elven or mortal languages.
c.bg: Half-elves belong here rather than as a separate humanoid species because their defining status is stable mixed inheritance.

c.g: The common half-elf is the widespread human-elven hybrid form found in border settlements and mixed courts.
c.tax: Species of Half-Elf defined by balanced mortal and elven inheritance without strong sublineage specialization.
c.ap: Ears taper but do not reach full elven length; eyes reflect dim light; bones are fine but not fragile; aging slows after maturity.
c.eco: They persist through mixed households, diplomatic marriages, wanderer lineages, and communities where neither parent culture fully absorbs the other.
c.beh: They are not born diplomats, but many become social translators because their body forces constant interpretation.
c.traitsText: Traits include extended lifespan, low-light vision, sleep resistance, mixed facial structure, and high language acquisition.
c.abilities: Abilities include partial trance, keen hearing, charm resistance, and resistance to magical sleep.
c.bg: Common half-elves help the Throat catalogue avoid treating cultural liminality as a new species.

c.g: The wood half-elf carries wood-elven inheritance strongly enough to affect movement, scent, and environmental comfort.
c.tax: Species of Half-Elf distinguished by wood-elven sensory and movement traits.
c.ap: It has darker-adapted eyes, quiet tendons, slightly broader feet, and hair or skin tones that blend into bark, moss, or shadowed foliage when aboveground.
c.eco: It persists in forest-edge settlements, ranger clans, and mixed communities near old fey-wood routes.
c.beh: It is more likely than the common half-elf to prefer mobile, low-noise living, though upbringing matters.
c.traitsText: Traits include stealth aptitude, low-light vision, extended lifespan, and forest-route memory.
c.abilities: Abilities include quiet movement, charm resistance, partial trance, and improved tracking in wooded or fungal environments.
c.bg: Wood half-elves are relevant when Throat travelers interact with surface forest routes or fey-adjacent woodland clans.

c.g: Draconic Demi-Lineages are host creatures carrying true draconic inheritance as stable anatomy rather than worship, imitation, or kobold brood affinity.
c.tax: Phylum under Demi-Creatures for half-dragons and related draconic hybrids.
c.ap: Bodies show draconic overlay on a host skeleton: scale plates, horn buds, slit pupils, heavier sternum, clawed digits, altered lungs, tail growth, or wing structures depending on strength of inheritance.
c.eco: They arise through dragon ancestry, magical bloodline imposition, ritual incubation, or rare true-breeding hybrid houses.
c.beh: Behavior may include territorial confidence, hoard attachment, elemental pride, or dominance displays, but culture and individual choice remain important.
c.traitsText: Traits include elemental resistance, scale armor, darkvision, draconic language instinct, altered metabolism, and stronger bite or claw structures.
c.abilities: Abilities include breath weapon, elemental resistance, frightful presence in strong lines, claws, wings in some morphs, and inherited magical aptitude.
c.bg: They belong under Demi-Creatures rather than Dragons because they are hybrid host bodies, not true dragons, drakes, wyrms, or kobolds.

c.g: Half-Dragons are draconic demi-creatures in which dragon inheritance visibly restructures the host body.
c.tax: Genus of Draconic Demi-Lineages defined by stable host-dragon anatomy and lineage-specific elemental expression.
c.ap: A half-dragon has scale patches or plates, horn growth, draconic eyes, stronger jaw muscles, clawed hands, and a chest or throat modified to vent elemental breath.
c.eco: They persist through dragon-blooded families, magical breeding, pact inheritance, or isolated first-generation offspring.
c.beh: They often display territoriality and pride, but the exact personality depends on host culture and dragon lineage.
c.traitsText: Traits include elemental resistance, natural armor, darkvision, draconic speech instincts, and modified lungs or throat sacs.
c.abilities: Abilities include lineage breath weapon, claws, bite, resistance to the parent dragon element, and sometimes wings or fear aura.
c.bg: Half-dragons must be kept separate from kobolds and true dragons because their defining feature is hybrid host anatomy.

c.g: The red half-dragon carries red-dragon inheritance in a non-dragon host body.
c.tax: Species of Half-Dragon defined by fire affinity, red or dark copper scale plates, horned brow, heat-tolerant tissues, and a fire breath organ.
c.ap: The host silhouette remains visible beneath draconic traits: shoulders broaden, sternum deepens, jaw hinges strengthen, and heat vents appear along throat or nostrils.
c.eco: It persists by first-generation dragon ancestry or stabilized bloodline houses near volcanic, forge, or deep-heat environments.
c.beh: It tends toward dominance displays, heat-seeking lairs, and possessive guarding of valued objects, though upbringing can redirect those instincts.
c.traitsText: Traits include fire resistance, darkvision, scale armor, heat tolerance, draconic scent, and heavy bite.
c.abilities: Abilities include cone or line fire breath, clawing, biting, intimidation, and instinctive recognition of draconic hierarchy.
c.bg: Red half-dragons are not red dragons; their bones reveal a host skeleton carrying draconic overlay rather than a true-dragon body plan.

c.g: The silver half-dragon carries silver-dragon inheritance in a non-dragon host body.
c.tax: Species of Half-Dragon defined by cold affinity, silver-white scale plates, swept horn structures, reflective eyes, and cold breath adaptation.
c.ap: Its body shows dense pale scales over joints, a cooler resting temperature, strengthened lungs, and facial bones that sharpen toward a draconic muzzle line without becoming a true dragon skull.
c.eco: It persists through silver-dragon ancestry, oath-bound bloodlines, or magically stabilized descent in cold or highland communities.
c.beh: It is often socially curious and protective, reflecting silver-dragon tendencies, but culture and host ancestry remain decisive.
c.traitsText: Traits include cold resistance, darkvision, scale armor, controlled breath temperature, and draconic language instinct.
c.abilities: Abilities include cold breath, paralysis mist in some lines, claw and bite attacks, and protective aura expression.
c.bg: Silver half-dragons demonstrate why demi-creature classification must track inherited anatomy rather than moral reputation alone.

c.g: Deific Entities are divine or god-adjacent beings whose existence is not adequately explained by Inner, Outer, Far-Realm, Material, or mutagenic ancestry. They are placed in their own domain because their defining condition is worship-mediated divinity: power, body, memory, and influence are stabilized by portfolios, cults, oaths, names, relics, and cosmic offices rather than by ordinary descent.
c.tax: Domain-tier category for entities whose taxological definition is deific status rather than biological ancestry, planar species membership, or host mutation. A deity, demigod, sainted power, or vestigial god-remnant may possess bodies, avatars, and descendants, but the defining taxon is sustained divinity.
c.ap: No single anatomy defines the domain. Deific bodies range from avatar-shaped humanoids to colossal symbolic beasts, radiant intelligences, shrine-bound presences, and fossilized god-remnants. Field identification depends on impossible persistence of iconography: wounds become holy signs, shed material becomes relic, and footprints become sites of repeated ritual behavior.
c.eco: Deific entities persist through worship, sacrifice, oath-keeping, memory, divine inheritance, portfolio transfer, or relic anchoring. Their reproduction is usually theological rather than sexual: aspects, avatars, saints, scions, chosen vessels, and broken portfolios function as life stages or daughter-forms.
c.beh: Their behavior follows portfolio pressure. A sea god behaves like tide and bargain; a war god seeks contest; a harvest saint answers need; a dead god repeats the last commandment that still remembers it. Individual personality exists, but it is bent around the office that feeds and defines the being.
c.traitsText: Stable traits include portfolio fixation, worship sensitivity, iconographic consistency, avatar projection, relic production, oath-binding, miracle response, and exceptional resistance to ordinary death. Their spoor is liturgical: repeated symbols, involuntary kneeling, altered dreams, consecrated wounds, and matter that refuses profane use.
c.abilities: Abilities include miracles, avatar manifestation, divine curse and blessing, portfolio-scale perception, sanctification, relic creation, oath enforcement, resurrection or denial of resurrection, and limited rewriting of local natural law when worship or domain rights are strong enough.
c.bg: The Throat requires this domain because miners, Heralds, cults, and Nadir sects encounter divine residues that are not simply outer-planar outsiders. A fiend is a living Lower-Planar being; a god of chains may use fiends, create fiendish servants, or wear a fiendish mask without belonging to fiend taxonomy.

c.g: Deities are portfolio-bearing divine entities with active worship or cosmic office. They are the clearest members of the deific domain and include gods whose power can project avatars, answer prayers, mark sacred ground, and bind oaths across distance.
c.tax: Kingdom of active divine powers. Distinguished from Celestials and Fiends by worship-mediated authority rather than planar species descent, and from Archfey by portfolio office rather than faerie sovereignty alone.
c.ap: Deities manifest through chosen iconography: a smith-god as heated iron hands and hammer-shadow, a cavern-mother as a robed giantess of limestone milk, a war-power as a body made of banners, wounds, and bronze. Avatars may be slain without exhausting the deity.
c.eco: They persist through cults, temples, vows, names, relics, divine realms, sacred stories, and office succession. A deity can weaken when forgotten but may survive as a vestige if the portfolio remains needed by the world.
c.beh: Deities behave according to portfolio and worship. They bargain in commandments, answer through signs, and punish violation of sacred contracts. Even benevolent deities can be dangerous when their office is invoked improperly.
c.traitsText: Traits include prayer response, consecration, avatar projection, divine senses within portfolio, resistance to mundane death, and the ability to mark mortals as priests, champions, or taboo-breakers.
c.abilities: Abilities include miracles, blessings, curses, avatar manifestation, sanctified terrain, oath-binding, prophecy, healing, smiting, and portfolio-specific interventions such as storm-calling, harvest renewal, or death-denial.
c.bg: Deities appear in the catalogue because the Throat contains cult practice, divine absences, sacred fossils, and Nadir worship that cannot be responsibly filed as ordinary Outsider biology.

c.g: Throat Patron Gods are active gods whose worship has adapted to vertical caverns, stone routes, black water, and clan survival.
c.tax: Genus of active gods whose cults are native or strongly localized to the Throat.
c.ap: Avatars show cave-adapted iconography: lantern eyes, mineral crowns, rope scars, wet stone robes, or hands blackened by soot.
c.eco: They persist through clan rites, route blessings, grave songs, and seasonal offerings at safe ledges.
c.beh: They are protective but exacting, punishing oath-breakers and careless delvers more severely than outsiders expect.
c.traitsText: Traits include route-sense, shrine anchoring, stone consecration, and clan-mark miracles.
c.abilities: Abilities include safe-passage blessings, cave-in warnings, water purification, and curse marks on oath-breakers.
c.bg: They frame how grey-folk and allied peoples distinguish sacred hazard from ordinary ecology.

c.g: The lantern-mother is a cave-route patron worshiped by families who travel between lit stations.
c.tax: Species-level divine office represented by repeated mother-lantern iconography and safe-passage miracles.
c.ap: She appears as a hooded woman whose ribs glow like a covered lamp and whose feet never disturb dust.
c.eco: Her cult persists through lamp-lighting vows, child-naming rites, and offerings of oil at dangerous crossings.
c.beh: She is gentle to lost children and merciless to those who abandon dependents in the dark.
c.traitsText: Traits include warmth without smoke, recognition by children, and consecration of resting niches.
c.abilities: Abilities include light-calling, fear-calming, lost-path guidance, and curse brands on betrayers.
c.bg: Her worship explains why certain minor ledges remain inexplicably safe even near predator routes.

c.g: The stone oath-keeper is a contract and boundary god invoked when clans swear passage rights.
c.tax: Species-level divine office identified by stone tablets that crack only when oaths are broken.
c.ap: Manifestations resemble a faceless grey figure with slate skin and finger joints like chisel marks.
c.eco: The cult persists through carved vows, boundary stones, and public punishments for false witness.
c.beh: It is patient, literal, and incapable of forgetting an oath made in its name.
c.traitsText: Traits include perfect oath memory, tablet manifestation, and taboo marks visible under dust.
c.abilities: Abilities include oath-binding, geas-like compulsion, stone witness, and curse enforcement.
c.bg: It matters because route treaties in the Throat often rely on divine enforcement where military enforcement is impossible.

c.g: Divine Vestiges are remnants of gods whose worship, body, or portfolio has been broken but not erased.
c.tax: Phylum of damaged deific entities retaining enough divine structure to influence matter, dreams, or cult behavior.
c.ap: They appear as fragments: a voice in a fossil skull, a bleeding idol, a starved avatar, or a sacred wound in stone.
c.eco: Vestiges persist through relic anchoring, taboo memory, failed rituals, and portfolio fragments that no successor has claimed.
c.beh: Their behavior is repetitive, hungry, and often confused, because a vestige remembers commands after losing the god who understood them.
c.traitsText: Traits include relic dependence, dream intrusion, partial miracles, and unstable iconography.
c.abilities: Abilities include possession of relics, curse leakage, partial blessings, cult hallucination, and local sanctity or blight.
c.bg: They are crucial near the Nadir, where broken worship can masquerade as fiendish or undead influence.

c.g: Dead-God Remnants are vestiges anchored to a corpse, fossil, idol, or severed divine name.
c.tax: Genus of deific vestige whose remaining activity comes from a damaged divine body or name.
c.ap: They are identified by relic matter that behaves like a corpse and a shrine at once: bone that accepts offerings, bloodless wounds, and dust that forms symbols.
c.eco: They persist when worship has ceased but the divine body still holds office residue.
c.beh: They are obsessive, repeating final miracles or last prohibitions without context.
c.traitsText: Traits include corpse-relic anchoring, dream pressure, dead miracles, and fear in ordinary priests.
c.abilities: Abilities include curse seepage, miracle fragments, relic animation, and memory infection.
c.bg: Dead-god remnants prevent the catalogue from mislabeling every sacred corpse as undead.

c.g: A Nadir dead-god fragment is a buried remnant whose divine office has been crushed into the deepest stone.
c.tax: Species-level vestige known from recurring cult geometry, impossible relic heat, and prayer responses without a living god.
c.ap: It appears as black-veined bone or horn embedded in stone, surrounded by symbols no cult admits inventing.
c.eco: It persists on accidental worship, fear, and the pressure of the Nadir itself.
c.beh: It answers prayers by narrowing choices rather than granting gifts, pushing worshipers toward repetition.
c.traitsText: Traits include relic heat, dream geometry, cult attraction, and resistance to consecration by living gods.
c.abilities: Abilities include nightmare revelation, curse pressure, minor miracles, and the creation of taboo zones.
c.bg: This entry explains some Nadir cult behavior without forcing the fragment into Fiend, Undead, or Aberration taxonomy.

c.g: The bell-saint vestige is a broken protective divinity remembered only through alarm rites.
c.tax: Species-level vestige identified by bells ringing without hands when cave-ins, raids, or ghosts approach.
c.ap: It manifests as a cracked bronze halo, a child-sized handprint, or a sound from a bell that no longer exists.
c.eco: It persists through emergency prayers and old bells kept by clans that have forgotten the saint’s name.
c.beh: It is protective but simple, warning of danger without explaining it.
c.traitsText: Traits include sound manifestation, protective taboo, and attraction to bronze or shell bells.
c.abilities: Abilities include alarm miracles, ghost-repulsion, falling-stone warnings, and momentary courage blessings.
c.bg: It helps distinguish benevolent divine residue from ordinary haunting.

c.note: Expanded branch: Deities now includes Major Deities, Minor Deities, Demigods, and Divine Vestiges.

eco: Use the root to move among origin systems, transformations, deities, and guide pages without implying that all entries share one biological ancestor.
beh: The root has no behavior. Its editorial role is to keep taxonomy, transformation pathways, divine entities, and reference material separated.
traitsText: Root-level traits are catalogue traits: stable IDs, rank badges, tags, generated entry numbers, tree filters, tabs, and validation marks.
abilities: Root-level abilities are interface abilities only: navigation, filtering, display, and validation. Creature abilities belong on specific entries.
bg: The root exists so the Throat record can be revised branch by branch without losing the distinction between taxa, transformations, deities, and guides.
